Since $A$ and $k$ are also constant, we can rearrange: $$q \cdot \frac{dx}{A} = -k \, dT$$ $$q \, dx = -kA \, dT$$ **Step 2 — Integrate Across the Wall:** Apply limits: at $x = 0$, $T = T_1$; at $x = L$, $T = T_2$. $$q \int_{0}^{L} dx = -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Integrals:** $$q [x]_0^L = -kA [T]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$ $$q \cdot L = -kA (T_2 - T_1)$$ $$q \cdot L = kA (T_1 - T_2)$$ **Step 4 — Final Heat Transfer Equation:** $$\boxed{q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}}$$ **Step 5 — Derive Temperature Profile (Linear Variation):** Starting again from Fourier's Law, using the value of $q$ from above: $$-kA\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$ Cancel $kA$ from both sides: $$-\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}$$ Integrate with $T(0) = T_1$: $$T(x) = T_1 - \left(\frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}\right) x$$ This is a **linear equation** in $x$ → **straight-line temperature profile** (key KTU takeaway). --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Composite Wall in Series Consider **3 layers** in series: Layer 1 (brick), Layer 2 (insulation), Layer 3 (plaster). Temperatures at interfaces are $T_1 > T_2 > T_3 > T_4$. **Step 1 — Write Fourier's Law for Each Layer:** Layer 1: $q = \dfrac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1}$ Layer 2: $q = \dfrac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2}$ Layer 3: $q = \dfrac{k_3 A (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}$ **Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Condition:** Heat flow is the **same** through every layer (continuity): $$\frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} = \frac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2} = \frac{k_3 A (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3} = q$$ **Step 3 — Define Thermal Resistances:** $$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A}, \quad R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A}, \quad R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A}$$ **Step 4 — Use Ohm's Law Analogy (Series):** $$q = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R_1} = \frac{T_2 - T_3}{R_2} = \frac{T_3 - T_4}{R_3} = \frac{(T_1 - T_4)}{R_1 + R_2 + R_3}$$ **Step 5 — Generalize for $n$ Layers:** $$\boxed{q = \frac{(T_1 - T_{n+1})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A}}}$$ **Step 6 — Interface Temperature Formula (Highly Tested in KTU):** For any interface $j$ (between layer $j$ and layer $j+1$): $$T_j = T_1 - q \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{j-1} \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$$ This lets you calculate the unknown temperature at any interface once $q$ is known. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example #1: Single Plane Wall (Model KTU Problem) > **Problem:** A concrete wall, 0.20 m thick, has a thermal conductivity of 0.85 W/m·K. The inner surface temperature is 35 °C and the outer surface is 5 °C. The wall area is 15 m². Calculate: > (a) The rate of heat transfer through the wall > (b) The temperature at the mid-plane of the wall > (c) The heat flux **Given Data:** - $L = 0.20$ m, $k = 0.85$ W/m·K, $A = 15$ m² - $T_1 = 35$ °C, $T_2 = 5$ °C **Solution (a):** Using plane wall equation: $$q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{0.85 \times 15 \times (35 - 5)}{0.20}$$ $$q = \frac{0.85 \times 15 \times 30}{0.20}$$ $$q = \frac{382.5}{0.20} = 1912.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.91 \text{ kW}$$ **Solution (b):** Temperature at $x = L/2 = 0.10$ m: $$T(0.10) = 35 - \left(\frac{35 - 5}{0.20}\right)(0.10)$$ $$T(0.10) = 35 - (150)(0.10) = 35 - 15 = 20 \text{ °C}$$ **Solution (c):** $$q'' = \frac{q}{A} = \frac{1912.5}{15} = 127.5 \text{ W/m}^2$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Example #2: Composite Wall with Interface Temperature (KTU Classic) > **Problem:** A furnace wall is composed of three layers: > - Layer 1 (Refractory brick): $L_1 = 0.115$ m, $k_1 = 1.4$ W/m·K > - Layer 2 (Insulating brick): $L_2 = 0.115$ m, $k_2 = 0.21$ W/m·K > - Layer 3 (Building brick): $L_3 = 0.230$ m, $k_3 = 0.70$ W/m·K > > Inside surface temperature: $T_1 = 760$ °C, Outside surface temperature: $T_4 = 50$ °C. Area $A = 1$ m². > Find: (a) Heat flow per m², (b) Interface temperatures $T_2$ and $T_3$ **Given Data:** $L_1 = 0.115$ m, $k_1 = 1.4$ W/m·K; $L_2 = 0.115$ m, $k_2 = 0.21$ W/m·K; $L_3 = 0.230$ m, $k_3 = 0.70$ W/m·K; $T_1 = 760$ °C, $T_4 = 50$ °C; $A = 1$ m². **Solution (a) — Heat Flow:** First, compute the thermal resistances per unit area (since $A = 1$ m²): $$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.115}{1.4} = 0.0821 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.115}{0.21} = 0.5476 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.230}{0.70} = 0.3286 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ Total resistance: $$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = 0.0821 + 0.5476 + 0.3286 = 0.9583 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ Heat flux (per m², since $A = 1$): $$q'' = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{760 - 50}{0.9583} = \frac{710}{0.9583} \approx 740.9 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Solution (b) — Interface Temperatures:** Temperature after Layer 1 (interface $T_2$): $$T_2 = T_1 - q'' \cdot R_1 = 760 - 740.9 \times 0.0821$$ $$T_2 = 760 - 60.8 = 699.2 \text{ °C}$$ Temperature after Layer 2 (interface $T_3$): $$T_3 = T_2 - q'' \cdot R_2 = 699.2 - 740.9 \times 0.5476$$ $$T_3 = 699.2 - 405.7 = 293.5 \text{ °C}$$ **Verification (Using $T_4$):** $$T_4 = T_3 - q'' \cdot R_3 = 293.5 - 740.9 \times 0.3286 = 293.5 - 243.5 = 50.0 \text{ °C} \checkmark$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Pattern Alert:** When asked for interface temperatures, **always show the verification step** ($T_4$ calculated should match the given value). Examiners award partial marks for this. --- ### 3.5 Worked Example #3: Composite Wall (Parallel Arrangement) > **Problem:** A wall 4 m high × 8 m wide consists of a 0.20 m × 8 m concrete section ($k_c = 1.4$ W/m·K) and a 0.20 m × 8 m brick section ($k_b = 0.70$ W/m·K) arranged side-by-side (parallel to heat flow direction). The inside temperature is 30 °C and outside is 10 °C. Calculate the total heat loss per hour. Neglect surface convection. **Given Data:** - Concrete: $A_c = 0.20 \times 8 = 1.6$ m², $k_c = 1.4$ W/m·K - Brick: $A_b = 0.20 \times 8 = 1.6$ m², $k_b = 0.70$ W/m·K - $L = 4$ m (height — heat flows vertically downward through this thickness) - $\Delta T = 20$ °C Wait — re-reading: "wall 4 m high × 8 m wide" with sections 0.20 m thick. The **0.20 m is the thickness** in the direction of heat flow, and the 4 m × 8 m gives the area. The 0.20 m × 8 m sections are **side by side horizontally** (parallel heat flow paths). **Re-clarification:** Each section has: - Thickness (heat flow direction) = 4 m? No — if "0.20 m × 8 m" is the section dimension, then 0.20 m is the thickness. Let me re-interpret: Each section is 0.20 m thick (heat flows through this 0.20 m), and 8 m wide (parallel). So: - $L = 0.20$ m - $A_c = 4 \times ?$ ... Actually, the most sensible reading is: the wall area is partitioned. Let me use the standard textbook interpretation. **Standard interpretation:** - Wall: 4 m high × 8 m wide, total area = 32 m² - Composed of: half concrete, half brick (side by side vertically in the 4 m height) - Each layer thickness = 0.20 m (in the direction perpendicular to the wall = heat flow direction) So: - Concrete section: height = 2 m, width = 8 m, thickness = 0.20 m → $A_c = 16$ m² - Brick section: height = 2 m, width = 8 m, thickness = 0.20 m → $A_b = 16$ m² **Solution:** Heat through concrete: $$q_c = \frac{k_c A_c (T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{1.4 \times 16 \times 20}{0.20} = \frac{448}{0.20} = 2240 \text{ W}$$ Heat through brick: $$q_b = \frac{k_b A_b (T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{0.70 \times 16 \times 20}{0.20} = \frac{224}{0.20} = 1120 \text{ W}$$ Total heat loss: $$q_{total} = q_c + q_b = 2240 + 1120 = 3360 \text{ W} = 3.36 \text{ kW}$$ Heat loss per hour: $$Q = q_{total} \times 3600 = 3360 \times 3600 = 1.21 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 12.1 \text{ MJ}$$ --- ### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use What? | Problem Condition | Equation to Apply | Common KTU Trap | |---|---|---| | Single layer, constant k | $q = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ | Forgetting to convert L to meters | | Single layer, variable k | $q = \dfrac{k_m A \Delta T}{L}$ | Using $k_0$ instead of $k_m$ | | Multi-layer (different materials stacked) | Series resistance: $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$ | Treating parallel arrangement as series | | Side-by-side materials, same $\Delta T$ | Parallel: $q_{total} = \sum q_i$ | Adding resistances instead of heat flows | | Interface temperature needed | $T_j = T_1 - q \cdot \sum R_i$ (layers above $j$) | Forgetting area $A$ if $A$ varies | | Variable area across layers | Use $R_i = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | Cylindrical — NOT plane wall | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Heat Flow & Resistance Network for a Composite Wall The following Mermaid block illustrates the **physical composite wall** on the left, the **thermal resistance network** in the middle, and the **temperature gradient** on the right — the three perspectives examiners expect you to master. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["HOT FLUID
T_inf1"] --> B1["CONVECTION
h1 outside"] B1 --> C1["FOULING
R_f1"] C1 --> D1["LAYER 1
Brick, L1, k1"] D1 --> E1["INTERFACE 1
Temp T2"] E1 --> F1["LAYER 2
Insulation, L2, k2"] F1 --> G1["INTERFACE 2
Temp T3"] G1 --> H1["LAYER 3
Plaster, L3, k3"] H1 --> I1["FOULING
R_f2"] I1 --> J1["CONVECTION
h2 outside"] J1 --> K1["COLD FLUID
T_inf2"] R1["R1 = L1 / k1A"] -.resistance.-> D1 R2["R2 = L2 / k2A"] -.resistance.-> F1 R3["R3 = L3 / k3A"] -.resistance.-> H1 RC1["R_c1 contact"] -.resistance.-> C1 RC2["R_c2 contact"] -.resistance.-> I1 RCV1["1 / h1A"] -.resistance.-> B1 RCV2["1 / h2A"] -.resistance.-> J1 Q1["HEAT FLOW q
SAME IN ALL LAYERS
STEADY STATE"] -.drives.-> D1 Q1 -.drives.-> F1 Q1 -.drives.-> H1 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Electrical Resistance Analogy (Series vs Parallel) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SER["SERIES ARRANGEMENT (COMPOSITE WALL)"] direction LR TS1["T1 HOT"] --> RS1["R1 = L1/k1A"] --> TS2["T2 INTERFACE"] --> RS2["R2 = L2/k2A"] --> TS3["T3 INTERFACE"] --> RS3["R3 = L3/k3A"] --> TS4["T4 COLD"] EQ1["q = T1 - T4 / R1 + R2 + R3"] end subgraph PAR["PARALLEL ARRANGEMENT (SIDE BY SIDE)"] direction TB TP1["T1 HOT"] --> RP1["Path 1 k1A1"] TP1 --> RP2["Path 2 k2A2"] RP1 --> TPB["T2 COLD"] RP2 --> TPB EQ2["q = q1 + q2 + ... qn"] end SER --- PAR ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Temperature Profile Through a Composite Wall The temperature distribution through a composite wall consists of **straight line segments** with **different slopes** in each layer. The slope of each segment is proportional to the **thermal resistance** of that layer: $$-\frac{dT}{dx} = q \cdot \frac{R_i}{L_i}$$ - A **steep slope** (large temperature drop) → **high resistance** layer (e.g., insulation, low $k$) - A **gentle slope** (small temperature drop) → **low resistance** layer (e.g., metal, high $k$) | Position in Wall | Material | Slope of T(x) | |---|---|---| | 0 to L₁ | Brick | Moderate | | L₁ to L₂ | Insulation | **Steep** (largest $\Delta T$) | | L₂ to L₃ | Plaster | Gentle | > [!NOTE] > In a furnace wall, the **insulation layer** is intentionally placed near the **hot face** because its large temperature drop keeps the outer structural layers cool — a design principle called **"insulation on the hot side."** --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Fourier's law of heat conduction. Explain the physical significance of the negative sign in the equation.** **Model Answer:** Fourier's law of heat conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous material is directly proportional to the area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient along the direction of flow. $$q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$ **Physical significance of the negative sign:** The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the **direction of decreasing temperature**, i.e., from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature. Mathematically, the temperature gradient $\frac{dT}{dx}$ is negative when temperature decreases with increasing $x$ (since $T_1 > T_2$ for $x_1 < x_2$). The negative sign makes the heat flow $q$ come out as a **positive quantity**, consistent with the physical direction of energy transfer. **(3 marks)** ✅ --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define thermal resistance. Explain the electrical analogy for steady-state heat conduction through a composite wall.** **Model Answer:** **Thermal resistance** $R_{th}$ is the opposition offered by a material to the flow of heat, defined as the ratio of temperature difference to the heat flow rate: $$R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{q} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad \text{(units: K/W)}$$ **Electrical Analogy:** Heat flow $q$ is analogous to electric current $I$, temperature difference $\Delta T$ is analogous to potential difference $V$, and thermal resistance $R_{th}$ is analogous to electrical resistance $R$. Fourier's law reduces to **Ohm's law**: $$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad I = \frac{V}{R}$$ For a composite wall (layers in **series** along heat flow direction), thermal resistances add up like electrical resistances in series: $$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \cdots$$ $$q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{R_{total}}$$ This analogy allows complex heat conduction problems to be solved using **circuit analysis techniques**. **(3 marks)** ✅ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)** carrying **7 marks each**. Internal choice means you can attempt **either full Question A or full Question B**. --- ### **Question A** (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q. (a)** A composite wall of a furnace consists of three layers in series: - Layer 1: 0.10 m of fireclay brick ($k_1 = 1.05$ W/m·K) - Layer 2: 0.08 m of insulating brick ($k_2 = 0.21$ W/m·K) - Layer 3: 0.12 m of red brick ($k_3 = 0.70$ W/m·K) The inner and outer surface temperatures are 1100 °C and 60 °C, respectively. Calculate the heat loss per unit area through the wall and the interface temperatures. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate thermal resistances per unit area:** $$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.05} = 0.0952 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.08}{0.21} = 0.3810 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.12}{0.70} = 0.1714 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Total resistance:** $$R_{total} = 0.0952 + 0.3810 + 0.1714 = 0.6476 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Heat flux:** $$q'' = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{1100 - 60}{0.6476} = \frac{1040}{0.6476} \approx 1605.9 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q'' \approx 1606 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.61 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$ **Step 4 — Interface temperatures:** $$T_2 = T_1 - q'' \cdot R_1 = 1100 - 1605.9 \times 0.0952 = 1100 - 152.9 = 947.1 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 - q'' \cdot R_2 = 947.1 - 1605.9 \times 0.3810 = 947.1 - 611.8 = 335.3 \text{ °C}$$ **Verification:** $T_4 = T_3 - q'' \cdot R_3 = 335.3 - 1605.9 \times 0.1714 = 335.3 - 275.2 = 60.1$ °C ✓ **(7 marks)** ✅ --- **Q. (b)** A brick wall 0.30 m thick has thermal conductivity varying with temperature as $k = 0.85(1 + 0.001T)$ W/m·K, where $T$ is in °C. If the inner surface is at 200 °C and the outer surface at 50 °C, find the heat flux through the wall. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up Fourier's law with variable k:** $$q'' = -k\frac{dT}{dx} = -0.85(1 + 0.001T)\frac{dT}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:** $$q'' \, dx = -0.85(1 + 0.001T) \, dT$$ Integrate across the wall ($x: 0 \to L$, $T: T_1 \to T_2$): $$q'' \int_0^L dx = -0.85 \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (1 + 0.001T) \, dT$$ $$q'' \cdot L = -0.85 \left[ T + \frac{0.001 T^2}{2} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute limits** ($T_1 = 200$ °C, $T_2 = 50$ °C, $L = 0.30$ m): $$q'' \cdot L = -0.85 \left[ (T_2 - T_1) + 0.0005 (T_2^2 - T_1^2) \right]$$ $$q'' \cdot L = -0.85 \left[ (50 - 200) + 0.0005 (2500 - 40000) \right]$$ $$q'' \cdot L = -0.85 \left[ -150 + 0.0005(-37500) \right]$$ $$q'' \cdot L = -0.85 \left[ -150 - 18.75 \right] = -0.85 \times (-168.75) = 143.44$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $q''$:** $$q'' = \frac{143.44}{0.30} = 478.1 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q'' \approx 478.1 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **(7 marks)** ✅ --- ### **Question B** (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative* **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q. (a)** A cold storage wall is built in two layers: an inner layer of corkboard ($k = 0.043$ W/m·K) and an outer layer of concrete ($k = 0.85$ W/m·K). The wall area is 20 m², and the layer thicknesses are 0.10 m (cork) and 0.15 m (concrete). The temperature inside the cold storage is $-10$ °C and the outside ambient is 30 °C. The inside and outside convective heat transfer coefficients are $h_i = 8$ W/m²·K and $h_o = 15$ W/m²·K, respectively. Calculate the total resistance, heat leakage rate, and the temperature at the cork-concrete interface. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate all thermal resistances (including convection):** Inside convection: $$R_i = \frac{1}{h_i A} = \frac{1}{8 \times 20} = \frac{1}{160} = 0.00625 \text{ K/W}$$ Cork layer: $$R_{cork} = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.10}{0.043 \times 20} = \frac{0.10}{0.86} = 0.1163 \text{ K/W}$$ Concrete layer: $$R_{conc} = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.15}{0.85 \times 20} = \frac{0.15}{17.0} = 0.00882 \text{ K/W}$$ Outside convection: $$R_o = \frac{1}{h_o A} = \frac{1}{15 \times 20} = \frac{1}{300} = 0.00333 \text{ K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Total resistance:** $$R_{total} = R_i + R_{cork} + R_{conc} + R_o$$ $$R_{total} = 0.00625 + 0.1163 + 0.00882 + 0.00333 = 0.1347 \text{ K/W}$$ **Step 3 — Heat leakage rate:** $$\Delta T = 30 - (-10) = 40 \text{ K}$$ $$q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{total}} = \frac{40}{0.1347} = 296.9 \text{ W} \approx 297 \text{ W}$$ **Step 4 — Interface temperature (cork-concrete, between Layer 1 and Layer 2):** The cork layer is the inner layer (between inside air and interface). The interface temperature $T_2$ is the temperature on the **concrete side** of the cork. So we drop temperature from the inside air through $R_i$ and $R_{cork}$: $$T_2 = T_{inside} - q(R_i + R_{cork})$$ $$T_2 = -10 - 296.9 \times (0.00625 + 0.1163)$$ $$T_2 = -10 - 296.9 \times 0.1225$$ $$T_2 = -10 - 36.4 = -46.4 \text{ °C}$$ **Verification:** Outside surface $T_o = T_2 - q(R_{conc} + R_o) = -46.4 - 296.9(0.01215) = -46.4 - 3.6 = -50.0$ °C... wait, let me re-check. Re-verify: $T_{outside surface} = T_{inside} - q \cdot R_{total} = -10 - 296.9 \times 0.1347 = -10 - 40.0 = -50.0$ °C? But the problem says outside ambient is 30 °C. The 30 °C is the **fluid** temperature, not the wall surface. The wall outer surface will be different from 30 °C. Let me re-verify the heat flow equation: $q = \frac{T_{o,ambient} - T_{i,ambient}}{R_{total}} = \frac{30 - (-10)}{0.1347} = 296.9$ W ✓ Outer surface temperature: $T_{os} = T_{o,ambient} - q \cdot R_o = 30 - 296.9 \times 0.00333 = 30 - 0.99 = 29.0$ °C ✓ (reasonable, close to ambient) Inner surface temperature: $T_{is} = T_{i,ambient} + q \cdot R_i = -10 + 296.9 \times 0.00625 = -10 + 1.86 = -8.14$ °C (reasonable, close to inside air) The **cork-concrete interface** (between $R_{cork}$ and $R_{conc}$): $T_{interface} = T_{is} - q \cdot R_{cork} = -8.14 - 296.9 \times 0.1163 = -8.14 - 34.53 = -42.67$ °C Or equivalently: $T_{interface} = T_{os} + q \cdot (R_{conc} + R_o) = 29.0 + 296.9 \times (0.01215) = 29.0 + 3.61 = 32.6$ °C... There's an inconsistency. Let me re-check the calculation. Looking at the **temperature drop across each resistance**: - Across $R_i$: $\Delta T = 296.9 \times 0.00625 = 1.86$ K - Across $R_{cork}$: $\Delta T = 296.9 \times 0.1163 = 34.53$ K - Across $R_{conc}$: $\Delta T = 296.9 \times 0.00882 = 2.62$ K - Across $R_o$: $\Delta T = 296.9 \times 0.00333 = 0.99$ K Total: $1.86 + 34.53 + 2.62 + 0.99 = 40.0$ K ✓ (matches $\Delta T = 30 - (-10) = 40$ K) Inner surface: $T_{is} = -10 + 1.86 = -8.14$ °C Cork-concrete interface: $T_{i,interface} = -8.14 - 34.53 = -42.67$ °C Concrete-outer surface: $T_{os} = -42.67 - 2.62 = -45.29$ °C Then convection to ambient: $T_{ambient} = -45.29 + 0.99 = -44.3$ °C... This is **wrong**. Let me re-think the direction of heat flow. Heat flows from **outside (hot, 30 °C)** to **inside (cold, -10 °C)**. So $T$ decreases as heat moves inward. Starting from outside ambient $T = 30$ °C, moving inward: - After $R_o$ (drop of 0.99 K): $T_{os} = 30 - 0.99 = 29.01$ °C (outer wall surface) - After $R_{conc}$ (drop of 2.62 K): $T_{i,interface} = 29.01 - 2.62 = 26.39$ °C (cork-concrete interface) - After $R_{cork}$ (drop of 34.53 K): $T_{is} = 26.39 - 34.53 = -8.14$ °C (inner wall surface) - After $R_i$ (drop of 1.86 K): $T_{inside air} = -8.14 - 1.86 = -10.0$ °C ✓ So the **cork-concrete interface temperature** = **26.39 °C** (not -42.67 °C as I mistakenly calculated). $$\boxed{R_{total} = 0.1347 \text{ K/W}, \quad q = 296.9 \text{ W}, \quad T_{cork-concrete} = 26.4 \text{ °C}}$$ **(7 marks)** ✅ --- **Q. (b)** A furnace wall is constructed with 0.20 m of firebrick ($k = 1.40$ W/m·K), 0.10 m of insulating brick ($k = 0.20$ W/m·K), and 0.15 m of red brick ($k = 0.70$ W/m·K). The inner and outer surface temperatures are 1500 °C and 100 °C, respectively. If the wall area is 2 m², determine: (i) The total thermal resistance and rate of heat loss (ii) The temperature at each interface (iii) Show that the sum of interface temperature drops equals the total temperature difference (verification) (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Calculate per-layer resistances (with $A = 2$ m²):** $$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} = \frac{0.20}{1.40 \times 2} = \frac{0.20}{2.80} = 0.0714 \text{ K/W}$$ $$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} = \frac{0.10}{0.20 \times 2} = \frac{0.10}{0.40} = 0.2500 \text{ K/W}$$ $$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3 A} = \frac{0.15}{0.70 \times 2} = \frac{0.15}{1.40} = 0.1071 \text{ K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Total resistance and heat loss:** $$R_{total} = 0.0714 + 0.2500 + 0.1071 = 0.4285 \text{ K/W}$$ $$q = \frac{1500 - 100}{0.4285} = \frac{1400}{0.4285} = 3267 \text{ W} \approx 3.27 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Interface temperatures (heat flows from inside to outside):** $$T_2 = T_1 - q R_1 = 1500 - 3267 \times 0.0714 = 1500 - 233.3 = 1266.7 \text{ °C}$$ $$T_3 = T_2 - q R_2 = 1266.7 - 3267 \times 0.2500 = 1266.7 - 816.8 = 449.9 \text{ °C}$$ **Step 4 — Verification:** Temperature drops: - $\Delta T_1 = T_1 - T_2 = 1500 - 1266.7 = 233.3$ K - $\Delta T_2 = T_2 - T_3 = 1266.7 - 449.9 = 816.8$ K - $\Delta T_3 = T_3 - T_4 = 449.9 - 100 = 349.9$ K Sum: $233.3 + 816.8 + 349.9 = 1400$ K = $\Delta T_{total}$ ✓ $$\boxed{R_{total} = 0.4285 \text{ K/W}, \quad q = 3267 \text{ W}, \quad T_2 = 1266.7 \text{ °C}, \quad T_3 = 449.9 \text{ °C}}$$ **(7 marks)** ✅ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):** > 1. **Unit Error in Thermal Resistance:** Writing $R$ in m²·K/W when it should be K/W (per unit area vs. with area included). Always state the basis: "per unit area" or "for area = A". > 2. **Forgetting Area $A$:** If the problem gives a wall area, resistances are $L/(kA)$ — students often write $L/k$ and lose 1 mark. > 3. **Wrong Direction in Interface Temperature:** When heat flows inward (e.g., cold storage), the wall surface near the cold side is the *coldest*. Students sometimes get confused with sign convention. > 4. **No Verification Step:** Always end composite wall problems with a verification check ($T_{last}$ should match the given boundary temperature). Examiners award **partial marks** for this. > 5. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion:** A wall with **layers stacked** (brick on insulation on plaster) is **series**. A wall with **columns of different materials side by side** is **parallel**. This is the #1 conceptual mistake. > 6. **Mixing Up Mean $k$:** For variable $k$, use the **arithmetic mean** of $k$ at $T_1$ and $T_2$, not the value at one temperature. > 7. **Neglecting Heat Loss Direction:** $\Delta T$ is always positive in the formula $q = \Delta T / R_{total}$. Use $|T_1 - T_2|$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 📌 **High-Yield Rapid Revision — KTU 2024 Scheme** ✅ **Fourier's Law** is the foundation: $q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$ — always include the negative sign in derivations. ✅ **Plane wall (constant k)**: $q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ — temperature profile is **linear** in $x$. ✅ **Plane wall (variable k)**: Use $k_m$ (mean thermal conductivity between $T_1$ and $T_2$) or integrate the temperature-dependent $k(T)$. ✅ **Composite wall (series)**: Resistances **add**, area is usually the same → $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$. ✅ **Composite wall (parallel)**: Heat flows **add**, temperature drop is the same → $q_{total} = \sum q_i$. ✅ **Interface temperature** formula: $T_j = T_1 - q \sum_{i=1}^{j-1} R_i$ — verify with the last interface! ✅ **Electrical analogy** (Ohm's Law): $q = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$ — every composite wall is a series circuit. ✅ **Contact resistance** $R_c = \dfrac{1}{h_c A}$ — add in series when surfaces meet (e.g., pressed joints, gaskets). ✅ **Temperature profile** in a composite wall: **piecewise linear**, with slope $\propto R_i$ (steeper in high-resistance layers). ✅ **Units check** (every KTU numerical): $k$ in W/m·K, $L$ in m, $A$ in m², $T$ in K or °C (use difference), $q$ in W. ✅ **Convection resistances** (when surfaces exposed to fluids): $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ — add in series with conduction resistances. ✅ **Sign convention**: Heat always flows from high T to low T; $\Delta T$ in formulas is always **positive**. ✅ **KTU-favorite twist**: "Insulation on the hot side" — placing a low-$k$ layer near the hot face keeps outer layers cool and reduces heat loss most effectively. ✅ **Order of layers matters for interface temperatures** but **not for total heat loss** (same $R_{total}$ regardless of order — but the *interface temperatures* differ). --- > **END OF NOTES — Conduction Through Plane and Composite Walls | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT413 Module 1**

topics.title: Critical Thickness of Insulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Critical Thickness of Insulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Radius of Insulation (r_c):** The outer radius of insulation at which the **rate of heat transfer** from the insulated body to the surroundings attains a **maximum value**. Beyond this radius, further addition of insulation causes the heat loss to *decrease*, while below this radius, adding insulation paradoxically *increases* heat loss.

The critical thickness of insulation is therefore defined as:
$$\delta_c = r_c - r_i$$

where $r_c$ is the critical radius of insulation and $r_i$ is the inner radius (radius of the bare pipe/wire/object).

### 1.2 Why Does This Phenomenon Exist?

When insulation is added to a small-diameter cylindrical or spherical body, **two competing effects** occur simultaneously:

1. **Conduction Resistance Increases** → Tends to *reduce* heat loss (intuitive).
2. **Outer Surface Area Increases** → The surface exposed to convection becomes larger, which tends to *increase* heat loss.

> [!NOTE]
> For cylinders with $r_i \le r_o \le r_c$, the surface area effect dominates, so heat loss **increases** with insulation thickness. For $r_o > r_c$, the conduction resistance effect dominates, so heat loss **decreases**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a thin electrical wire carrying current:**
> If you try to insulate a *very thin* wire with a thick rubber coating, the wire will actually *overheat* faster than if it were bare! This is because the increased outer surface area "sweeps" more air for convection than the small conduction resistance gained. Only when the insulation becomes thick enough (beyond the critical radius) does it start to do its job. Conversely, for a **thick steam pipe** in a factory, the diameter is already so large that any insulation (no matter how thin) will reduce heat loss.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Quantities (Highlighted)

- **Thermal Conductivity of Insulation ($k$):** Typically **0.025 – 0.5 W/m·K** (e.g., fiberglass, foam).
- **Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$):** Typically **5 – 100 W/m²·K** (e.g., natural convection in air ≈ 5–25; forced convection ≈ 25–250).
- **Critical Radius (Cylinder):** $r_c = \dfrac{k}{h}$ → for $k = 0.05$ and $h = 10$: $r_c = 5$ mm.
- **Critical Radius (Sphere):** $r_c = \dfrac{2k}{h}$ → for $k = 0.05$ and $h = 10$: $r_c = 10$ mm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Assumptions

Consider a long cylindrical pipe of outer radius $r_i$, surface temperature $T_s$, covered with insulation of outer radius $r_o$, thermal conductivity $k$, exposed to ambient temperature $T_\infty$ with convective coefficient $h$.

**Steady-state assumptions:**
- One-dimensional radial heat conduction through insulation.
- Constant thermal conductivity $k$ of insulation.
- Constant surface temperature $T_s$ at the inner boundary (high $h_i$ on the inside).
- Constant $h$ at the outer surface.
- Constant $T_\infty$ (lumped environment).

## 2.2 Derivation Logic – Why Maximum Occurs

The total thermal resistance between the surface and ambient is the **series combination** of:

$$R_{total} = R_{cond} + R_{conv}$$

For a cylinder of length $L$:

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_o L)}$$

So the total resistance for the cylinder is:

$$R_{cyl} = \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_o L h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> As $r_o$ increases:
> - $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ increases → $R_{cond}$ increases (reduces heat loss).
> - $r_o$ in denominator of $R_{conv}$ → $R_{conv}$ decreases (increases heat loss).
>
> These two opposing trends cause $R_{total}$ to first **decrease**, reach a **minimum**, and then **increase**. Since $Q = \Delta T / R_{total}$, a minimum in $R$ corresponds to a **maximum in $Q$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference)

| Geometry | Critical Radius $r_c$ | Heat Transfer Rate $Q$ (per unit length) | Condition for Heat Loss to Increase |
|:--------:|:---------------------:|:----------------------------------------:|:-----------------------------------:|
| **Cylinder** | $r_c = \dfrac{k}{h}$ | $\dfrac{q}{L} = \dfrac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_o}}$ | $r_i \le r_o < r_c$ |
| **Sphere** | $r_c = \dfrac{2k}{h}$ | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_i} - \dfrac{1}{r_o}\right) + \dfrac{1}{h r_o^{2}}}$ | $r_i \le r_o < r_c$ |
| **Plane Wall** | **No critical radius** | $q = \dfrac{T_s - T_\infty}{\dfrac{L}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h}}$ | Heat loss always decreases with insulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **plane wall**, both conduction resistance and convection resistance scale with the *same* area. Hence there is no competition, and adding insulation *always* reduces heat loss.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Electrical Cables & Wires:** Small diameter (e.g., 1–3 mm copper) means $r_i$ may be **less than $r_c$**. Insulating such wires with thick insulation is *counterproductive* — it leads to overheating and insulation failure. **Design rule:** For wires, use the thinnest insulation necessary for electrical safety.
2. **Steam Pipes & Industrial Furnaces:** Diameter is large (50–500 mm) so $r_i \gg r_c$, meaning any insulation immediately reduces heat loss. Maximum economic benefit from insulation.
3. **Cryogenic Storage Tanks & Refrigerated Piping:** Sphere/cylinder shapes common. The critical radius dictates the minimum thickness required to actually reduce boil-off losses.
4. **Hot Water/Heating Coils:** Important for HVAC engineering to optimize insulation thickness for energy conservation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation for a Cylinder

### Step 1: Set Up the Thermal Resistance Network

The heat transfer path from inner surface (at $T_s$) to ambient ($T_\infty$) consists of two resistances in **series**:

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{cond} + R_{conv}}$$

**Conduction resistance through the cylindrical insulation shell:**

$$R_{cond} = \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$

This is derived from Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates at steady state, where the heat flow through any cylindrical shell is constant.

**Convection resistance at the outer surface:**

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h A_o} = \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_o L)}$$

### Step 2: Express Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Length

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_o}}$$

### Step 3: Differentiate to Find the Maximum

To find the critical radius where heat transfer is maximum, differentiate $\dfrac{Q}{L}$ with respect to $r_o$ and set the derivative to zero. It is mathematically easier to minimize the denominator $f(r_o)$:

$$f(r_o) = \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \frac{1}{h r_o}$$

**Compute the derivative of $f(r_o)$ with respect to $r_o$:**

$$\frac{df}{dr_o} = \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{d}{dr_o}\left[\ln(r_o/r_i)\right] + \frac{d}{dr_o}\left[\frac{1}{h r_o}\right]$$

Apply the standard logarithmic derivative $\dfrac{d}{dr_o}\ln(r_o/r_i) = \dfrac{1}{r_o}$:

$$\frac{df}{dr_o} = \frac{1}{k r_o} - \frac{1}{h r_o^{2}}$$

**Set the derivative to zero for an extremum:**

$$\frac{1}{k r_o} - \frac{1}{h r_o^{2}} = 0$$

**Solve for $r_o$:**

$$\frac{1}{k r_o} = \frac{1}{h r_o^{2}}$$

$$h r_o^{2} = k r_o$$

$$r_o = \frac{k}{h}$$

### Step 4: Verify It Is a Maximum (Second Derivative Test)

$$\frac{d^{2}f}{dr_o^{2}} = -\frac{1}{k r_o^{2}} + \frac{2}{h r_o^{3}}$$

At $r_o = k/h$:

$$\frac{d^{2}f}{dr_o^{2}} = -\frac{1}{k (k/h)^{2}} + \frac{2}{h (k/h)^{3}} = -\frac{h^{2}}{k^{3}} + \frac{2 h^{3}}{k^{3}} = \frac{h^{2}}{k^{3}} > 0$$

Since $\dfrac{d^{2}f}{dr_o^{2}} > 0$, $f(r_o)$ has a **minimum** at $r_o = k/h$, which means $Q/L$ has a **maximum** there. ✓

### Step 5: Define the Critical Radius

$$\boxed{r_{c,cyl} = \frac{k}{h}}$$

### Step 6: Behaviour Analysis

| Range of $r_o$ | Behaviour of $R_{total}$ | Behaviour of $Q$ | Practical Implication |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $r_i \le r_o < r_c$ | $R$ decreases (surface area effect dominates) | $Q$ **increases** | Adding insulation **worsens** heat loss |
| $r_o = r_c$ | $R$ at **minimum** | $Q$ at **maximum** | Threshold thickness |
| $r_o > r_c$ | $R$ increases (conduction effect dominates) | $Q$ **decreases** | Adding insulation reduces heat loss |

## 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation for a Sphere

### Step 1: Set Up Resistances for the Sphere

**Conduction resistance through spherical shell:**

$$R_{cond} = \frac{1}{4\pi k}\left(\frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_o}\right)$$

**Convection resistance at outer spherical surface:**

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{h (4\pi r_o^{2})}$$

### Step 2: Total Heat Transfer Rate

$$Q = \frac{4\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_i} - \dfrac{1}{r_o}\right) + \dfrac{1}{h r_o^{2}}}$$

### Step 3: Define Denominator Function for Sphere

$$g(r_o) = \frac{1}{k}\left(\frac{1}{r_i} - \frac{1}{r_o}\right) + \frac{1}{h r_o^{2}}$$

### Step 4: Differentiate

$$\frac{dg}{dr_o} = \frac{1}{k r_o^{2}} - \frac{2}{h r_o^{3}}$$

**Setting to zero:**

$$\frac{1}{k r_o^{2}} = \frac{2}{h r_o^{3}}$$

$$h r_o^{3} = 2k r_o^{2}$$

$$r_o = \frac{2k}{h}$$

### Step 5: Critical Radius for Sphere

$$\boxed{r_{c,sph} = \frac{2k}{h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The sphere has a critical radius **twice** that of the cylinder (for the same $k$ and $h$), because in a sphere, the outer surface area scales as $r_o^{2}$, causing a stronger convection-driving effect.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Plane Wall | Cylinder | Sphere |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Critical radius exists? | **No** | **Yes**, $k/h$ | **Yes**, $2k/h$ |
| Heat loss with insulation | Always ↓ | ↓ only when $r_o > r_c$ | ↓ only when $r_o > r_c$ |
| Practical relevance | Building walls, slabs | Pipes, wires | Tanks, pressure vessels |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Insulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Insulation Application] --> B{Geometry?}
    B -- Plane Wall --> C[Heat loss always decreases with insulation]
    B -- Cylinder or Sphere --> D{Compare r_inner with r_critical}
    D -- r_i greater than r_c --> E[Adding insulation reduces heat loss]
    D -- r_i less than r_c --> F[Warning: Adding insulation INCREASES heat loss]
    F --> G[Use minimum insulation thickness required]
    E --> H[Apply economic insulation thickness]
    C --> I[End: Standard Insulation Design]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat Transfer Path in an Insulated Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Inner Surface T_s at radius r_i] -- R_cond conduction --> S2[Outer Surface T at radius r_o]
    S2 -- R_conv convection --> S3[Ambient T_infinity]
    R_cond[Resistance ln r_o over r_i divided by 2 pi k L]
    R_conv[Resistance 1 over h times 2 pi r_o L]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Heat Loss vs Insulation Radius Curve

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Heat Loss vs Outer Radius of Insulation"
    x-axis "r_o (mm)" [0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16]
    y-axis "Q/L (W/m)" 0 to 120
    line [40, 70, 95, 110, 115, 110, 95, 75, 55]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the chart above, the **peak** corresponds to the critical radius $r_c$. Heat transfer is **maximum** at this point. The curve is *rising* for $r_o < r_c$ and *falling* for $r_o > r_c$.

## 4.4 Engineering Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Identify k and h] --> L[Compute r_c = k/h for cylinder, 2k/h for sphere]
    L --> M{Know r_i of object?}
    M -- Yes --> N{r_i greater than r_c?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Insulation will reduce heat loss]
    N -- No --> P[Insulation will increase heat loss up to r_c]
    P --> Q[Choose thickness greater than r_c to obtain net reduction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "critical radius of insulation." Derive the expression for critical radius for a cylindrical pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The critical radius of insulation ($r_c$) is defined as the outer radius of insulation at which the heat transfer rate from the insulated body to the surroundings attains a **maximum value**.

For a long cylindrical pipe, the heat loss per unit length is given by:

$$\frac{Q}{L} = \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_o}}$$

Differentiating the denominator with respect to $r_o$ and equating to zero gives:

$$r_{c,cyl} = \frac{k}{h}$$

> **Mark Split (3):** Definition 1M, Expression 1M, Condition 1M.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Why does a plane wall have no critical radius of insulation, while cylinders and spheres do? Explain in 2–3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

In a plane wall, both the conduction resistance and the convection resistance are calculated using the **same surface area** $A$. As insulation thickness increases, the conduction resistance rises monotonically, but the convection resistance stays constant. Hence, the total resistance always increases, and heat transfer always decreases — there is **no maximum** point.

In a cylinder or sphere, however, the conduction resistance uses the inner surface area while the convection resistance uses the **outer surface area**, which grows as insulation is added. This opposing effect creates a **minimum in total resistance**, giving a **maximum in heat transfer**, i.e., a critical radius.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A 50 mm diameter steam pipe carries steam at 200 °C. The outside surface convective heat transfer coefficient is 10 W/m²·K, and the surrounding air temperature is 30 °C. The pipe is insulated with a material having thermal conductivity $k = 0.08$ W/m·K.

**(a)** Determine the critical radius and critical thickness of insulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the heat loss per unit length with and without insulation, taking the insulation outer radius as 60 mm. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Inner radius of pipe: $r_i = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- Steam temperature: $T_s = 200$ °C
- Ambient temperature: $T_\infty = 30$ °C
- Convective coefficient: $h = 10$ W/m²·K
- Thermal conductivity of insulation: $k = 0.08$ W/m·K
- Outer radius of insulation: $r_o = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m

**Part (a): Critical Radius and Critical Thickness**

Apply the critical radius formula for a cylinder:

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.08}{10} = 0.008 \text{ m} = 8 \text{ mm}$$

Critical thickness of insulation:

$$\delta_c = r_c - r_i = 8 - 25 = -17 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $\delta_c$ is **negative**, the bare pipe radius $r_i = 25$ mm is **already greater than the critical radius** $r_c = 8$ mm. Hence, **any insulation added will reduce heat loss**.

**Part (b): Heat Loss With and Without Insulation**

**Heat loss without insulation** (bare pipe, $r_o = r_i = 0.025$ m):

$$q_{bare} = \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{h r_i}} = 2\pi (0.025) (10) (170) = 266.9 \text{ W/m}$$

**Heat loss with insulation** ($r_o = 0.060$ m):

$$q_{ins} = \frac{2\pi (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h r_o}}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$2\pi (170) = 1068.14$$

Compute the conduction term:
$$\frac{\ln(0.060/0.025)}{0.08} = \frac{\ln(2.4)}{0.08} = \frac{0.8755}{0.08} = 10.943$$

Compute the convection term:
$$\frac{1}{10 \times 0.060} = 1.667$$

Total denominator:
$$10.943 + 1.667 = 12.610$$

Therefore:
$$q_{ins} = \frac{1068.14}{12.610} = 84.7 \text{ W/m}$$

**Comment on the Result:**

- Heat loss reduced from $266.9$ W/m (bare) to $84.7$ W/m (insulated) — a **68% reduction**.
- This is because $r_i \gg r_c$, so the system is already on the "decreasing" branch of the $Q$ vs $r_o$ curve.
- This confirms that for **large industrial pipes**, insulation is highly effective.

> **Mark Split (14):** Part (a) formula 2M, calculation 3M, conclusion 2M; Part (b) bare calculation 3M, insulated calculation 3M, comment 1M.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

A 3 mm diameter electrical wire has a surface temperature of 90 °C and is exposed to air at 30 °C with $h = 12$ W/m²·K. The wire is to be insulated with a material of $k = 0.18$ W/m·K.

**(a)** Calculate the critical radius of insulation and explain whether the bare wire or the insulated wire loses more heat. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the insulation outer diameter is increased to 12 mm, find the new heat loss per unit length and the percentage change compared to the bare wire. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Bare wire radius: $r_i = 1.5$ mm $= 0.0015$ m
- Wire surface temperature: $T_s = 90$ °C
- Ambient: $T_\infty = 30$ °C
- $h = 12$ W/m²·K
- $k = 0.18$ W/m·K
- Insulated radius: $r_o = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m

**Part (a): Critical Radius and Heat Loss Comparison**

$$r_c = \frac{k}{h} = \frac{0.18}{12} = 0.015 \text{ m} = 15 \text{ mm}$$

Since $r_i = 1.5$ mm $< r_c = 15$ mm, the wire is in the regime where adding insulation **increases** heat loss. The bare wire will actually lose **less** heat than the lightly insulated one.

**Heat loss from bare wire** ($r_o = r_i = 0.0015$ m):

$$q_{bare} = 2\pi r_i h (T_s - T_\infty) = 2\pi (0.0015)(12)(60) = 6.79 \text{ W/m}$$

**Part (b): Heat Loss with Insulation at $r_o = 6$ mm**

Numerator:
$$2\pi (60) = 376.99$$

Conduction term:
$$\frac{\ln(0.006/0.0015)}{0.18} = \frac{\ln(4)}{0.18} = \frac{1.3863}{0.18} = 7.701$$

Convection term:
$$\frac{1}{12 \times 0.006} = 13.889$$

Total denominator:
$$7.701 + 13.889 = 21.590$$

$$q_{ins} = \frac{376.99}{21.590} = 17.46 \text{ W/m}$$

**Percentage change:**

$$\% \text{ change} = \frac{q_{ins} - q_{bare}}{q_{bare}} \times 100 = \frac{17.46 - 6.79}{6.79} \times 100 = +157\%$$

The heat loss has **increased by approximately 157%** compared to the bare wire — confirming the critical radius paradox.

> **Mark Split (14):** Part (a) critical radius 2M, comparison explanation 3M, bare Q 2M; Part (b) Q with insulation 5M, percentage 2M.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (deducted marks shown in brackets):**
> 1. **Forgetting to compare $r_i$ with $r_c$** before drawing conclusions about heat loss [−2M].
> 2. **Using the sphere formula** $2k/h$ when the question asks for a cylinder, or vice-versa [−2M].
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — using $r_i$ in mm and $h$ in W/m²·K without converting radius to metres [−1M].
> 4. **Forgetting the negative critical thickness** means the bare surface is already above the critical radius — students wrongly say "critical thickness is positive" [−2M].
> 5. **In Step-by-step derivation questions**, students often skip the second-derivative test to confirm maximum — KTU expects it [−1M].

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Critical Thickness of Insulation**

- **Critical Radius of Insulation:** The radius at which heat loss is **maximum**, beyond which insulation starts reducing heat loss.
- **Cylinder formula:** $r_c = \dfrac{k}{h}$ (most commonly asked).
- **Sphere formula:** $r_c = \dfrac{2k}{h}$.
- **Plane wall:** **No critical radius** — heat loss monotonically decreases.
- **Paradoxical behaviour:** Adding insulation *increases* heat loss when $r_i < r_c$.
- **Insulation effectiveness** depends on whether $r_i$ is greater than or less than $r_c$.
- **Lower $k$** → smaller $r_c$ → easier to be in the "safe" regime (reduce heat loss).
- **Lower $h$** (e.g., natural convection in stagnant air) → larger $r_c$ → more likely to encounter the paradox.
- **Practical application — Electrical wires:** Small $r_i$ → high risk of overheating; use minimum insulation needed.
- **Practical application — Steam pipes:** Large $r_i$ → insulation always beneficial.
- **Derivative procedure:** Minimise the denominator of $Q$ expression OR maximise $Q$ directly. Set $\dfrac{dQ}{dr_o} = 0$.
- **Total resistance** = conduction resistance + convection resistance (series).
- **The conduction resistance** scales with $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ (cylinder) or $1/r_i - 1/r_o$ (sphere).
- **The convection resistance** scales with $1/r_o$ (cylinder) or $1/r_o^2$ (sphere).
- **Second derivative test** must be applied to confirm a maximum (positive second derivative of $f$ means minimum in $R$, i.e., maximum in $Q$).
- **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Most problems are cylinder-based; expect a 7-M numerical + 7-M derivation/practical split.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convection: Thermal Boundary Layer Concept, Individual and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Convection: Thermal Boundary Layer Concept, Individual and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Convection — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convection** is the mode of heat transfer that occurs between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas), where heat transfer takes place by the **combined action of molecular conduction within the fluid and the bulk (macroscopic) motion (advection) of the fluid particles**. Mathematically, convection is quantified using **Newton's Law of Cooling**, expressed as $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$, where $h$ is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** measured in **W/m²·K**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, convection is classified into three canonical regimes:

| Regime | Driver Mechanism | Engineering Example |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Forced Convection** | External agency (pump, fan, blower) | Air-cooled condenser, car radiator |
| **Natural (Free) Convection** | Buoyancy due to density difference (gravity) | Refrigerator cooling coils, building ventilation |
| **Mixed Convection** | Both forced and natural contribute comparably | Room with fan + heater |

### 1.2 Thermal Boundary Layer — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Boundary Layer (TBL)** is defined as the thin region of fluid adjacent to a solid heated (or cooled) surface, within which the **fluid temperature varies smoothly from the surface temperature $T_s$ at the wall to the free-stream (bulk) fluid temperature $T_\infty$ away from the surface**. The outer edge of this layer is conventionally taken at the location where $(T - T_s)/(T_\infty - T_s) = 0.99$, i.e., where the fluid has effectively reached 99% of the free-stream temperature.

The thickness of the thermal boundary layer is denoted by **$\delta_t$** (measured normal to the surface).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine placing a hot tawa (griddle) on a kitchen counter at rest.** The air directly touching the tawa heats up instantly and rises. But if you gently blow across it (forced convection), the hot air is swept away much faster and replaced by cool air. The thin invisible "skin" of air clinging to the tawa is your **thermal boundary layer**. The faster the air moves, the thinner this skin becomes, and the faster the tawa cools — because heat has less fluid "insulation" to conduct through. This is precisely why **$h$ increases with fluid velocity**.

For the **velocity boundary layer** ($\delta$, hydrodynamics) and the **thermal boundary layer** ($\delta_t$), the relative magnitudes are governed by the **Prandtl number**, $\Pr = \nu/\alpha$ (ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity).

- For gases ($\Pr \approx 0.7$): $\delta_t \approx \delta$
- For liquid metals ($\Pr \ll 1$): $\delta_t \gg \delta$ (heat diffuses faster than momentum)
- For oils ($\Pr \gg 1$): $\delta_t \ll \delta$ (momentum diffuses faster than heat)

### 1.4 Individual and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient — Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Individual Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$)** is the convective heat transfer coefficient at a single fluid–solid interface. It depends on the fluid properties, surface geometry, flow regime (laminar/turbulent), and the nature of convection (forced/natural).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)** is the **effective combined coefficient** that accounts for heat transfer through a composite wall or a system involving **multiple convective resistances on either side of the solid wall(s), plus the conductive resistance of the wall(s)**. It is defined such that the rate of heat transfer through the entire composite system is expressed as $Q = UA \Delta T_{overall}$, where $\Delta T_{overall}$ is the total temperature difference between the two extreme bulk fluids.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure for property tables:** $P = 1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity (natural convection):** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant (revisit later):** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314\ \text{kJ/kmol·K}$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thermal Boundary Layer — Conceptual Breakdown

When a fluid at free-stream temperature $T_\infty$ flows over a heated surface at $T_s$:

1. **At the wall** ($y = 0$): The no-slip condition (for velocity) AND the no-temperature-jump condition force $T = T_s$. Heat transfer across this interface occurs purely by **conduction** at the wall (because the fluid is stagnant in the differential limit).
2. **Diffusion layer**: Heat is conducted normal to the surface into the moving fluid by molecular collisions.
3. **Outer edge** ($y = \delta_t$): The fluid temperature reaches $T_\infty$, and the bulk flow carries the heat away by **advection** — no further conduction in the normal direction.
4. **Growth of $\delta_t$**: Downstream, $\delta_t$ grows parabolically in laminar flow over a flat plate: $\delta_t \propto \sqrt{x}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: At the wall, since the fluid velocity is zero, **heat transfer from the wall into the fluid is by pure conduction**. Hence, by Fourier's law applied at $y = 0$:
> $$q'' = -k_{fluid} \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$$
> This **defines** $h$ in terms of the wall temperature gradient — a fundamental KTU exam point.

### 2.2 Individual vs Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient — Why Both?

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality**: In real heat exchangers (car radiators, condensers, boilers, building walls), heat does not transfer from one fluid to another through a single surface. It passes through a sequence of resistances:
> **Hot fluid → Convective film → Tube wall → Possible scale/fouling → Cold fluid convective film**

Each of these steps has its own resistance. The **individual coefficient** $h$ describes ONE step; the **overall coefficient** $U$ combines ALL of them into a single effective coefficient. This is the engineering bridge between microscopic film theory and macroscopic heat exchanger design.

### 2.3 Electrical Analogy (CRITICAL KTU Concept)

| Thermal Quantity | Electrical Analogue |
|------------------|--------------------|
| Heat transfer rate $Q$ [W] | Current $I$ [A] |
| Temperature difference $\Delta T$ [K] | Voltage $V$ [V] |
| Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ [K/W] | Electrical resistance $R$ [Ω] |
| $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$ | Ohm's law $I = V/R$ |

**Thermal Resistance Definitions:**
- Convective: $R_{conv} = 1/(hA)$
- Conductive (plane wall): $R_{cond} = L/(kA)$
- Conductive (cylindrical): $R_{cond} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE — these are the highest-yield formulas for KTU Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---------|---------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling (definition of $h$) | W |
| 2 | $q'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat flux at a single surface | W/m² |
| 3 | $Q = UA\,\Delta T_{overall}$ | Overall heat transfer (definition of $U$) | W |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{UA} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Overall resistance (cylindrical, no fouling) | K/W |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{U A} = \sum R_i$ | Resistances in series add | K/W |
| 6 | $\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Overall resistance based on outer area | K/W |
| 7 | $U_i = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{A_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{A_i}{h_o A_o}}$ | $U$ referred to inner area (cylindrical) | W/m²·K |
| 8 | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ | Plane wall, referred to common area | m²·K/W |
| 9 | $R_{f,i} = \frac{1}{U_{dirty}} - \frac{1}{U_{clean}}$ | Fouling factor (additional resistance due to deposits) | m²·K/W |
| 10 | $r_{cr} = \frac{k}{h}$ | Critical radius of insulation for cylinder | m |
| 11 | $r_{cr} = \sqrt{\frac{kL}{h}}$ | Critical radius for small object/fin-like geometry | m |
| 12 | $\delta_t / \delta = \Pr^{-1/3}$ (approx) | Ratio of thermal to velocity BL for liquids | — |
| 13 | $q'' = -k_f \left.\partial T/\partial y\right|_w$ | Wall heat flux from conduction (defines $h$) | W/m² |
| 14 | $\Pr = \nu/\alpha = c_p \mu/k$ | Prandtl number definition | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fouling Factor ($R_f$)**: In real engineering systems, surfaces accumulate scale, rust, soot, biological growth, etc. These deposits ADD a thermal resistance. The fouling factor is defined as the **additional resistance** beyond the clean-surface resistance. **KTU commonly asks:** *Why is $U$ always smaller than the smaller of $h_i$ and $h_o$?* Because additional series resistances always reduce the overall coefficient.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Exchanger Design**: $U$ is the cornerstone parameter for sizing shell-and-tube, plate, and finned heat exchangers in power plants, refineries, and HVAC systems.
- **Building Insulation**: Overall $U$-value (in W/m²·K) is the metric used in green building codes (ECBC in India).
- **Critical Radius of Insulation**: For insulated pipes (e.g., steam lines), adding insulation actually INCREASES heat loss initially up to a critical radius $r_{cr} = k/h$, then decreases it. This counterintuitive result is a **KTU favorite** in derivation/numerical questions.
- **Fouling Monitoring**: Plant operators track $U$ over time; a declining $U$ indicates fouling and the need for cleaning (planned shutdown).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Individual $h$ to Overall $U$ for a Plane Composite Wall

Consider a plane wall of thickness $L$ and thermal conductivity $k$, separating a hot fluid (with $h_i$ on the hot side) from a cold fluid (with $h_o$ on the cold side). Bulk temperatures are $T_{\infty,i}$ and $T_{\infty,o}$.

**Step 1 — Write heat flux through each layer at steady state** (since $Q$ is constant through any cross-section, we can use $q'' = Q/A$):

- Hot fluid film: $q'' = h_i (T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i})$
- Wall conduction: $q'' = (k/L)(T_{s,i} - T_{s,o})$
- Cold fluid film: $q'' = h_o (T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o})$

**Step 2 — Express each interface temperature drop in terms of $q''$**:

$$T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i} = \frac{q''}{h_i}$$

$$T_{s,i} - T_{s,o} = \frac{q''\, L}{k}$$

$$T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{q''}{h_o}$$

**Step 3 — Add all three equations** (telescoping sum, since intermediate temperatures cancel):

$$T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = q'' \left[ \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the form $q'' = U\,\Delta T_{overall}$** and compare:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}}$$

**Interpretation**: For a plane wall, all areas are equal ($A_i = A_o = A$), so $U$ is independent of area reference. Thermal resistances add in **series** — exactly like electrical resistances in series.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Overall $U$ for a Cylindrical Pipe (with Multiple Resistances)

Consider a cylindrical pipe of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, length $L$, wall conductivity $k$. Hot fluid inside has $h_i$, ambient air outside has $h_o$.

**Step 1 — Heat transfer rates (constant at steady state)**:

$$Q = h_i (2\pi r_i L)(T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i})$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_{s,i} - T_{s,o})}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

$$Q = h_o (2\pi r_o L)(T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o})$$

**Step 2 — Divide each equation by its respective area to express each temperature drop**:

$$T_{\infty,i} - T_{s,i} = \frac{Q}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)}$$

$$T_{s,i} - T_{s,o} = \frac{Q \ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}$$

$$T_{s,o} - T_{\infty,o} = \frac{Q}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}$$

**Step 3 — Add and identify overall driving temperature difference**:

$$T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o} = Q \left[ \frac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)} + \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Define $U_i$ based on inner area $A_i = 2\pi r_i L$**:

$$\frac{Q}{A_i} = U_i (T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o})$$

Substituting:

$$U_i = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{r_i \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{r_i}{r_o h_o}}$$

Similarly, on outer area basis:

$$U_o = \frac{1}{\dfrac{r_o}{r_i h_i} + \dfrac{r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the critical difference from the plane wall**: For a cylinder, $U$ depends on WHICH area you refer it to. The product $U \cdot A$ is **invariant**: $U_i A_i = U_o A_o = (1/R_{total})$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Radius of Insulation for a Cylinder

This is a **classic KTU derivation**. Consider a bare cylinder of radius $r$ with insulation of outer radius $r_{ins}$ and thermal conductivity $k_{ins}$. The outside convection coefficient is $h$.

**Step 1 — Total resistance from inner surface (radius $r$) to ambient**:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_{ins}/r)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_{ins} L)}$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer rate**:

$$Q = \frac{T_s - T_\infty}{R_{total}} = \frac{2\pi L (T_s - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{\ln(r_{ins}/r)}{k_{ins}} + \dfrac{1}{h\, r_{ins}}}$$

**Step 3 — To find the maximum of $Q$ (or equivalently, minimum of $R_{total}$), differentiate $R_{total}$ with respect to $r_{ins}$**:

$$\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_{ins}} = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[\frac{1}{k_{ins}\, r_{ins}} - \frac{1}{h\, r_{ins}^2}\right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve**:

$$\frac{1}{k_{ins}\, r_{ins}} = \frac{1}{h\, r_{ins}^2} \implies \boxed{r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**:
- For $r < r_{cr}$: Adding insulation **INCREASES** heat loss (decreases $Q$? NO — increases $Q$!). Counterintuitive but true.
- For $r > r_{cr}$: Adding insulation **decreases** heat loss as expected.
- This happens because increasing $r_{ins}$ increases the outer surface area for convection ($2\pi r_{ins} L$), and this **area effect dominates** until the radius exceeds $r_{cr}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical example**: Electrical wires carrying current have a small radius. If $r < r_{cr}$, adding insulation makes them HOTTER, not cooler. This is why electrical wire insulation thickness is designed carefully.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Relationship Between Boundary Layer Thicknesses and $h$

For laminar flow over an isothermal flat plate, dimensional analysis (and exact solution) gives:

$$h_x = \frac{k_f}{x}(0.332)\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$$

For $\Pr \geq 0.6$ (most fluids except liquid metals). The local Nusselt number is:

$$\text{Nu}_x = \frac{h_x x}{k_f} = 0.332\,\text{Re}_x^{1/2}\,\Pr^{1/3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to thermal boundary layer**: Since $h_x = -k_f (\partial T/\partial y)_w / (T_s - T_\infty)$ and the boundary layer grows as $\delta_t \sim x / \sqrt{\text{Re}_x}$, one can show that $h_x \propto 1/\delta_t$. This means **thinner thermal boundary layer implies larger $h$**, which is the key physical link.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermal Boundary Layer on a Heated Flat Plate

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **physical structure and energy transport pathways** in a thermal boundary layer over an isothermal plate.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Stream Fluid at T infinity] --> B[Edge of Velocity BL at delta]
    A --> C[Edge of Thermal BL at delta t]
    B --> D[Inner Mixed Layer: Conduction plus Advection]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Wall at T s y equals 0]
    E --> F[Heat Flux q double prime equals h times T s minus T infinity]
    F --> G[Conduction through fluid film at wall]
    G --> H[Bulk advection carries heat downstream]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Series Thermal Resistance Network

This block diagram represents how heat traverses a composite system, emphasizing the **electrical-resistance analogy** — a KTU-favored visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOT[Hot Fluid Bulk at T inf i] --> R1[R conv i equals 1 over h i A i]
    R1 --> R2[R wall equals Ln r o over r i over 2 pi k L]
    R2 --> R3[R fouling inner equals R f i]
    R3 --> R4[R fouling outer equals R f o]
    R4 --> R5[R conv o equals 1 over h o A o]
    R5 --> COLD[Cold Fluid Bulk at T inf o]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Effect of Insulation Radius on Heat Loss (Critical Radius)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Bare cylinder radius r] --> P1{Compare r with r cr equals k over h}
    P1 -- r less than r cr --> P2[Adding insulation INCREASES heat loss]
    P1 -- r greater than r cr --> P3[Adding insulation DECREASES heat loss as expected]
    P2 --> P4[Q rises until r equals r cr then falls]
    P3 --> P4
```

### 4.4 Concept Map: From Microscopic Boundary Layer to Macroscopic $U$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Fluid Motion at Wall y equals 0 v equals 0] --> M2[Heat Transfer by Pure Conduction only]
    M2 --> M3[Fourier Law at Wall q double prime equals minus k partial T over partial y at wall]
    M3 --> M4[Define h equals q double prime over T s minus T infinity]
    M4 --> M5[Local h x depends on Re and Pr]
    M5 --> M6[Average h over surface]
    M6 --> M7[Combine convective plus conductive resistances]
    M7 --> M8[Overall U]
    M8 --> M9[Heat Exchanger Sizing and Building U value]
```

### 4.5 Functional Architecture: Fouling and Aging of a Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start: Clean Surface U high] --> S2[Operation Hours increase]
    S2 --> S3[Scale plus Soot deposit on walls]
    S3 --> S4[Additional Resistance R f]
    S4 --> S5[U decreases over time]
    S5 --> S6[Performance Drop Detected]
    S6 --> S7[Scheduled Cleaning and Restoration]
```

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define thermal boundary layer. How is its thickness $\delta_t$ defined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The thermal boundary layer is the thin region of fluid adjacent to a heated/cooled surface in which the fluid temperature varies from the surface temperature $T_s$ at the wall to the free-stream temperature $T_\infty$ away from the surface. The thermal boundary layer thickness $\delta_t$ is conventionally defined as the distance from the surface (measured normal to it) at which the ratio $(T - T_s)/(T_\infty - T_s) = 0.99$, i.e., where the fluid has effectively reached 99% of the free-stream temperature. Beyond $\delta_t$, the fluid is essentially at $T_\infty$ and no significant temperature gradient exists.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between individual heat transfer coefficient ($h$) and overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$).**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Individual $h$ | Overall $U$ |
|--------|----------------|-------------|
| Definition | Convective coefficient at a single fluid-solid interface | Effective coefficient for the entire composite system |
| Resistances included | One convective resistance only | All convective + conductive + fouling resistances in series |
| Magnitude | Typically large (10–25,000 W/m²·K) | Always less than the smaller of $h_i$ and $h_o$ |
| Application | Local analysis of a single surface | Heat exchanger and wall design |
| Equation | $q'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | $q'' = U\,\Delta T_{overall}$ |

**(Mark split: 1 mark each for definition, dependency, and magnitude/application distinction)**
