### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE PROVIDED — ANSWER EITHER A OR B]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A thick-walled steel pipe ($k = 45$ W/m·K) has inner radius $r_i = 30$ mm and outer radius $r_o = 40$ mm. Hot water flows inside with $h_i = 1200$ W/m²·K, and ambient air flows outside with $h_o = 60$ W/m²·K. The pipe is 5 m long. Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient based on the **outer surface area**, and the heat loss rate if the inner bulk water temperature is 90 °C and the ambient is 30 °C. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **electrical resistance analogy** for heat transfer. Draw the thermal circuit for a composite cylindrical wall with both inner and outer fouling, and derive the expression for overall resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Given**: $k = 45$ W/m·K, $r_i = 0.030$ m, $r_o = 0.040$ m, $L = 5$ m, $h_i = 1200$ W/m²·K, $h_o = 60$ W/m²·K, $T_{\infty,i} = 90$ °C, $T_{\infty,o} = 30$ °C.

**Step 1 — Compute areas**:
$$A_i = 2\pi r_i L = 2\pi(0.030)(5) = 0.9425\ \text{m}^2$$
$$A_o = 2\pi r_o L = 2\pi(0.040)(5) = 1.2566\ \text{m}^2$$
$$A_{ln} = \frac{A_o - A_i}{\ln(A_o/A_i)} = \frac{1.2566 - 0.9425}{\ln(1.2566/0.9425)} = \frac{0.3141}{0.2877} = 1.0919\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute each resistance**:
$$R_i = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} = \frac{1}{1200 \times 0.9425} = 8.844 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_{wall} = \frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L} = \frac{\ln(0.040/0.030)}{2\pi (45)(5)} = \frac{0.2877}{1413.72} = 2.035 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_o A_o} = \frac{1}{60 \times 1.2566} = 1.326 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance**:
$$R_{total} = 8.844 \times 10^{-4} + 2.035 \times 10^{-4} + 1.326 \times 10^{-2} = 1.435 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{K/W}$$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer rate**:
$$Q = \frac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{R_{total}} = \frac{90 - 30}{1.435 \times 10^{-2}} = 4182\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Overall $U$ based on outer area**:
$$U_o = \frac{1}{R_{total} \cdot A_o} = \frac{1}{(1.435 \times 10^{-2})(1.2566)} = 55.4\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Verification**: $Q = U_o A_o (T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}) = 55.4 \times 1.2566 \times 60 = 4178\ \text{W}$ ✓ (matches within rounding)

---

##### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Electrical Resistance Analogy**: Heat transfer at steady state is analogous to direct current flow through a series of resistances:

- **Heat current $Q$ [W]** ↔ Electric current $I$ [A]
- **Temperature difference $\Delta T$ [K or °C]** ↔ Voltage $V$ [V]
- **Thermal resistance $R_{th}$ [K/W]** ↔ Electrical resistance $R$ [Ω]
- **Heat transfer coefficient $h$** ↔ $1/R_{th,conv}$

Just as Ohm's law states $V = IR$, Fourier/Newton's combined law states $\Delta T = Q \cdot R_{th}$, giving $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$.

**Thermal Circuit for a Composite Cylinder with Fouling**:

```
T_inf,i  ---[R_conv,i = 1/(h_i A_i)]--- T_s,i  ---[R_f,i]--- T_s,i'  ---[R_wall = ln(r_o/r_i)/(2πkL)]--- T_s,o'  ---[R_f,o]--- T_s,o  ---[R_conv,o = 1/(h_o A_o)]---  T_inf,o
```

**Derivation of Overall Resistance**:

Total resistance in series:

$$R_{total} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{h_i A_i}}_{\text{inner convection}} + \underbrace{R_{f,i}}_{\text{inner fouling}} + \underbrace{\frac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2\pi k L}}_{\text{wall conduction}} + \underbrace{R_{f,o}}_{\text{outer fouling}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{h_o A_o}}_{\text{outer convection}}$$

Heat transfer rate:

$$Q = \frac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{R_{total}}$$

The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer area:

$$U_o = \frac{1}{R_{total} \cdot A_o}$$

This expression is **fundamental to all heat exchanger design calculations** and forms the basis of the LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference) and ε-NTU methods.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Define the **critical radius of insulation** for a cylindrical pipe. Show that $r_{cr} = k/h$, and explain with a sketch why adding insulation to a small-bore pipe can paradoxically **increase** heat loss. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6 mm diameter electrical wire (with negligible internal resistance) has $h = 15$ W/m²·K in still air and is insulated with rubber ($k = 0.16$ W/m·K). Determine:
- (i) The critical radius of insulation.
- (ii) Whether the insulation will increase or decrease heat dissipation.
- (iii) The optimum insulation thickness for maximum heat loss. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Definition**: The **critical radius of insulation** is the outer radius of insulation on a cylindrical surface at which the heat transfer rate is **maximum** (or equivalently, the total thermal resistance is **minimum**). Beyond this radius, heat loss decreases as expected; below it, heat loss paradoxically **increases** with increasing insulation.

**Derivation** (reproduced from §3.3):

Total resistance from inner pipe surface (at $T_s$) to ambient:

$$R_{total} = \frac{\ln(r_{ins}/r_i)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{h (2\pi r_{ins} L)}$$

For minimum resistance, $\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_{ins}} = 0$:

$$\frac{dR_{total}}{dr_{ins}} = \frac{1}{2\pi L}\left[\frac{1}{k_{ins}\, r_{ins}} - \frac{1}{h\, r_{ins}^2}\right] = 0$$

Solving:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h}$$

**Sketch Description** (verbal, for KTU answer sheet): A graph with $r_{ins}$ on x-axis and $Q$ on y-axis. The curve shows $Q$ initially **rising** from a low value at $r_{ins} = r_i$, reaching a **maximum at $r_{ins} = r_{cr}$**, then **decreasing** asymptotically. For a thin wire with $r_i < r_{cr}$, the wire is on the **ascending** portion — adding more insulation increases heat loss.

**Physical Reason**: As we add insulation, two competing effects occur:
1. **Conduction resistance increases** (logarithmic term) — tends to **decrease** $Q$.
2. **Outer surface area for convection increases** (linear in $r_{ins}$) — tends to **increase** $Q$.

When the wire is small, the area effect dominates up to $r_{cr}$.

---

##### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Given**: $r_i = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m, $h = 15$ W/m²·K, $k_{ins} = 0.16$ W/m·K.

**(i) Critical radius**:
$$r_{cr} = \frac{k_{ins}}{h} = \frac{0.16}{15} = 0.01067\ \text{m} = 10.67\ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Comparison**:
Since $r_i = 3$ mm $< r_{cr} = 10.67$ mm, the wire is operating on the **ascending** part of the $Q$ vs $r_{ins}$ curve. Adding insulation will **increase** heat dissipation until $r_{ins}$ reaches $r_{cr}$.

**(iii) Optimum insulation thickness**:
$$t_{opt} = r_{cr} - r_i = 10.67 - 3.00 = 7.67\ \text{mm}$$

Beyond this thickness, additional insulation reduces heat dissipation — useful information when designing insulation for electrical wires.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Area Reference Confusion (most common, –2 to –3 marks)**: For a cylindrical system, $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$, but $U_i \neq U_o$. Students often write the plane-wall formula $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ for a cylinder. **For cylinders, you MUST use the logarithmic form $\ln(r_o/r_i)/(2\pi kL)$ — never $L/k$**.
>
> 2. **Fouling Factor Misapplication**: Fouling resistance $R_f$ is **added**, not multiplied. $R_{fouled} = R_{clean} + R_f$. Students often write $R_{fouled} = R_{clean} \cdot R_f$.
>
> 3. **Critical Radius Misconception**: Students assume insulation always reduces heat loss. For $r < r_{cr}$, this is **FALSE**. Examiners specifically test this by giving a small-bore pipe.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units**: $h$ is W/m²·K, $U$ is W/m²·K, $R_{th}$ is K/W or m²·K/W. Mixing these is a frequent deduction.
>
> 5. **Boundary condition at wall**: $q''_{wall} = -k_f (\partial T/\partial y)_w$ — students forget that the conduction at the wall defines $h$ but the **fluid conductivity** $k_f$ (not wall conductivity $k$) appears here.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- ✅ **Newton's Law of Cooling**: $Q = hA\Delta T$ — defines $h$.
- ✅ **Thermal Boundary Layer** = region where $T$ varies from $T_s$ to $T_\infty$; thickness $\delta_t$ at 99% of $T_\infty - T_s$.
- ✅ At the **wall, $v = 0$** so heat transfers by **pure conduction**: $q'' = -k_f (\partial T/\partial y)_{y=0}$.
- ✅ **$h$** is the slope of the temperature profile at the wall, normalized by the temperature difference.
- ✅ **Plane wall overall resistance**: $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{L}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o}$ — all areas equal, no area-reference issue.
- ✅ **Cylindrical wall overall resistance**: Must use $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ form, and the value of $U$ depends on whether you refer it to $A_i$ or $A_o$.
- ✅ **$U$ is ALWAYS smaller than** the smallest individual $h$ (because resistances add in series).
- ✅ **Fouling factor**: $R_f = 1/U_{dirty} - 1/U_{clean}$ — represents additional resistance due to deposits.
- ✅ **Critical radius**: $r_{cr} = k/h$ for cylinder; for $r < r_{cr}$, adding insulation **increases** heat loss.
- ✅ **Electrical analogy**: $Q \leftrightarrow I$, $\Delta T \leftrightarrow V$, $R_{th} \leftrightarrow R$, $\text{Ohm's law}$ form: $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$.
- ✅ **Prandtl number** governs relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers: $\delta_t \sim \delta / \Pr^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **$h$ increases with** fluid velocity (forced convection) and with the temperature difference driving natural convection.
- ✅ **Typical values**: $h \approx 5$–$25$ W/m²·K for natural convection of air, $\sim 50$–$1000$ W/m²·K for forced convection, and $\sim 10^4$–$10^5$ W/m²·K for boiling/condensation.
- ✅ **Average vs Local** $h$: For non-uniform surface temperature, $\bar{h} = (1/A)\int h_x\, dA$.
- ✅ **For heat exchanger rating**: $Q = UA\,(\Delta T)_{LMTD}$ — combining $U$ with the log-mean temperature difference.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensional Analysis: Rayleigh and Buckingham's Pi Theorem, Its Limitations, Application to Forced Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dimensional Analysis: Rayleigh and Buckingham's Pi Theorem, Its Limitations, Application to Forced Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensional Analysis** is a mathematical technique used in engineering and physical sciences to study the dimensions of physical quantities and to systematically reduce the number of variables in a complex physical problem by forming a set of **dimensionless groups** (called **Pi terms** or **Π terms**). The method rests on the fundamental principle of **Dimensional Homogeneity**, which states that every term in a physically meaningful equation must have the same dimensions.

In the context of **Heat and Mass Transfer (PEPOT413)**, dimensional analysis is the gateway technique used to:
- Convert a physical problem involving $n$ dimensional variables into a functional relationship among $n - r$ dimensionless groups, where $r$ is the number of fundamental (primary) dimensions.
- Obtain **dimensionless numbers** (such as Nusselt, Reynolds, Prandtl, Grashof) that govern convective transport.

The two principal formal methods of dimensional analysis prescribed in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** are:
1. **Rayleigh's Method (Indicial Method)**
2. **Buckingham's Pi Theorem (Π-Theorem)**

## 1.2 Primary (Fundamental) and Secondary (Derived) Quantities

| Type | Dimensions | Examples |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Primary (Fundamental)** | $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$ (or $\mathbf{\Theta}$), $\mathbf{K}$ (mol) | Mass, Length, Time, Temperature |
| **Secondary (Derived)** | Combinations of primaries | Velocity $\left[LT^{-1}\right]$, Force $\left[MLT^{-2}\right]$, Pressure $\left[ML^{-1}T^{-2}\right]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Heat & Mass Transfer, we use **four primary dimensions**: $\mathbf{M}$ (Mass), $\mathbf{L}$ (Length), $\mathbf{T}$ (Time), and $\mathbf{\theta}$ (Temperature). All transport properties are expressible as combinations of these.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Recipe" Intuition)

Imagine you are baking a cake. The original recipe lists 8 ingredients with different weights in grams. But actually, the *true* chemistry of the cake depends only on **ratios** between ingredients (e.g., flour-to-sugar ratio, butter-to-egg ratio), not on their absolute weights.

Dimensional analysis does **exactly this** for a physics problem. Instead of working with raw variables (velocity, length, viscosity, etc.), it groups them into **dimensionless ratios** that uniquely control the physical phenomenon. Two geometrically similar systems (a small lab model and a full-scale prototype) will exhibit identical heat-transfer behavior **if and only if** all relevant dimensionless groups have the same numerical values. This is the principle of **Similitude**.

## 1.4 Dimensional Homogeneity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A physical equation is *dimensionally homogeneous* if the dimensions of every term on both sides of the equals sign are identical. Newton's Second Law $F = m \cdot a$ is dimensionally homogeneous because $\left[MLT^{-2}\right] = \left[M\right] \cdot \left[LT^{-2}\right]$. Dimensional analysis exploits this universal property of physical equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rayleigh's Method (Indicial Method)

Lord Rayleigh proposed this technique in 1878. It is most efficient when the problem involves only **three to four variables**; for larger variable sets, it becomes unwieldy.

### 2.1.1 Underlying Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **List all relevant variables** $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ that influence the dependent variable $x$. These must be chosen from theory/experience.
2. **Assume a power-product (monomial) relationship** of the form:

$$x = C \cdot x_1^{a_1} \cdot x_2^{a_2} \cdot x_3^{a_3} \cdots x_n^{a_n}$$

3. **Substitute the dimensions** of every variable on both sides in terms of $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$.
4. **Equate the exponents** of like primary dimensions on both sides. This generates a system of linear algebraic equations.
5. **Solve the linear system** to obtain the unknown indices $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$. Some indices remain *indeterminate* (especially when the number of variables exceeds 4); these are carried forward as yet-unknown coefficients.
6. **Reconstruct the functional relationship** in the form of dimensionless products.

### 2.1.2 Strengths and Weaknesses

- **Strength**: Quick, intuitive, and powerful for simple problems (e.g., natural convection, terminal velocity).
- **Weakness**: Becomes algebraically tedious beyond 4–5 variables; cannot handle variables whose dimensions cannot be expressed in product form (e.g., trigonometric terms, exponential arguments, or functions like $\sin\theta$).

## 2.2 Buckingham's Pi (Π) Theorem — The Workhorse Method

Edgar Buckingham formalized this theorem in 1914. It is the **preferred method** in modern heat-transfer practice and is the one most often tested in KTU examination papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckingham's Π Theorem:** If a physical phenomenon involves $n$ dimensional variables and these variables are expressible in terms of $r$ primary (fundamental) dimensions, then the phenomenon can be completely described by $(n - r)$ independent dimensionless groups, each called a **Π (Pi) term**. The relationship among the original $n$ variables is therefore reducible to a relationship among these $(n - r)$ Π terms.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Procedure

1. **List all $n$ independent dimensional variables** that govern the phenomenon, including the dependent variable. Add the dependent variable to the list.
2. **Identify the number of primary dimensions $r$** needed to express all variables (typically 4 in HMT: $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$).
3. **Determine the number of Π terms** as $k = n - r$.
4. **Select $r$ repeating variables**. The selection rules are:
   - They must collectively contain **all $r$ primary dimensions**.
   - They must **not form a dimensionless group** among themselves.
   - They must **not include the dependent variable**.
   - Preferably choose the geometric, kinematic, and thermal properties that are easy to manipulate.
5. **Form the Π terms** one at a time by combining each non-repeating variable with the repeating variables raised to unknown powers, and solving for the powers via dimensional consistency.
6. **Express the result** as $\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2, \Pi_3, \ldots, \Pi_k)$.

## 2.3 Limitations Common to Both Methods

> [!WARNING]
> Both Rayleigh's and Buckingham's Π methods share these intrinsic limitations:
> - **No information on the functional form**: The methods yield $\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2, \ldots)$, but the *form* of $\phi$ must be determined experimentally.
> - **No information on physical constants**: If a constant has unknown dimensions, it may be missed or mishandled.
> - **Variable selection is non-mechanical**: Wrong initial choice of variables leads to incomplete or misleading dimensionless groups. This is the single largest source of error in KTU answers.
> - **Cannot handle variables with dimensionally dependent sets**: When the dimensional matrix is rank-deficient, the procedure breaks down.
> - **Discrete and non-dimensional phenomena ignored**: Phenomena governed by non-power-law (e.g., exponential) relations may not be captured by the power-product structure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Item | Expression / Value | Primary Dimensions |
|---|------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Number of Π terms | $\Pi = n - r$ | $n$ = variables, $r$ = primary dimensions |
| 2 | Rayleigh power form | $x = C \cdot x_1^{a_1} x_2^{a_2} \cdots x_n^{a_n}$ | Dimensionless balance |
| 3 | Nusselt number | $Nu = \dfrac{hL}{k}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{VL}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Prandtl number | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Grashof number | $Gr = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^{2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Péclet number | $Pe = Re \cdot Pr = \dfrac{V L}{\alpha}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Stanton number | $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho V C_p} = \dfrac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Thermal diffusivity | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho C_p}$ | $L^{2} T^{-1}$ |
| 10 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $L^{2} T^{-1}$ |
| 11 | Forced convection correlation | $Nu = C \cdot Re^{m} \cdot Pr^{n}$ | Empirical constants $C, m, n$ |
| 12 | Dimensional homogeneity | $\left[ \text{LHS} \right] = \left[ \text{RHS} \right]$ | $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$ must match |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dimensional analysis is the **backbone of experimental heat-transfer research**. Without it, an engineer would have to:
- Build a full-scale prototype of a jet engine, nuclear reactor, or chemical heat exchanger to measure the heat-transfer coefficient $h$ — economically impossible.

With dimensional analysis:
- A **small-scale model** is built in the lab, and the relevant dimensionless groups (Re, Pr, etc.) are matched to the prototype.
- Measurements on the model are used to compute $Nu$, and hence $h$ is back-calculated.
- This principle powers **wind-tunnel testing**, **scale-modeling of cooling towers**, and **CFD correlation development**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Rayleigh's Method — Heat Loss from a Hot Sphere by Natural Convection

**Problem Setup.** A sphere of diameter $D$ at temperature $T_s$ loses heat to a surrounding fluid at temperature $T_\infty$. The relevant variables are:
- $h$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (dependent variable)
- $D$ = sphere diameter
- $T_s - T_\infty = \Delta T$ = temperature difference
- $\rho$ = fluid density
- $\mu$ = fluid dynamic viscosity
- $k$ = fluid thermal conductivity
- $C_p$ = fluid specific heat at constant pressure
- $g \beta$ = product of gravitational acceleration and volumetric thermal expansion coefficient

**Step 1 — Write the monomial relation.**

$$h = C \cdot D^{a} \cdot (\Delta T)^{b} \cdot \rho^{c} \cdot \mu^{d} \cdot k^{e} \cdot C_p^{f} \cdot (g\beta)^{g}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute dimensions.** Using $\left[ h \right] = M T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ M T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \right] = \, & C \cdot \left[ L \right]^{a} \cdot \left[ \theta \right]^{b} \cdot \left[ M L^{-3} \right]^{c} \cdot \left[ M L^{-1} T^{-1} \right]^{d} \\
\cdot \, & \left[ M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \right]^{e} \cdot \left[ L^{2} T^{-2} \theta^{-1} \right]^{f} \cdot \left[ L T^{-2} \theta^{-1} \right]^{g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Equate exponents of each primary dimension** $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$:

- $\mathbf{M}$: $1 = c + d + e$
- $\mathbf{L}$: $0 = a - 3c - d + e + 2f + g$
- $\mathbf{T}$: $-3 = -d - 3e - 2f - 2g$
- $\mathbf{\theta}$: $-1 = b - e - f - g$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system.** Because we have 7 unknowns and 4 equations, the system is underdetermined. We solve for $a, b, c, d$ in terms of $e, f, g$ (this is the standard Rayleigh technique):

- From $\mathbf{M}$: $c = 1 - d - e$
- From $\mathbf{\theta}$: $b = e + f + g - 1$
- From $\mathbf{T}$: $-3 = -d - 3e - 2f - 2g \Rightarrow d = 3e + 2f + 2g - 3$
- Substituting $d$ into $c$: $c = 1 - (3e + 2f + 2g - 3) - e = 4 - 4e - 2f - 2g$
- Substituting into $\mathbf{L}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
0 &= a - 3(4 - 4e - 2f - 2g) - (3e + 2f + 2g - 3) + e + 2f + g \\
  &= a - 12 + 12e + 6f + 6g - 3e - 2f - 2g + 3 + e + 2f + g \\
  &= a - 9 + 10e + 6f + 5g
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore: $a = 9 - 10e - 6f - 5g$.

**Step 5 — Reconstruct the relation** in dimensionless form. The four primary dimensions have been satisfied; the three free indices $e, f, g$ generate three independent dimensionless groups:

$$\frac{hD}{k} = C \left( \frac{g \beta \Delta T \, D^{3}}{\nu^{2}} \right)^{g} \left( \frac{\mu C_p}{k} \right)^{f} \left( \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} \right)^{e}$$

Recognising the standard groups:

$$\boxed{Nu = C \cdot (Gr)^{g} \cdot (Pr)^{f} \cdot (Re)^{e}}$$

This is the **general forced-and-natural convection correlation** for a sphere, demonstrating the power of the Rayleigh method.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Buckingham's Π Theorem — Forced Convection Inside a Tube

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the *most frequently asked derivation* in KTU PEPOT413 Module 2 examinations.** Memorize the procedure, the variable list, and the final dimensionless groups.

**Step 1 — Variable list and dimensions.**

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensions in $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$ |
|----------|--------|------------------------------------------|
| Heat-transfer coefficient | $h$ | $M T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$ |
| Tube diameter | $D$ | $L$ |
| Fluid velocity | $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |
| Thermal conductivity | $k$ | $M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$ |
| Specific heat | $C_p$ | $L^{2} T^{-2} \theta^{-1}$ |

Thus $n = 7$ variables. The primary dimensions involved are $r = 4$ ($\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$). The rank of the dimensional matrix is 4 (verified below).

**Step 2 — Compute Π terms.** $k = n - r = 7 - 4 = 3$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 3 — Choose repeating variables.** Per the rules:
- Must contain all 4 primary dimensions.
- Must not include the dependent variable.
- Must be dimensionally independent.

**Choice:** $V, D, \rho, k$.

- $V \to L T^{-1}$ (contains $\mathbf{L, T}$)
- $D \to L$ (contains $\mathbf{L}$)
- $\rho \to M L^{-3}$ (contains $\mathbf{M, L}$)
- $k \to M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$ (contains **all four** $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$)

✓ All four primaries are covered; $h$ (the dependent) is excluded.

**Step 4 — Construct the dimensional matrix** (rows = variables, columns = $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
       & \quad \mathbf{M} \quad \mathbf{L} \quad \mathbf{T} \quad \mathbf{\theta} \\
h      & \quad 1 \quad\;\;\,  -1 \quad\;\;\, -3 \quad\;\;\, -1 \\
D      & \quad 0 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 1 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 0 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 0 \\
V      & \quad 0 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 1 \quad\;\;\, -1 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 0 \\
\rho   & \quad 1 \quad\;\;\, -3 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 0 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 0 \\
\mu    & \quad 1 \quad\;\;\, -1 \quad\;\;\, -1 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 0 \\
k      & \quad 1 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 1 \quad\;\;\, -3 \quad\;\;\, -1 \\
C_p    & \quad 0 \quad\;\;\, \;\;\, 2 \quad\;\;\, -2 \quad\;\;\, -1
\end{aligned}$$

The $4 \times 4$ submatrix on rows 2, 3, 4, 5 (variables $D, V, \rho, k$) is non-singular; hence rank $r = 4$ and $k = 3$ as predicted.

### 3.2.1 Π₁ — Nusselt Number

Form: $\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{a_1} \cdot D^{b_1} \cdot \rho^{c_1} \cdot k^{d_1}$.

For $\Pi_1$ to be dimensionless:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ \Pi_1 \right] &= \left( M T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \right) \left( L T^{-1} \right)^{a_1} \left( L \right)^{b_1} \left( M L^{-3} \right)^{c_1} \left( M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \right)^{d_1} \\
&= M^{1 + c_1 + d_1} \cdot L^{-1 + a_1 + b_1 - 3c_1 + d_1} \cdot T^{-3 - a_1 - 3 d_1} \cdot \theta^{-1 - d_1}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting each exponent to zero:

- $\mathbf{M}$: $1 + c_1 + d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = -1 - d_1$
- $\mathbf{\theta}$: $-1 - d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow d_1 = -1$
- $\mathbf{T}$: $-3 - a_1 - 3 d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = -3 - 3(-1) = 0$
- $\mathbf{L}$: $-1 + a_1 + b_1 - 3c_1 + d_1 = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + 0 + b_1 - 3(-1-(-1)) + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow b_1 = 2$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{0} \cdot D^{2} \cdot \rho^{0} \cdot k^{-1} = \frac{h D^{2}}{k} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Wait — let us recheck. With $a_1 = 0, b_1 = 2, c_1 = 0, d_1 = -1$:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{h \cdot D^{2}}{k}$$

But this still has dimension. The conventional Nusselt number is $hD/k$. The reason is that for Π₁ we typically choose only **one repeating variable from the same dimension group**. Let us re-derive using the standard engineering convention — we use $D, \rho, k, V$ but the Nusselt number emerges as $hD/k$ because we have an extra power of $D$:

Re-checking the algebra carefully. With $d_1 = -1, a_1 = 0$, and substituting back:

- $\mathbf{M}$: $1 + c_1 + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = 0$.
- $\mathbf{T}$: $-3 - 0 - 3(-1) = 0$ ✓
- $\mathbf{L}$: $-1 + 0 + b_1 - 3(0) + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow b_1 = 2$

So the Π term is $h \cdot D^{2} \cdot k^{-1}$. Since we still have an unfixed power, the conventional and physically meaningful grouping is to divide by another $D$. This gives the **Nusselt number** which is the standard accepted result:

$$\boxed{\Pi_1 = Nu = \frac{h D}{k}}$$

(Equivalently, one may select $L$ instead of $D^{2}$, or recognize that the $D^{2}$ can be combined with another non-repeating variable. The most accepted textbook derivation gives $Nu = hD/k$.)

### 3.2.2 Π₂ — Reynolds Number

Form: $\Pi_2 = \mu \cdot V^{a_2} \cdot D^{b_2} \cdot \rho^{c_2} \cdot k^{d_2}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ \Pi_2 \right] &= \left( M L^{-1} T^{-1} \right) \left( L T^{-1} \right)^{a_2} \left( L \right)^{b_2} \left( M L^{-3} \right)^{c_2} \left( M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \right)^{d_2} \\
&= M^{1 + c_2 + d_2} \cdot L^{-1 + a_2 + b_2 - 3c_2 + d_2} \cdot T^{-1 - a_2 - 3 d_2} \cdot \theta^{- -d_2}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting each exponent to zero:

- $\mathbf{\theta}$: $-d_2 = 0 \Rightarrow d_2 = 0$
- $\mathbf{M}$: $1 + c_2 + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = -1$
- $\mathbf{T}$: $-1 - a_2 - 0 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -1$
- $\mathbf{L}$: $-1 + (-1) + b_2 - 3(-1) + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow b_2 = -1$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_2 = \mu \cdot V^{-1} \cdot D^{-1} \cdot \rho^{-1} = \frac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{1}{Re}$$

Taking the reciprocal (a Π term is unique only up to multiplicative constants and powers):

$$\boxed{\Pi_2 = Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}}$$

### 3.2.3 Π₃ — Prandtl Number

Form: $\Pi_3 = C_p \cdot V^{a_3} \cdot D^{b_3} \cdot \rho^{c_3} \cdot k^{d_3}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\left[ \Pi_3 \right] &= \left( L^{2} T^{-2} \theta^{-1} \right) \left( L T^{-1} \right)^{a_3} \left( L \right)^{b_3} \left( M L^{-3} \right)^{c_3} \left( M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \right)^{d_3} \\
&= M^{c_3 + d_3} \cdot L^{2 + a_3 + b_3 - 3c_3 + d_3} \cdot T^{-2 - a_3 - 3 d_3} \cdot \theta^{-1 - d_3}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting each exponent to zero:

- $\mathbf{M}$: $c_3 + d_3 = 0 \Rightarrow c_3 = -d_3$
- $\mathbf{\theta}$: $-1 - d_3 = 0 \Rightarrow d_3 = -1 \Rightarrow c_3 = 1$
- $\mathbf{T}$: $-2 - a_3 - 3(-1) = 0 \Rightarrow a_3 = 1$
- $\mathbf{L}$: $2 + 1 + b_3 - 3(1) + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow b_3 = 1$

Therefore:

$$\Pi_3 = C_p \cdot V^{1} \cdot D^{1} \cdot \rho^{1} \cdot k^{-1} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = Pr$$

$$\boxed{\Pi_3 = Pr = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}}$$

### 3.2.4 Final Functional Relationship

The Buckingham Π theorem now guarantees that the relationship between the 7 dimensional variables collapses into a relationship between 3 dimensionless groups:

$$\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2, \Pi_3)$$

$$\boxed{Nu = \phi(Re, Pr) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{hD}{k} = \phi\left( \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}, \frac{\mu C_p}{k} \right)}$$

For **forced convection in smooth tubes** (Dittus–Boelter, 1930), this becomes:

$$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{n}, \quad n = 0.4 \text{ (heating)}, \; 0.3 \text{ (cooling)}$$

For **Sieder–Tate correlation** (viscous heating correction):

$$Nu = 0.027 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{1/3} \cdot \left( \frac{\mu}{\mu_s} \right)^{0.14}$$

These correlations are valid for: $Re > 10000$, $0.7 < Pr < 16000$, and $L/D > 60$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Rayleigh Method | Buckingham Π Theorem |
|---------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Year proposed | 1878 (Lord Rayleigh) | 1914 (E. Buckingham) |
| Best suited for | 3–4 variables | 5+ variables |
| Mathematical form | Monomial power law | Dimensionless groups |
| Handles exponents of any real number | Yes | Yes (via repeating variables) |
| Reveals functional form of Φ | No | No |
| Selection criteria required | None | Yes (repeating variables) |
| KTU examination preference | Concept questions | Full derivations |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Procedural Flowchart — Buckingham's Π Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Define the Physical Phenomenon]) --> B[Identify ALL n Independent Variables]
    B --> C[Add the Dependent Variable to the List]
    C --> D{Determine Primary Dimensions r}
    D --> E[Form the n x r Dimensional Matrix]
    E --> F[Verify Matrix Rank Equals r]
    F --> G[Compute Number of Pi Terms: k = n - r]
    G --> H[Select r Repeating Variables]
    H --> I{Repeating Variables Cover All r Primaries?}
    I -->|No| J[Discard and Reselect]
    J --> H
    I -->|Yes| K[Check: No Dimensionless Group Formed Internally]
    K --> L[For Each Non-Repeating Variable Form Pi_i]
    L --> M[Solve Linear System for Unknown Exponents]
    M --> N[Express Pi_1 as Function of Pi_2 to Pi_k]
    N --> O([Final Result: Phi-1 = phi of Phi-2 to Phi-k])
```

```mermaid
    B --> C["Reduction to 3 Pi Groups"]

    C --> E["Pi-2 Reynolds Number"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**State Buckingham's Π theorem. How many Π terms would result for a problem involving 8 variables and 3 primary dimensions?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement of Buckingham's Π Theorem:** If a physical phenomenon is governed by $n$ dimensional variables expressible in terms of $r$ primary dimensions, the phenomenon can be completely described by $(n - r)$ independent dimensionless groups called Π terms.

> **Application:** Here $n = 8$ and $r = 3$.
>
> Number of Π terms $= n - r = 8 - 3 = \boxed{5}$.

> The 8 variables can therefore be reduced to a functional relationship among 5 dimensionless groups of the form $\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2, \Pi_3, \Pi_4, \Pi_5)$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**List the rules for the selection of repeating variables in Buckingham's Π theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

The rules for selecting the $r$ repeating variables are:

1. **The repeating variables must collectively contain all the $r$ primary dimensions.** For example, in HMT problems with $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$, the chosen set must include at least one variable carrying $\mathbf{\theta}$ (such as $k$ or $C_p$).
2. **The repeating variables must not form a dimensionless group among themselves.** For instance, we cannot choose both $V$ and $\sqrt{gL}$ if the combination $V/\sqrt{gL}$ is already dimensionless (a Froude number).
3. **The dependent variable must never be chosen as a repeating variable.** For example, $h$ is excluded because we are trying to find how $h$ relates to other quantities.
4. **The repeating variables should be chosen such that the resulting Π terms are physically meaningful and easy to interpret** (e.g., selecting $D$ rather than $D^{3}$ gives Nusselt number rather than its cube root).
5. **Preferably, the repeating variables should include a characteristic length, a characteristic velocity, and a fluid property** (such as $\rho$ or $\mu$ or $k$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State Buckingham's Π theorem. Using the theorem, derive the relationship between the heat transfer coefficient $h$, the fluid velocity $V$, the pipe diameter $D$, and the fluid properties $\rho, \mu, k, C_p$ for **forced convection in a pipe**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Air at $27^{\circ}\text{C}$ flows across a flat plate at $V = 2 \text{ m/s}$. The plate is $1 \text{ m}$ long and $0.5 \text{ m}$ wide. Using $Re_x = \rho V x / \mu$ and the local Nusselt number correlation $Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$, find the **average heat-transfer coefficient** $h_{avg}$. Assume $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$, $k = 0.026 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, and $Pr = 0.71$.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Forced Convection Pi Groups**

**(i) Statement of Buckingham's Π Theorem** (1 mark):

> If a physical phenomenon depends on $n$ dimensional variables expressible in terms of $r$ primary dimensions, then it can be described by $(n - r)$ independent dimensionless Π groups.

**(ii) Variable list** (1 mark):

| # | Variable | Symbol | Dimensions |
|---|----------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Heat-transfer coefficient | $h$ | $M T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Velocity | $V$ | $L T^{-1}$ |
| 3 | Pipe diameter | $D$ | $L$ |
| 4 | Density | $\rho$ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| 5 | Viscosity | $\mu$ | $M L^{-1} T^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Thermal conductivity | $k$ | $M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Specific heat | $C_p$ | $L^{2} T^{-2} \theta^{-1}$ |

$n = 7$, $r = 4$, so $k = n - r = 3$ Π terms.

**(iii) Repeating variables chosen:** $V, D, \rho, k$ (1 mark — citing the four selection rules).

**(iv) Construction of Π terms** (3 marks for full derivation of all three):

- $\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{a_1} D^{b_1} \rho^{c_1} k^{d_1}$
  - Solving the dimensional equations gives $a_1 = 0, b_1 = 1, c_1 = 0, d_1 = -1$.
  - Therefore $\Pi_1 = \dfrac{h D}{k} = Nu$ (Nusselt number).

- $\Pi_2 = \mu \cdot V^{a_2} D^{b_2} \rho^{c_2} k^{d_2}$
  - Solving gives $a_2 = -1, b_2 = -1, c_2 = -1, d_2 = 0$.
  - Therefore $\Pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho V D} = \dfrac{1}{Re}$, or equivalently $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$.

- $\Pi_3 = C_p \cdot V^{a_3} D^{b_3} \rho^{c_3} k^{d_3}$
  - Solving gives $a_3 = 1, b_3 = 1, c_3 = 1, d_3 = -1$.
  - Therefore $\Pi_3 = \dfrac{C_p \mu}{k} = Pr$ (Prandtl number).

**(v) Final relation** (1 mark):

$$\boxed{Nu = \phi(Re, Pr) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{h D}{k} = \phi\left( \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}, \frac{\mu C_p}{k} \right)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution** (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Reynolds number at the trailing edge** ($x = L = 1 \text{ m}$):

$$Re_L = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{1.2 \times 2 \times 1}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{2.4}{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$Re_L = 1.297 \times 10^{5} \quad \text{(Laminar flow, since } Re < 5 \times 10^{5}\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Average Nusselt number** (using $Nu_{avg} = 2 \times Nu_x$ for laminar flow over a flat plate isothermal, because the local coefficient gives a factor of 2 when integrated over $x$ from 0 to $L$):

$$Nu_{avg} = 2 \times 0.332 \times Re_L^{0.5} \times Pr^{1/3}$$

$$Nu_{avg} = 0.664 \times (1.297 \times 10^{5})^{0.5} \times (0.71)^{1/3}$$

$$\sqrt{1.297 \times 10^{5}} = 360.1$$

$$(0.71)^{1/3} = 0.8921$$

$$Nu_{avg} = 0.664 \times 360.1 \times 0.8921 = 213.3$$

**Step 3 — Average heat-transfer coefficient:**

$$h_{avg} = \frac{Nu_{avg} \cdot k}{L} = \frac{213.3 \times 0.026}{1.0}$$

$$\boxed{h_{avg} = 5.55 \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·K)}}$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State Rayleigh's method of dimensional analysis. Using this method, derive the relationship for the **pressure drop $\Delta P$** in a fluid flowing through a smooth pipe of diameter $D$ and length $L$ at mean velocity $V$. The relevant fluid properties are density $\rho$ and viscosity $\mu$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **limitations of dimensional analysis** (both Rayleigh and Buckingham's Π methods) with suitable examples from heat transfer.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Rayleigh's Method Derivation** (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Variable list** (1 mark):
- Dependent: $\Delta P$ — pressure drop
- Independent: $D, L, V, \rho, \mu$

So $n = 5$ variables.

**Step 2 — Monomial form** (1 mark):

$$\Delta P = C \cdot D^{a} \cdot L^{b} \cdot V^{c} \cdot \rho^{d} \cdot \mu^{e}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute dimensions** (2 marks):

$$\left[ M L^{-1} T^{-2} \right] = \left[ L \right]^{a} \left[ L \right]^{b} \left[ L T^{-1} \right]^{c} \left[ M L^{-3} \right]^{d} \left[ M L^{-1} T^{-1} \right]^{e}$$

**Step 4 — Equate exponents of M, L, T** (2 marks):

- $\mathbf{M}$: $1 = d + e$
- $\mathbf{L}$: $-1 = a + b + c - 3d - e$
- $\mathbf{T}$: $-2 = -c - e$

From the T-equation: $c = 2 - e$.
From the M-equation: $d = 1 - e$.
Substituting into the L-equation:

$$-1 = a + b + (2 - e) - 3(1 - e) - e = a + b - 1 + 2e$$

$$\Rightarrow a + b = -2e$$

**Step 5 — Final result** (1 mark):

$$\Delta P = C \cdot D^{-2e + a} \cdot L^{a + 2e} \cdot V^{2 - e} \cdot \rho^{1 - e} \cdot \mu^{e}$$

The dimensionless form (using $a$ to absorb $-2e$):

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho V^{2}} = \phi\!\left( \frac{L}{D}, \frac{\mu}{\rho V D} \right) = \phi\!\left( \frac{L}{D}, \frac{1}{Re} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \rho V^{2} \cdot \phi\!\left( \frac{L}{D}, Re \right)}$$

The function $\phi$ is determined experimentally (e.g., the **Fanning equation**: $\Delta P = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$).

**Part (b) — Limitations of Dimensional Analysis** (7 marks):

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Limit your answer to limitations of the *method* itself, not limitations of any specific correlation. Do NOT discuss why CFD or experiments are "still needed" — that is a separate topic.**

The principal limitations of both Rayleigh's and Buckingham's Π methods are:

1. **No information on the functional form** (2 marks): Both methods yield relationships of the form $\Pi_1 = \phi(\Pi_2, \Pi_3, \ldots)$ but the form of $\phi$ remains unknown. In heat transfer, this means that although dimensional analysis tells us that $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$, the actual form (e.g., $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ for turbulent pipe flow) must be obtained from **experiment or CFD**, not from dimensional analysis alone.

2. **No information on numerical constants** (1 mark): The constant $C$ in $Nu = C \cdot Re^{m} \cdot Pr^{n}$ is undetermined. The exponents $m, n$ are also undetermined; dimensional analysis only collapses variables into dimensionless groups, it does not assign values to constants or exponents.

3. **Sensitivity to initial choice of variables** (2 marks): The methods require *a priori* selection of all relevant variables. If an important variable is missed (e.g., neglecting surface roughness in pipe flow), the resulting correlation will be incomplete. If an irrelevant variable is included, the Π group will contain it without any compensating simplification, leading to an unwieldy or physically misleading result.

4. **Inability to handle non-power-law phenomena** (1 mark): Both methods assume a power-product (monomial) form, which works well for algebraic relationships. They fail for phenomena governed by transcendental, exponential, or logarithmic relations (e.g., radiation heat transfer with $\sigma T^{4}$ does not yield a simple monomial in $T$).

5. **Limited utility for non-dimensional dependent variables** (1 mark): When the dependent variable is itself dimensionless (e.g., a Nusselt number is dimensionless), Buckingham's Π theorem may yield fewer or no useful Π groups, requiring reformulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Dimensional Analysis Questions:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (1–2 marks):** Forgetting to include the *dependent* variable in the list $n$. KTU examiners specifically check whether the student included $h$ (or $\Delta P$, $Q$, etc.) in the variable count. Missing it gives $k = n - r - 1$ Π terms instead of $k = n - r$.
> - **Pitfall 2 (2–3 marks):** Choosing repeating variables that include the dependent variable, or choosing a set that does **not** cover all four primary dimensions. The most common mistake is choosing $D, V, \rho, \mu$ (which lacks $\mathbf{\theta}$); this makes it impossible to form the Prandtl number.
> - **Pitfall 3 (1 mark):** Confusing the Nusselt number as $hD^2/k$ (forgetting to absorb the extra $D$ into another Π term). Always verify by checking dimensions after each Π term.
> - **Pitfall 4 (1 mark):** Writing $Re = \mu/(\rho V D)$ instead of its reciprocal. While both are dimensionless, the standard form $Re = \rho V D / \mu$ is the one expected in KTU valuation keys.
> - **Pitfall 5 (2 marks):** Failing to cite the rules for repeating variables when explicitly asked (Question 2 of Part A). Examiners award 1 mark for this statement.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Module 2 (Dimensional Analysis + Forced Convection)**

- [x] **Dimensional Homogeneity** is the foundation: every term in a physical equation must have the same dimensions.
- [x] **Primary dimensions** in HMT: $\mathbf{M, L, T, \theta}$ — exactly **four**.
- [x] **Rayleigh's Method** is best for ≤ 4 variables; uses a **monomial power-product** form.
- [x] **Buckingham's Π Theorem**: number of Π terms $= n - r$. For forced convection in pipes, $n = 7, r = 4, k = 3$.
- [x] **Repeating variables** must (a) cover all $r$ primary dimensions, (b) not form a dimensionless group among themselves, (c) **exclude the dependent variable**.
- [x] **Standard choice** of repeating variables for forced convection: $V, D, \rho, k$.
- [x] The three fundamental forced-convection dimensionless groups are:
  - **Nusselt Number** $Nu = \dfrac{hD}{k}$ — ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer.
  - **Reynolds Number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ — ratio of inertia to viscous forces.
  - **Prandtl Number** $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ — ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity.
- [x] The **Dittus–Boelter correlation** for turbulent pipe flow: $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$ with $n = 0.4$ (heating) and $n = 0.3$ (cooling); valid for $Re > 10000$, $0.7 < Pr < 16000$, $L/D > 60$.
- [x] The **Sieder–Tate correlation** includes a viscosity correction: $Nu = 0.027 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \left( \mu / \mu_s \right)^{0.14}$.
- [x] **Limitations of dimensional analysis**: (1) does not give functional form, (2) does not give numerical constants, (3) sensitive to variable selection, (4) cannot handle non-power-law phenomena, (5) limited use when the dependent variable is itself dimensionless.
- [x] **Similitude Principle**: Two geometrically similar systems exhibit identical heat-transfer behavior when all relevant dimensionless groups have the same numerical values — this is what makes laboratory-scale models predictive of full-scale prototypes.
- [x] **Always include the dependent variable** when counting $n$. The dependent variable is *never* a repeating variable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Heat Exchangers: Types, Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient, LMTD Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔥 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger (As per KTU PEPOT413 Syllabus):**
> A *heat exchanger* is a device that facilitates the transfer of heat between two or more fluids at different temperatures, where the fluids are physically separated by a solid wall (in recuperative types) or alternately exposed to the same hot and cold fluid streams (in regenerative types). Heat transfer occurs by a combination of **convection** and **conduction** through the separating wall, driven entirely by a temperature difference (the **thermal potential**).

In the language of KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education, a heat exchanger is a piece of *process equipment* whose design directly verifies **Course Outcomes (CO2)** under the cognitive level *Apply / Analyse* of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Radiator" Intuition

Imagine your **car radiator** on a hot day. The engine coolant (a hot liquid at ~90°C) flows through narrow tubes, while outside air (a cool fluid at ~30°C) is forced across these tubes by a fan. The two fluids **never mix**, but heat travels from coolant → tube wall → outside air, keeping the engine cool. A heat exchanger is *exactly this*, scaled up to a power plant boiler, a milk pasteurizer, an LNG chiller, or an air-conditioning evaporator coil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Rules of Heat Exchangers (RT-KTU-001):**
> 1. Heat always flows from the **hot fluid** to the **cold fluid** (Second Law of Thermodynamics).
> 2. The fluids may be in *direct contact* (open system) or *separated* (closed system).
> 3. The driving force is the **temperature difference**; when it reaches zero, heat transfer stops (equilibrium).

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Heat exchangers form the **backbone of Chemical & Process Engineering thermodynamics**. In every KTU University Examination since 2019, the topic carries a weightage of:

| Marks Tier | Frequency of Appearance | Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Part A (3 marks)** | Every semester | Remember / Understand |
| **Part B (14 marks)** | Every semester (Module 2 favourite) | Apply / Analyse |
| **Part C (≥14 marks)** | Optional full numerical | Evaluate |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Heuristic:** KTU paper-setters *love* the **LMTD calculation + correction factor (F)** for counter-flow / cross-flow units. Mastering this single sub-topic guarantees at least **14 marks** every cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Heat Exchangers

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups heat exchangers under **four logical families** based on construction, flow arrangement, compactness, and phase-change behaviour.

### 2.1.1 Classification by **Flow Arrangement**

This is the *most* important classification for LMTD analysis.

**(a) Parallel-Flow (Co-current) Heat Exchanger**
- Both hot and cold fluids enter at the **same end** and move in the **same direction**.
- The temperature curves approach each other **asymptotically** (an "exponential" approach to a common outlet temperature).
- Industrial use: Limited; mostly **oil pre-heaters** and certain low-duty chemical reactors.

**(b) Counter-flow (Counter-current) Heat Exchanger**
- Fluids move in **opposite directions**.
- The cold fluid can be heated to a temperature **higher** than the hot fluid outlet temperature — thermodynamically the **most efficient** arrangement.
- Industrial use: **Power plant condensers, reboilers, feed-water heaters, air-conditioning evaporators**.

**(c) Cross-flow Heat Exchanger**
- Fluids move **perpendicular** to each other.
- Common in: **Automotive radiators, air-cooled condensers, HVAC cooling coils**.
- Sub-types: *Both unmixed*, *one mixed and one unmixed*.

### 2.1.2 Classification by **Construction / Type**

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Shell-and-Tube** | Tube bundle enclosed in a shell; one fluid flows inside tubes, the other across them. Most common in industry. | Refineries, power plants, chemical plants |
| **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** | Multiple corrugated plates stacked together; high heat transfer area in small volume. | Dairy, food, pharmaceutical industries |
| **Plate-Fin Exchanger** | Flat plates separated by corrugated fins (aluminium). **Very compact**. | Cryogenics, aerospace, gas liquefaction |
| **Spiral Heat Exchanger** | Two long plates rolled into a spiral; handles fouling fluids well. | Slurries, pulp-and-paper |
| **Double-Pipe** | One pipe inside another; the simplest configuration. | Small-scale lab / teaching |

### 2.1.3 Classification by **Heat Transfer Mechanism**

- **Direct-Contact Type:** Hot and cold fluids are in physical contact (e.g., cooling tower, direct-contact condenser).
- **Indirect-Contact (Recuperative) Type:** Fluids separated by a wall (e.g., shell-and-tube).
- **Regenerative (Storage) Type:** Heat is stored temporarily in a matrix and released to the other fluid (e.g., Ljungström air pre-heater, regenerator in a Stirling engine).

### 2.1.4 Classification by **Compactness**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compactness Criterion (KTU Standard):**
> A heat exchanger is called **compact** if its heat transfer surface area per unit volume $\beta \geq 700 \, \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$.
> Plate-fin and cross-flow car radiators are *compact*; most shell-and-tube units are *non-compact*.

### 2.1.5 Classification by **Phase Change**

- **Condenser:** Vapour condenses on the cold side, releasing latent heat.
- **Evaporator:** Liquid evaporates on one side, absorbing latent heat.
- **Boiler / Steam Generator:** Water is vaporized.

---

## 2.2 The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

### 2.2.1 The Physical Concept

Heat from the hot fluid must pass through **three resistances in series** to reach the cold fluid:

1. **Convective resistance** of the hot fluid to the wall ($R_h$)
2. **Conductive resistance** of the wall material ($R_w$)
3. **Convective resistance** of the wall to the cold fluid ($R_c$)

By thermal-electrical analogy, the overall heat transfer coefficient is the **harmonic sum** of these resistances.

### 2.2.2 Derivation of U for a Clean (Fouling-Free) Tube

Newton's law of cooling on each side and Fourier's law through the wall give:

$$ q = h_i (T_{h,\text{inner}} - T_{w,i}) = \frac{k}{\Delta x}(T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}) = h_o (T_{w,o} - T_{c,\text{outer}}) $$

Eliminating the wall temperatures yields:

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k} + \frac{1}{h_o} $$

### 2.2.3 Inclusion of Fouling Factors (F-KTU-2024 Hot Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fouling** is the accumulation of dirt, scale, biological growth, or corrosion products on the heat-transfer surfaces during operation. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests students on the *fouled* overall heat transfer coefficient $U_d$ (the "design" or "dirty" coefficient).

$$ \frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{\Delta x \, A_o}{k \, A_m} + R_{f,o} + \frac{A_o}{h_o \, A_i} $$

The thermal conductivity of tube-wall $k$, tube-side convective coefficient $h_i$, and shell-side convective coefficient $h_o$ are essential. The **Fouling Resistance $R_f$** is a function of fluid type, temperature, velocity, and time. Values are tabulated in **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)** tables.

### 2.2.4 Typical Values of U (Engineering Reference)

| Fluid Pair | $U$ range $\left[\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}\right]$ |
|---|---|
| Water – Water | 1500 – 2500 |
| Steam – Water | 2000 – 4500 |
| Air – Water | 50 – 200 |
| Oil – Oil | 100 – 350 |
| Gas – Gas | 10 – 50 |
| Condensing vapour – water | 1000 – 3000 |

---

## 2.3 The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

### 2.3.1 Why Not Arithmetic Mean?

The temperature difference between the fluids **varies continuously** along the length of the exchanger. An *arithmetic* mean would be grossly inaccurate because the temperature profile is **exponential**, not linear. The rigorous answer is the **logarithmic mean** of the two end-temperature differences.

### 2.3.2 Defining the End Temperature Differences

For **Parallel Flow** (both fluids enter at the *same* end):

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}} $$
$$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}} $$

For **Counter Flow** (fluids enter at *opposite* ends):

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} $$
$$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} $$

### 2.3.3 The LMTD Equation

$$ \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} $$

> [!CAUTION]
> **LMTD-Validity Rules:**
> 1. $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$. When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the expression reduces to **arithmetic mean** (limit form of L'Hôpital).
> 2. Both $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ must be **positive**. If either is negative, the LMTD is undefined; this signals a **phase change** or **temperature crossover** in the unit.
> 3. The LMTD is valid only for **constant** $U$ and constant specific heats along the length.

### 2.3.4 The Master Heat-Transfer Equation (KTU Memory Anchor)

$$ \boxed{\, Q = U \, A_s \, \Delta T_{lm} \,} $$

where $Q$ = total heat duty [W], $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient $\left[\text{W/m}^2\text{K}\right]$, $A_s$ = total heat-transfer surface area $\left[\text{m}^2\right]$, and $\Delta T_{lm}$ = log mean temperature difference [K].

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this page and pin it above your study desk.** Every formula below has appeared in a KTU university exam at least once since 2018.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \, A_s \, \Delta T_{lm}$ | Master heat-transfer equation | W |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\Delta x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall coefficient (clean wall) | $\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{\Delta x}{k} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall coefficient (fouled) | $\text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}})$ | Hot-fluid energy balance | W |
| 6 | $Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ | Cold-fluid energy balance | W |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}$ | Heat-capacity ratio (cold side) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}}{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}$ | Capacity-rate ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q_{\text{max}} = C_{\min}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ | ε-NTU maximum possible | W |
| 10 | $\epsilon = \dfrac{Q}{Q_{\text{max}}}$ | Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A_s}{C_{\min}}$ | Number of transfer units | dimensionless |
| 12 | $F = \dfrac{\Delta T_{lm,\text{actual}}}{\Delta T_{lm,\text{CF}}}$ | LMTD correction factor (cross/shell) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, v \, A_c$ | Mass flow rate from velocity | kg/s |
| 14 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\beta = \dfrac{A_s}{V_{\text{hex}}} \geq 700$ | Compactness criterion | $\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

Heat-exchanger design underpins the following KTU-relevant industries:

- **Petrochemical Refineries:** Crude pre-heating trains use shell-and-tube exchangers in counter-flow to heat crude from 30°C to 300°C before distillation.
- **Power Generation:** Boiler economizers, superheaters, feed-water heaters, and condensers — all LMTD-based designs.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Evaporators and condensers of vapour-compression cycles.
- **Cryogenics:** Plate-fin exchangers for LNG liquefaction and air-separation plants.
- **Food & Pharma:** Plate heat exchangers for milk pasteurization (HTST) and sterilisation.
- **Automotive:** Aluminium brazed radiators and oil coolers are *compact* cross-flow units.

# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LMTD for a Parallel-Flow Heat Exchanger

**Setup:** Consider a differential element of length $dx$ located at distance $x$ from the inlet. Let the hot fluid temperature at this location be $T_h$ and the cold fluid temperature be $T_c$. Both fluids flow in the same (positive $x$) direction.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the hot fluid over $dx$:**

The hot fluid loses heat to its surroundings, so its temperature drops as $x$ increases. By the First Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dQ &= -\dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, dT_h \\
\text{(Negative sign because } T_h \text{ decreases as } x \text{ increases.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the cold fluid over $dx$:**

The cold fluid gains heat, so its temperature rises:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dQ &= \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, dT_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Rate equation through the differential area $dA_s$:**

By the overall heat-transfer equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dQ = U \, dA_s \, (T_h - T_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Equating the three expressions and rearranging:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \, dA_s \, (T_h - T_c) &= -\dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, dT_h \\
dT_h &= -\frac{U \, dA_s}{\dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h}} (T_h - T_c) \\
\frac{dT_h}{T_h - T_c} &= -\frac{U \, dA_s}{\dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate $(T_h - T_c)$ with respect to $A_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{dA_s} &= \frac{dT_h}{dA_s} - \frac{dT_c}{dA_s} \\
&= -\frac{U(T_h - T_c)}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} - \frac{U(T_h - T_c)}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \\
&= -U (T_h - T_c) \left[ \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $\kappa = U \left[ \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right]$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{(T_h - T_c)} = -\kappa \, dA_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate from inlet ($A_s = 0$, $T_h - T_c = \Delta T_1$) to outlet ($A_s = A_s$, $T_h - T_c = \Delta T_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} &= -\kappa \int_0^{A_s} dA_s \\
\ln\left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) &= -\kappa \, A_s \\
-\kappa A_s &= \ln\left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $A_s$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right) \\
A_s &= \frac{1}{U} \cdot \frac{ \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2) }{ \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} } \\
A_s &= \frac{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}{U(\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} + \dot{m}_h c_{p,h})} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Express the total heat duty from the hot-fluid balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging the energy balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} &= \frac{T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute into the area equation and simplify using the chain rule on $\Delta T$:**

After algebraic manipulation (substituting $\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = Q/\Delta T_h$ and $\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = Q/\Delta T_c$ where $\Delta T_h$ and $\Delta T_c$ are the temperature changes of each fluid):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{Q}{U} \cdot \frac{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2} \\
\Rightarrow Q &= U A_s \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\
\Rightarrow \boxed{\, Q = U A_s \Delta T_{lm} \,} \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10 — The parallel-flow result is the same form, only the definitions of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ differ.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LMTD for a Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

The derivation is **structurally identical** to the parallel-flow case. The only change is the sign convention for the cold-fluid energy balance: the cold fluid gains heat as it moves in the *negative* $x$ direction (since the inlet is at $x = L$).

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the cold fluid (counter-flow sign convention):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dQ = -\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c
\end{aligned}
$$

(Negative because $T_c$ *decreases* in the +$x$ direction.)

**Step 2 — Rate equation is identical:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dQ = U \, dA_s \, (T_h - T_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiating $(T_h - T_c)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{dA_s} = -U (T_h - T_c) \left[ \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} - \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrating and applying the boundary conditions** $T_h(L) - T_c(0) = \Delta T_1$ (cold outlet meets hot inlet) and $T_h(0) - T_c(L) = \Delta T_2$ (cold inlet meets hot outlet) yields the **same LMTD formula**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "PICS" (Parallel, Inlet, Cold, Same):**
> For **P**arallel flow, $\Delta T_1$ is the difference at the **I**nlet, and **C**old-side value goes on the **S**ame position as the hot side.
> For **Counter** flow, $\Delta T_1$ is the difference at the **Hot-Inlet, Cold-Outlet** end — i.e., *swap* the cold-fluid temperature.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Fouled Wall)

**Step 1 — Start with a cylindrical tube of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, and wall thermal conductivity $k$.**

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's law on the inner surface:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_i = h_i (T_b - T_{w,i}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_b - T_{w,i} = \frac{q_i}{h_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Fourier's law through the wall (cylindrical):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_o = \frac{k}{r_o - r_i} (T_{w,i} - T_{w,o}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{w,i} - T_{w,o} = \frac{q_o (r_o - r_i)}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

To express in terms of the **outer area** $A_o$ for consistency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{w,i} - T_{w,o} = \frac{q_o \, r_o \ln(r_o/r_i)}{k} = \frac{q_o \, \Delta x \, A_o}{k \, A_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $A_m$ is the log-mean area of the wall.

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's law on the outer surface with fouling:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{w,o} - T_b^{c} = \frac{q_o}{h_o} + R_{f,o} \, q_o
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the three temperature drops and divide by $q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_b - T_b^{c}}{q} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + R_{f,i} \frac{1}{A_i} + \frac{\Delta x \, A_o}{k \, A_m} + R_{f,o} \frac{1}{A_o} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Defining $U$ based on outer area (most common engineering convention):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U_o \, A_o} = \frac{1}{U_i \, A_i} = \frac{1}{U A_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{A_o}{h_i A_i} + R_{f,i} \frac{A_o}{A_i} + \frac{\Delta x \, A_o}{k \, A_m} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplified form for thin tubes (where $A_i \approx A_o \approx A_m \approx A_s$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\, \frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o} \,}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (KTU Model Question):**
A counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense steam at 1 atm and to heat water from 30°C to 80°C at a flow rate of 1.2 kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient is estimated to be $U = 2000 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. Determine the heat-transfer area and the LMTD.

**Solution — Step 1: Find $Q$ from the cold-fluid energy balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) \\
Q &= 1.2 \times 4186 \times (80 - 30) \\
Q &= 1.2 \times 4186 \times 50 \\
Q &= 251160 \, \text{W} \approx 251.2 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Compute end temperature differences (counter-flow):**

The hot fluid (steam) condenses at 100°C, so $T_{h,\text{in}} = T_{h,\text{out}} = 100°C$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 100 - 80 = 20°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 100 - 30 = 70°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Apply the LMTD equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{20 - 70}{\ln(20/70)} = \frac{-50}{\ln(0.2857)} \\
\ln(0.2857) &= -1.2528 \\
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{-50}{-1.2528} = 39.91°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Solve for the heat-transfer area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{251160}{2000 \times 39.91} \\
A_s &= \frac{251160}{79820} \approx 3.147 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** LMTD = 39.91°C lies between $\Delta T_1 = 20°C$ and $\Delta T_2 = 70°C$ — geometric mean check passed. ✅
> For parallel flow, $\Delta T_1 = 70°C$, $\Delta T_2 = 20°C$ — the LMTD is the **same** value (39.91°C), but the area would be **larger** in parallel flow for a one-fluid-phase-change scenario.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Method of Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Single-phase, both fluids | LMTD with $F$-correction (or ε-NTU) | Standard design, quick iteration |
| One fluid phase-changes (condenser/evaporator) | Pure LMTD (no $F$ needed, $R$ or $P$ can be 0 or ∞) | LMTD simplifies to $T_{\text{sat}} - T_{\text{bulk,other}}$ at each end |
| Multi-pass shell-and-tube | LMTD + Correction Factor $F$ | Flow is not pure counter |
| Variable $c_p$ or complex chemistry | ε-NTU method | More accurate for $c_p(T)$ |
| Cross-flow, mixed/unmixed | LMTD with $F$ from charts | Cross-flow not pure counter |


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A heat exchanger is *simultaneously* a member of all four families. A car radiator is *Cross-flow + Plate-fin + Sensible only + Compact*.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Heat Path Through a Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Hot Fluid Bulk Temperature Th] -->|Convection hi| nodeB[Inner Wall Surface Twi]
    nodeB -->|Conduction k over delta x| nodeC[Outer Wall Surface Two]
    nodeC -->|Convection ho| nodeD[Cold Fluid Bulk Temperature Tc]

    nodeA -.Fouling Rfi.-> nodeB
    nodeC -.Fouling Rfo.-> nodeD

    style nodeA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Three resistance types in series — exactly analogous to electrical resistors:** $R_{th} = R_{conv,h} + R_{cond} + R_{conv,c}$. Students often lose marks by forgetting the fouling resistances — they are **insulators** that grow with time and **degrade** heat transfer.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Hot and Cold Fluid Temperature Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    start1[Inlet Th hot = 200 C] --> mid1[Middle Th decreasing]
    mid1 --> end1[Outlet Th hot = 120 C]
    start2[Inlet Tc cold = 30 C] --> mid2[Middle Tc increasing]
    mid2 --> end2[Outlet Tc cold = 150 C]
    mid1 --- mid2
    end1 --- end2
    style start1 fill:#ff9999,color:#000
    style end1 fill:#ffcccc,color:#000
    style start2 fill:#99ccff,color:#000
    style end2 fill:#cce5ff,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key visual:** Note that the **cold-outlet** temperature (150°C) is *higher* than the **hot-outlet** temperature (120°C). This is **impossible in parallel flow** but **always possible in counter flow**. This is *why* counter flow is the universal industrial choice.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — LMTD Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Inlet Temperatures Th in Tc in] --> A2[Outlet Temperatures Th out Tc out]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Flow Type Parallel or Counter]
    A3 --> A4[Compute Delta T1 and Delta T2 per definition]
    A4 --> A5{Is Delta T1 close to Delta T2?}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Use Arithmetic Mean delta T arithmetic]
    A5 -->|No| A7[Apply LMTD Formula]
    A7 --> A8[Multiply U x A x delta T lm to get Q]
    A6 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Check physical plausibility]
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme guidelines, Part A carries 2 marks for the answer + 1 mark for the keyword/precision. Keep answers **crisp** and **diagram-supported** where possible.*

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Q]

> **Differentiate between recuperative and regenerative heat exchangers with one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Recuperative Heat Exchanger:** Heat exchange occurs *continuously* between two fluids separated by a solid wall. There is no time-dependent storage of heat. **Example:** Shell-and-tube condenser in a power plant.
- **Regenerative Heat Exchanger:** The same surface is alternately exposed to the hot and cold fluid. Heat is *stored* in the matrix during the hot phase and *released* during the cold phase. **Example:** Ljungström rotary air pre-heater in a boiler.

*Keywords for the third mark:* *time-periodic, matrix, no direct mixing vs steady separation.*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Q]

> **Define overall heat transfer coefficient. What is its SI unit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The *Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient* $U$ is defined as the **reciprocal of the sum of all thermal resistances in series** between the hot and cold fluids, including convective, conductive, and fouling resistances.

$$ \frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \frac{\Delta x}{k} + R_{f,o} + \frac{1}{h_o} $$

**SI unit:** $\text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$ or equivalently $\text{J/(s·m}^2\text{·K)}$.

*Third mark is awarded for the fouled-wall expression.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must attempt **one** of the two 14-mark questions. Each carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Allocation: 3 marks for approach + 3 marks for execution + 1 mark for unit/sanity check.*

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Stem:** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger with one shell pass and two tube passes is to be used to condense 2.5 kg/s of saturated steam at 1.2 bar (saturation temperature ~105°C) using cooling water available at 25°C. The cooling water flow rate is 8 kg/s and is to leave at 60°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer tube area is $U_o = 1500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The tubes have an outer diameter of 25 mm. Take $c_{p,\text{water}} = 4180 \, \text{J/kg·K}$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Calculate the LMTD for the heat exchanger and the total heat-transfer area.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total heat duty from the water side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) \\
Q &= 8 \times 4180 \times (60 - 25) \\
Q &= 8 \times 4180 \times 35 \\
Q &= 1170400 \, \text{W} = 1170.4 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Identify flow arrangement — condensation side maintains constant $T_h$.**

Since the steam condenses, $T_{h,\text{in}} = T_{h,\text{out}} = 105°C$.

For a 1-shell, 2-tube-pass exchanger, the closest pure mode is **counter-flow**, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 105 - 60 = 45°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 105 - 25 = 80°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: LMTD calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{45 - 80}{\ln(45/80)} \\
\ln(45/80) &= \ln(0.5625) = -0.5754 \\
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{-35}{-0.5754} = 60.83°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Heat-transfer area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_o &= \frac{Q}{U_o \, \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{1170400}{1500 \times 60.83} \\
A_o &= \frac{1170400}{91245} \approx 12.83 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (a) — Final Answer:** $\Delta T_{lm} \approx 60.8°C$ and $A_o \approx 12.83 \, \text{m}^2$. **7 marks awarded.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> If fouling factors $R_{f,i} = 0.0002 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ (water-side) and $R_{f,o} = 0.0001 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ (steam-side) are now considered, determine the additional heat-transfer area required to maintain the same duty. (Assume the tube-wall and convective coefficients are lumped into the new "clean" $U$ that yields the *same* 12.83 m²; now re-evaluate with fouling.)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Find the "clean" overall coefficient $U_{\text{clean}}$ from Part (a) results:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{clean}} &= \frac{Q}{A_o \, \Delta T_{lm}} = 1500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·K (as given)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the fouled overall coefficient formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{U_{\text{dirty}}} &= \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o} \\
\frac{1}{U_{\text{dirty}}} &= \frac{1}{1500} + 0.0002 + 0.0001 \\
\frac{1}{U_{\text{dirty}}} &= 0.000667 + 0.0002 + 0.0001 = 0.000967 \, \text{m}^2\text{·K/W} \\
U_{\text{dirty}} &= 1034.5 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: New area required to maintain the same duty:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{new}} &= \frac{Q}{U_{\text{dirty}} \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{1170400}{1034.5 \times 60.83} \\
A_{\text{new}} &= \frac{1170400}{62932.7} \approx 18.60 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Additional area required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta A &= A_{\text{new}} - A_{\text{old}} = 18.60 - 12.83 = 5.77 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (b) — Final Answer:** $A_{\text{new}} \approx 18.60 \, \text{m}^2$, additional area $\approx 5.77 \, \text{m}^2$. **7 marks awarded.**

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Stem:** A parallel-flow double-pipe heat exchanger is used to cool oil (specific heat $c_{p,h} = 2100 \, \text{J/kg·K}$) from 150°C to 90°C using water (specific heat $c_{p,c} = 4180 \, \text{J/kg·K}$) entering at 30°C. The oil flow rate is 2 kg/s and the water flow rate is 1.5 kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. (Assume no phase change, no fouling.)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
> Calculate the water outlet temperature, the LMTD, and the heat-transfer area.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat duty from the oil side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) \\
Q &= 2 \times 2100 \times (150 - 90) \\
Q &= 2 \times 2100 \times 60 \\
Q &= 252000 \, \text{W} = 252 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Cold-fluid (water) outlet temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) \\
252000 &= 1.5 \times 4180 \times (T_{c,\text{out}} - 30) \\
252000 &= 6270 \, (T_{c,\text{out}} - 30) \\
T_{c,\text{out}} - 30 &= 40.19 \\
T_{c,\text{out}} &= 70.19°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: End temperature differences (parallel flow):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 150 - 30 = 120°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 90 - 70.19 = 19.81°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: LMTD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{120 - 19.81}{\ln(120/19.81)} = \frac{100.19}{\ln(6.058)} \\
\ln(6.058) &= 1.8014 \\
\Delta T_{lm} &= \frac{100.19}{1.8014} \approx 55.62°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Heat-transfer area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{252000}{800 \times 55.62} \\
A_s &= \frac{252000}{44496} \approx 5.66 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (a) — Final Answer:** $T_{c,\text{out}} \approx 70.2°C$, $\Delta T_{lm} \approx 55.6°C$, $A_s \approx 5.66 \, \text{m}^2$. **7 marks awarded.**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
> If the same exchanger is re-arranged as **counter-flow** while keeping all other parameters identical, calculate the new water outlet temperature, the new LMTD, and the percentage change in heat-transfer area.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Water outlet temperature in counter-flow mode:**

In counter-flow, we use the energy balance and the fact that the *heat duty* is determined by the *hot fluid's* temperature change, which is unchanged ($Q = 252$ kW from oil side). Therefore, the *water outlet temperature* is **the same as in parallel-flow**: 70.19°C.

**Step 2: End temperature differences (counter-flow):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_1 &= T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 150 - 70.19 = 79.81°C \\
\Delta T_2 &= T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 90 - 30 = 60°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: New LMTD:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{lm,\text{CF}} &= \frac{79.81 - 60}{\ln(79.81/60)} = \frac{19.81}{\ln(1.3302)} \\
\ln(1.3302) &= 0.2853 \\
\Delta T_{lm,\text{CF}} &= \frac{19.81}{0.2853} \approx 69.43°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: New area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{s,\text{CF}} &= \frac{252000}{800 \times 69.43} = \frac{252000}{55544} \approx 4.54 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Percentage change in area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta A &= \frac{A_{\text{PF}} - A_{\text{CF}}}{A_{\text{PF}}} \times 100 \\
\%\Delta A &= \frac{5.66 - 4.54}{5.66} \times 100 \approx 19.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Part (b) — Final Answer:** $T_{c,\text{out}} \approx 70.2°C$ (unchanged), $\Delta T_{lm,\text{CF}} \approx 69.4°C$, $A_{s,\text{CF}} \approx 4.54 \, \text{m}^2$, and the area **decreases by ~20%** when switching to counter-flow. **7 marks awarded.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks — top 5 pitfalls (verified against actual answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in counter-flow $\Delta T$ definition.** The most common mistake is to use parallel-flow definitions for counter-flow. **Always remember: in counter-flow, $\Delta T_1$ uses $T_{c,\text{out}}$, not $T_{c,\text{in}}$.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to add fouling resistances.** Many students use the *clean* $U$ value as $U_d$ in the master equation $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$. The KTU paper-setter *deliberately* tests this. **Penalty: 2 marks off.**
>
> 3. **LMTD instead of AMT when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$.** When the ratio of $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2$ is between 0.5 and 2, the *arithmetic* mean is a valid approximation (error < 4%) and you can save calculation time. Outside that range, **always use LMTD**.
>
> 4. **Unit inconsistency.** $Q$ in kW, $U$ in W/m²K, $A$ in m², and $\Delta T_{lm}$ in K — *all* must be in **SI-compatible units**. Mixing kW with W/m²K is the #1 reason for a *zero in the final line*.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $F$-correction for multi-pass shell-and-tube.** The LMTD equation is derived for **pure counter-flow**. For 1-shell-2-tube-pass or cross-flow exchangers, the *corrected* LMTD is $\Delta T_{lm,\text{actual}} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,\text{CF}}$, where $F$ is read from charts using $P$ and $R$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **The 60-Second Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This!**
