- 🔥 **Heat Exchanger** = device that transfers heat between two fluids at different temperatures, separated by a wall (recuperative) or alternated over time (regenerative).
- 🔥 **Four classification axes:** Flow arrangement, construction, phase change, compactness.
- 🔥 **Compact criterion:** $\beta = A_s / V \geq 700 \, \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$.
- 🔥 **Master Equation:** $\boxed{Q = U A_s \Delta T_{lm}}$ — the "king" of heat-exchanger design.
- 🔥 **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$.
- 🔥 **Parallel flow:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$.
- 🔥 **Counter flow:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$.
- 🔥 **Counter flow is thermodynamically superior** — cold fluid can be heated above the hot-fluid outlet temperature.
- 🔥 **Overall coefficient (clean):** $1/U = 1/h_i + \Delta x/k + 1/h_o$.
- 🔥 **Overall coefficient (fouled):** $1/U_d = 1/h_i + R_{f,i} + \Delta x/k + R_{f,o} + 1/h_o$.
- 🔥 **Typical $U$ values:** Water-water ~2000, Steam-water ~3000, Air-water ~150 W/m²K.
- 🔥 **Fouling $R_f$** is given by TEMA tables; *always* include in *design* calculations, *omit* in *rating* problems.
- 🔥 **LMTD limit:** if $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, use arithmetic mean (degenerate case of L'Hôpital).
- 🔥 **Correction factor $F$** is required for cross-flow and multi-pass shell-and-tube units.
- 🔥 **P and R parameters:** $P = (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})/(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ and $R = (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}})/(T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$.
- 🔥 **Effectiveness** $\epsilon = Q/Q_{\max} = (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})/(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ for $C_{\min} = C_c$.
- 🔥 **NTU** $= U A / C_{\min}$ — alternative design method (preferred when outlet temperatures are not specified).
- 🔥 **Unit golden rule:** $Q$ in W (not kW), $U$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$, $A$ in m², $\Delta T$ in K (or °C, since these are *differences*).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Mechanism of Radiation Heat Transfer and Radiation Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical Mechanism of Radiation Heat Transfer and Radiation Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Radiation** is the energy emitted by a body in the form of **electromagnetic waves (or photons)** as a direct consequence of its **temperature**, without requiring any intervening medium. It is one of the three modes of heat transfer, alongside conduction and convection, and is the *only* mode that does not require a material medium — it propagates perfectly through vacuum.

Every body above **absolute zero (0 K)** radiates thermal energy. The energy emitted depends on:
1. The **absolute temperature** of the body (in Kelvin).
2. The **nature of the surface** (smooth, rough, polished, oxidized, etc.).
3. The **wavelength** at which emission is considered.

The total emitted energy per unit area per unit time from a *perfect emitter* (blackbody) is governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$E_b = \sigma \, T^4$$

where **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bonfire Without Touching It"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world intuition:** Stand a few feet away from a campfire. You *feel* warmth on your face even though the hot air and smoke rise upward, away from you. The heat reaches you through **invisible electromagnetic waves** emitted by the glowing embers. This is radiation in its purest form — *no medium required*. The Sun warming the Earth across 150 million km of empty space is the ultimate demonstration of this phenomenon.

If you place a **polished silver plate** between yourself and the fire, you feel *much less* heat. The polished surface **reflects** most of the radiation away. If you replace it with a **matte black plate**, the same plate heats up dramatically because it **absorbs** most of the radiation. This contrast between polished and blackened surfaces is the foundation of **radiation properties** — α, ρ, τ, and ε.

---

### 1.3 The Electromagnetic Spectrum & Wavelengths of Interest

Thermal radiation occupies a narrow but critical band of the electromagnetic spectrum, primarily between **0.1 µm and 100 µm**, with peak emission typically in the **infrared (IR)** region for engineering temperatures (300 K – 3000 K).

| Region | Wavelength Range | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Ultraviolet (UV)** | 0.01 – 0.4 µm | Solar radiation, high-temperature furnaces (>3000 K) |
| **Visible Light** | 0.4 – 0.7 µm | Glowing metals, flames, incandescent lamps |
| **Near Infrared (NIR)** | 0.7 – 3 µm | Moderate-high temperature sources (1000–2000 K) |
| **Far Infrared (FIR)** | 3 – 100 µm | Room-temperature bodies, low-grade heat recovery |
| **Microwave / Radio** | >100 µm | Not thermal radiation in classical sense |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **spectrum of thermal radiation is continuous**; its distribution with wavelength is given by **Planck's Spectral Distribution Law**, and its peak shifts toward shorter wavelengths as temperature rises (**Wien's Displacement Law**).

---

### 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants You Must Memorize for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴
> - **Speed of light in vacuum:** c₀ = 3.00 × 10⁸ m/s
> - **Planck's constant:** h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
> - **Boltzmann constant:** k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K
> - **Wien's displacement constant:** b = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K
> - **c₁ = 2πhc₀²** = 3.742 × 10⁸ W·µm⁴/m² (first radiation constant)
> - **c₂ = hc₀/k_B** = 1.439 × 10⁴ µm·K (second radiation constant)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Mechanism — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Radiation heat transfer occurs through the following physical chain:

1. **Thermal agitation of molecules and atoms:** At any temperature above 0 K, the molecules, atoms, and electrons within a material undergo random thermal motion.
2. **Charged particle acceleration:** This motion causes acceleration of charged particles (electrons, ions), which in turn generates oscillating electric and magnetic fields perpendicular to each other.
3. **Electromagnetic wave emission:** These oscillating fields propagate outward as **electromagnetic (EM) waves** at the speed of light **c₀ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**.
4. **Photon transport (quantum view):** From a quantum-mechanical standpoint, EM energy is carried by discrete packets called **photons**, with energy per photon:

$$E_{photon} = h \, \nu = \frac{h \, c_0}{\lambda}$$

where **ν** is the frequency (Hz) and **λ** is the wavelength (µm).

5. **Absorption at the receiving surface:** When the wave strikes another body, its energy is either **absorbed**, **reflected**, or **transmitted**, depending on the surface's radiation properties.

---

### 2.2 Radiation Properties (The Four α, ρ, τ, ε)

When radiation energy **G** (W/m²) strikes a semi-transparent body, it is partitioned into three fractions:

- **Absorptivity (α):** fraction of incident energy absorbed.
- **Reflectivity (ρ):** fraction reflected away.
- **Transmissivity (τ):** fraction transmitted through the body.

$$ \alpha + \rho + \tau = 1 $$

For an **opaque body** (most engineering solids), τ = 0, giving:

$$ \alpha + \rho = 1 $$

**Emissivity (ε):** ratio of the radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a *blackbody* at the same temperature:

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b} $$

where E_b is the blackbody emissive power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spectral (monochromatic) vs. Total (band-integrated) Properties:**
> Spectral properties are functions of wavelength (denoted α_λ, ρ_λ, τ_λ, ε_λ). Total properties are integrated over all wavelengths:
>
> $$\alpha = \int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda \, G_\lambda \, d\lambda \Big/ \int_0^\infty G_\lambda \, d\lambda$$
>
> The same integral form applies to **ε**. Total properties depend on the spectral distribution of the *incoming* radiation, which makes them **directional and source-dependent**.

---

### 2.3 Blackbody, Gray Body, and Real Body

> [!NOTE]
> **Blackbody (Ideal Radiator):** A body that **absorbs all incident radiation** at every wavelength and every direction. α = 1, ρ = 0, τ = 0. By Kirchhoff's Law, it also **emits the maximum possible radiation** at every wavelength. A small cavity with a tiny hole approximates a perfect blackbody.
>
> **Gray Body:** A surface whose emissivity is **constant and independent of wavelength** (ε_λ = ε = constant). For such bodies, α = ε irrespective of incident spectrum.
>
> **Real (Selective) Body:** A surface whose properties vary strongly with wavelength. Example: white paint reflects visible light but absorbs IR strongly — making it a poor emitter in visible range but good in IR.

---

### 2.4 Kirchhoff's Law — The α = ε Identity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Law** states that, under **thermal equilibrium**, the **monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity** at the same wavelength and temperature:
>
> $$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$
>
> **Why it matters:** A surface that is a good absorber is also a good emitter. Polished silver is a poor absorber *and* a poor emitter. Matte black paint is an excellent absorber *and* an excellent emitter. This single law explains everything from solar collector design to thermal insulation strategy.

---

### 2.5 Planck's Spectral Distribution Law

The spectral emissive power of a blackbody at wavelength λ and temperature T:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c_0^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c_0}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2 / \lambda T) - 1 \right]}$$

where:
- **C₁ = 2π h c₀² = 3.742 × 10⁸ W·µm⁴/m²** (first radiation constant)
- **C₂ = h c₀ / k_B = 1.439 × 10⁴ µm·K** (second radiation constant)

This is the **most fundamental radiation law**; Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's laws are direct consequences.

---

### 2.6 Wien's Displacement Law

Differentiating Planck's law with respect to λ and setting the result to zero gives:

$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering use:** A hot iron at 1000 K peaks at ~2.9 µm (infrared). The Sun at ~5800 K peaks at ~0.5 µm (visible green-yellow). This is why **hotter objects glow first red, then orange, then white** — as T rises, the peak shifts into shorter wavelengths.

---

### 2.7 Stefan-Boltzmann Law

Integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths from 0 to ∞ yields:

$$E_b = \sigma \, T^4$$

where **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**.

---

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All Key Equations — Memorize Before Board Exam**

| # | Law / Property | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Energy partitioning** | α + ρ + τ = 1 (opaque: α + ρ = 1) | All dimensionless |
| 2 | **Emissivity definition** | ε = E / E_b | 0 ≤ ε ≤ 1 |
| 3 | **Kirchhoff's Law** | ε_λ(T) = α_λ(T) | Spectral form (most general) |
| 4 | **Kirchhoff's Law (gray)** | ε = α | Valid when ε_λ = constant |
| 5 | **Planck's Distribution** | E_bλ = C₁ / [λ⁵ (e^(C₂/λT) − 1)] | W/m²·µm |
| 6 | **Wien's Displacement Law** | λ_max · T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K | Peak wavelength |
| 7 | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | E_b = σ T⁴ | σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴ |
| 8 | **Photon Energy** | E = hν = hc₀/λ | J (single photon) |
| 9 | **Spectral → Total emissivity** | ε = ∫₀^∞ ε_λ E_bλ dλ / E_b | Weighted average |
| 10 | **First radiation constant** | C₁ = 2π h c₀² = 3.742 × 10⁸ | W·µm⁴/m² |
| 11 | **Second radiation constant** | C₂ = h c₀ / k_B = 1.439 × 10⁴ | µm·K |
| 12 | **Real-body emissive power** | E = ε σ T⁴ | W/m² (hemispherical total) |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** These laws govern the design of **furnaces, solar collectors, satellite thermal control, re-entry vehicle heat shields, infrared thermography, pyrometers, and fire-detection sensors.** Every thermal engineer must internalize these relationships.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Distribution

The Stefan-Boltzmann law is obtained by integrating Planck's spectral emissive power over all wavelengths from 0 to ∞:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b(T) &= \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda \\[6pt]
&= \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1 \right]} \, d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Introduce the substitution** $x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so that $\lambda = \frac{C_2}{xT}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{C_2}{x^2 T} dx$.

**Step 2 — Transform the limits:** When λ → 0, x → ∞; when λ → ∞, x → 0. The minus sign flips the limits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b(T) &= \int_\infty^0 \frac{C_1}{\left(\dfrac{C_2}{xT}\right)^5 \left(e^x - 1\right)} \left(-\frac{C_2}{x^2 T}\right) dx \\[6pt]
&= \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1 \, x^5 \, T^5}{C_2^5 \, (e^x - 1)} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^2 T} \, dx \\[6pt]
&= \frac{C_1 \, T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the standard integral:**

$$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

(This integral is evaluated by expanding $\frac{1}{e^x - 1}$ as a geometric series $\sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-nx}$ and integrating term-by-term.)

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b(T) &= \frac{C_1 \, T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 \, c_0^2 \, h^3} \, T^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:**

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 \, c_0^2 \, h^3} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}^4$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma \, T^4}$$

> **Physical meaning:** Total blackbody emission depends **only on the fourth power of absolute temperature**. Doubling T increases emission by a factor of 16.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law

To find the wavelength at which E_bλ is maximum, differentiate Planck's law with respect to λ and set the derivative equal to zero.

**Step 1 — Let** $y = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so $\lambda = \frac{C_2}{yT}$ and $\frac{d\lambda}{dy} = -\frac{C_2}{y^2 T}$.

**Step 2 — Express E_bλ in terms of y:**

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1 \, y^5 \, T^5}{C_2^5 \, (e^y - 1)}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to y and set to zero:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(E_{b\lambda})}{dy} &= \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{d}{dy}\left[\frac{y^5}{e^y - 1}\right] = 0 \\[6pt]
\frac{d}{dy}\left[\frac{y^5}{e^y - 1}\right] &= \frac{5y^4(e^y - 1) - y^5 e^y}{(e^y - 1)^2} = 0 \\[6pt]
5(e^y - 1) - y \, e^y &= 0 \\[6pt]
5 e^y - 5 - y \, e^y &= 0 \\[6pt]
e^y (5 - y) &= 5 \\[6pt]
\Rightarrow \; y = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} &\approx 4.965
\end{aligned}
$$

This transcendental equation has the numerical solution **y ≈ 4.9651**.

**Step 4 — Solve for λ_max:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{C_2}{4.9651 \, T} \\[6pt]
\lambda_{max} \, T &= \frac{1.439 \times 10^4}{4.9651} \\[6pt]
\lambda_{max} \, T &= 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Blackbody at Furnace Temperature

**Problem:** A furnace operates at 1500 K. Determine:
(a) the total emissive power,
(b) the wavelength of maximum emission.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Apply Stefan-Boltzmann:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \sigma \, T^4 \\[4pt]
&= (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \, (1500)^4 \\[4pt]
&= (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \, (5.0625 \times 10^{12}) \\[4pt]
&= 2.870 \times 10^{5} \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Apply Wien's Displacement Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} \\[4pt]
&= 1.932 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} = 1.93 \; \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is in the **near-infrared** region — consistent with what you'd observe: the furnace glows dull red-orange.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Solar Irradiance on Earth's Surface

**Sun's surface temperature:** T_sun ≈ 5800 K. Peak wavelength:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{5800} = 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m} = 0.50 \; \mu\text{m}$$

(Visible green-yellow — consistent with the Sun's apparent color.)

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Problem-Solver

| Given | Find | Governing Law |
|---|---|---|
| Surface temperature T | Total emissive power E_b | E_b = σT⁴ |
| Spectral dist. E_bλ(λ,T) | Total E_b | Integrate λ: 0 → ∞ |
| T | λ_max | λ_max · T = b |
| Emissivity ε, T | Real-body emission E | E = εσT⁴ |
| α_λ(T) | ε_λ(T) at equilibrium | ε_λ = α_λ (Kirchhoff) |
| α, ρ (opaque) | Missing fraction | τ = 0, so ρ = 1 − α |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Mechanism Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Thermal Energy of Body at Temperature T above 0 K"] --> B["Molecular, Atomic, and Electronic Agitation"]
    B --> C["Acceleration of Charged Particles"]
    C --> D["Oscillating Electric and Magnetic Fields"]
    D --> E["Electromagnetic Wave Emission at Speed of Light c0"]
    E --> F["Propagation Through Vacuum or Medium"]
    F --> G["Incident Radiation Strikes Receiving Surface"]
    G --> H1["Fraction alpha ABSORBED"]
    G --> H2["Fraction rho REFLECTED"]
    G --> H3["Fraction tau TRANSMITTED"]
    H1 --> I["Energy Converted to Internal Energy of Receiver"]
    H2 --> J["Returns to Surroundings"]
    H3 --> K["Passes Through Body"]
```

### 4.2 Radiation Property Classification Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SURF["Real Surface at Temperature T"] --> P1["Spectral Property Variation with Wavelength"]
    SURF --> P2["Directional Property Variation with Angle"]
    P1 --> Q1["Blackbody alpha=1, epsilon=1, rho=0, tau=0"]
    P1 --> Q2["Gray Body alpha=lambda epsilon=constant"]
    P1 --> Q3["Selective Real Body alpha lambda varies with lambda"]
    P2 --> R1["Diffuse Surface Independent of Direction"]
    P2 --> R2["Specular Mirror like Directional"]
    Q1 --> LB["Emits Maximum Energy per Planck Law"]
    Q2 --> LB2["Emits Fraction epsilon of Blackbody"]
    Q3 --> LB3["Emits via Spectral Integration"]
```

### 4.3 Radiation Laws Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PLANCK["Planck Spectral Distribution E_b lambda lambda T"] --> SB["Stefan Boltzmann E_b = sigma T power 4"]
    PLANCK --> WIEN["Wien Displacement lambda max T = b"]
    PLANCK --> RAY["Rayleigh Jeans Classical Limit Low lambda T"]
    PLANCK --> WIEN2["Wien Limit High lambda T"]
    KIRCH["Kirchhoff Law epsilon lambda = alpha lambda"] --> SB
    KIRCH --> GRAY["For Gray Body epsilon = alpha"]
```

### 4.4 Blackbody Emissive Power Spectrum (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Temperature T1 Low"] -->|Wien Peak shifts right| B["Long wavelength peak near IR"]
    A2["Temperature T2 High"] -->|Wien Peak shifts left| B2["Short wavelength peak near Visible UV"]
    A --> AREA["Total Area under Curve E_b = sigma T power 4"]
    A2 --> AREA2["Total Area Larger because T2 power 4 much greater"]
```

### 4.5 Energy Partitioning at a Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Incident Radiation G W per m2"] --> ALPHA["alpha times G ABSORBED"]
    G --> RHO["rho times G REFLECTED"]
    G --> TAU["tau times G TRANSMITTED"]
    ALPHA --> SUM["alpha plus rho plus tau = 1"]
    RHO --> SUM
    TAU --> SUM
    SUM --> OPAQUE["Opaque Body tau = 0 implies alpha plus rho = 1"]
```

> **Figure interpretation for KTU exam:** Always begin by labeling incident radiation **G**, the surface with its **α, ρ, τ** fractions, and the absorbed/reflected/transmitted streams. The arrow "absorbed" is conventionally drawn entering the body, while "reflected" returns back at an equal angle to the incident ray.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Style, Module 2]**

---

**Q1. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**State and explain Kirchhoff's Law of radiation. Why is it important in heat transfer?**

**Model Answer:**

> Kirchhoff's Law states that at thermal equilibrium, the **monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity** at the same wavelength and temperature:
>
> $$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$
>
> **Physical significance:**
> - A body that absorbs strongly at a given wavelength also emits strongly at that wavelength.
> - This couples the *emission* and *absorption* properties of a surface.
> - For a **gray body** (where ε_λ = constant = ε), the total emissivity equals total absorptivity: ε = α.
> - **Practical importance:** It enables us to predict emissivity from a simpler absorptivity measurement, and guides the selection of surfaces for solar absorbers (high α, high ε → matte black) and reflective insulations (low α → polished metal).
>
> **(3 marks – Definition 1 m, Explanation 1 m, Importance 1 m)**

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define emissivity, absorptivity, and reflectivity. For an opaque surface, what is the relation between these?**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Emissivity (ε):** Ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to the radiation emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature, ε = E/E_b.
> - **Absorptivity (α):** Fraction of incident radiation energy absorbed by the surface, 0 ≤ α ≤ 1.
> - **Reflectivity (ρ):** Fraction of incident radiation reflected by the surface, 0 ≤ ρ ≤ 1.
>
> **Energy balance for incident radiation G:**
>
> $$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$
>
> For an **opaque body** (no transmission), τ = 0, therefore:
>
> $$\boxed{\alpha + \rho = 1}$$
>
> **(3 marks – Three definitions 1.5 m, opaque relation 1.5 m)**

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – ESE Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** of radiation from Planck's spectral distribution. Show all integration steps clearly. State the value and significance of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A blackbody sphere of diameter 50 mm is maintained at 1277 °C. Determine:
1. The total emissive power.
2. The wavelength of maximum emission.
3. The total energy emitted in 5 minutes from the sphere's surface.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law

**Step 1:** The total blackbody emissive power is the integral of Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths:

$$E_b(T) = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, hence $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{xT}$, $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{x^2 T} dx$.

**Step 3:** Substitute and flip integration limits:

$$E_b(T) = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the standard integral:

$$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

**Step 5:** Combine and identify the constant:

$$E_b = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c_0^2 h^3} T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

**Step 6:** Numerical value: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**

**Step 7:** **Significance** — Emission is proportional to the fourth power of absolute temperature; it is the basis for radiation heat-transfer calculations and for radiation pyrometers.

**(7 marks: statement 1 m, substitution 1 m, integral identification 1 m, integral value 1 m, final expression 1 m, σ value + significance 2 m)**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Diameter D = 50 mm = 0.05 m → Radius r = 0.025 m
- Temperature T = 1277 °C = 1277 + 273 = **1550 K**
- Time t = 5 minutes = 300 s

**Step 1 — Total emissive power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \sigma T^4 = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(1550)^4 \\[4pt]
&= (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5.77 \times 10^{12}) \\[4pt]
&= 3.27 \times 10^{5} \; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Wavelength of maximum emission (Wien's Law):**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1550} = 1.87 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy emitted in 5 minutes:**

Surface area of sphere:

$$A = 4 \pi r^2 = 4 \pi (0.025)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2$$

Total energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= E_b \cdot A \cdot t \\[4pt]
&= (3.27 \times 10^5)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(300) \\[4pt]
&= 7.704 \times 10^{5} \; \text{J} \approx 770.4 \; \text{kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: Part 1 – 2 m, Part 2 – 2 m, Part 3 – 3 m)**

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – ESE Model, Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a **blackbody, gray body, and selective (real) body** with the help of neat sketches of spectral emissivity variation. State Planck's spectral distribution law and discuss how Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's Displacement laws are obtained as special cases.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A tungsten filament has a diameter of 0.8 mm and length 80 mm. It operates at 2500 K with total emissivity ε = 0.35. Calculate:
1. The total radiation emitted by the filament.
2. If the filament is treated as a blackbody, by what percentage is the actual emission lower than the blackbody case?

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Concepts and Laws

**Blackbody:** A body that absorbs all incident radiation at every wavelength and every direction. By Kirchhoff's law, it also emits maximum radiation at every wavelength. Its emissivity ε_λ = 1 for all λ.

**Gray body:** A surface whose monochromatic emissivity is *constant* (independent of wavelength). ε_λ = ε < 1. For such bodies ε = α, simplifying calculations considerably.

**Selective (real) body:** A surface whose ε_λ varies strongly with wavelength. Example: glass is transparent (ε_λ ≈ 0) in visible range but opaque (ε_λ ≈ 0.9) in IR.

**Planck's spectral distribution:**

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)}$$

**As special cases of Planck's law:**
- Integrate over all wavelengths → **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** E_b = σT⁴
- Differentiate and set to zero → **Wien's Displacement Law** λ_max · T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K

**(7 marks: 3 body types with sketches 3 m, Planck law statement 1 m, derivations of two special cases 3 m)**

---

#### Part (b) — Tungsten Filament Numerical

**Given:**
- Diameter D = 0.8 mm = 8 × 10⁻⁴ m
- Length L = 80 mm = 0.08 m
- T = 2500 K
- ε = 0.35

**Step 1 — Surface area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi D L = \pi (8 \times 10^{-4})(0.08) \\[4pt]
&= 2.011 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Blackbody emissive power:**

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(2500)^4 = 2.215 \times 10^6 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Real-body emissive power:**

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.35 \times 2.215 \times 10^6 = 7.752 \times 10^5 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total emission (real):**

$$Q_{real} = E \cdot A = (7.752 \times 10^5)(2.011 \times 10^{-4}) = 155.9 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Blackbody emission:**

$$Q_{BB} = E_b \cdot A = (2.215 \times 10^6)(2.011 \times 10^{-4}) = 445.4 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \; \text{reduction} = \frac{Q_{BB} - Q_{real}}{Q_{BB}} \times 100 = \frac{445.4 - 155.9}{445.4} \times 100 = 65\%$$

> This 65% reduction matches ε = 0.35 exactly, since Q_real/Q_BB = ε.

**(7 marks: Part 1 – 3 m, Part 2 – 4 m)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Temperature in Kelvin!** Many students substitute Celsius into σT⁴. This gives a *wildly wrong answer* (T in °C gives negative T⁴ for sub-zero). **Always convert °C → K first.** Penalty: full marks lost for the numerical.
>
> 2. **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** must be σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴ — written with K⁴, not K. Some students mistakenly use σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·°C⁴, which is incorrect because °C⁴ has no physical meaning.
>
> 3. **Confusing spectral vs. total properties:** ε_λ is a *function*; ε is the integrated value. Don't write α = ε if the surface is *selective*. Always specify "**for gray body**" or "**at given wavelength**".
>
> 4. **Wien's displacement law units:** λ_max in meters, T in Kelvin, and constant b in m·K. Using mm·K or µm·K without unit conversion loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Opaque vs. semi-transparent:** For metal surfaces and most engineering solids, **τ = 0** is the default. State this assumption explicitly.
>
> 6. **Don't write α + ρ + τ = 1** for opaque bodies — write **α + ρ = 1**. Examiners notice.
>
> 7. **Show the substitution step** in the Stefan-Boltzmann derivation — examiners award partial credit for *each* major step. Skipping straight to "E_b = σT⁴" yields only 2 of 7 marks.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — Radiation Properties & Mechanism**

- ☑ **Radiation** is the only heat-transfer mode that requires **no medium**; energy travels as **EM waves** or **photons**.
- ☑ **Photon energy:** E = hν = hc₀/λ (h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s).
- ☑ **Energy balance:** α + ρ + τ = 1 (general); α + ρ = 1 (**opaque** solids).
- ☑ **Emissivity:** ε = E/E_b, range 0–1.
- ☑ **Kirchhoff's Law:** ε_λ(T) = α_λ(T) at thermal equilibrium; for gray bodies, ε = α.
- ☑ **Planck's Spectral Distribution:** E_bλ = C₁ / [λ⁵ (e^(C₂/λT) − 1)], with C₁ = 3.742 × 10⁸ W·µm⁴/m² and C₂ = 1.439 × 10⁴ µm·K.
- ☑ **Wien's Displacement Law:** λ_max · T = 2.898 × 10⁻³ m·K.
- ☑ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** E_b = σT⁴ with σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴.
- ☑ **Real-body emission:** E = ε σT⁴.
- ☑ **Blackbody** = perfect absorber (α = 1) and perfect emitter (ε = 1).
- ☑ **Gray body:** ε_λ = constant; selective body: ε_λ varies with λ.
- ☑ **T MUST be in Kelvin** for all radiation formulas.
- ☑ **Higher T** → peak emission shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (visible glow at very high T).
- ☑ **Engineering applications:** Furnace design, solar collectors, satellite thermal control, IR thermography, pyrometers.
- ☑ **Conversion check:** 1 µm = 10⁻⁶ m; 1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m.

**Last-Mantra for KTU Board:** *"Temperature goes in Kelvin. Emissivity in [0, 1]. Kirchhoff couples emission and absorption. Stefan-Boltzmann is the fourth power, not the first."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Body Radiation: Planck's Law, Wein's Displacement Law, Stefan Boltzmann Law, and Kirchoff's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Black Body Radiation: Planck's Law, Wein's Displacement Law, Stefan Boltzmann Law, and Kirchoff's Law ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Black Body? A **Black Body** is an idealized physical object that absorbs **all incident electromagnetic radiation**, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. It is a perfect absorber and, consequently, a perfect emitter of thermal radiation. No radiation is reflected or transmitted. By definition, a black body has an **absorptivity (α) = 1** and an **emissivity (ε) = 1** at all wavelengths and temperatures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A black body is a hypothetical object that serves as the **standard reference** for all real (gray) body radiation analysis. All real surfaces are compared against the black body as a benchmark. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Coal and the Furnace" Imagine a small opening (a cavity) drilled into a large, hollow, opaque, isothermal enclosure kept at a uniform temperature. Light entering this small hole gets trapped inside — bouncing back and forth, getting absorbed at each bounce — and essentially **never comes out**. Therefore, the hole behaves as a perfect black body, even though the material of the enclosure itself may not be black. Think of a **glowing furnace window** at night: the opening looks much darker than the surroundings, yet glows brilliantly when the furnace is hot because it emits as a black body. ### 1.3 Key Governing Laws (Overview) | Law | Core Statement (Plain English) | | :--- | :--- | | **Planck's Law** | Describes exactly *how much* energy a black body emits at each wavelength — the fundamental spectral distribution. | | **Wien's Displacement Law** | Tells us *where* (at what wavelength) the emission peaks as temperature changes. | | **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | Tells us the *total* energy emitted by a black body across all wavelengths. | | **Kirchhoff's Law** | Relates a body's emissivity to its absorptivity — *good absorbers are good emitters*. | > [!NOTE] > The **physical constants** you must memorize for KTU exams: > - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$ > - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}$ > - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ > - **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ > - **First radiation constant:** $C_1 = 2\pi hc^2 = 3.742 \times 10^8 \text{ W}\cdot\mu\text{m}^4/\text{m}^2$ > - **Second radiation constant:** $C_2 = hc/k_B = 1.439 \times 10^4 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Planck's Law of Black Body Radiation Max Planck (1900) proposed that energy is emitted in discrete packets called **quanta** of magnitude $E = h\nu$. The monochromatic emissive power of a black body is: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]} \quad \left[\frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \mu\text{m}}\right]$$ **Why this matters:** This single equation is the "mother equation" of all radiation heat transfer. Wien's and Stefan-Boltzmann laws are direct mathematical consequences of Planck's law. **Physical interpretation:** - As $T$ increases, the peak shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (Wien's law). - The total area under the curve grows rapidly with $T$ (Stefan-Boltzmann). - The curve is **asymmetric** — it rises sharply on the short-wavelength side and decays gradually on the long-wavelength side. ### 2.2 Wien's Displacement Law The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power is **maximum** is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature: $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K} = 2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$ **Why this matters:** This is why a piece of iron heated to 1000 K glows **red**, but at 3000 K it glows **white** (peak moves into the visible blue region). It is widely used in **optical pyrometers** for non-contact temperature measurement. ### 2.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law The total hemispherical emissive power of a black body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature: $$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \left[\frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2}\right]$$ **Real-world utility:** This is the basis for **solar collector design**, **furnace sizing**, **nuclear reactor thermal analysis**, and **stellar astrophysics** (used to classify star temperatures). ### 2.4 Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation At thermal equilibrium, the ratio of emissive power to absorptivity is the same for **all bodies** and equals the emissive power of a black body at the same temperature: $$\frac{E_\lambda(\lambda, T)}{a_\lambda(\lambda, T)} = E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$$ For a given wavelength and temperature, this reduces to: $$\varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) = a_\lambda(\lambda, T)$$ **Key Engineering Insight:** A polished silver mirror (low absorptivity) is also a **poor emitter**. A black painted surface (high absorptivity) is a **good emitter**. This is the principle behind **thermos flask vacuum bottles** — the inner surface is silvered to minimize radiation loss. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Law | Formula | Units | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Planck's Law (spectral) | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$ | $C_1, C_2$ are radiation constants | | 2 | Planck's Law (frequency form) | $E_{b\nu} = \dfrac{2\pi h\nu^3}{c^2\left[e^{h\nu/k_BT} - 1\right]}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{Hz}$ | Alternative spectral form | | 3 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{max}\,T = 2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ | $\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ | Peak wavelength location | | 4 | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Total hemispherical emission | | 5 | Stefan-Boltzmann (with surface area) | $Q_b = \sigma A T^4$ | $\text{W}$ | Total power emitted | | 6 | Kirchhoff (spectral) | $\varepsilon_\lambda = a_\lambda$ | dimensionless | For same $\lambda, T$ | | 7 | Kirchhoff (integrated/gray body) | $\varepsilon = a$ | dimensionless | Over all wavelengths | | 8 | Gray body emission | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | $\varepsilon < 1$ for real surfaces | | 9 | Intensity (Lambert's cosine) | $I_b = \dfrac{E_b}{\pi} = \dfrac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{sr}$ | Diffuse black body intensity | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write the **units** alongside the final numerical answer. Marks are specifically deducted in Section B for missing unit specifications. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law **Step 1 — Begin with Planck's spectral distribution:** $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]}$$ **Step 2 — To find the peak, take the derivative with respect to $\lambda$ and set it to zero:** $$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$$ Let us define the convenient variable $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$. Then $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{xT}$. **Step 3 — Rewrite the Planck function in terms of x:** $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1 \cdot (xT)^5}{C_2^5 \left[e^x - 1\right]} = \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{x^5}{e^x - 1}$$ **Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $\lambda$ (chain rule through x):** Since $x$ is inversely proportional to $\lambda$, the condition $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$ is equivalent to setting the derivative of the function $f(x) = \dfrac{x^5}{e^x - 1}$ with respect to $x$ equal to zero (the $C_1 T^5/C_2^5$ factor is constant). $$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{x^5}{e^x - 1}\right] = 0$$ Applying the quotient rule: $$\frac{5x^4 (e^x - 1) - x^5 (e^x)}{(e^x - 1)^2} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the numerator:** $$5x^4 (e^x - 1) - x^5 e^x = 0$$ $$x^4 e^x [5(1 - e^{-x}) - x] = 0$$ Since $x \neq 0$ and $e^x \neq 0$, the bracketed term must vanish: $$5(1 - e^{-x}) - x = 0$$ **Step 6 — Solve the transcendental equation:** The non-trivial root of $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$ is found numerically: $$x = 4.9651142317\ldots$$ **Step 7 — Substitute back to obtain Wien's Law:** $$\frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} = 4.9651$$ $$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^4}{4.9651} = 2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law **Step 1 — Total emissive power is the area under the spectral curve:** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]} d\lambda$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\eta = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{\eta T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{\eta^2 T}\, d\eta$.** **Step 3 — Substitute into the integral:** $$E_b = \int_\infty^0 \frac{C_1}{\left(\frac{C_2}{\eta T}\right)^5 [e^\eta - 1]} \cdot \left(-\frac{C_2}{\eta^2 T}\right) d\eta$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the powers of $\eta$ and the constants:** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{C_1 T^5}{C_2^5} \cdot \frac{\eta^5}{e^\eta - 1} \cdot \frac{C_2}{\eta^2 T} d\eta$$ $$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{\eta^3}{e^\eta - 1} d\eta$$ **Step 5 — Expand the denominator using the geometric series:** $$\frac{1}{e^\eta - 1} = \frac{e^{-\eta}}{1 - e^{-\eta}} = \sum_{n=1}^\infty e^{-n\eta}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute the series into the integral:** $$\int_0^\infty \frac{\eta^3}{e^\eta - 1} d\eta = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \int_0^\infty \eta^3 e^{-n\eta} d\eta$$ **Step 7 — Evaluate using the standard Gamma function identity:** $$\int_0^\infty \eta^3 e^{-n\eta} d\eta = \frac{3!}{n^4} = \frac{6}{n^4}$$ **Step 8 — The sum becomes the Riemann zeta function:** $$\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{6}{n^4} = 6 \cdot \zeta(4) = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{90} = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ **Step 9 — Assemble the result:** $$E_b = \frac{C_1 T^4}{C_2^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ **Step 10 — Insert the definitions $C_1 = 2\pi hc^2$ and $C_2 = hc/k_B$:** $$E_b = \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot T^4}{(hc/k_B)^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15\, c^2\, h^3} \cdot T^4$$ **Step 11 — Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15\, c^2\, h^3} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$$ $$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary | Property | Planck's Law | Wien's Displacement | Stefan-Boltzmann | Kirchhoff's Law | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Origin** | Quantum hypothesis | Consequence of Planck | Consequence of Planck | Thermodynamic equilibrium | | **Output** | Spectral distribution | Peak wavelength | Total emissive power | Relation between $\varepsilon$ and $a$ | | **Key Constant** | $C_1, C_2$ | $2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ | $\sigma$ | None (pure relation) | | **Dependence** | Both $\lambda$ and $T$ | Inverse with $T$ | Fourth power of $T$ | None (equality holds) | | **Application** | Spectral analysis | Pyrometry, color of stars | Furnace, solar collectors | Coating design | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Radiation Laws The following block diagram illustrates how the four radiation laws are interconnected. Note that **Planck's Law is the foundation** from which Wien's Displacement Law and Stefan-Boltzmann Law are mathematically derived, and **Kirchhoff's Law** is a parallel thermodynamic identity that operates alongside them. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[QUANTUM HYPOTHESIS
Energy emitted in discrete quanta] --> B[PLANCKS LAW
Spectral Distribution of Blackbody] B --> C[WIEINS DISPLACEMENT LAW
Peak wavelength location] B --> D[STEFAN BOLTZMANN LAW
Total emissive power] E[THERMODYNAMIC EQUILIBRIUM
Energy balance of surfaces] --> F[KIRCHHOFFS LAW
Emissivity equals Absorptivity] B --> G[BLACKBODY REFERENCE BENCHMARK] F --> G G --> H[REAL GRAY BODY ANALYSIS
Use emissivity epsilon less than 1] C --> I[ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS] D --> I F --> I I --> J[Optical Pyrometers] I --> K[Solar Collectors] I --> L[Furnace Design] I --> M[Thermos Flask Insulation] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Flow — Spectral Distribution Behavior The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional behavior** of the spectral emissive power as a function of wavelength for varying black body temperatures, illustrating Wien's Displacement (peak shifts to shorter wavelengths) and Stefan-Boltzmann (total area grows rapidly). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS] P1[Absolute Temperature T1 LOW] P2[Absolute Temperature T2 MEDIUM] P3[Absolute Temperature T3 HIGH] end subgraph SPECTRUM[SPECTRAL EMISSIVE POWER CURVE] S1[Curve peaks at LONG wavelength
SMALL total area] S2[Curve peaks at MID wavelength
LARGER total area] S3[Curve peaks at SHORT wavelength
LARGEST total area] end subgraph OUTPUT[KTU OBSERVABLE OUTPUTS] O1[Color RED orange if visible] O2[Color YELLOW white] O3[Color BLUE WHITE] end P1 --> S1 --> O1 P2 --> S2 --> O2 P3 --> S3 --> O3 S1 -.->|Total Area sigma T1 to power 4| STB[Stefan Boltzmann] S2 -.->|Total Area sigma T2 to power 4| STB S3 -.->|Total Area sigma T3 to power 4| STB S1 -.->|Peak at lambda max T1 equals 2898| WDL[Wiens Displacement] S2 -.->|Peak at lambda max T2 equals 2898| WDL S3 -.->|Peak at lambda max T3 equals 2898| WDL ``` ### 4.3 Schematic of a Practical Black Body Cavity ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CAVITY[ISOTHERMAL OPAQUE CAVITY AT TEMPERATURE T] W1[Wall A absorbs radiation] W2[Wall B absorbs and re-emits] W3[Wall C absorbs and re-emits] W4[Wall D absorbs and re-emits] end HOLE[SMALL OPENING] -->|Acts as perfect black body| CAVITY EXT[External radiation entering hole] -->|Trapped by multiple reflections| HOLE HOLE -->|Emerges with intensity sigma T4 divided by pi| OBS[Observer detects perfect blackbody radiation] W1 -.->|Multipass absorption| W2 W2 -.->|Multipass absorption| W3 W3 -.->|Multipass absorption| W4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers above are strictly alphanumeric (e.g., `CAVITY`, `W1`, `HOLE`) and contain no markdown formatting inside the labels, in strict accordance with KTU diagram rendering safety protocols. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]** **State and explain Stefan-Boltzmann Law. A black body has a temperature of 1500 K. Calculate the total hemispherical emissive power. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** The total hemispherical emissive power of a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature. $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$ **Calculation:** $$E_b = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1500)^4$$ $$E_b = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5.0625 \times 10^{12})$$ $$\boxed{E_b = 2.87 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** Always carry the $\sigma$ constant to **four significant figures** ($5.670 \times 10^{-8}$) to match KTU official answer key precision. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]** **Explain Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation. Why is a polished surface a poor emitter of radiation? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Kirchhoff's Law Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, the ratio of monochromatic emissive power to monochromatic absorptivity is the same for all bodies and equals the emissive power of a black body at the same temperature and wavelength: $$\frac{E_\lambda}{a_\lambda} = E_{b\lambda} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_\lambda = a_\lambda$$ **Why polished surfaces are poor emitters:** A polished metal surface has a very **low absorptivity** (it reflects most incident radiation). By Kirchhoff's law, its emissivity must also be low. Since $\varepsilon_\lambda = a_\lambda$, a low $a_\lambda$ implies a low $\varepsilon_\lambda$. Hence, polished surfaces emit very little thermal radiation compared to rough, blackened surfaces. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Planck's + Wien's** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Starting from Planck's Law, derive **Wien's Displacement Law**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The surface temperature of the Sun is approximately 5778 K. Treating it as a black body, determine:     (i) The wavelength at which maximum emission occurs.     (ii) The total emissive power of the Sun's surface.     (iii) The spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. For examination purposes, present the key steps: 1. Start with $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1\right]}$ 2. Introduce substitution $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$ 3. Differentiate and set $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$ 4. Show the result: $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$ 5. State numerical solution: $x = 4.9651$ 6. Conclude: $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Peak wavelength using Wien's Law:** $$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2898}{T} = \frac{2898}{5778}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{max} = 0.5016 \text{ }\mu\text{m} = 501.6 \text{ nm}}$$ This lies in the **green visible spectrum** — consistent with the Sun's apparent yellow-white color. **(ii) Total emissive power using Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5778)^4$$ Computing $(5778)^4$: $$5778^2 = 3.3385 \times 10^7$$ $$5778^4 = (3.3385 \times 10^7)^2 = 1.1146 \times 10^{15}$$ $$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.1146 \times 10^{15}$$ $$\boxed{E_b = 6.32 \times 10^7 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **(iii) Spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{m}$:** Calculate the exponent: $$\frac{C_2}{\lambda T} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^4}{1 \times 5778} = 2.4905$$ $$e^{2.4905} = 12.073$$ $$e^{2.4905} - 1 = 11.073$$ Substitute into Planck's equation: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{3.742 \times 10^8}{(1)^5 \times 11.073}$$ $$\boxed{E_{b\lambda}(1\,\mu\text{m}) = 3.38 \times 10^7 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Stefan-Boltzmann + Kirchhoff** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Starting from Planck's Law, derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A small metal plate has an emissivity of 0.3 on one side and 0.9 on the other. The plate is at 800 K. Compute:     (i) The emissive power from each side.     (ii) The absorptivity of each side (using Kirchhoff's law), if it is placed inside a furnace at 1200 K.     (iii) The net heat flux absorbed by the side with $\varepsilon = 0.9$ when facing the furnace. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution (a):** The full derivation is in **Section 3.2** of these notes. Key steps for exam: 1. Write $E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} d\lambda$ with Planck's law. 2. Substitute $\eta = C_2/(\lambda T)$ → $d\lambda = -C_2/(\eta^2 T) d\eta$. 3. Simplify the integrand to $\dfrac{\eta^3}{e^\eta - 1}$. 4. Expand $1/(e^\eta - 1)$ as geometric series $\sum e^{-n\eta}$. 5. Use Gamma function identity $\int_0^\infty \eta^3 e^{-n\eta} d\eta = 6/n^4$. 6. Recognize Riemann zeta function $\zeta(4) = \pi^4/90$. 7. Combine: $E_b = \dfrac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3} T^4 = \sigma T^4$. **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Emissive power of each side (gray body):** $$E_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T^4 = 0.3 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^4$$ Computing $(800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$: $$E_1 = 0.3 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.096 \times 10^{11}$$ $$\boxed{E_1 = 6968 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ $$E_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T^4 = 0.9 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^4$$ $$\boxed{E_2 = 20904 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ **(ii) Absorptivity by Kirchhoff's Law:** For a gray body, $\varepsilon = a$ at the same temperature. By Kirchhoff's law, the ratio is conserved, so: $$a_1 = \varepsilon_1 = 0.3$$ $$a_2 = \varepsilon_2 = 0.9$$ **(iii) Net heat flux absorbed on the high-emissivity side:** The side with $\varepsilon_2 = 0.9$ faces the furnace. The irradiation from the furnace (black body at $T_f = 1200$ K): $$G = \sigma T_f^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1200)^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12}$$ $$G = 1.176 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^2$$ Absorbed irradiation (using $a_2 = 0.9$): $$G_{abs} = a_2 \cdot G = 0.9 \times 1.176 \times 10^5 = 1.058 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^2$$ Emission from the plate side at 800 K: $$E_2 = 20904 \text{ W/m}^2$$ Net heat flux absorbed: $$q_{net} = G_{abs} - E_2 = 105840 - 20904$$ $$\boxed{q_{net} = 84{,}936 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 8.49 \times 10^4 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ The plate side gains heat (positive net flux) because the furnace is hotter. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Sign convention trap:** When the furnace is hotter than the plate, the plate *gains* energy — net flux is **positive** going into the plate. Many students incorrectly subtract in the wrong order and report a negative value, losing **2 marks** unnecessarily. > 2. **Unit inconsistency trap:** $\sigma$ must be used with temperature in **Kelvin (K)**, not Celsius. Mixing units costs **1 mark**. > 3. **Stefan-Boltzmann constant trap:** Use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ (not $5.67 \times 10^{-5}$ or $5.67 \times 10^{-11}$). This is a common numerical error worth **1 mark**. > 4. **Wien's constant trap:** Write $2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ — do **not** write $0.2898 \text{ cm}\cdot\text{K}$ or $2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}$ without specifying the unit. Examiners expect the $\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ form. > 5. **Kirchhoff's law trap:** Many students confuse $a$ (absorptivity) with $\alpha$ (sometimes used for thermal diffusivity in conduction). Use **$a$ for absorptivity** in radiation contexts. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Black body** = perfect absorber + perfect emitter ($\alpha = \varepsilon = 1$). - **Planck's Law** is the **fundamental** spectral distribution; everything else is derived from it. - **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{max} T = 2898 \text{ }\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ — peak shifts to shorter wavelengths as T increases. - **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$ — total energy grows as the **fourth power** of temperature. - **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = a_\lambda$ — good absorbers are good emitters (spectral equality). - For a **gray body**, the integrated form $\varepsilon = a$ holds over all wavelengths. - Use $C_1 = 3.742 \times 10^8$ and $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^4$ for Planck's law in $\mu\text{m}$. - A **cavity with a small hole** is the practical realization of a black body. - Optical pyrometers rely on **Wien's law** for non-contact temperature measurement. - Solar collector design and furnace design use **Stefan-Boltzmann law** for total heat flux. - Thermos flasks use **Kirchhoff's law** — silvered (low $\varepsilon$) surfaces minimize radiation loss.

topics.title: Molecular Diffusion in Liquids and Gases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Molecular Diffusion in Liquids and Gases ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Molecular Diffusion** is the transport mechanism in which individual molecules of a substance move through another substance (or through itself) due to their random thermal (Brownian) motion. In the context of **Heat & Mass Transfer**, molecular diffusion is the dominant mode of mass transport in stationary or laminar media, where bulk fluid motion is absent or negligible. Mathematically, molecular diffusion in a binary mixture (A + B) is governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**, which states that the molar flux of species A due to diffusion is proportional to the negative concentration gradient of species A. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > In **PEPOT413 - Module 3**, the focus is restricted to *steady-state, one-dimensional* molecular diffusion in **gaseous** and **liquid** systems under isothermal and isobaric conditions. ### 1.2 Two Engineering Scenarios (KTU Board Focus) The KTU syllabus specifically demands mastery over two canonical diffusion configurations: | # | Case Name | Physical Situation | |---|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)** | Both species A and B diffuse in *opposite* directions at equal and opposite molar rates. Example: Ammonia (A) and Nitrogen (B) in a vertical tube. | | 2 | **Diffusion of A through Stagnant B (Stefan's Diffusion)** | Species A diffuses through a *stationary* medium of species B. Example: Water vapor (A) diffusing through stagnant air (B). | > [!IMPORTANT] > The total pressure **P** and temperature **T** are held **constant** throughout the analysis. This is a board-exam-favorite simplification. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Ink Drop in Water" 🍵 Imagine dropping a single drop of **red ink** into a still glass of clear water. You will not stir the glass. Yet, after some hours, the water turns uniformly pink. *Why?* Each dye molecule is in constant, random thermal motion. The molecules at the high-concentration center statistically have *more neighbors to bump into* than those at the edges, so the net result of all these random walks is a *bulk migration* from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration. This macroscopic, statistical net flow is what we call **molecular diffusion**. The same principle — but governed by **Fick's First Law** — explains: * The dispersion of perfume vapors across a room * Oxygen absorption into blood through alveolar membranes * Moisture migration through a polymer packaging film * Acid leaching through a liquid membrane in separation processes ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics * **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** * **Standard Temperature (T_std)** = **298.15 K** * **Standard Pressure (P_std)** = **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa** * The unit of **Diffusivity (D_AB)** is **m²/s** (in SI) or **cm²/s** (in CGS). * Typical magnitudes: **D_AB ≈ 10⁻⁵ m²/s** for gases, **D_AB ≈ 10⁻⁹ m²/s** for liquids, and **D_AB ≈ 10⁻¹¹ m²/s** for solids. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Driving Force — Concentration Gradient The **driving potential** for molecular diffusion is the *spatial gradient* of species concentration. When concentration varies along one coordinate, say `z`, the local gradient is `dC_A/dz`. Molecules statistically flow from the higher to the lower concentration, hence the **negative sign** in Fick's law. ### 2.2 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (The Governing Equation) The fundamental constitutive equation for one-dimensional, steady-state molecular diffusion is: $$J_{A,z} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ where: * `J_{A,z}` = molar diffusive flux of A in the `z` direction (kmol/(m²·s)) * `D_{AB}` = binary diffusion coefficient or **diffusivity** of A in B (m²/s) * `dC_A/dz` = concentration gradient of A (kmol/m⁴) > [!IMPORTANT] > **J** is the *diffusive* flux relative to a *molar-average velocity*. The total molar flux **N** (relative to a fixed frame) is **N = J + C·v**, where `v` is the molar-average velocity. In KTU problems, you will be asked to find `N_A`. ### 2.3 The "Why" — Physical Origin of Fick's Law 1. **Random Motion:** Every molecule undergoes random thermal motion with mean free path `λ`. 2. **Gradient Existence:** If concentration is non-uniform, more molecules cross a plane from the high-C side than from the low-C side in any given time interval. 3. **Net Flux:** The imbalance produces a *net* mass transport. The larger the gradient, the larger the imbalance → linear proportionality (valid for dilute systems, ideal gases). ### 2.4 Case 1 — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) Here, `N_A = -N_B`. The net molar flow is zero, so the molar-average velocity `v* = 0`. Therefore, **the diffusive flux `J_A` equals the total flux `N_A`**. The final integrated form across a distance `z₂ - z₁ = δ` is: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} \left( C_{A1} - C_{A2} \right)$$ For an **ideal gas**, `C_A = P_A / (RT)`, leading to the pressure-form: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \left( \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{P} \right)$$ But since in EMCD, `P_{B1} = P_{B2}` (because `P_{A1} + P_{B1} = P_{A2} + P_{B2} = P`), the term `(P_{A1} - P_{A2})/P` simplifies to just a difference in mole fractions. ### 2.5 Case 2 — Diffusion of A through Stagnant B (Stefan's Law) This is the most heavily tested KTU topic. Here, `N_B = 0` (B is stagnant), so `v* ≠ 0`. We must use the full frame-fixed flux equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stefan's Diffusion (Integrated Form):** > > $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}} \right)$$ > > Equivalently, in terms of mole fractions and total molar concentration `C = P/(RT)`: > > $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{\delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}} \right)$$ The **logarithmic mean** of the non-diffusing species partial pressures is defined as: $$P_{B,M} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$ This allows the compact form: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta \cdot P_{B,M}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$ ### 2.6 Liquid-Phase Diffusion — Analogous Form For **dilute liquid solutions**, the gas equations carry over by replacing `P/(RT)` with the **total molar concentration `C`** of the liquid (in kmol/m³), and using `D_{AB}^L` (liquid diffusivity). For liquids, **Stokes-Einstein** gives the diffusivity correlation: $$D_{AB}^L = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$$ where `μ_B` is the viscosity of the solvent and `r_A` is the radius of the solute molecule. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — At a Glance > [!NOTE] > **Master this table.** Every KTU board question on diffusion is solved using one (or more) of these equations. | # | Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Substitution | |---|----------|---------------------|------------------| | 1 | Fick's First Law (differential) | `J_A = -D_{AB} dC_A/dz` | None | | 2 | EMCD — gas, concentration form | `N_A = (D_{AB}/δ)(C_{A1} - C_{A2})` | `C_A = P_A/(RT)` | | 3 | EMCD — gas, partial pressure form | `N_A = (D_{AB}/(RTδ))(P_{A1} - P_{A2})` | Dalton's law | | 4 | EMCD — gas, mole fraction form | `N_A = (D_{AB}·P/(RTδ))(x_{A1} - x_{A2})` | `P_A = x_A P` | | 5 | Stefan's Diffusion (A through stagnant B) | `N_A = (D_{AB}·P/(RTδ)) ln[(P - P_{A2})/(P - P_{A1})]` | Stagnant B condition | | 6 | Stefan's — log-mean form | `N_A = (D_{AB}·P)/(RT·δ·P_{B,M}) (P_{A1} - P_{A2})` | `P_{B,M} = (P_{B2} - P_{B1})/ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})` | | 7 | Stefan's — mole fraction form | `N_A = (D_{AB}·C/δ) ln[(1 - x_{A2})/(1 - x_{A1})]` | `C = P/(RT)` | | 8 | Equimolar: D_AB vs D_BA | `D_{AB} = D_{BA}` (for gases) | Reciprocity relation | | 9 | Total flux relation | `N_A + N_B = C·v*` | Molar-average velocity | | 10 | Liquid-phase Stokes-Einstein | `D_{AB}^L = k_B T / (6πμ_B r_A)` | Hydrodynamic theory | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility * **Distillation Columns:** Molecular diffusion governs interphase mass transfer at tray interfaces. * **Packed-Bed Absorbers:** Determines the height of a transfer unit (HTU) and the overall gas-phase mass-transfer coefficient. * **Food Packaging:** Predicts oxygen ingress and moisture loss in polymer films. * **Pharmaceutical Drying:** Estimates the rate of solvent removal from porous tablets. * **Environmental Engineering:** Quantifies pollutant dispersion in still air or stagnant groundwater. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Equimolar Counter-Diffusion Equation (Concentration Form) **Starting Point:** Fick's First Law in one dimension. $$N_A = J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Step 1 — Apply the steady-state condition.** At steady state, `N_A` does not depend on `z`. Therefore, separating variables is legitimate. $$N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} \, dC_A$$ **Step 2 — Identify the limits of integration.** The geometry is a stagnant film of thickness `δ` between two parallel planes. * At `z = 0`, `C_A = C_{A1}` (the high-concentration side). * At `z = δ`, `C_A = C_{A2}` (the low-concentration side). **Step 3 — Perform the integration.** $$N_A \int_{0}^{\delta} dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A$$ $$N_A \cdot \delta = -D_{AB} \left( C_{A2} - C_{A1} \right)$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the sign.** Since `C_{A1} > C_{A2}`, the right side is positive: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} \left( C_{A1} - C_{A2} \right)$$ **Step 5 — Convert to partial pressure form for an ideal gas.** Using `C_A = P_A/(RT)`: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RT \cdot \delta} \left( P_{A1} - P_{A2} \right)$$ This is the **EMCD formula** the KTU board expects verbatim. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Stefan's Law (Diffusion of A through Stagnant B) This is the most important derivation in Module 3. **Starting Point:** The full frame-fixed molar flux equation for a binary mixture. $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the stagnant-B condition.** Since B is stagnant in the fixed frame: $$N_B = 0$$ The flux equation therefore reduces to: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_A$$ **Step 2 — Use the isothermal, isobaric assumption.** Then `C` is constant, and Dalton's law gives `x_A = C_A / C`. Substitute: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to separate variables.** Move the `C_A N_A / C` term to the left: $$N_A \left( 1 - \frac{C_A}{C} \right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Step 4 — Recognize the term in parentheses.** Since `C_B = C - C_A`: $$1 - \frac{C_A}{C} = \frac{C - C_A}{C} = \frac{C_B}{C}$$ So: $$N_A \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Step 5 — Separate variables for integration.** $$N_A \, dz = -D_{AB} \, C \frac{dC_A}{C_B}$$ Since `C` is constant, divide by `C` and rearrange: $$\frac{N_A}{C} \, dz = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{C - C_A}$$ **Step 6 — Integrate across the film.** Lower limit: `z = 0, C_A = C_{A1}`. Upper limit: `z = δ, C_A = C_{A2}`. $$\frac{N_A}{C} \int_{0}^{\delta} dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} \frac{dC_A}{C - C_A}$$ **Step 7 — Evaluate both integrals.** $$\frac{N_A \cdot \delta}{C} = -D_{AB} \Big[ -\ln(C - C_A) \Big]_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}}$$ $$\frac{N_A \cdot \delta}{C} = D_{AB} \Big[ \ln(C - C_{A2}) - \ln(C - C_{A1}) \Big]$$ $$\frac{N_A \cdot \delta}{C} = D_{AB} \ln\!\left( \frac{C - C_{A2}}{C - C_{A1}} \right)$$ **Step 8 — Solve for `N_A`.** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{\delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{C - C_{A2}}{C - C_{A1}} \right)$$ **Step 9 — Convert to partial pressure form** using `C = P/(RT)` and `C_A = P_A/(RT)`: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}} \right)$$ This is the celebrated **Stefan's Law** for gas-phase diffusion of A through stagnant B. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Logarithmic-Mean Form The argument of the logarithm in Stefan's law can be expanded: $$\ln\!\left( \frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}} \right) = \ln\!\left( \frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}} \right)$$ because `P - P_A = P_B` (Dalton's law for the stagnant species). Now define the **logarithmic mean of P_B** (i.e., `P_{B,M}`): $$P_{B,M} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$ Therefore: $$\ln\!\left( \frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}} \right) = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{P_{B,M}} = \frac{(P - P_{A2}) - (P - P_{A1})}{P_{B,M}} = \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{P_{B,M}}$$ Substituting back into Stefan's equation: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta \cdot P_{B,M}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This form is the **favourite of the KTU paper-setter**, because the answer can be computed directly without invoking a logarithm, provided the student identifies and computes `P_{B,M}` correctly. --- ### 3.4 Illustrative Numerical Problem — KTU Style > **Question (Reference):** Ammonia (A) is absorbed from a gas mixture into water at 1 atm and 25 °C. The gas contains 20 mol% NH₃. The gas film is 1 mm thick. `D_{AB}` (NH₃–air) at this condition is `2.30 × 10⁻⁵` m²/s. The partial pressure of NH₃ at the water interface is essentially zero. Find the steady-state flux of NH₃ through the stagnant air film. **Given Data:** * `P = 101.325 kPa` * `T = 298 K` * `δ = 1 mm = 1 × 10⁻³ m` * `D_{AB} = 2.30 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s` * `x_{A1} = 0.20` → `P_{A1} = 0.20 × 101.325 = 20.265 kPa` * `P_{A2} = 0` (absorbed instantly at the water interface) * `P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 81.060 kPa` * `P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 101.325 kPa` **Solution — Using Stefan's Law (log-mean form):** **Step 1 — Compute `P_{B,M}`.** $$P_{B,M} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{101.325 - 81.060}{\ln(101.325/81.060)}$$ $$P_{B,M} = \frac{20.265}{\ln(1.2500)} = \frac{20.265}{0.22314} = 90.821 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the log-mean Stefan's equation.** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P \cdot (P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta \cdot P_{B,M}}$$ **Step 3 — Plug in numerical values.** $$N_A = \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5}) \times (101.325 \times 10^3) \times (20.265 \times 10^3)}{(8.314) \times (298) \times (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (90.821 \times 10^3)}$$ **Step 4 — Compute numerator.** $$\text{Numerator} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.01325 \times 10^{5} \times 2.0265 \times 10^{4}$$ $$\text{Numerator} = 2.30 \times 1.01325 \times 2.0265 \times 10^{(-5+5+4)} = 4.7241 \times 10^{4}$$ **Step 5 — Compute denominator.** $$\text{Denominator} = 8.314 \times 298 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.0821 \times 10^{4}$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 2477.572 \times 9.0821 \times 10^{( -3 + 4)} = 2477.572 \times 908.21 = 2.2497 \times 10^{6}$$ **Step 6 — Final division.** $$N_A = \frac{4.7241 \times 10^{4}}{2.2497 \times 10^{6}} = 2.10 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$ **Answer:** `N_A ≈ 2.10 × 10⁻² kmol/(m²·s)` ≈ **0.021 mol/(m²·s)** of ammonia diffuses through the stagnant air film. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to forget that in Stefan's law, the denominator uses `P_{B,M}` (the log-mean of the *non-diffusing* species), **not** `P_A` or just `P`. Marks are routinely lost for this single mis-substitution. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Molecular Diffusion Regimes ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start Molecular Diffusion Analysis]:::root --> A1{Is the medium
gas or liquid?} A1 -- Gas --> A2{Are both species
moving at equal
and opposite rates?} A1 -- Liquid --> A3{Dilute solution
approximation valid?} A2 -- Yes --> A4[Equimolar Counter-Diffusion EMCD]:::box A2 -- No --> A5{Is one species
stationary in
fixed frame?} A5 -- Yes --> A6[Stefans Diffusion A through stagnant B]:::box A5 -- No --> A7[General Binary Diffusion Both species moving]:::box A3 -- Yes --> A4 A3 -- No --> A8[Use activity-based
driving force]:::box A4 --> B1[Governing Equation NA = DAB over delta times CA1 minus CA2]:::final A6 --> B2[Governing Equation NA = DAB P over RT delta times ln of P minus PA2 over P minus PA1]:::final A7 --> B3[Governing Equation NA = J diffusive plus bulk convection term]:::final A8 --> B4[Use thermodynamic
activity gradient]:::final classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef box fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a classDef final fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Process Flow for Solving a Stefan's Diffusion Problem ```mermaid flowchart LR step1[Step 1: Read problem and list given data P T delta DAB xA1 xA2]:::st --> step2[Step 2: Convert mole fractions to partial pressures PA1 equals xA1 P PA2 equals xA2 P]:::st step2 --> step3[Step 3: Compute PB1 equals P minus PA1 and PB2 equals P minus PA2]:::st step3 --> step4[Step 4: Compute logarithmic mean PBM equals PB2 minus PB1 over ln of PB2 over PB1]:::st step4 --> step5[Step 5: Choose Stefans equation form log or direct log-mean]:::st step5 --> step6[Step 6: Substitute all values with consistent SI units Pa m s mol]:::st step6 --> step7[Step 7: Compute final flux NA in kmol per m2 s]:::st step7 --> step8[Step 8: State answer with three significant figures and unit]:::st classDef st fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#1f2937 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Block Schematic of a Gas Absorption Setup ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Bulk_Gas GBulk[Gas Bulk Phase yA1 high NH3 rich air]:::gas end subgraph Gas_Film Film[Stagnant Gas Film Thickness delta
Species A diffuses through stagnant B]:::film end subgraph Interface IFace[Gas Liquid Interface yA2 approaches zero]:::interface end subgraph Liquid_Bulk LBulk[Liquid Bulk Phase absorbed NH3]:::liq end GBulk -->|Bulk transport by convection| Film Film -->|Molecular diffusion of A through stagnant B| IFace IFace -->|Absorption into liquid| LBulk classDef gas fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef film fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef interface fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef liq fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams act as **functional block schematics** that map the engineering problem onto a sequence of solvable algebraic steps — a strategy highly recommended for the KTU valuation scheme. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term **"Equimolar Counter-Diffusion"** in a binary gas mixture. State Fick's First Law and the final integrated form of the molar flux for this case. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Equimolar Counter-Diffusion* refers to a binary gas transfer process in which the two species A and B move in **opposite directions** with **equal and opposite molar fluxes**, i.e., `N_A = -N_B`. Since there is no net molar transfer, the molar-average velocity is zero. **Fick's First Law:** $$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Integrated molar flux for EMCD:** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{\delta} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$ *(Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for Fick's law, 1 mark for the integrated form.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Differentiate clearly between **"Equimolar Counter-Diffusion"** and **"Diffusion of A through Stagnant B"** with one real-world example of each. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | EMCD | Stefan's Diffusion | |---------|------|---------------------| | Net molar flow | Zero (`N_A = -N_B`) | Non-zero (`N_B = 0`) | | Bulk velocity `v*` | Zero | Non-zero | | Driving force form | Linear in concentration | Logarithmic in partial pressure | | Example | Distillation of benzene–toluene | Water evaporating into stagnant air | *(Award 1.5 marks for the table; 1.5 marks for the two examples.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]** **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** Starting from the general frame-fixed flux equation for a binary gas mixture, derive the integrated form of **Stefan's Law** for the steady-state diffusion of species A through a stagnant layer of species B. Clearly state the underlying assumptions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In an ammonia absorption tower operating at 1 atm and 30 °C, ammonia (A) is absorbed from an air–ammonia mixture by water. The gas contains 15 mol% NH₃ at the bulk, and the partial pressure of NH₃ at the water interface is 0. The effective gas film thickness is `δ = 0.5 mm`. The binary diffusivity `D_{AB} = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s` at this condition. Calculate the **steady-state molar flux** of NH₃ through the stagnant air film using Stefan's equation. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** *Assumptions:* 1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion. 2. Isothermal and isobaric process (`T` and `P` constant). 3. Ideal gas behavior. 4. B is stagnant in the fixed frame: `N_B = 0`. 5. Constant total molar concentration `C`. *Derivation:* **Step 1 — Starting point (frame-fixed flux):** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ **Step 2 — Apply `N_B = 0`:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A N_A$$ **Step 3 — Substitute `x_A = C_A / C` and rearrange:** $$N_A \left( 1 - \frac{C_A}{C} \right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ $$N_A \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ **Step 4 — Separate and integrate from `z = 0` to `z = δ`:** $$\frac{N_A}{C} \int_0^\delta dz = -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} \frac{dC_A}{C_B}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the integral (note `C_B = C - C_A`):** $$\frac{N_A \cdot \delta}{C} = D_{AB} \ln\!\left( \frac{C - C_{A2}}{C - C_{A1}} \right)$$ **Step 6 — Solve for `N_A` and convert to pressure form:** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}} \right)$$ This is **Stefan's Law**. *(Award 1 mark per step; full 7 marks for the complete derivation.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):** **Given:** * `P = 101.325 kPa`, `T = 303 K`, `δ = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m` * `x_{A1} = 0.15` → `P_{A1} = 15.199 kPa` * `P_{A2} = 0` (perfect absorption at water interface) * `D_{AB} = 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s` **Step 1 — Compute `P_{B1}` and `P_{B2}`:** $$P_{B1} = 101.325 - 15.199 = 86.126 \text{ kPa}$$ $$P_{B2} = 101.325 - 0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Compute log-mean `P_{B,M}`:** $$P_{B,M} = \frac{101.325 - 86.126}{\ln(101.325/86.126)} = \frac{15.199}{\ln(1.17647)} = \frac{15.199}{0.16252} = 93.521 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Stefan's equation (log-mean form):** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P \cdot (P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{R \cdot T \cdot \delta \cdot P_{B,M}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and compute:** $$N_A = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times (101.325 \times 10^3) \times (15.199 \times 10^3)}{(8.314) \times (303) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (93.521 \times 10^3)}$$ * Numerator = `2.5 × 10⁻⁵ × 1.01325 × 10⁵ × 1.5199 × 10⁴ = 3.8497 × 10⁴` * Denominator = `8.314 × 303 × 5 × 10⁻⁴ × 9.3521 × 10⁴ = 1.1783 × 10⁶` $$N_A = \frac{3.8497 \times 10^4}{1.1783 \times 10^6} = 3.27 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$ **Final Answer:** `N_A ≈ 0.0327 kmol/(m²·s)` = **3.27 × 10⁻² kmol/(m²·s)** *(Award 2 marks for partial pressures, 2 marks for `P_{B,M}`, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer.)* --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative:** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the physical situation of **"Diffusion of A through Stagnant B"** at a gas–liquid interface. State why the molar-average velocity is non-zero in this case. Define the **logarithmic mean partial pressure `P_{B,M}`** of the stagnant species. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Two large reservoirs of **helium (A)** and **nitrogen (B)** are connected by a 5 mm long tube of 2 cm inner diameter at 25 °C and 1.2 atm total pressure. The mole fraction of helium is maintained at `x_{A1} = 0.10` on one side and `x_{A2} = 0.02` on the other side. The binary diffusivity is `D_{AB} = 7.2 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s` at this condition. Compute the **steady-state molar flux `N_A`** of helium assuming **equimolar counter-diffusion**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):** *Sketch:* A vertical cross-section showing a gas bulk region at `x_{A1}` (high) on the left, a stagnant film of thickness `δ`, and the gas–liquid interface at `x_{A2} ≈ 0` on the right. Arrows show A moving from left to right; B remains stationary. *Explanation:* In a real gas absorption unit, the liquid solvent immediately removes species A at the interface, so the bulk of the gas-phase B does not move. Therefore, `N_B = 0` and the molar-average velocity `v* = (N_A + N_B)/C = N_A/C ≠ 0`.
