This bulk convection of A in the `+z` direction enhances the diffusive transport, leading to the logarithmic form of the equation. *Definition:* $$P_{B,M} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$ This is the log-mean of the non-diffusing species' partial pressure at the two ends of the stagnant film. *(Award 2 marks for the sketch, 2 marks for the velocity explanation, 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the logic of why log form arises.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):** **Given:** * `P = 1.2 atm = 1.2 × 101.325 = 121.59 kPa` * `T = 298 K` * `δ = 5 mm = 5 × 10⁻³ m` * `x_{A1} = 0.10` → `P_{A1} = 0.10 × 121.59 = 12.159 kPa` * `x_{A2} = 0.02` → `P_{A2} = 0.02 × 121.59 = 2.4318 kPa` * `D_{AB} = 7.2 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s` * Condition: **EMCD** **Step 1 — Apply the EMCD equation:** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{R T \delta} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$ **Step 2 — Compute the difference `P_{A1} - P_{A2}`:** $$P_{A1} - P_{A2} = 12.159 - 2.4318 = 9.7272 \text{ kPa} = 9.7272 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the denominator `R·T·δ`:** $$R T \delta = 8.314 \times 298 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 12.388 \text{ J·m/mol}$$ **Step 4 — Compute `N_A`:** $$N_A = \frac{(7.2 \times 10^{-5}) \times (9.7272 \times 10^3)}{12.388}$$ $$N_A = \frac{0.70036}{12.388} = 5.65 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$ **Final Answer:** `N_A ≈ 5.65 × 10⁻² kmol/(m²·s)` = **0.0565 kmol/(m²·s)** of helium diffuses from the high-concentration side to the low-concentration side. *(Award 2 marks for choosing EMCD equation, 2 marks for `P_{A1} - P_{A2}`, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Deductions):** > 1. **Forgetting the log-mean `P_{B,M}`** in Stefan's law. Direct use of `(P - P_{A2})` as the denominator costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Mixing up CGS and SI units** in `D_{AB}` (cm²/s vs m²/s). A factor of `10⁴` slips in silently. > 3. **Wrong sign in Fick's Law.** `J_A = -D dC/dz` — the negative sign is mandatory. > 4. **Stagnant-B condition misapplied:** Students often write `N_A = -N_B` when the problem states "stagnant air." Read carefully: **stagnant ≠ equimolar**. > 5. **Dropping the temperature conversion.** `T` in Stefan must be in **Kelvin**, not °C. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) * **Fick's First Law:** `J_A = -D_{AB} dC_A/dz` — the *constitutive equation* of molecular diffusion. * **EMCD condition:** `N_A = -N_B`; molar-average velocity `v* = 0`; flux is purely diffusive. * **Stefan's Law (A through stagnant B):** Always logarithmic in the driving force; use `P_{B,M}` for the algebraic shortcut. * **Molar-average velocity:** `v* = (N_A + N_B)/C`. Non-zero whenever there is net molar flow. * **Diffusivity values (memorize the order):** Gases `~10⁻⁵`, Liquids `~10⁻⁹`, Solids `~10⁻¹¹` m²/s. * **Stokes-Einstein equation** applies to *liquids* — remember the 6π in the denominator. * **D_AB = D_BA** for binary gas systems (reciprocity). * **Always work in SI units** for board exams; convert `cm²/s → m²/s` by multiplying by `10⁻⁴`. * **Log-mean rule:** Use log-mean only when the *non-diffusing* species' partial pressure is in the denominator. * **Check `T` is in Kelvin** before any `RT` substitution. * **Sketch the geometry** before starting the calculation; identify which species is stagnant and at which boundary. * **Final answer units:** `N_A` is in **kmol/(m²·s)** — always state units explicitly to avoid losing one mark.

topics.title: Fick's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fick's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fick's Law of Diffusion — The Foundation of Mass Transfer

**Fick's Law** is the fundamental governing equation of molecular mass transfer, mathematically analogous to Fourier's Law of heat conduction and Newton's Law of viscous flow. It quantifies the rate at which a chemical species migrates through a medium under the driving force of a **concentration gradient**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a species by molecular diffusion is proportional to the concentration gradient of that species, and the flux occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration.* Mathematically:

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

where:
- $J_A^*$ = Molar flux of species **A** with respect to the mean molar velocity $\left[\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]$
- $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusion coefficient (or mass diffusivity) of **A** through **B** $\left[\frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}\right]$
- $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$ = Concentration gradient of **A** in the x-direction $\left[\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^4}\right]$
- The **negative sign** confirms that mass flows from regions of high concentration to regions of low concentration (analogous to heat flow down a temperature gradient).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowded Hallway" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Perfume in a Room":**
> Imagine you open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a closed room. The air near the bottle becomes saturated with perfume molecules (high concentration), while the far corner still has fresh air (low concentration). The perfume molecules, due to their random thermal motion (Brownian motion), will slowly migrate to the low-concentration region until the entire room smells uniform.
>
> **Why does this happen?**
> Molecules in the high-concentration zone collide more frequently and statistically "push" outward toward the dilute zone. Fick's Law mathematically captures this statistically averaged net motion. The constant $D_{AB}$ describes *how easily* the molecules can squeeze through the surrounding medium — high $D_{AB}$ means fast spreading (gases), low $D_{AB}$ means sluggish spreading (solids, dense liquids).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diffusion Coefficient (Gas) | $D_{AB}$ | $1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Diffusion Coefficient (Liquid) | $D_{AB}$ | $1 \times 10^{-9}$ to $1 \times 10^{-10}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Diffusion Coefficient (Solid) | $D_{AB}$ | $1 \times 10^{-13}$ or smaller | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | $\text{J}/(\text{mol} \cdot \text{K})$ |
| Standard Molar Volume | $V_M$ | **22.4** (at STP) | $\text{L/mol}$ |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³** | $\text{molecules/mol}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Module 3 Focus:**
> Module 3 of PEPOT413 specifically covers *Molecular Diffusion*, *Fick's First Law*, *Fick's Second Law*, *Stefan's Law (Diffusion through Stagnant Film)*, and the *Analogy between Heat and Mass Transfer*. Numerical problems frequently test equimolar counter-diffusion and the stagnant gas film scenario.

---

## 1.4 Types of Fluxes Used in KTU Problems

A critical distinction in KTU problem-solving is the difference between three different flux definitions. Confusing these is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in mass transfer.

1. **Molar flux with respect to stationary coordinates** ($N_A$): Flux measured in the lab frame. Used in **Fick's First Law** for **equimolar counter-diffusion**.
2. **Molar flux with respect to molar average velocity** ($J_A^*$): Flux relative to the bulk molar flow. The "true" Fick's Law flux.
3. **Mass flux with respect to mass average velocity** ($j_A$): Mass-basis analog of $J_A^*$.

> [!CAUTION]
> Always check what type of flux the problem is asking for. The "$\partial C_A / \partial x$" gradient alone gives $J_A^*$, **not** $N_A$, when there is a net molar flow.

---

## 1.5 Historical Context (Brief)

Adolf **Fick** (1829–1901), a German physiologist, proposed the law in **1855**, drawing direct inspiration from Fourier's heat conduction law (1822). He published it in the *Annals of Physics* while studying diffusion in biological membranes. The form remains unchanged after 170 years because it is a direct consequence of **statistical mechanics** and the **random walk** of molecules.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Fick's Law — Statistical Derivation Intuition

At the molecular scale, each molecule undergoes **random thermal motion (Brownian motion)**. In an isotropic, isothermal, isobaric mixture, the *average* motion is zero. However, when a concentration gradient exists, there is a **statistical bias**:

- On the high-concentration side, more molecules "leak" toward the low-concentration side per unit time than vice versa.
- This imbalance, averaged over billions of molecules, produces a **net macroscopic flux**.

> [!NOTE]
> Fick's Law is a **constitutive equation** — it does not derive from first principles of mechanics but emerges from a **linear approximation** valid when the mean free path is much smaller than the macroscopic length scale (continuum assumption). When this fails (e.g., Knudsen diffusion in nanopores), more complex models such as the **Dusty Gas Model** are used.

---

## 2.2 Fick's First Law — Three Important Working Forms

Depending on how concentration is expressed, Fick's First Law takes three equivalent forms:

### Form 1: Molar Concentration Form (Most Common in KTU)

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kmol}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]$$

### Form 2: Partial Pressure Form (Used for Gases via Ideal Gas Law)

Since for an ideal gas, $C_A = \dfrac{p_A}{RT}$, we can write:

$$J_A^* = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dx}$$

### Form 3: Mass Fraction / Mole Fraction Form

$$J_A^* = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx}$$

where $y_A$ is the mole fraction of **A** and $C$ is the total molar concentration.

---

## 2.3 Fick's Second Law — The Time-Dependent Form

When concentration varies with **both position and time** (transient or unsteady-state mass transfer), Fick's First Law is combined with a **species mass balance** to yield Fick's Second Law:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

For three-dimensional isotropic diffusion:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \left( \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:**
> Notice the **striking analogy** with the heat equation (Fourier's Second Law): $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$. The thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ is replaced by mass diffusivity $D_{AB}$. This analogy is the foundation of the heat-mass transfer analogy (Reynolds, Chilton-Colburn) tested in Module 3.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Memorize these — they cover 90% of KTU mass transfer numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (basic) |
| 2 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + y_A (N_A + N_B)$ | Flux in stationary frame |
| 3 | $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB} \, C}{1 - y_A} \dfrac{dy_A}{dx}$ | General molar flux relation |
| 4 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, C \, (y_{A1} - y_{A2})}{\delta}$ | Steady flux through stagnant B (Stefan's Law) |
| 5 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, \delta} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}}\right)$ | Stefan's Law in pressure form |
| 6 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, p_{BM}} \cdot \dfrac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{\delta}$ | Linearized form for dilute systems |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law (1-D transient) |
| 8 | $D_{AB} \cdot P = \text{const}$ (at constant T) | Pressure-dependence of gas diffusivity |
| 9 | $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.75}$ (Chapman-Enskog) | Temperature scaling for gases |
| 10 | $\text{Sh} = \dfrac{k_c L}{D_{AB}} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Sc})$ | Sherwood number analogy to Nusselt |

### 2.4.1 Boundary Conditions for Fick's Second Law (Standard Cases)

| Condition Type | Mathematical Form | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Constant surface conc. (Dirichlet) | $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,s}$ | Gas absorbed at a liquid surface |
| Impermeable wall (Neumann) | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | Symmetry line |
| Initial condition | $C_A(x, 0) = C_{A,0}$ | Initial uniform concentration |
| Convective boundary | $-D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ | Mass transfer with convection |

---

## 2.5 Equimolar Counter-Diffusion — The Simplest Special Case

When two species **A** and **B** diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** ($N_A = -N_B$), there is **no net molar flow** and therefore no bulk motion.

In this case, the stationary-frame flux $N_A$ equals the diffusion flux $J_A^*$:

$$N_A = J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

**Engineering Examples of Equimolar Counter-Diffusion:**
- Distillation of a binary mixture (e.g., benzene-toluene)
- Counter-diffusion of two ideal gases in a closed tube
- Membrane separation of equimolar binary liquid mixtures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:**
> If a problem says *"A and B diffuse equimolarly in opposite directions"*, you may use the simple form $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ directly. This avoids the full Stefan's Law derivation and saves 3-4 minutes.

---

## 2.6 Stefan's Law (Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B)

The most general and **most frequently asked** case in KTU Module 3: species **A** diffuses through a **stationary** medium **B** ($N_B = 0$).

Starting from the general flux relation and applying $N_B = 0$:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} C}{1 - y_A} \frac{dy_A}{dx}$$

Integrating across a stagnant film of thickness $\delta$ from $y_{A1}$ to $y_{A2}$:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{\delta} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$$

Equivalently, in partial pressure form (ideal gas, $C = P/RT$):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, \delta} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}}\right)$$

> [!CAUTION]
> For **dilute systems** ($y_A \ll 1$, i.e., $p_{BM} \approx P$), the log-mean approximation is used: $N_A \approx \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RT \delta} \cdot \dfrac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}$. This is the form most often asked in KTU Part B problems.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Field | Application of Fick's Law |
|---|---|
| **Chemical Engineering** | Design of absorption columns, distillation trays, membrane separators |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Pollutant dispersion in atmosphere and groundwater |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Drug release from polymer matrices, transdermal patches |
| **Food Industry** | Drying of foods, salting, sugar diffusion in fruits |
| **Metallurgy** | Carburization of steel, doping of semiconductors |
| **Biomedical** | Oxygen transport in tissues, dialysis machines |

> [!NOTE]
> In **carburization** of steel (a favorite KTU 2-mark question), carbon diffuses through a stagnant iron matrix. The time required to achieve a target surface carbon concentration is calculated directly from Fick's Second Law solution.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion Flux in a Gas Mixture

### Problem Setup
Two large reservoirs containing binary gas mixture (A + B) are connected by a tube of length $L$ and cross-section $S$. Species A diffuses from reservoir 1 (high $p_{A1}$) to reservoir 2 (low $p_{A2}$), and B diffuses in the opposite direction. The system is at constant $T$ and $P$.

**Assumption:** $N_A = -N_B$ (equimolar counter-diffusion).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Start with Fick's First Law in partial pressure form (for ideal gases).

$$J_A^* = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dx}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that there is no net molar flow, so the molar flux in the stationary frame equals the diffusion flux.

$$N_A = J_A^* = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate the differential.

$$N_A \, dx = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \, dp_A$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $x = 0$ (point 1) to $x = L$ (point 2).

$$\int_0^L N_A \, dx = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} dp_A$$

**Step 5:** $N_A$ is constant (steady state), so it comes out of the integral.

$$N_A \cdot L = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} [p_A]_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} (p_{A2} - p_{A1})$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $N_A$.

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RTL} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$

**Step 7:** Convert to mole fraction form using $p_A = y_A P$.

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RTL} (y_{A1} - y_{A2})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important result** in KTU Module 3 mass transfer. Memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Stefan's Law for Diffusion Through Stagnant Film

### Problem Setup
Species A diffuses through stagnant B (e.g., ammonia A diffusing through stagnant air B). At point 1 (x = 0): $p_{A1}$, $p_{B1} = P - p_{A1}$. At point 2 (x = $\delta$): $p_{A2}$, $p_{B2} = P - p_{A2}$. The total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ are constant.

**Assumption:** $N_B = 0$ (B is stagnant).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** General flux relation for species A in the stationary frame.

$$N_A = -C D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx} + y_A (N_A + N_B)$$

**Step 2:** Apply $N_B = 0$.

$$N_A = -C D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx} + y_A N_A$$

**Step 3:** Bring $y_A N_A$ to the left.

$$N_A (1 - y_A) = -C D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the gradient.

$$N_A = -\frac{C D_{AB}}{1 - y_A} \frac{dy_A}{dx}$$

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate. Note that $C$ is constant (constant $T$ and $P$ for ideal gas) and $N_A$ is constant (steady state).

$$N_A \int_0^\delta dx = -C D_{AB} \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integral. Recall that $\int \dfrac{dy_A}{1 - y_A} = -\ln(1 - y_A)$.

$$N_A \cdot \delta = -C D_{AB} \left[ -\ln(1 - y_A) \right]_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}}$$

$$N_A \cdot \delta = C D_{AB} \left[ \ln(1 - y_{A2}) - \ln(1 - y_{A1}) \right]$$

**Step 7:** Combine the logarithms.

$$N_A \cdot \delta = C D_{AB} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}} \right)$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $N_A$.

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{C D_{AB}}{\delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}} \right)}$$

**Step 9:** For ideal gas, $C = \dfrac{P}{RT}$. Substitute.

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}} \right) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}} \right)$$

**Step 10:** For dilute systems, use the log-mean approximation: $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) \approx \dfrac{y_{A1} - y_{A2}}{y_{BM}} = \dfrac{y_{A1} - y_{A2}}{(1 - y_A)_{lm}}$.

$$\boxed{N_A \approx \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \cdot \frac{y_{A1} - y_{A2}}{y_{BM}} = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \cdot \frac{p_{A1} - p_{A2}}{p_{BM}}}$$

where $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of B**.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Fick's Second Law from First Principles

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a differential element of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area. Apply **conservation of mass for species A**.

$$\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Rate in} - \text{Rate out} + \text{Rate of generation}$$

**Step 2:** Assume:
- No chemical reaction (generation = 0)
- Steady molar flux gradient in space
- One-dimensional diffusion

$$N_{A,x}\big\vert_x - N_{A,x}\big\vert_{x+dx} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx$$

**Step 3:** Expand the first term using Taylor series.

$$N_{A,x} - \left( N_{A,x} + \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x} dx \right) = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$-\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x} dx = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute Fick's First Law $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$ (for equimolar counter-diffusion, $D_{AB}$ constant).

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Since $D_{AB}$ is constant, it comes out of the derivative.

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

This is **Fick's Second Law** — the mass transfer analog of Fourier's heat equation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Form of Fick's Law

> [!TIP]
> Use this decision matrix to select the correct formula on the KTU exam. It is the most important "exam survival" skill for Module 3.

| Scenario | Key Indicator | Correct Formula to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Equimolar counter-diffusion | "$N_A = -N_B$" stated | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ |
| A through stagnant B (general) | $N_B = 0$, $y_A$ not negligible | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}}\right)$ |
| A through stagnant B (dilute) | $y_A < 0.1$ approximately | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta \cdot p_{BM}}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ |
| Unsteady, 1-D | "with time", "transient" | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ |
| Concentration given in mole fraction | $y_A$ instead of $C_A$ | Use $J_A^* = -C D_{AB} \dfrac{dy_A}{dx}$ |
| Mass-basis (not molar) | "mass flux", "kg" units | $j_A = -\rho D_{AB} \dfrac{d\omega_A}{dx}$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (Dec 2023 Pattern):**
Hydrogen (A) diffuses through a stagnant film of nitrogen (B) of thickness 2 mm at 298 K and 1 atm total pressure. The partial pressure of H₂ at one side is 0.08 atm and at the other side is 0.02 atm. The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB} = 6.9 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of H₂. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the scenario. This is **A through stagnant B**, so we use Stefan's Law.

**Step 2:** Compute the partial pressures of B at the two sides.

$$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 1.0 - 0.08 = 0.92 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 1.0 - 0.02 = 0.98 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the log-mean partial pressure of B.

$$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{0.98 - 0.92}{\ln(0.98/0.92)}$$

$$\ln(0.98/0.92) = \ln(1.0652) = 0.06313$$

$$p_{BM} = \frac{0.06}{0.06313} = 0.9504 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Stefan's Law in the linearized form (system is reasonably dilute).

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T \delta \cdot p_{BM}} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values. Convert atm to Pa: 1 atm = 101325 Pa.

$$N_A = \frac{(6.9 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (101325)}{8.314 \cdot 298 \cdot (0.002) \cdot (0.9504 \cdot 101325)} (0.08 \cdot 101325 - 0.02 \cdot 101325)$$

Wait — to simplify, use atm directly and the $P$ in the numerator will cancel the $p_{BM}$ in atm if $P$ and $p_{BM}$ are both in atm:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T \delta \cdot p_{BM}} (p_{A1} - p_{A2}) = \frac{(6.9 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 1.0}{8.314 \cdot 298 \cdot 0.002 \cdot 0.9504} (0.08 - 0.02)$$

$$N_A = \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 0.06}{8.314 \cdot 298 \cdot 0.002 \cdot 0.9504}$$

$$N_A = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-6}}{4.706} = 8.80 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to more readable units if needed.

$$N_A \approx 8.80 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} = 8.80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** $N_A \approx 8.80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$

## 4.1 Fick's Law in the Mass Transfer Architecture

The following block diagram maps how Fick's Law integrates into the broader mass transfer framework, showing the relationship between molecular diffusion, convection, and the overall mass transfer coefficient.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Concentration Gradient Exists]) --> input1[Concentration C_A at x = 0]
    input1 --> process1{Fick's First Law Applied}
    process1 --> output1[Calculate Molar Flux J_A]
    output1 --> decider{Net Molar Flow?}
    decider -->|No, N_A = -N_B| path1[Equimolar Counter-Diffusion]
    decider -->|Yes, N_B = 0| path2[Stefan's Law: A through Stagnant B]
    decider -->|General Case| path3[Full Fick Equation]
    path1 --> formula1[N_A = D_AB P / RTL times yA1 - yA2]
    path2 --> formula2[N_A = D_AB P / RT delta times ln P-pB2 over P-pB1]
    path3 --> formula3[N_A = -D_AB C / 1-yA times dyA/dx]
    formula1 --> unify[Unified Output: Molar Flux N_A]
    formula2 --> unify
    formula3 --> unify
    unify --> trans{Time Dependence?}
    trans -->|Steady| finalA[Design of Equipment, Absorption Column]
    trans -->|Transient| finalB[Apply Fick's Second Law]
    finalB --> finalC[Solve PDE for C_A of x and t]
    finalC --> application[Engineering Applications]
    finalA --> application
    application --> app1[Distillation]
    application --> app2[Drying]
    application --> app3[Carburization]
    application --> app4[Membrane Separation]
    app1 --> endNode([End: Mass Transfer Process Designed])
    app2 --> endNode
    app3 --> endNode
    app4 --> endNode
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Physical Setup of Stagnant Film Diffusion

```
        x = 0                                    x = delta
          |                                          |
          v                                          v
  +-------------+    STAGNANT GAS FILM B           +-------------+
  |             |                                   |             |
  |   GAS A     |    <- A diffuses to right ->      |   GAS A     |
  | p_A1 high   |    <- B does NOT move ->          | p_A2 low    |
  | p_B1 = P-p_A1|                                  | p_B2 = P-p_A2|
  +-------------+                                   +-------------+
       Plane 1                                        Plane 2
       
   (e.g., Ammonia A diffusing through stagnant air B in a pipe)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:**
> - At Plane 1 (x = 0): high concentration of A → A starts to diffuse rightward.
> - B is stagnant — it neither moves left nor right.
> - At Plane 2 (x = δ): low concentration of A.
> - The bulk motion of the gas is zero, but the **diffusion flux** of A is nonzero.

## 4.5 Schematic: Fick's First Law — Concentration Profile in Steady Diffusion

```
   C_A (concentration of A)
    ^
    |  *
    |    *
    |      *
    |        *
    |          *
    |            *
    |              *
    |                *
    |                  *
    |                    *
    |                      *
    |                        *
    +--------------------------*------>  x (distance)
    0                          delta
    (high C_A)               (low C_A)
    
    Slope = -dC_A/dx = constant > 0 (linear profile in steady state)
    Flux J_A = -D_AB * slope = positive (in +x direction)
```

> [!TIP]
> For **steady-state diffusion** in a stagnant film, the concentration profile of A is **linear** (when B is stagnant AND the system is dilute). For concentrated systems, the profile is **slightly nonlinear** (logarithmic). For unsteady diffusion, the profile is **time-dependent** and given by error function solutions of Fick's Second Law.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Define Fick's First Law of Diffusion. State the units of the diffusion coefficient.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the molar flux of a species A by molecular diffusion in a binary mixture is proportional to the concentration gradient of that species, and the flux occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration. Mathematically: $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$.
>
> **Units (1 mark):** The units of the diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ are **m²/s** (square meters per second). This is verified by dimensional analysis: $[J_A^*] = \text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$ and $[dC_A/dx] = \text{kmol/m}^4$, so $[D_{AB}] = \dfrac{\text{kmol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}{\text{kmol/m}^4} = \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] — *Differentiate between Fick's First Law and Fick's Second Law.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fick's First Law | Fick's Second Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Governs** | Steady-state diffusion | Unsteady (transient) diffusion |
| **Variables** | Spatial only | Spatial and time |
| **Math form** | $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ |
| **Type of equation** | Algebraic (after integration) | Partial differential equation |
| **Analogy** | Fourier's First Law | Fourier's Second Law (heat equation) |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** State and derive Stefan's Law for the diffusion of species A through a stagnant gas film B. Clearly state all assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mixture of H₂S (A) and air (B) is in contact with water at 300 K and 1.5 atm total pressure. The mole fraction of H₂S at the gas-liquid interface is $y_{A1} = 0.05$ and in the bulk gas $y_{A2} = 0.0$ (essentially zero, as H₂S is completely absorbed by water). The gas film thickness is $\delta = 1.5$ mm. The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the molar flux of H₂S absorbed. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Statement (2 marks):**
*Stefan's Law describes the steady-state molar diffusion flux of species A through a stagnant medium B in which B has zero net molar flux. The general result is:*

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}} \right)$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady state (concentrations do not change with time).
2. One-dimensional diffusion in the x-direction.
3. Ideal gas behavior.
4. Constant temperature and total pressure.
5. Species B is stagnant ($N_B = 0$).
6. Constant diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$.

Starting from the general flux relation:

$$N_A = -C D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx} + y_A (N_A + N_B)$$

Apply $N_B = 0$:

$$N_A (1 - y_A) = -C D_{AB} \frac{dy_A}{dx}$$

$$N_A \, dx = -\frac{C D_{AB}}{1 - y_A} dy_A$$

Integrate from $x = 0$ (point 1) to $x = \delta$ (point 2):

$$N_A \int_0^\delta dx = -C D_{AB} \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} \frac{dy_A}{1 - y_A}$$

$$N_A \cdot \delta = C D_{AB} \ln\!\left( \frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}} \right)$$

Substituting $C = P/RT$ for ideal gas:

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}} \right)}$$

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Identify the scenario. A is absorbed completely by water ($y_{A2} = 0$), B is stagnant air. **Use Stefan's Law.**

**Step 2:** Calculate partial pressures.

$$p_{A1} = y_{A1} P = 0.05 \times 1.5 = 0.075 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{A2} = y_{A2} P = 0.0 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 1.5 - 0.075 = 1.425 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 1.5 - 0.0 = 1.5 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Stefan's Law in logarithmic form (the system is not extremely dilute, so we use the exact form).

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{P - p_{B2}}{P - p_{B1}} \right) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta} \ln\!\left( \frac{1.5}{1.425} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Compute the log term.

$$\ln(1.5/1.425) = \ln(1.0526) = 0.05129$$

**Step 5:** Convert all to SI units.
- $D_{AB} = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
- $P = 1.5 \text{ atm} = 1.5 \times 101325 = 151987.5$ Pa
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 300$ K
- $\delta = 0.0015$ m

**Step 6:** Substitute.

$$N_A = \frac{(1.85 \times 10^{-5}) (151987.5)}{(8.314)(300)(0.0015)} \times 0.05129$$

$$N_A = \frac{2.8118}{3.7413} \times 0.05129$$

$$N_A = 0.7515 \times 0.05129$$

$$N_A = 0.03855 \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}$$

**Step 7:** Final answer with units.

$$\boxed{N_A \approx 3.86 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)}}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of diffusion starting from a differential mass balance on a control volume. State the assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A slab of polymer of thickness 6 mm contains a uniform initial concentration of a diffusing species $C_0 = 0.05$ kmol/m³. At time $t = 0$, the surface concentration is suddenly reduced to $C_s = 0.01$ kmol/m³ and held constant. Calculate the time required for the concentration at a depth of 2 mm to drop to 0.02 kmol/m³. Take $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. The solution of Fick's Second Law for a semi-infinite slab is:

$$\frac{C(x,t) - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \text{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t}} \right)$$

Use the error function table to find the required time. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. One-dimensional diffusion along x-direction.
2. No chemical reaction in the control volume.
3. Constant diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ (independent of concentration and position).
4. No bulk motion (or equimolar counter-diffusion).
5. No sources or sinks within the volume.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a differential element of thickness $dx$ and unit cross-sectional area. Apply the **species mass balance**:

$$\left( \text{Rate of A in} \right) - \left( \text{Rate of A out} \right) = \left( \text{Rate of accumulation} \right)$$

**Rate in** at $x$: $N_A \big\vert_x$

**Rate out** at $x + dx$: $N_A \big\vert_{x+dx}$

**Rate of accumulation**: $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx$

Therefore:

$$N_A \big\vert_x - N_A \big\vert_{x+dx} = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx$$

Expanding the outlet term using Taylor series:

$$N_A \big\vert_{x+dx} \approx N_A \big\vert_x + \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x} dx$$

Substituting:

$$-\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x} dx = \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} dx$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x}$$

Substitute Fick's First Law: $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$ (with $D_{AB}$ constant):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left( -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} \right) = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

This is **Fick's Second Law**.

---

#### Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Identify the scenario. This is **transient diffusion** in a semi-infinite slab with a step change in surface concentration. We use the given error function solution.

**Step 2:** Plug in the given values.

- $C_0 = 0.05$ kmol/m³ (initial)
- $C_s = 0.01$ kmol/m³ (surface)
- $C(x, t) = 0.02$ kmol/m³ (at depth $x = 2$ mm = 0.002 m)
- $D_{AB} = 2 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s
- $x = 0.002$ m

**Step 3:** Compute the left-hand side (dimensionless concentration ratio).

$$\frac{C - C_s}{C_0 - C_s} = \frac{0.02 - 0.01}{0.05 - 0.01} = \frac{0.01}{0.04} = 0.25$$

**Step 4:** Equate to error function.

$$\text{erf}(\eta) = 0.25, \quad \text{where} \quad \eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t}}$$

**Step 5:** From the error function table, $\text{erf}(\eta) = 0.25$ corresponds to $\eta \approx 0.2252$ (interpolating between $\text{erf}(0.2) = 0.2227$ and $\text{erf}(0.3) = 0.3286$).

**Step 6:** Solve for $t$.

$$\eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}} = 0.2252$$

$$\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t} = \frac{x}{2 \times 0.2252} = \frac{0.002}{0.4504} = 4.4403 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$D_{AB} \, t = (4.4403 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.972 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$t = \frac{1.972 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 9858 \text{ s}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to hours.

$$t = \frac{9858}{3600} \approx 2.74 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 8:** Final answer.

$$\boxed{t \approx 2.74 \text{ hours} = 9858 \text{ s}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Fick's Law Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (loss: 1-2 marks):** Many students mix atm and Pa, or use $R$ in different units (8.314 vs. 82.06 cm³·atm/mol·K). The KTU board examiner will deduct marks if units in the numerator and denominator don't match. **Always convert $P$ to Pa and $R$ to 8.314 J/(mol·K) OR use $P$ in atm and $R$ in 82.06 cm³·atm/(mol·K).**
> 
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion (loss: 1 mark):** Fick's Law has a negative sign. A positive flux is in the direction of decreasing concentration. Students often forget the negative sign or assign the wrong sign based on the direction.
> 
> 3. **Equimolar vs. Stagnant Confusion (loss: 2-3 marks):** Equimolar counter-diffusion has $N_A = -N_B$ and uses a simple linear formula. Stagnant B uses Stefan's Law with the logarithm. **Mixing these up is the most common error in KTU Module 3.**
> 
> 4. **Forgetting $p_{BM}$ in the Linearized Stefan's Law (loss: 2 marks):** For dilute systems, students often use $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{R T \delta}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ — this is the equimolar formula, NOT the stagnant B formula. The correct dilute form has $p_{BM}$ in the denominator.
> 
> 5. **Fick's Second Law Boundary Conditions (loss: 1-2 marks):** When asked to derive or solve, students must clearly state **initial and boundary conditions**. Examiners specifically look for these.
> 
> 6. **Dimensional Errors in $D_{AB}$ (loss: 1 mark):** Always check: gas $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s, liquid $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s, solid $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-13}$ m²/s. Wrong order of magnitude = full mark deduction.
> 
> 7. **Confusing Mole Fraction with Partial Pressure (loss: 1 mark):** Remember $p_A = y_A P$ for ideal gases. Both appear in problems, and converting correctly is critical.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Fick's Law — Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> 
> ✅ **Fick's First Law (Steady State):**
> - Basic form: $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$
> - Flux is in direction of **decreasing** concentration (negative sign is critical).
> - $D_{AB}$ units: **m²/s** (same units as thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$).
> 
> ✅ **Fick's Second Law (Unsteady State):**
> - $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$
> - Direct analog of Fourier's heat equation and momentum equation.
> - Solved with appropriate **initial and boundary conditions**.
> 
> ✅ **Three Most Asked Scenarios:**
> 1. **Equimolar counter-diffusion** → simple linear formula.
> 2. **A through stagnant B (general)** → logarithmic Stefan's Law.
> 3. **A through stagnant B (dilute)** → linearized with $p_{BM}$.
> 
> ✅ **Key Constants & Conversions:**
> - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) or 82.06 cm³·atm/(mol·K) or 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)
> - 1 atm = 101325 Pa = 1.01325 bar
> - At STP, $C = P/RT = 1/(0.0821 \times 273) = 0.0446$ kmol/m³
> 
> ✅ **Order-of-Magnitude Values of $D_{AB}$:**
> - Gases: $10^{-5}$ m²/s
> - Liquids: $10^{-9}$ m²/s
> - Solids: $10^{-13}$ m²/s or smaller
> 
> ✅ **Diffusivity Scaling Laws:**
> - $D_{AB} \cdot P = $ constant (at constant T) for gases.
> - $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.75}$ (Chapman-Enskog theory for gases).
> - $D_{AB} \propto T / \mu$ (Stokes-Einstein for liquids).
> 
> ✅ **Three-Analog Table (must memorize):**
> - Heat: $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$, diffusivity $\alpha$
> - Momentum: $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$, diffusivity $\nu$
> - Mass: $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$, diffusivity $D_{AB}$
> 
> ✅ **Nusselt → Sherwood Analogy:**
> - $\text{Nu} = hL/k$ (heat) → $\text{Sh} = k_c L / D_{AB}$ (mass)
> - $\text{Pr} = \nu / \alpha$ (heat) → $\text{Sc} = \nu / D_{AB}$ (mass)
> - $\text{Re}$ is the same in both.
> 
> ✅ **Common KTU Question Patterns:**
> - "State and derive Stefan's Law" — must derive, not just state.
> - "Calculate the molar flux" — must show unit conversions.
> - "Differentiate Fick's First and Second Law" — tabular format preferred.
> - "Explain heat-mass transfer analogy" — emphasize form similarity, NOT physical similarity.
> 
> ✅ **Examiner's Pet Topics:**
> - Carburization of steel (transient diffusion).
> - Ammonia absorption in water (stagnant B scenario).
> - Equimolar counter-diffusion in distillation.
> - Transdermal drug delivery (Fick's Second Law with error function solution).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusivity of Gases and Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffusivity of Gases and Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Diffusivity (Mass Diffusivity)

**Mass Diffusivity (D<sub>AB</sub>)** is a fundamental transport property in mass transfer that quantifies the rate at which a species A diffuses through a medium B under the influence of a concentration gradient. It is analogous to thermal diffusivity (α) in heat transfer and kinematic viscosity (ν) in momentum transfer — both of which are momentum/energy transport analogs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Diffusivity is defined as the proportionality constant in Fick's First Law of Diffusion, which relates the molar flux of a species to its concentration gradient. It physically represents the ability of molecules to interpenetrate and intermix due to random molecular motion. The SI unit of diffusivity is **m²/s**.*

**Mathematical Expression (Fick's First Law):**

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

Where:
- **J<sub>A</sub>** = Molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s)
- **D<sub>AB</sub>** = Binary diffusivity of A in B (m²/s)
- **dC<sub>A</sub>/dz** = Concentration gradient of A in the z-direction (kmol/m⁴)
- **Negative sign** = Diffusion occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Perfume Room:**
> Imagine you spray perfume in one corner of a closed room. Within seconds, the fragrance spreads throughout the entire room, even without any fan or forced convection. This is **molecular diffusion** in action. The molecules of perfume vapor (A) move randomly through air molecules (B) and gradually occupy the entire space. The **diffusivity** is essentially a measure of *how fast* this molecular mixing happens. A higher diffusivity means faster spreading (like a light gas in air), while a lower diffusivity means slower spreading (like a heavy vapor in a dense liquid).

**Why is this important for engineers?**
- **Chemical Engineers:** Design distillation columns, absorbers, and reactors.
- **Petroleum Engineers:** Predict gas separation and oil recovery.
- **Food Engineers:** Model drying, packaging, and flavor retention.
- **Environmental Engineers:** Model pollutant dispersion in air/water.
- **Biomedical Engineers:** Design drug delivery and oxygen transport in tissues.

---

## 1.3 Orders of Magnitude of Diffusivity

| Phase | Typical D<sub>AB</sub> Range | Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gases** at atmospheric pressure | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s | Fast diffusion |
| **Liquids** | $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s | Slow diffusion (≈ 10,000× slower than gases) |
| **Solids** | $10^{-14}$ to $10^{-12}$ m²/s | Extremely slow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Diffusion in liquids is **~10,000 times slower** than in gases because liquid molecules are densely packed and have restricted mobility. This is why a sugar cube takes minutes to dissolve, while a perfume smell fills a room in seconds!

---

## 1.4 Classification of Diffusivity

1. **Mutual (Binary) Diffusivity — D<sub>AB</sub> or D<sub>BA</sub>:** Diffusion of species A through stagnant B (or vice versa) in a two-component system.
2. **Self-Diffusivity — D<sub>AA</sub>:** Diffusion of a species through itself (measured using radioactive tracers).
3. **Tracer Diffusivity — D<sub>A*</sub>:** Diffusion of a labelled species in an inert medium.
4. **Knudsen Diffusivity — D<sub>K</sub>:** Diffusion in narrow pores where molecule-pore wall collisions dominate.
5. **Effective Diffusivity — D<sub>eff</sub>:** Used in porous media, accounts for tortuosity and porosity.

---

## 1.5 Physical Significance — The Three Analogous Transport Properties

| Transport Mechanism | Property | Units | Governing Law |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Momentum Transfer | Kinematic Viscosity (ν = μ/ρ) | m²/s | Newton's Law |
| Heat Transfer | Thermal Diffusivity (α = k/ρC<sub>p</sub>) | m²/s | Fourier's Law |
| Mass Transfer | Mass Diffusivity (D<sub>AB</sub>) | m²/s | Fick's Law |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Reynolds, Prandtl, and Schmidt Triplet:** The dimensionless group analogies:
> - **Schmidt Number:** Sc = ν/D<sub>AB</sub> (analog of Prandtl number)
> - **Lewis Number:** Le = α/D<sub>AB</sub> (heat vs. mass transfer)
> - **Sherwood Number:** Sh = k<sub>c</sub>·L/D<sub>AB</sub> (analog of Nusselt number)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Diffusivity

### 2.1.1 Fick's First Law (Steady-State Diffusion)

Fick's First Law states that the molar flux of a species by molecular diffusion is proportional to its concentration gradient. The proportionality constant is the **diffusivity (D<sub>AB</sub>)**.

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

### 2.1.2 Fick's Second Law (Unsteady-State Diffusion)

For unsteady-state diffusion (where concentration changes with time), Fick's First Law combined with a mass balance yields:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$$

For constant D<sub>AB</sub> and 1-D diffusion. This is the **heat equation analog** for mass transfer.

---

## 2.2 Diffusivity in Gases — Theoretical Background

### 2.2.1 Kinetic Theory Approach (Chapman–Enskog Theory)

The rigorous theoretical basis for gas-phase diffusivity comes from the **Chapman-Enskog kinetic theory of gases**, which treats molecules as hard spheres undergoing elastic collisions. For a binary mixture of A and B, the diffusivity is:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_{D,AB}}$$

Where:
- **T** = Absolute temperature (K)
- **P** = Absolute pressure (atm)
- **M<sub>A</sub>, M<sub>B</sub>** = Molecular weights of A and B (g/mol)
- **σ<sub>AB</sub>** = Collision diameter (Ångstroms, Å) = (σ<sub>A</sub> + σ<sub>B</sub>)/2
- **Ω<sub>D,AB</sub>** = Collision integral (dimensionless, function of k<sub>B</sub>T/ε<sub>AB</sub>)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** This correlation gives D<sub>AB</sub> in **cm²/s** when T is in K, P in atm, M in g/mol, and σ in Å.

### 2.2.2 The Lennard-Jones Potential

The collision integral **Ω<sub>D</sub>** is obtained from the Lennard-Jones (6-12) potential:

$$\phi(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

For mixtures, the combining rules are:
- σ<sub>AB</sub> = (σ<sub>A</sub> + σ<sub>B</sub>)/2
- ε<sub>AB</sub>/k<sub>B</sub> = √[(ε<sub>A</sub>/k<sub>B</sub>)(ε<sub>B</sub>/k<sub>B</sub>)]

Where **k<sub>B</sub>** = **1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann constant).

### 2.2.3 Fuller-Schettler-Giddings (FSG) Correlation (Empirical, More Accurate)

A widely used empirical alternative to Chapman-Enskog is the FSG correlation:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.00100 \, T^{1.75} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \left[ \left( \sum v_A \right)^{1/3} + \left( \sum v_B \right)^{1/3} \right]^2}$$

Where **∑v** is the sum of atomic diffusion volumes (empirical, tabulated). D<sub>AB</sub> is in cm²/s.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite:** The FSG correlation is often preferred in industry because it avoids the need to compute the collision integral Ω<sub>D</sub> and works well for non-polar and slightly polar systems.

### 2.2.4 Effect of Pressure and Temperature on Gas Diffusivity

From kinetic theory: **D<sub>AB</sub> ∝ T<sup>3/2</sup> / P**

- **Doubling T (absolute) at constant P:** D<sub>AB</sub> increases by a factor of **2<sup>3/2</sup> ≈ 2.83**
- **Doubling P at constant T:** D<sub>AB</sub> decreases by a factor of **2**

The temperature dependence is often expressed as:

$$D_{AB}(T_2) = D_{AB}(T_1) \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{3/2} \left( \frac{\Omega_{D,1}}{\Omega_{D,2}} \right) \approx D_{AB}(T_1) \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right)^{1.75} \text{ (FSG)}$$

---

## 2.3 Diffusivity in Liquids — Theoretical Background

### 2.3.1 The Stokes-Einstein Equation (Hydrodynamic Theory)

For a large spherical solute molecule (A) diffusing through a small solvent molecule (B), the **Stokes-Einstein equation** applies:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$$

Where:
- **k<sub>B</sub>** = Boltzmann constant = **1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **T** = Absolute temperature (K)
- **μ<sub>B</sub>** = Viscosity of solvent B (Pa·s or kg/m·s)
- **r<sub>A</sub>** = Radius of solute molecule A (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Stokes-Einstein works well when the solute molecule is **much larger** than the solvent molecule (e.g., proteins in water). It fails for solutes of similar size to the solvent.

### 2.3.2 The Wilke-Chang Equation (Most Widely Used for Liquids)

The **Wilke-Chang equation** is an empirical modification of Stokes-Einstein, valid for dilute, non-electrolyte solutions:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, T \left( \phi_B M_B \right)^{1/2}}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

Where:
- **D<sub>AB</sub>** = Diffusivity (cm²/s)
- **T** = Temperature (K)
- **M<sub>B</sub>** = Molecular weight of solvent B (g/mol)
- **μ<sub>B</sub>** = Viscosity of solvent B (cP) — *centipoise, not Pa·s!*
- **V<sub>A</sub>** = Molar volume of solute A at its normal boiling point (cm³/mol)
- **φ<sub>B</sub>** = Association factor of solvent B (dimensionless):
  - Water: **2.6**
  - Methanol: **1.9**
  - Ethanol: **1.5**
  - Benzene: **1.0**
  - Ether, heptane, etc.: **1.0**

### 2.3.3 Effect of Temperature on Liquid Diffusivity

For liquids, **D<sub>AB</sub> increases with T** mainly due to decrease in viscosity μ<sub>B</sub>. An Arrhenius-type relation often applies:

$$D_{AB} = D_0 \exp\left( -\frac{E_a}{RT} \right)$$

Or, in terms of the ratio at two temperatures:

$$\frac{D_{AB}(T_2)}{D_{AB}(T_1)} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \cdot \frac{\mu_B(T_1)}{\mu_B(T_2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Effect on Liquids:** Unlike gases, liquid diffusivity is **weakly dependent on pressure** (only ~10-20% change per 1000 atm). For engineering calculations, pressure effect on liquid diffusivity is usually neglected.

---

## 2.4 📊 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Fick's First Law: $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | J<sub>A</sub>, D<sub>AB</sub>, C<sub>A</sub> | kmol/m²·s |
| 2 | Fick's Second Law: $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}$ | Unsteady 1-D | kg/m³, s, m |
| 3 | Chapman-Enskog: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_{D,AB}}$ | Gas | D in **cm²/s** |
| 4 | FSG Correlation: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.00100 \, T^{1.75} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \left[ \left( \sum v_A \right)^{1/3} + \left( \sum v_B \right)^{1/3} \right]^2}$ | Gas (empirical) | D in **cm²/s** |
| 5 | Stokes-Einstein: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$ | Liquid (large solute) | D in **m²/s** |
| 6 | Wilke-Chang: $D_{AB} = \dfrac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, T \left( \phi_B M_B \right)^{1/2}}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$ | Liquid (dilute) | D in **cm²/s**, μ in **cP** |
| 7 | Combining Rule (σ): $\sigma_{AB} = \dfrac{\sigma_A + \sigma_B}{2}$ | Lennard-Jones | Ångstroms |
| 8 | Combining Rule (ε): $\dfrac{\varepsilon_{AB}}{k_B} = \sqrt{ \dfrac{\varepsilon_A}{k_B} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_B}{k_B} }$ | Lennard-Jones | K |
| 9 | T-dependence (gas): $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$ | Gas | Empirical |
| 10 | T-dependence (FSG): $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.75}/P$ | Gas (empirical) | Empirical |
| 11 | T-dependence (liquid): $D_{AB} \propto T/\mu_B$ | Liquid | Wilke-Chang |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Quick Reference:**
> - 1 cm²/s = 10⁻⁴ m²/s
> - 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s = 10⁻³ kg/(m·s)
> - For Wilke-Chang: **μ must be in cP** (not Pa·s!) — this is a common KTU exam trap!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Distillation Column Design:** Gas-phase diffusivity is needed to predict the number of theoretical stages and column height.
2. **Gas Absorption (Scrubbing):** Used to size packed columns for removing CO₂, SO₂, H₂S from gas streams.
3. **Membrane Separations:** Critical for designing gas separation and reverse osmosis membranes.
4. **Catalyst Design:** Diffusivity in porous catalysts determines reaction rates.
5. **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Drug dissolution rates in the human body depend on liquid diffusivity.
6. **Food Processing:** Design of drying, extraction, and aroma retention processes.
7. **Environmental Engineering:** Predicting contaminant plume migration in groundwater (using effective diffusivity in porous media).

---

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's First Law (From Kinetic Theory of Gases)

### Step 1: Setting up the problem
Consider a gas A diffusing through stagnant gas B. Let n be the total molar concentration (constant for ideal gas at constant T, P). At a plane z = 0, molecules of A cross from above and below.

### Step 2: Mean Free Path and Random Motion
According to kinetic theory, the average distance a molecule travels between collisions (mean free path) is:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \, \pi \, d^2 \, n}$$

Where d is the molecular diameter.

### Step 3: Net Flux Calculation
The number of molecules crossing unit area per unit time from above is (1/6) n v̄ (where v̄ is the mean molecular speed). At position z, the concentration is C<sub>A</sub>(z), and the number crossing from above (at z + λ) is (1/6) n v̄ C<sub>A</sub>(z + λ), and from below (at z - λ) is (1/6) n v̄ C<sub>A</sub>(z - λ).

### Step 4: Taylor Series Expansion
Expand C<sub>A</sub> around z = 0:

$$C_A(z + \lambda) = C_A(z) + \lambda \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} + \frac{\lambda^2}{2} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} + \cdots$$

$$C_A(z - \lambda) = C_A(z) - \lambda \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} + \frac{\lambda^2}{2} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} - \cdots$$

### Step 5: Net Number of Molecules Crossing
Subtracting (above - below):

$$N_{net} = \frac{1}{6} n \bar{v} \left[ C_A(z+\lambda) - C_A(z-\lambda) \right] = \frac{1}{6} n \bar{v} \left[ 2\lambda \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z} \right] = \frac{1}{3} n \bar{v} \lambda \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$$

### Step 6: Defining Diffusivity
Substituting the mean free path expression:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \bar{v} \lambda = \frac{\bar{v}}{3 \sqrt{2} \pi d^2 n}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}}$$

This recovers **Fick's First Law**, and the proportionality constant is identified as the **diffusivity**.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Chapman-Enskog Equation (Binary Gas Diffusivity)

The rigorous derivation uses the Boltzmann equation and the assumption of **hard-sphere molecules**. The result, after substantial algebra involving the Sonine polynomial expansion of the perturbation distribution function, is:

### Step 1: Defining Reduced Properties
The Lennard-Jones parameters are converted to dimensionless form:
- Reduced temperature: T* = k<sub>B</sub>T/ε<sub>AB</sub>
- Reduced collision integral: Ω<sub>D</sub> = f(T*)

### Step 2: Mean Molecular Speed
For a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, the mean relative speed between two unlike molecules is:

$$\bar{v}_{rel} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k_B T}{\pi \mu_{AB}}}$$

Where μ<sub>AB</sub> = M<sub>A</sub>M<sub>B</sub>/(M<sub>A</sub> + M<sub>B</sub>) is the reduced mass per molecule.

### Step 3: Diffusion Coefficient from Kinetic Theory
The diffusivity is:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{3}{16} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2\pi k_B T / \mu_{AB}}}{n \pi \sigma_{AB}^2 \Omega_{D,AB}}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Macroscopic Units
Substituting n = P/(k<sub>B</sub>T), and converting atomic mass units to grams:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_{D,AB}}$$

Where:
- D<sub>AB</sub> in cm²/s
- T in K
- P in atm
- M in g/mol
- σ in Å
- Ω<sub>D</sub> = dimensionless collision integral (from tables or Neufeld-Hossen correlation)

### Step 5: Neufeld-Hossen Correlation for Ω<sub>D</sub>
For T* between 0.3 and 100:

$$\Omega_{D,AB} = \frac{A}{T^{*B}} + \frac{C}{\exp(D \, T^*)} + \frac{E}{\exp(F \, T^*)} + \frac{G}{\exp(H \, T^*)}$$

With A = 1.06036, B = 0.15610, C = 0.19300, D = 0.47635, E = 1.03587, F = 1.52996, G = 1.76474, H = 3.89411.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation of the Stokes-Einstein Equation

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Spherical Particle
A spherical solute particle of radius r<sub>A</sub> moving through a viscous fluid of viscosity μ<sub>B</sub> experiences a **Stokes drag force**:

$$F_{drag} = 6 \pi \mu_B r_A v$$

Where v is the velocity of the particle.

### Step 2: Terminal Velocity Under Diffusion
In a concentration gradient, the driving force (osmotic in nature) on a single molecule is:

$$F_{driving} = -\frac{k_B T}{C_A} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$$

### Step 3: Equating Forces at Steady State
At terminal velocity, driving force = drag force:

$$6 \pi \mu_B r_A v = -\frac{k_B T}{C_A} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$$

### Step 4: Relating Velocity to Flux
The flux is J<sub>A</sub> = v · C<sub>A</sub> (since flux = velocity × concentration):

$$J_A = v \, C_A = -\frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial z}$$

### Step 5: Identification of Diffusivity
Comparing with Fick's First Law:

$$\boxed{D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity:** This equation assumes:
> 1. Solute radius r<sub>A</sub> >> solvent molecule size (continuum assumption)
> 2. No-slip boundary condition at particle surface
> 3. Dilute, creeping flow (Re << 1)

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Wilke-Chang Equation (Empirical)

### Step 1: Starting from Stokes-Einstein
Begin with:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$$

### Step 2: Replace r<sub>A</sub> with Molar Volume
The molar volume V<sub>A</sub> relates to the molecular volume as:

$$V_A = N_A \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi r_A^3 \implies r_A = \left( \frac{3 V_A}{4 \pi N_A} \right)^{1/3}$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify
Substituting into Stokes-Einstein and lumping constants:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B} \left( \frac{4 \pi N_A}{3 V_A} \right)^{1/3}$$

### Step 4: Empirical Constants
Wilke and Chang fitted the constants to experimental data for many systems, introducing the association factor φ<sub>B</sub> (to account for solvent hydrogen bonding and structure):

$$D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, T \left( \phi_B M_B \right)^{1/2}}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

The exponent 0.6 (instead of 1/3 ≈ 0.333) was found empirically to fit the data better. The **2.6 factor for water** accounts for the strong hydrogen bonding that effectively "increases" the solvent size.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Calculation of Gas Diffusivity

**Problem:** Estimate the diffusivity of benzene (A) in air (B) at 1 atm and 25°C using the FSG correlation.

**Given:**
- T = 25 + 273 = **298 K**
- P = 1 atm
- M<sub>A</sub> (benzene, C₆H₆) = 78 g/mol
- M<sub>B</sub> (air) = 28.97 g/mol
- Atomic diffusion volumes:
  - ∑v<sub>A</sub> (C₆H₆) = 6 × (15.9) + 6 × (2.31) = 95.4 + 13.86 = **109.26**
  - ∑v<sub>B</sub> (air) = **20.1**

**Solution:**

$$\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} = \frac{1}{78} + \frac{1}{28.97} = 0.01282 + 0.03452 = 0.04734 \text{ mol/g}$$

$$\left( 0.04734 \right)^{1/2} = 0.2176$$

$$\left( \sum v_A \right)^{1/3} = (109.26)^{1/3} = 4.778$$

$$\left( \sum v_B \right)^{1/3} = (20.1)^{1/3} = 2.714$$

$$\left( 4.778 + 2.714 \right)^2 = (7.492)^2 = 56.13$$

$$T^{1.75} = (298)^{1.75} = 298 \times 298^{0.75}$$

Computing 298<sup>0.75</sup>: ln(298) = 5.697, × 0.75 = 4.273, exp(4.273) = 71.84, so 298<sup>1.75</sup> ≈ 21,408

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.00100 \times 21408 \times 0.2176}{1 \times 56.13}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{4.658}{56.13} = 0.0830 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 8.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Experimental value:** D<sub>AB</sub> ≈ 0.096 cm²/s — the FSG estimate is within ~14% accuracy, which is excellent for engineering purposes.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Calculation of Liquid Diffusivity

**Problem:** Estimate the diffusivity of acetic acid (CH₃COOH) in water at 25°C using the Wilke-Chang equation.

**Given:**
- T = 298 K
- M<sub>B</sub> (water) = 18 g/mol
- φ<sub>B</sub> (water) = 2.6
- μ<sub>B</sub> (water at 25°C) = 0.894 cP
- V<sub>A</sub> (acetic acid) = **63.8 cm³/mol** (from tables at normal boiling point)

**Solution:**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 298 \times (2.6 \times 18)^{1/2}}{0.894 \times (63.8)^{0.6}}$$

Numerator: 7.4 × 10⁻⁸ × 298 × √46.8 = 7.4 × 10⁻⁸ × 298 × 6.841

$$= 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2038.5 = 1.5085 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator: 0.894 × (63.8)<sup>0.6</sup>

Computing (63.8)<sup>0.6</sup>: ln(63.8) = 4.156, × 0.6 = 2.494, exp(2.494) = 12.11

Denominator = 0.894 × 12.11 = 10.83

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1.5085 \times 10^{-4}}{10.83} = 1.393 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$= 1.393 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Experimental value:** D<sub>AB</sub> ≈ 1.24 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s — the Wilke-Chang estimate is within ~12% accuracy.
