# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### ✏️ Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define mass diffusivity. State Fick's first law of diffusion and mention its analogy with Fourier's law of heat conduction.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mass Diffusivity (D<sub>AB</sub>):** It is a transport property that quantifies the rate of molecular diffusion of a species A through a medium B. It is defined as the proportionality constant in Fick's First Law. **SI unit: m²/s.**

**Fick's First Law:** The molar flux by molecular diffusion is proportional to the concentration gradient:

$$J_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$

**Analogy with Fourier's Law:**

| Fourier's Law (Heat) | Fick's Law (Mass) |
| :--- | :--- |
| $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ |
| k: thermal conductivity | D<sub>AB</sub>: mass diffusivity |
| T: temperature | C<sub>A</sub>: concentration |

Both laws state that the flux is **proportional to the driving force gradient** and flow occurs from high to low potential.

---

### ✏️ Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`
**Compare the orders of magnitude of diffusivity in gases, liquids, and solids. Why is liquid diffusivity so much smaller than gas diffusivity?**

**Model Answer:**

| Phase | Typical D<sub>AB</sub> (m²/s) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Gases** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |
| **Liquids** | $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ |
| **Solids** | $10^{-14}$ to $10^{-12}$ |

**Reason:** Liquid diffusivity is ~10,000 times smaller than gas diffusivity because:
1. **Molecular density:** Liquid molecules are ~1000× more densely packed than gas molecules, drastically reducing the **mean free path**.
2. **Intermolecular forces:** Strong cohesive forces in liquids restrict molecular mobility.
3. **Hydrogen bonding and structure** in many liquids (e.g., water) further slows diffusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### ✏️ Question B-A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **(CHOICE 1)**

**Question B-A:** 
**(a)** Explain the **Chapman-Enskog kinetic theory** of binary gas diffusivity. Starting from kinetic theory, derive the relationship and discuss the effect of temperature and pressure on gas-phase diffusivity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the diffusivity of **ethanol (A) in air (B) at 40°C and 1.5 atm** using the **Fuller-Schettler-Giddings (FSG) correlation**. Given:
- M<sub>A</sub> (ethanol, C₂H₅OH) = 46 g/mol
- M<sub>B</sub> (air) = 28.97 g/mol
- ∑v<sub>A</sub> (C₂H₅OH) = 2(15.9) + 6(2.31) + 1(12.7) = 31.8 + 13.86 + 12.7 = 58.36
- ∑v<sub>B</sub> (air) = 20.1
**(7 marks)**

---

### 🔑 Model Solution for Question B-A

#### Part (a) — Chapman-Enskog Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1: Introduction**
The Chapman-Enskog theory (1916-1917) provides the **most rigorous theoretical basis** for binary gas diffusivity. It solves the Boltzmann transport equation for a dilute gas mixture, treating molecules as hard spheres that interact via the **Lennard-Jones (6-12) potential**.

**Step 2: Lennard-Jones Potential**
The intermolecular potential is:

$$\phi(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$$

The first term represents repulsion, the second attraction.

**Step 3: Combining Rules**
For unlike pairs (A-B):

$$\sigma_{AB} = \frac{\sigma_A + \sigma_B}{2}, \quad \frac{\varepsilon_{AB}}{k_B} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_A}{k_B} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_B}{k_B}}$$

**Step 4: The Final Equation**
After solving the Boltzmann equation:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_{D,AB}}$$

**Step 5: Effect of T and P**
- **D<sub>AB</sub> ∝ T<sup>3/2</sup>/P** (when collision integral is approximately constant)
- The collision integral Ω<sub>D</sub> decreases weakly with T (typically by factor 1.5-2 from 300 K to 1000 K).
- FSG empirical form: **D<sub>AB</sub> ∝ T<sup>1.75</sup>/P** (slightly stronger T-dependence to account for the non-constant Ω<sub>D</sub>).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** T = 40 + 273 = **313 K**, P = 1.5 atm

**Step 1:** Compute the mass term:

$$\left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right) = \frac{1}{46} + \frac{1}{28.97} = 0.02174 + 0.03452 = 0.05626 \text{ mol/g}$$

$$\left( 0.05626 \right)^{1/2} = 0.2372$$

**Step 2:** Compute diffusion volume terms:

$$\left( \sum v_A \right)^{1/3} = (58.36)^{1/3} = 3.876$$

$$\left( \sum v_B \right)^{1/3} = (20.1)^{1/3} = 2.714$$

**Step 3:** Sum and square:

$$\left( 3.876 + 2.714 \right)^2 = (6.590)^2 = 43.43$$

**Step 4:** Compute T<sup>1.75</sup>:

$$T^{1.75} = (313)^{1.75} = 313 \times 313^{0.75}$$

ln(313) = 5.746, × 0.75 = 4.310, exp(4.310) = 74.55, so T<sup>1.75</sup> ≈ 23,334

**Step 5:** Substitute into FSG:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.00100 \times 23334 \times 0.2372}{1.5 \times 43.43}$$

$$D_{AB} = \frac{5.534}{65.15} = 0.0849 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:** 

$$\boxed{D_{AB} \approx 0.0849 \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 8.49 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

---

### ✏️ Question B-B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3]` **(CHOICE 2)**

**Question B-B:**
**(a)** Discuss the **Stokes-Einstein equation** for liquid-phase diffusivity. What are its limitations? Explain the **Wilke-Chang equation** and discuss the significance of the **association factor φ<sub>B</sub>**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the diffusivity of **acetone (CH₃COCH₃) in water at 20°C** using the **Wilke-Chang equation**. Given:
- T = 20°C = 293 K
- M<sub>B</sub> (water) = 18 g/mol
- μ<sub>B</sub> (water at 20°C) = 1.002 cP
- φ<sub>B</sub> (water) = 2.6
- V<sub>A</sub> (acetone) at normal boiling point = **74.05 cm³/mol**
**(7 marks)**

---

### 🔑 Model Solution for Question B-B

#### Part (a) — Stokes-Einstein and Wilke-Chang (7 marks)

**Stokes-Einstein Equation:**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$$

**Derivation Concept:** Force balance on a spherical particle — Stokes drag = thermodynamic driving force.

**Assumptions & Limitations:**
1. **Large solute, small solvent** — fails when molecules are of comparable size (e.g., ethanol in water).
2. **Continuum fluid assumption** — fails in very narrow pores.
3. **No-slip boundary condition** at the particle surface.
4. **No convection or coupling** with other species.
5. **Dilute, non-electrolyte solution** — fails for ionic solutions or concentrated solutions.

**Wilke-Chang Equation (Empirical Modification):**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{7.4 \times 10^{-8} \, T \left( \phi_B M_B \right)^{1/2}}{\mu_B \, V_{A}^{0.6}}$$

**Why empirical?** Stokes-Einstein often **underpredicts** liquid diffusivity. Wilke and Chang fitted an empirical exponent (0.6 vs. theoretical 0.333) and an empirical prefactor to better match data.

**Association Factor φ<sub>B</sub>:**
- Accounts for the **degree of molecular association** of the solvent.
- For **water (φ = 2.6)**, strong hydrogen bonding causes clustering, effectively increasing the solvent "drag" on the solute.
- For **non-associated solvents** (benzene, heptane), φ = 1.0.
- For **partially associated** solvents (alcohols), φ = 1.5-1.9.

| Solvent | φ<sub>B</sub> |
| :--- | :--- |
| Water | 2.6 |
| Methanol | 1.9 |
| Ethanol | 1.5 |
| Benzene | 1.0 |
| Ether | 1.0 |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** T = 293 K, μ<sub>B</sub> = 1.002 cP, M<sub>B</sub> = 18, φ<sub>B</sub> = 2.6, V<sub>A</sub> = 74.05 cm³/mol

**Step 1:** Compute numerator:

$$7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times T \times \sqrt{\phi_B M_B}$$

$$\phi_B M_B = 2.6 \times 18 = 46.8$$

$$\sqrt{46.8} = 6.841$$

$$7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 293 \times 6.841 = 7.4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2004.3 = 1.483 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator:

$$\mu_B \times V_A^{0.6} = 1.002 \times (74.05)^{0.6}$$

Computing (74.05)<sup>0.6</sup>: ln(74.05) = 4.305, × 0.6 = 2.583, exp(2.583) = 13.24

$$1.002 \times 13.24 = 13.27$$

**Step 3:** Compute D<sub>AB</sub>:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1.483 \times 10^{-4}}{13.27} = 1.117 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to SI:

$$D_{AB} = 1.117 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-4} = 1.117 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:** 

$$\boxed{D_{AB} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s} = 1.12 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Experimental value: ~1.16 × 10⁻⁵ cm²/s — agreement within ~4% is excellent for engineering work.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Wilke-Chang:** Using μ<sub>B</sub> in **Pa·s instead of cP** is the **#1 mistake**. The constant 7.4 × 10⁻⁸ was derived with μ in cP. Always check.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in Chapman-Enskog:** The constant 0.001858 requires σ in **Ångstroms**, not meters. Convert before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting Combining Rules:** Using σ<sub>A</sub> or ε<sub>A</sub> directly without computing σ<sub>AB</sub> = (σ<sub>A</sub>+σ<sub>B</sub>)/2 will yield a **wrong answer**.
> 4. **Pressure Units:** P in **atm**, not Pa or bar (unless converted).
> 5. **Confusing the Constants:** Wilke-Chang's exponent is **0.6** (not 0.5 or 0.333).
> 6. **Mixing Up Molar Volume Definitions:** V<sub>A</A> is the molar volume at the **normal boiling point**, not at 25°C.
> 7. **Skipping Final Unit Conversion:** Always state the answer in **both cm²/s and m²/s** to demonstrate unit awareness.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

### 🔹 Definitions
- ☐ **Diffusivity (D<sub>AB</sub>):** Proportionality constant in Fick's First Law; SI unit m²/s.
- ☐ **Fick's First Law:** $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dz$ (steady state).
- ☐ **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB} \, \partial^2 C_A/\partial z^2$ (unsteady).
- ☐ **Self-Diffusivity:** D<sub>AA</sub> of species through itself (radio-tracer).
- ☐ **Mutual Diffusivity:** D<sub>AB</sub> between two different species.

### 🔹 Gas-Phase Diffusivity
- ☐ **Chapman-Enskog Equation** is the rigorous kinetic-theory result.
- ☐ **FSG Correlation** is the empirical, industry-preferred alternative.
- ☐ **Combining rules:** σ<sub>AB</sub> = arithmetic mean; ε<sub>AB</sub> = geometric mean.
- ☐ **T-dependence:** $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.75}$ (FSG) or $T^{3/2}$ (CE).
- ☐ **P-dependence:** $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$ (linear inverse).
- ☐ **Order of magnitude:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s at 1 atm.

### 🔹 Liquid-Phase Diffusivity
- ☐ **Stokes-Einstein** for large solutes; **Wilke-Chang** for general use.
- ☐ **Wilke-Chang constant:** 7.4 × 10⁻⁸; **μ must be in cP**; **V<sub>A</sub> in cm³/mol**.
- ☐ **Association factor φ<sub>B</sub>:** water = 2.6, methanol = 1.9, ethanol = 1.5, benzene = 1.0.
- ☐ **T-dependence:** $D_{AB} \propto T/\mu_B$ (viscosity decreases with T, so D increases).
- ☐ **P-dependence:** Negligible for engineering purposes.
- ☐ **Order of magnitude:** $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-9}$ m²/s.

### 🔹 Dimensionless Numbers (Mass Transfer Analogies)
- ☐ **Schmidt Number:** $Sc = \nu/D_{AB} = \mu/(\rho D_{AB})$
- ☐ **Lewis Number:** $Le = \alpha/D_{AB} = k/(\rho C_p D_{AB})$
- ☐ **Sherwood Number:** $Sh = k_c L/D_{AB}$
- ☐ **Schmidt analogy:** $St_{mass} \cdot Sc^{2/3} = St_{heat} \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ (Chilton-Colburn).

### 🔹 Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ Don't confuse **molar volume V<sub>A</sub>** (at boiling point) with molar volume at 25°C.
- ☐ Don't use **molar mass in kg/mol** with the standard constants.
- ☐ Don't forget to **convert** D from cm²/s to m²/s when required (× 10⁻⁴).
- ☐ Don't apply **gas equations to liquids** or vice-versa — they have very different physics.

### 🔹 Practical Engineering Insights
- ☐ For gas diffusion at high pressures (>10 atm), use **pressure-corrected** forms.
- ☐ For **non-ideal gases**, use **fugacity** instead of partial pressure.
- ☐ For **concentrated solutions**, the Wilke-Chang accuracy degrades — use experimental data.
- ☐ For **polymers and large molecules**, Stokes-Einstein with hydrodynamic radius works best.
- ☐ In **porous media**, use **effective diffusivity** $D_{eff} = D_{AB} \cdot \epsilon / \tau$, where $\epsilon$ = porosity, $\tau$ = tortuosity.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State and Equimolar Counter Diffusion in Gases and Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Steady State and Equimolar Counter Diffusion in Gases and Liquids # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Steady-State Molecular Diffusion** is the process in which the concentration of the diffusing species at any fixed point in the medium does **not change with time**; i.e., $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = 0$ for every species $A$ inside the system. Mathematically, the molar flux of any species $A$ relative to a stationary observer (molar average velocity) is governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**: $$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ where $D_{AB}$ is the **mass diffusivity** (also called the **diffusion coefficient**) of species $A$ in $B$, having units of **m²/s**. **Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD)** is a special, highly idealized steady-state molecular diffusion scenario in which two species $A$ and $B$ diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates**, so that: $$N_A = -N_B \quad \text{and} \quad N_A + N_B = 0$$ This condition occurs in practical systems such as **binary isothermal gas-phase reactions at stoichiometric equivalence** (e.g., $A \rightarrow B$ with one mole of $A$ yielding one mole of $B$), **opposing diffusion of ammonia and nitrogen through a stagnant inert**, and **binary distillation** where each mole of vapour condensing produces one mole of vapour rising. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Equimolar Counter Diffusion (EMCD) in **gases** and **liquids** is a guaranteed question in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). The key takeaway is that the **total molar concentration $C$ and total pressure $P$ remain constant** throughout the diffusion path, which is the foundational assumption that simplifies Fick's law considerably. ## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **two-lane corridor** in a busy mall. One lane carries shoppers moving from **Point 1 → Point 2** (these are the molecules of species $A$), while the other lane carries shoppers moving from **Point 2 → Point 1** (these are the molecules of species $B$). - The corridor has a **fixed width** (analogous to **constant total molar concentration $C$**). - The number of people crossing any cross-section per second in **one direction exactly balances** the number crossing in the opposite direction (analogous to $N_A = -N_B$). - Once the flow becomes **steady**, the crowd density at every location on the corridor stays the same — nobody is piling up anywhere (this is $\partial C_A/\partial t = 0$). This is **Equimolar Counter Diffusion** in plain language: two streams, equal and opposite, perfectly balanced, producing a stable concentration profile. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Unimolar Diffusion:** > - **EMCD:** Two species diffuse equally and oppositely. The total molar flux through any plane = **0**. > - **Diffusion of A through stagnant B:** Only $A$ moves; $B$ is stationary. The bulk mixture moves to compensate. This is **NOT** EMCD and uses a different flux equation (Module 3, next topic). > - **Stefan Diffusion:** A special case of stagnant $B$ where bulk flow dominates. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (in Bold) - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \;\mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$ - **Standard Temperature & Pressure:** $T = 273.15\;\mathrm{K}$, $P = 101.325\;\mathrm{kPa}$ - **Typical Diffusivity in Gases:** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$ - **Typical Diffusivity in Liquids:** $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$ - **Total Molar Concentration (ideal gas):** $C = P/(RT)$ — typically around $\mathbf{40.9\;mol/m^3}$ at STP. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The derivation of EMCD flux rests on three layered physical assumptions. Each one is critical for KTU valuation. ### Step 1 — Invoke Fick's First Law for the Diffusive Flux For species $A$ moving relative to the molar-average velocity, the **molar diffusive flux** is: $$J_A = -C \cdot D_{AB} \cdot \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ Here $x_A$ is the **mole fraction of $A$**, and the sign convention ensures flux flows from **high to low concentration**. ### Step 2 — Relate Diffusive Flux to Total Flux via Bulk Motion The actual flux observed in a **stationary frame** must include the **convective (bulk) contribution** carried by the molar-average velocity $v^*$: $$N_A = J_A + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ This is the fundamental **flux transformation equation**, valid for **all** binary gas/liquid diffusion problems in KTU Module 3. ### Step 3 — Apply the EMCD Constraint For equimolar counter diffusion, $N_A + N_B = 0$, so the convective bulk term **vanishes entirely**: $$x_A (N_A + N_B) = 0 \implies N_A = J_A$$ This is the **mathematical heart of EMCD** — the observed flux equals the pure diffusive flux. ### Step 4 — Substitute and Integrate Substituting the expression for $J_A$ and integrating across the film of thickness $z$ from point 1 (composition $x_{A1}$) to point 2 (composition $x_{A2}$): $$N_A \int_0^z dz = -C \cdot D_{AB} \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} dx_A$$ The molar flux for **EMCD in gases** is obtained by replacing $C = P/(RT)$: $$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, z} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)\;}$$ For **EMCD in liquids** (incompressible binary solution, $C \approx$ constant): $$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{z} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)\;}$$ Both expressions can be re-written in terms of partial pressures (gases) or mole fractions (liquids): $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, z} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right) \quad \text{(gas form)}$$ ## 2.2 Why This Equation Matters in Engineering | Engineering Domain | Real-World Use of EMCD Equation | |--------------------|----------------------------------| | **Distillation Columns** | Vapour-liquid equilibrium calculations; equimolar transfer is the *theoretical limit* | | **Catalytic Reactors** | Modelling equimolar reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B$ on porous catalyst pellets | | **Polymer Film Permeation** | Designing packaging films where equal fluxes enter and exit | | **Evaporation from Pure Liquid Pools** | When vapour $A$ diffuses into stagnant air — *this is NOT EMCD*, but the EMCD equation is often used as a comparison baseline | | **Heat Pipe / Mass Pipe Analysis** | Steady-state mass transfer in wicks | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains **every equation** required to solve Module 3 KTU EMCD problems. Memorize the units carefully. | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Valid For | |---|---------------|-------------------|--------------------|-----------| | 1 | Fick's First Law | $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A / dz$ | $D_{AB} \to \mathrm{m^2/s}$, $C_A \to \mathrm{mol/m^3}$ | All media | | 2 | Flux Transformation | $N_A = J_A + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ | $N_A \to \mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ | All binary systems | | 3 | EMCD Constraint | $N_A + N_B = 0$ | Dimensionless constraint | EMCD only | | 4 | EMCD Molar Flux — **Gas** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)$ | $P \to \mathrm{Pa}$, $T \to \mathrm{K}$, $z \to \mathrm{m}$ | EMCD in gases | | 5 | EMCD — Gas — Partial Pressure Form | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P-P_{A2}}{P-P_{A1}}\right)$ | $P_A \to \mathrm{Pa}$ | EMCD in gases | | 6 | EMCD Molar Flux — **Liquid** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)$ | $C \to \mathrm{mol/m^3}$ | EMCD in liquids | | 7 | Ideal Gas Relation | $C = P/(RT)$ | — | Gas phase link | | 8 | Mean Concentration (Dilute Liquid) | $C_{avg} = (C_1 + C_2)/2$ | Used when $x_A$ is small | Liquid EMCD | | 9 | Log-Mean Concentration | $C_{lm} = \dfrac{C_2 - C_1}{\ln(C_2/C_1)}$ | For non-dilute solutions | Liquid EMCD | | 10 | Diffusivity Temperature Correction | $D \propto T^{3/2}$ (Chapman-Enskog) | — | Gas diffusivity estimation | > [!NOTE] > **Trap alert:** Students often confuse $\vert 1 - x_A \vert$ (which is just the mole fraction of the *other* species) with absolute value. In KTU, **always use parentheses and the log term explicitly**; never drop the logarithm. ## 2.4 The 'Why' Behind the Logarithmic Form The reason the EMCD equation is **logarithmic** rather than linear comes from the coupling between the diffusive flux and the concentration gradient: - If $A$ is dilute ($x_A \ll 1$), then $1 - x_A \approx 1$, the log simplifies to $x_{A1} - x_{A2}$, and the flux becomes **linear** (like Ohm's law): $$N_A \approx \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z} \cdot C \cdot (x_{A1} - x_{A2})$$ - For concentrated mixtures (e.g., distillation), the log form is **mandatory** — KTU problems often feature $x_A$ values like 0.3 and 0.7. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of EMCD Flux in Gases (Exhaustive) **Starting Equation — Fick's First Law for a binary gas mixture:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ This equation has **two unknowns in flux form**: $N_A$ and $N_B$. We need a second equation — the EMCD constraint. **Apply the EMCD condition:** $$N_B = -N_A \implies N_A + N_B = 0$$ Substituting this into equation (i), the bulk convection term $x_A (N_A + N_B)$ **vanishes exactly**: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Convert concentration to mole fraction using ideal gas law:** For an ideal gas at constant $T$ and $P$: $$C_A = \frac{P_A}{RT} = \frac{y_A P}{RT} = y_A \cdot \frac{P}{RT}$$ Differentiating with respect to $z$ (note $P$, $R$, $T$ are constants under EMCD): $$\frac{dC_A}{dz} = \frac{P}{RT} \, \frac{dy_A}{dz} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ **Substitute (iii) into (ii):** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{P}{RT} \, \frac{dy_A}{dz}$$ **Separate variables and integrate across the film thickness $z$ (from point 1 at $z=0$ to point 2 at $z=z$):** $$N_A \, \int_0^z dz = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} dy_A$$ Evaluate both integrals: $$N_A \cdot z = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \, (y_{A2} - y_{A1}) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ This gives a **linear approximation**: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT \, z} \, (y_{A1} - y_{A2})$$ **However, this linear form is only valid when $y_A \ll 1$ (dilute limit). For the general case**, we must use the more rigorous form derived from **Fick's law expressed in terms of mole fraction gradient of the other species**: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, C \, \frac{dy_A}{dz} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 - y_A)} \quad \text{...(v)}$$ (The factor $1/(1-y_A)$ arises from expressing $dC/dz$ via $C_A = C \cdot y_A$ with $C = $ const.) **Separate variables and integrate using log-mean concentration:** $$N_A (1 - y_A) \, dy_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \, dz$$ Integrating: $$N_A \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} (1 - y_A) \, dy_A = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \int_0^z dz$$ $$N_A \left[ y_A - \frac{y_A^2}{2} \right]_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \, z$$ To avoid the quadratic term, **rewrite the integrand** using the substitution $1 - y_A = y_B$: $$N_A \int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} (1 - y_A) \, dy_A = -N_A \int_{1-y_{A1}}^{1-y_{A2}} y_B \, dy_B = -N_A \cdot \frac{y_B^2}{2} \Big\vert_{y_{B1}}^{y_{B2}}$$ Combining both sides, after cancellation: $$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, z} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)\;} \quad \text{— Final EMCD gas flux}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters for KTU valuation:** Examiners often award partial marks for showing the substitution of the EMCD condition into Fick's law (Step 2), the conversion to mole fractions (Step 3), and the integration across the film thickness (Step 4). Skipping any step can cost **2–3 marks**. ## 3.2 Derivation of EMCD Flux in Liquids **Starting Point — Fick's First Law for a binary liquid mixture:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A (N_A + N_B) \quad \text{...(i)}$$ For liquids, $D_{AB}$ is typically of order $10^{-9}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$, but the **form of Fick's law is identical** to the gas case. Only the **boundary conditions** change because the total molar concentration $C$ is approximately constant in liquids. **Apply EMCD constraint:** $N_A + N_B = 0$, so again: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dz} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Express $C_A$ in terms of mole fraction $x_A$ and total concentration $C$:** $$C_A = x_A \cdot C$$ Since $C$ is constant for a liquid, differentiating: $$\frac{dC_A}{dz} = C \, \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ **Substitute back:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \, C \, \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ **Now invoke the rigorous Fick's law form for liquids (using $1 - x_A = x_B$):** $$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, C}{1 - x_A} \, \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ **Separate variables:** $$N_A \, (1 - x_A) \, dx_A = -D_{AB} \, C \, dz$$ **Integrate from $x_{A1}$ to $x_{A2}$ across film thickness $z$:** $$N_A \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} (1 - x_A) \, dx_A = -D_{AB} \, C \int_0^z dz$$ $$N_A \left[ x_A - \frac{x_A^2}{2} \right]_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} = -D_{AB} \, C \cdot z$$ Using the same log-form manipulation as for gases: $$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, C}{z} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)\;} \quad \text{— Final EMCD liquid flux}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Table | Parameter | EMCD in Gases | EMCD in Liquids | |-----------|---------------|-----------------| | Total concentration $C$ | Variable via $P/(RT)$, but $P$ and $T$ constant → $C$ constant | Approximately constant | | Key equation | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)$ | | Diffusivity magnitude | $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$ | $D_{AB} \sim 10^{-9}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$ | | Driving force | Concentration + bulk flow = **zero** (EMCD) | Same | | Typical industrial example | Distillation trays, equimolar reaction | Liquid-liquid extraction, membrane permeation | | Units of final flux | $\mathrm{kmol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ | $\mathrm{kmol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Gas EMCD (Step-by-Step) **Problem Statement (KTU-type):** Ammonia ($A$) and nitrogen ($B$) undergo equimolar counter diffusion through a 5 mm thick tube at $T = 298\;\mathrm{K}$ and $P = 101.325\;\mathrm{kPa}$. At one end, the partial pressure of $NH_3$ is $P_{A1} = 10.13\;\mathrm{kPa}$; at the other, $P_{A2} = 5.07\;\mathrm{kPa}$. The diffusivity $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$. Calculate the molar flux of ammonia. **Solution (step-by-step):** **Step 1:** Identify given values and convert to SI units. $$T = 298\;\mathrm{K}, \quad P = 101325\;\mathrm{Pa}, \quad z = 0.005\;\mathrm{m}$$ $$P_{A1} = 10130\;\mathrm{Pa}, \quad P_{A2} = 5070\;\mathrm{Pa}, \quad D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$$ $$R = 8.314\;\mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the denominator $R T z$. $$RTz = 8.314 \times 298 \times 0.005 = 12.3879\;\mathrm{J \cdot m / mol}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the numerator $D_{AB} \cdot P$. $$D_{AB} \cdot P = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 2.5331\;\mathrm{m^2 \cdot Pa / s}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the log term. $$\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}} = \frac{101325 - 5070}{101325 - 10130} = \frac{96255}{91195} = 1.05549$$ $$\ln(1.05549) = 0.05402$$ **Step 5:** Assemble the EMCD equation. $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)$$ $$N_A = \frac{2.5331}{12.3879} \times 0.05402 = 0.20449 \times 0.05402$$ $$\boxed{\;N_A = 1.1047 \times 10^{-2}\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}\;}$$ Or equivalently, $N_A = 1.1047 \times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{kmol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$. > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** The flux is positive (pointing from high to low partial pressure, as expected). The magnitude is physically reasonable for gas diffusion at ambient conditions. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Setup of EMCD — Conceptual Schematic The classic KTU diagram for EMCD is **two large bulbs connected by a narrow tube**. Initially, bulb 1 contains pure species $A$ at total pressure $P$, and bulb 2 contains pure species $B$ at the same pressure $P$. At time $t = 0$, the connecting valve is opened, and molecules of $A$ and $B$ diffuse in opposite directions through the tube. ``` BULB 1 BULB 2 ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ Pure A │═══[Tube, z]═══════│ Pure B │ │ (P, T) │ │ (P, T) │ │ │ │ │ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ Valve Valve closed closed ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram represents the **analytical framework** for solving an EMCD problem — the logical decision tree a student should follow during the KTU examination. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Identify Diffusion Scenario]) --> checkSpecies{Is the system
binary?} checkSpecies -- No --> rejectA[Use multi-component
diffusion theory] checkSpecies -- Yes --> checkFlow{Is bulk motion
zero? N_A + N_B = 0?} checkFlow -- No --> otherCase[Use Unimolar Diffusion
or Stefan Diffusion] checkFlow -- Yes --> emcdPath[EMCD Identified] emcdPath --> checkPhase{Phase of
mixture?} checkPhase -- Gas --> gasPath[Use EMCD Gas Equation] checkPhase -- Liquid --> liqPath[Use EMCD Liquid Equation] gasPath --> gasInputs[Collect: D_AB, P, T, z,
y_A1, y_A2] liqPath --> liqInputs[Collect: D_AB, C, z,
x_A1, x_A2] gasInputs --> gasCompute[Compute N_A using
P,RT,z formula] liqInputs --> liqCompute[Compute N_A using
C,z formula] gasCompute --> verifyGas{Flux magnitude
reasonable?} liqCompute --> verifyLiq{Flux magnitude
reasonable?} verifyGas -- Yes --> outGas([Final Answer: N_A]) verifyGas -- No --> debugGas[Check units &
log argument] verifyLiq -- Yes --> outLiq([Final Answer: N_A]) verifyLiq -- No --> debugLiq[Check C const. &
mole fractions] ``` ## 4.3 Concentration Profile Along the Diffusion Path ``` y_A (or x_A) │ 1.0│ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ │ ■ 0.0│______________________________■________ 0 z (Point 1) (Point 2) Slope is steeper near the high-concentration end because diffusion flux depends on gradient. ``` ## 4.4 Component-Level Interaction Map (Mermaid) This diagram shows how the **four key variables** ($D_{AB}$, $P/T$, $z$, and mole fraction ratio) interact in the EMCD equation. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeD[D_AB : Diffusivity] nodeP[P : Total Pressure] nodeT[T : Temperature] nodeZ[z : Film Thickness] nodeY[y_A1, y_A2 : Mole fractions] nodeR[R : Gas constant] nodeD --> numeratorA[Numerator] nodeP --> numeratorA nodeT --> numeratorB[Denominator] nodeR --> numeratorB nodeZ --> numeratorB nodeY --> logTerm[Logarithmic Term] logTerm --> fluxOut[N_A : Molar Flux] numeratorA --> fluxOut numeratorB --> fluxOut style fluxOut fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeY fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define equimolar counter diffusion (EMCD). Give two industrial examples where EMCD is applicable. **Model Answer:** Equimolar counter diffusion is a steady-state molecular diffusion process in a binary mixture in which species $A$ and species $B$ diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates**, satisfying $N_A = -N_B$. Under this condition, the **total molar flux** through any cross-section is zero, so bulk convective motion vanishes and Fick's law reduces to $N_A = -D_{AB}\,dC_A/dz$. **Industrial Examples:** 1. **Binary distillation** — when one mole of vapour condenses, exactly one mole of vapour is generated, producing equimolar transfer across the vapour-liquid interface. 2. **Equimolar gas-phase reaction** such as $A \rightarrow B$ on a catalyst surface — one mole of $A$ is consumed for every mole of $B$ produced, leading to counter-diffusion in the porous catalyst. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often cite evaporation of water into air as EMCD. **This is incorrect** — water evaporates into stagnant air, which is *unimolar* diffusion through a *stagnant* film (Stefan diffusion), not EMCD. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Write the equation for molar flux in equimolar counter diffusion for a binary gas system and explain the significance of each term. **Model Answer:** The molar flux of species $A$ in equimolar counter diffusion through a binary gas film is: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$$ **Significance of each term:** - $D_{AB}$ — **mass diffusivity** of $A$ in $B$ (m²/s); higher diffusivity → higher flux. - $P$ — **total pressure** (Pa); increasing $P$ raises concentration, raising flux. - $R$ — **universal gas constant** = 8.314 J/(mol·K). - $T$ — **absolute temperature** (K); raising $T$ decreases $C$, lowering flux at constant $P$. - $z$ — **film thickness** (m); longer path → lower flux (inverse linear in dilute limit). - $y_{A1}, y_{A2}$ — **mole fractions of $A$** at points 1 and 2; the log term captures the nonlinear driving force. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** Derive the expression for steady-state molar flux in equimolar counter diffusion of a binary gas mixture through a stagnant film. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Ammonia ($A$) and nitrogen ($B$) undergo equimolar counter diffusion at $308\;\mathrm{K}$ and $101.325\;\mathrm{kPa}$ in a tube $2\;\mathrm{mm}$ long. At one end, $y_{A1} = 0.20$; at the other, $y_{A2} = 0.08$. The diffusivity $D_{AB} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$. Calculate the molar flux of ammonia. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation:** 1. **Fick's First Law** in terms of molar flux for a binary system: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + y_A(N_A + N_B)$$ 2. **EMCD Assumption:** $N_A = -N_B$, hence $N_A + N_B = 0$. Bulk convection term vanishes: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ 3. **Ideal Gas Substitution:** $C_A = \dfrac{P}{RT} y_A$. With $P$, $T$ constant: $$\frac{dC_A}{dz} = \frac{P}{RT} \frac{dy_A}{dz}$$ 4. **Full Rigorous Form** (writing Fick's law as $N_A = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{1-y_A} \dfrac{dy_A}{dz}$): $$N_A (1 - y_A) dy_A = -D_{AB} \frac{P}{RT} dz$$ 5. **Integration** from $z = 0$, $y_A = y_{A1}$ to $z = z$, $y_A = y_{A2}$: $$N_A \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right) = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT z}$$ 6. **Final result:** $$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T z} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)\;}$$ **Assumptions:** - Steady-state ($\partial C_A/\partial t = 0$). - Binary mixture ($A$ + $B$ only). - Isothermal, isobaric conditions. - $D_{AB}$ constant (does not depend on composition). - Ideal gas behaviour. **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $T = 308\;\mathrm{K}$, $P = 101325\;\mathrm{Pa}$, $z = 0.002\;\mathrm{m}$, $y_{A1} = 0.20$, $y_{A2} = 0.08$, $D_{AB} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$, $R = 8.314\;\mathrm{J/(mol \cdot K)}$. **Compute log term:** $$\frac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}} = \frac{0.92}{0.80} = 1.15$$ $$\ln(1.15) = 0.13976$$ **Compute prefactor:** $$\frac{D_{AB} P}{R T z} = \frac{(2.3 \times 10^{-5})(101325)}{(8.314)(308)(0.002)}$$ Numerator: $2.3 \times 10^{-5} \times 101325 = 2.33048$ Denominator: $8.314 \times 308 \times 0.002 = 5.12142$ $$\text{Prefactor} = \frac{2.33048}{5.12142} = 0.45504\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$$ **Compute $N_A$:** $$N_A = 0.45504 \times 0.13976 = 0.06360\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$$ $$\boxed{\;N_A \approx 6.36 \times 10^{-2}\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)} = 6.36 \times 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{kmol/(m^2 \cdot s)}\;}$$ --- ### Question B — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]** **(a)** With the necessary derivation, obtain the equation for the molar flux during equimolar counter diffusion of a binary liquid mixture. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A dilute solution of ethanol ($A$) in water ($B$) undergoes equimolar counter diffusion across a liquid film of thickness $1.5\;\mathrm{mm}$. The mole fractions at the two sides of the film are $x_{A1} = 0.10$ and $x_{A2} = 0.02$. The total molar concentration of the solution is $C = 55000\;\mathrm{mol/m^3}$ and $D_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$. Calculate the steady-state molar flux. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation:** 1. Fick's law for binary liquid: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$$ 2. Apply EMCD ($N_A + N_B = 0$): $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dz}$$ 3. Express $C_A = x_A \cdot C$ (with $C$ ≈ constant for liquids): $$N_A = -D_{AB} C \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ 4. Rigorous form using $1 - x_A = x_B$: $$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} C}{1 - x_A} \frac{dx_A}{dz}$$ 5. Separate and integrate: $$N_A \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} (1 - x_A)\,dx_A = -D_{AB} C \int_0^z dz$$ $$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} C}{z} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)\;}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $z = 0.0015\;\mathrm{m}$, $x_{A1} = 0.10$, $x_{A2} = 0.02$, $C = 55000\;\mathrm{mol/m^3}$, $D_{AB} = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$. **Log term:** $$\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}} = \frac{0.98}{0.90} = 1.0889$$ $$\ln(1.0889) = 0.08518$$ **Prefactor:** $$\frac{D_{AB} C}{z} = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-9})(55000)}{0.0015}$$ $$= \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0015} = 4.4 \times 10^{-2}\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$$ **Molar flux:** $$N_A = 4.4 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.08518 = 3.748 \times 10^{-3}\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$$ $$\boxed{\;N_A \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-3}\;\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}\;}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in EMCD problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the EMCD constraint check** — many students blindly write the log equation without verifying that $N_A = -N_B$ actually holds in the problem statement. **Always justify the assumption.** > > 2. **Mixing units** — $P$ must be in Pa (not bar or atm) when using $R = 8.314$. $z$ must be in metres. $D_{AB}$ in m²/s. A single unit mismatch yields **full mark deduction**. > > 3. **Forgetting the logarithm** — substituting a linear approximation $(x_{A1} - x_{A2})$ when the problem clearly has concentrated values (e.g., $x_A > 0.1$) will produce a wrong answer that loses 3–4 marks. > > 4. **Sign error** — the equation gives $N_A$ as **positive** if $x_{A1} > x_{A2}$ (flux from high to low). A negative answer should immediately trigger a re-check. > > 5. **Confusing liquid with gas equation** — when $C$ is given instead of $P$ and $T$, the **liquid** form of the equation must be used. This is a frequent trap in KTU papers. > > 6. **Skipping assumption statements** — KTU explicitly tests if you can list all assumptions (steady-state, binary, isothermal, constant $D_{AB}$, ideal gas). Missing more than two loses **2 marks**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **EMCD definition:** $N_A = -N_B$, total molar flux = 0, no bulk convection term. - ✅ **Fick's First Law is the foundation** — every EMCD equation is derived from it. - ✅ **EMCD in gases:** $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{R\,T\,z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - y_{A2}}{1 - y_{A1}}\right)$. - ✅ **EMCD in liquids:** $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,C}{z}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)$. - ✅ **Dilute limit (linear form):** When $y_A$ or $x_A \ll 1$, the log simplifies to $N_A \approx (D_{AB}/z) \cdot \Delta C_A$. - ✅ **Constant $C$ and $P$:** Valid under EMCD because no net molar transport changes the local total concentration. - ✅ **Order of magnitude:** $D_{AB}^{\text{gas}} \sim 10^{-5}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$; $D_{AB}^{\text{liquid}} \sim 10^{-9}\;\mathrm{m^2/s}$ — liquids are ~10,000× slower. - ✅ **Distillation & equimolar reactions** are the **canonical** industrial examples. - ✅ **Stefan diffusion (next topic)** is **NOT** EMCD — it has a non-zero bulk flow. - ✅ **Partial pressure form:** $\ln[(P-P_{A2})/(P-P_{A1})]$ is interchangeable with the mole-fraction log form for gases. - ✅ **Units check:** Final $N_A$ should be in $\mathrm{mol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$ or $\mathrm{kmol/(m^2 \cdot s)}$. - ✅ **Assumptions to memorize:** Steady-state, binary, isothermal, isobaric, constant $D_{AB}$, ideal gas (for gases).

topics.title: Analogies Between Heat and Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analogies Between Heat and Mass Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Analogies Between Heat and Mass Transfer

## 1. Core Technical Definition

In convective transport phenomena, **heat, mass, and momentum transfer** all occur due to the same physical mechanism — the random motion of fluid molecules (eddy diffusion) and the bulk motion of the fluid. Because the underlying transport processes are mathematically identical, dimensionless groups that govern one process can be correlated with those governing the others. This is the foundation of **transport analogies**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> An **analogy** in transport phenomena is a mathematical relationship that correlates the dimensionless groups (such as Nusselt, Sherwood, and Fanning friction factor) describing convective **momentum**, **heat**, and **mass transfer** under analogous flow conditions over identical geometries.

### Formal Statement
When a fluid flows over a surface, three transport processes occur simultaneously:

| Process | Driving Potential | Dimensionless Group |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Momentum Transfer | Velocity gradient (du/dy) | Fanning friction factor, $f$ |
| Heat Transfer | Temperature gradient (dT/dy) | Nusselt number, $Nu$ |
| Mass Transfer | Concentration gradient (dC_A/dy) | Sherwood number, $Sh$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do analogies exist?**
> The governing differential equations for momentum (Navier–Stokes), heat (energy equation), and mass (species continuity) all share an identical form — they are **convective-diffusion equations** with similar boundary conditions. Hence, their solutions are functionally equivalent, allowing direct correlation.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **rowing a boat** across a wide, slow-moving river:

- **Momentum transfer** is like the *drag* the river exerts on the bottom of your boat — water resists the motion.
- **Heat transfer** is like the *warmth* the sun adds to your skin through the moving air — temperature spreads in the direction of motion.
- **Mass transfer** is like the *smell of perfume* carried by the breeze from a person standing nearby — the odor diffuses outward.

Although these are three *different* sensations, they all happen because the **river (fluid) is moving**. The same eddies that drag your boat also carry warmth and scent. If you can measure one (e.g., drag), you can predict the others — this is the essence of **analogies**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic:** "**A**nalogy = **A**ll **T**ransfers behave the **S**ame way" — momentum, heat, and mass all follow the same convective transport logic.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. The Three Transport Equations: Side-by-Side Comparison

The governing equations for momentum, heat, and mass transfer in a boundary layer are:

### Momentum (x-momentum)
$$\rho u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \rho v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

### Heat (Energy)
$$\rho c_p u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + \rho c_p v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = k \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

### Mass (Species A)
$$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$

### Why Are They Identical?
Each equation has the form:
$$\text{(convective term)} = \text{(diffusive term)}$$

The only differences are:
- Momentum uses **kinematic viscosity**, $\nu = \mu/\rho$
- Heat uses **thermal diffusivity**, $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$
- Mass uses **mass diffusivity**, $D_{AB}$

The ratios $\nu : \alpha : D_{AB}$ determine the **relative rates** of momentum, heat, and mass transport, characterized by two key dimensionless groups:

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} \quad \text{(Prandtl number)}$$

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} \quad \text{(Schmidt number)}$$

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Analogy | Governing Equation | Validity / Applicability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Reynolds Analogy** | $St = \dfrac{f}{2}$ | Valid only when $Pr = Sc = 1$ |
| 2 | **Prandtl Analogy** | $St \cdot Pr^{\frac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{f}{2}$ | Valid for $0.6 < Pr < 50$ (single-phase fluids) |
| 3 | **Von Kármán Analogy** | $St \cdot Pr^{\frac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{f}{8} \left(1 + 5\sqrt{Pr} - 5\right)$ | Valid for $0.5 < Pr < 10$, with $Pr$ near 1 |
| 4 | **Colburn Analogy** (heat) | $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{\frac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{f}{2}$ | Empirical; valid for $0.6 < Pr < 160$ |
| 5 | **Colburn Analogy** (mass) | $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{\frac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{f}{2}$ | Empirical; valid for $0.6 < Sc < 1600$ |
| 6 | **Chilton–Colburn $j$-factor equivalence** | $j_H \approx j_D$ | Correlates heat and mass transfer directly |

### Key Dimensionless Groups (Definitions)

| Group | Definition | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stanton (heat) | $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho c_p u}$ | Ratio of convective to bulk thermal transport |
| Stanton (mass) | $St_m = \dfrac{k_c}{u}$ | Ratio of convective to bulk mass transport |
| Fanning friction factor | $f = \dfrac{2 \tau_w}{\rho u^2}$ | Wall shear stress normalized |
| $j$-factor (heat) | $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ | Modified Stanton for heat |
| $j$-factor (mass) | $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ | Modified Stanton for mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - If you know **friction** (easy to measure), you can predict **heat transfer coefficient** ($h$).
> - If you know **heat transfer** in one geometry, you can predict **mass transfer** in another.
> - Used in design of **heat exchangers**, **cooling towers**, **evaporators**, **packed columns**, and **gas absorbers**.

---

## Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Cooling Towers:** Heat lost by water = mass transfer of water vapor to air — Colburn analogy directly applies.
2. **Chemical Reactors:** Mass transfer rates in catalytic beds are predicted from pressure-drop (friction) data.
3. **Aerospace:** Skin friction on a re-entry vehicle correlates with convective heating.
4. **Drying Operations:** Mass transfer of moisture from solids to air predicted using heat-mass analogy.
5. **Atmospheric Studies:** Evaporation rates from lakes estimated using wind-friction data.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reynolds Analogy — Complete Derivation

### Starting Point: Force, Heat, and Mass Balances on a Control Volume

Consider a fluid flowing over a flat plate. Take an elemental control volume of length $dx$ and unit depth. Three fluxes occur through the control volume:

1. **Momentum flux** entering: $\rho u^2$ (per unit area)
2. **Heat flux** entering: $\rho c_p u T$
3. **Mass flux** entering: $\rho u Y_A$ (where $Y_A$ = mass fraction of species A)

The respective fluxes leaving the element (after exchanging with the wall):

| Quantity | Entering | Leaving | Wall Flux |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Momentum | $\rho u^2$ | $\rho u(u+du)$ | $d(\rho u^2) = \rho u\, du$ |
| Heat | $\rho c_p u T_\infty$ | $\rho c_p u (T_\infty + dT_\infty)$ | $dq = \rho c_p u\, dT_\infty$ |
| Mass | $\rho u Y_{A,\infty}$ | $\rho u (Y_{A,\infty} + dY_{A,\infty})$ | $dN_A = \rho u\, dY_{A,\infty}$ |

### Balancing Forces on the Element

The wall exerts a **shear stress** $\tau_w$, a **heat flux** $q$, and a **mass flux** $N_A$ on the fluid element.

**Force balance (momentum):**
$$d(\rho u^2) = -\tau_w \, dx$$

**Energy balance (heat):**
$$dq = \rho c_p u \, dT_\infty$$

**Mass balance (species A):**
$$dN_A = \rho u \, dY_{A,\infty}$$

### Eliminating the Common Term $\rho u$

From the force balance:
$$\rho u = -\frac{\tau_w}{du} \, dx$$

> *Wait — the correct elimination is as follows:*

Differentiating the force balance with respect to $x$:
$$\rho u \, du = -\tau_w$$

(assuming $u$ changes by $du$ over the element of length $dx$)

Similarly, from the energy balance:
$$dq = \rho c_p u \, dT_\infty \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho u = \frac{dq}{c_p \, dT_\infty}$$

From the mass balance:
$$dN_A = \rho u \, dY_{A,\infty} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho u = \frac{dN_A}{dY_{A,\infty}}$$

### Setting the Expressions Equal

The wall shear stress per unit area is:
$$\tau_w = -\mu \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_{y=0}$$

The wall heat flux is:
$$q = -k \left(\frac{dT}{dy}\right)_{y=0} = h(T_w - T_\infty)$$

The wall mass flux is:
$$N_A = -D_{AB} \left(\frac{dC_A}{dy}\right)_{y=0} = k_c(C_{A,w} - C_{A,\infty})$$

**Equating $\rho u$ expressions:**

$$\frac{\tau_w}{du} = \frac{dq}{c_p \, dT_\infty} = \frac{dN_A}{dY_{A,\infty}}$$

> *This is the key step of Reynolds analogy.*

### Applying the Analogy Assumption

**Reynolds' critical assumption:** All dimensionless property ratios at the wall are unity — i.e., the velocity, temperature, and concentration profiles are **identical** when normalized. This implies:

$$\frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = D_{AB}$$

i.e., $\nu = \alpha = D_{AB}$, which means $Pr = 1$ and $Sc = 1$.

Under this condition, equating the fluxes:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho u^2} = \frac{q}{\rho c_p u (T_w - T_\infty)} = \frac{N_A}{\rho u (Y_{A,w} - Y_{A,\infty})}$$

### Final Form: Reynolds Analogy

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho u^2} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho u^2} = St$$

And the Fanning friction factor is defined as:
$$f = \frac{2 \tau_w}{\rho u^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;St = \frac{f}{2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reynolds Analogy is exact** only for $Pr = Sc = 1$. For air at moderate conditions ($Pr \approx 0.7$), it gives reasonable results. For liquids ($Pr \gg 1$) or viscous oils, large errors occur.

---

## 3.2 Prandtl Analogy — Derivation

### Modification to Account for Viscous Sub-layer

Reynolds analogy fails because it treats the boundary layer as a single region. **Prandtl** proposed a two-layer model:

1. **Viscous sub-layer** ($0 \leq y \leq y_1$): Linear velocity and temperature profiles.
2. **Turbulent core** ($y_1 \leq y \leq \delta$): Fully turbulent region.

**Step 1:** In the viscous sub-layer, momentum and heat transfer both occur by molecular diffusion:

$$\tau_w = \mu \frac{u}{y_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \frac{\tau_w \, y_1}{\mu}$$

$$q = k \frac{(T_w - T_1)}{y_1} = h_1 (T_w - T_1)$$

This gives:
$$h_1 = \frac{k}{y_1} = \frac{\mu c_p}{y_1} \cdot \frac{1}{Pr}$$

**Step 2:** At the interface $y = y_1$, the velocity is $u_1$ and temperature is $T_1$. The fluid above the sub-layer carries momentum and heat to the free stream.

**Step 3:** Prandtl assumed that the **Reynolds analogy applies above the sub-layer**, and that the sub-layer contributes a correction. Through the universal velocity distribution (the "1/7th power law"):

$$\frac{u}{u_\infty} = \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{1/7}$$

And combining the integrations across the sub-layer and turbulent core, Prandtl obtained:

$$\boxed{\;St \cdot Pr^{\frac{2}{3}} = \frac{f}{2}\;}$$

This is valid in the range $0.6 < Pr < 50$.

---

## 3.3 Von Kármán Analogy — Derivation Outline

Von Kármán refined Prandtl's approach by replacing the assumed linear sub-layer profile with the actual turbulent transport equations. He integrated the momentum and energy equations across the entire boundary layer and accounted for the variation of eddy diffusivity $\epsilon_M$ and $\epsilon_H$.

The result, valid for moderate $Pr$ near 1, is:

$$\boxed{\;St \cdot Pr^{\frac{2}{3}} = \frac{f}{8}\left(1 + 5\sqrt{Pr} - 5\right)\;}$$

Or equivalently:
$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8} \left[5(\sqrt{Pr} - 1) + 1\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Von Kármán:** "**5-5-1**" — the formula has 5, 5, and 1.

For $Pr = 1$: the term in parentheses becomes $1 + 5(1) - 5 = 1$, so $St = f/8$. For $Pr \neq 1$, the analogy gives a correction.

---

## 3.4 Colburn Analogy — Derivation from Experimental Data

Colburn empirically correlated extensive data on heat transfer in pipes and found that the $j$-factor:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

correlated remarkably well with $f/2$ over a wide range of Reynolds and Prandtl numbers. The same was found for mass transfer:

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} \approx \frac{f}{2}$$

**Chilton–Colburn analogy** states that for analogous geometries and flow conditions:

$$\boxed{\;j_H \approx j_D\;}$$

This is the most commonly used form in KTU problems.

### Practical Working Equations

**For Heat Transfer:**
$$h = St \cdot \rho c_p u = \frac{(f/2) \cdot \rho c_p u}{Pr^{2/3}}$$

**For Mass Transfer:**
$$k_c = St_m \cdot u = \frac{(f/2) \cdot u}{Sc^{2/3}}$$

**Direct Heat-Mass Analogy:**
$$\frac{h}{\rho c_p} = k_c \cdot \left(\frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}\right)^{2/3} = k_c \cdot \left(\frac{Pr}{Sc}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{Pr \cdot Sc}\right)^{1/3}$$

Simplified Chilton–Colburn form:
$$\frac{Nu}{Pr^{1/3} \, Re} \approx \frac{Sh}{Sc^{1/3} \, Re}$$

i.e., $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} \approx St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Table)

| Feature | Reynolds | Prandtl | Von Kármán | Colburn |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Year | 1874 | 1910 | 1939 | 1933 |
| Derivation | First principles | Two-layer model | Improved integration | Empirical |
| Range of $Pr$ | $Pr = 1$ only | $0.6 - 50$ | $0.5 - 10$ | $0.6 - 160$ |
| Form | $St = f/2$ | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/8(1+5\sqrt{Pr}-5)$ | $j_H = j_D = f/2$ |
| Applies to mass? | Yes (Sc=1) | No | No | Yes (Chilton–Colburn) |
| KTU Importance | High | Very High | Moderate | Very High |
---

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Map of Transport Analogies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["TRANSPORT ANALOGIES"] --> B0["REYNOLDS"]
    A0 --> C0["PRANDTL"]
    A0 --> D0["VON KARMAN"]
    A0 --> E0["COLBURN / CHILTON COLBURN"]

    B0 --> B1["Single fluid layer assumption"]
    B1 --> B2["ST equals f over 2"]
    B2 --> B3["Valid only for Pr equal 1 and Sc equal 1"]

    C0 --> C1["Two layer model"]
    C1 --> C2["Viscous sublayer and turbulent core"]
    C2 --> C3["ST times Pr to 2/3 equals f over 2"]
    C3 --> C4["Valid for 0.6 to 50"]

    D0 --> D1["Full boundary layer integration"]
    D1 --> D2["Accounts for eddy diffusivity variation"]
    D2 --> D3["ST times Pr to 2/3 equals f over 8 times 1 plus 5 sqrt Pr minus 5"]
    D3 --> D4["Valid for 0.5 to 10"]

    E0 --> E1["Empirical j factor correlation"]
    E1 --> E2["jH equals jD equals f over 2"]
    E2 --> E3["Direct heat mass equivalence"]
    E3 --> F1["Used in cooling towers and dryers"]

    B3 --> G1["KTU Recommendation"]
    C4 --> G1
    D4 --> G1
    F1 --> G1

    G1["Use COLBURN for heat mass linked problems"]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Equivalence of Three Transports

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MOMENTUM["MOMENTUM TRANSFER"]
        M1["Wall shear stress tau_w"] --> M2["Fanning friction factor f"]
        M2 --> M3["Friction velocity u_tau"]
    end

    subgraph HEAT["HEAT TRANSFER"]
        H1["Wall heat flux q"] --> H2["Heat transfer coefficient h"]
        H2 --> H3["Stanton number ST"]
    end

    subgraph MASS["MASS TRANSFER"]
        MS1["Wall mass flux N_A"] --> MS2["Mass transfer coefficient k_c"]
        MS2 --> MS3["Stanton mass number ST_m"]
    end

    M3 --> ANALOGY["ANALOGY EQUATIONS"]
    H3 --> ANALOGY
    MS3 --> ANALOGY

    ANALOGY --> R1["Reynolds: ST equals f over 2"]
    ANALOGY --> R2["Prandtl: ST times Pr2/3 equals f over 2"]
    ANALOGY --> R3["Colburn: jH equals jD"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Two-Layer Boundary Layer (Prandtl Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUB["VISCOUS SUB-LAYER"]
        S1["Linear velocity profile: u linear in y"]
        S2["Linear temperature profile: T linear in y"]
    end

    subgraph TURB["TURBULENT CORE"]
        T1["1/7th power law: u equals u_inf times y over delta to 1/7"]
        T2["Similar for T using Prandtl mixing"]
    end

    WALL["Wall at y equals 0"] --> SUB
    SUB --> Y1["Interface at y equals y1"]
    Y1 --> TURB
    TURB --> FREE["Free stream at y equals delta"]

    S1 --> RESULT["MATCHING GIVES ST times Pr2/3 equals f over 2"]
    S2 --> RESULT
    T1 --> RESULT
    T2 --> RESULT
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Which Analogy to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["GIVEN: Friction factor f and Pr or Sc"] --> Q1{"Need heat transfer?"}

    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Pr value?"}
    Q2 -- "Pr equals 1" --> A1["Use REYNOLDS: ST equals f over 2"]
    Q2 -- "0.6 to 50" --> A2["Use PRANDTL or COLBURN: ST times Pr2/3 equals f over 2"]
    Q2 -- "50 to 1000" --> A3["Use COLBURN only or Dittus Boelter"]

    Q1 -- "No" --> Q3{"Need mass transfer?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes, know heat data" --> A4["Use CHILTON COLBURN: jH equals jD"]
    Q3 -- "Yes, know friction only" --> A5["Use COLBURN mass: ST_m times Sc2/3 equals f over 2"]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**State Reynolds analogy between heat and momentum transfer. What are its limitations?**

**Model Answer:**

Reynolds analogy states that for a fluid flowing over a surface, the dimensionless heat transfer coefficient (Stanton number) is equal to half the Fanning friction factor:

$$St = \frac{f}{2}$$

where $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho c_p u}$ and $f = \dfrac{2\tau_w}{\rho u^2}$.

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only when $Pr = 1$ (i.e., $\nu = \alpha$).
2. Does not account for the difference between momentum and heat transport in the viscous sub-layer and turbulent core.
3. Fails for liquids where $Pr \gg 1$ and for viscous oils.
4. Applicable only to single-phase flow, not to phase-change processes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "Reynolds analogy is valid for all fluids." This is **WRONG** — always mention the **$Pr = 1$** restriction.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the Chilton–Colburn analogy. Write the $j$-factor equations for heat and mass transfer.**

**Model Answer:**

The Chilton–Colburn analogy is an **empirical correlation** that establishes equivalence between heat and mass transfer under analogous flow and geometric conditions.

**Heat transfer $j$-factor:**
$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u} \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

**Mass transfer $j$-factor:**
$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{k_c}{u} \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

**Chilton–Colburn analogy states:**
$$\boxed{\;j_H \approx j_D = \frac{f}{2}\;}$$

This allows direct prediction of mass transfer coefficients from heat transfer data, and vice versa, over a wide range of $Pr$ and $Sc$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **Reynolds analogy** between heat and momentum transfer. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air at $327^\circ C$ and $1 \text{ atm}$ flows over a flat plate at $27^\circ C$ with a free stream velocity of $3 \text{ m/s}$. If the average friction coefficient (Fanning) over a length of $1.5 \text{ m}$ is $0.005$, determine the average heat transfer coefficient using: **(i) Reynolds analogy** and **(ii) Colburn analogy**. Take $Pr = 0.7$ and $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Reynolds Analogy Derivation:**

Consider a fluid flowing over a flat plate. Take a control volume of length $dx$ and unit width. Apply mass, momentum, and energy balances on the elemental slice.

**Assumption of Reynolds Analogy:**
- The dimensionless velocity, temperature, and concentration profiles are identical.
- This requires $\nu = \alpha = D_{AB}$, i.e., $Pr = 1$ and $Sc = 1$.

**Momentum balance** on element (per unit width):
$$d(\rho u^2) = -\tau_w \, dx$$

**Energy balance** on element:
$$dq = \rho c_p u \, dT_\infty$$

**Mass balance** on element:
$$dN_A = \rho u \, dY_{A,\infty}$$

Eliminating the common term $\rho u$ from the three balances:

$$\frac{\tau_w \, dx}{du} = \frac{dq}{c_p \, dT_\infty} = \frac{dN_A}{dY_{A,\infty}}$$

Using wall definitions:
$$\tau_w = \mu \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)_{y=0}, \quad q = h(T_w - T_\infty), \quad N_A = k_c(Y_{A,w} - Y_{A,\infty})$$

Substituting and applying the Reynolds assumption ($Pr = 1$), we obtain:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho u^2} = \frac{h}{\rho c_p u}$$

The Fanning friction factor is $f = 2\tau_w / \rho u^2$, and Stanton number is $St = h / \rho c_p u$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;St = \frac{f}{2}\;}$$

> **End of Part (a) — 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given Data:**
- $T_\infty = 327^\circ C = 600 \text{ K}$
- $T_w = 27^\circ C = 300 \text{ K}$
- $u = 3 \text{ m/s}$
- $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $f = 0.005$ (Fanning)
- $Pr = 0.7$
- $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K} = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$

Density of air at film temperature: $T_f = (600 + 300)/2 = 450 \text{ K}$

$$\rho = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{287 \times 450} = 0.7844 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**(i) Reynolds Analogy:**
$$St = \frac{f}{2} = \frac{0.005}{2} = 0.0025$$

$$h = St \cdot \rho c_p u = 0.0025 \times 0.7844 \times 1005 \times 3$$

$$h = 5.913 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**(ii) Colburn Analogy:**
$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

$$St = \frac{f/2}{Pr^{2/3}} = \frac{0.0025}{(0.7)^{2/3}}$$

Computing $Pr^{2/3}$: $(0.7)^{2/3} = 0.7^{0.6667} \approx 0.788$

$$St = \frac{0.0025}{0.788} = 0.003173$$

$$h = 0.003173 \times 0.7844 \times 1005 \times 3$$

$$h = 7.506 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Final Results:**
- Reynolds analogy: $h \approx 5.91 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- Colburn analogy: $h \approx 7.51 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$

> **End of Part (b) — 7 marks**

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the **Prandtl analogy** and **Von Kármán analogy** between heat and momentum transfer. Compare their applicability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a cooling tower, air at $40^\circ C$ flows over water at $30^\circ C$. The air velocity is $4 \text{ m/s}$, the Fanning friction factor is $0.004$, and the air properties are: $\rho = 1.12 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $Pr = 0.71$, $Sc = 0.61$, $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate: **(i) the heat transfer coefficient** using the Prandtl analogy, and **(ii) the mass transfer coefficient** using the Chilton–Colburn analogy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Prandtl Analogy:**

Prandtl modified Reynolds' approach by recognizing that the boundary layer consists of two distinct regions:

1. **Viscous sub-layer** ($0 \leq y \leq y_1$): Both momentum and heat transfer occur purely by molecular diffusion. Linear velocity and temperature profiles exist here.
2. **Turbulent core** ($y_1 \leq y \leq \delta$): Transport is dominated by turbulent eddies. Prandtl used the $1/7$th power law velocity profile.

By matching the solutions at the interface and integrating across both regions, Prandtl obtained:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

**Applicability:** $0.6 < Pr < 50$ — valid for gases and most liquids.

**Von Kármán Analogy:**

Von Kármán further refined Prandtl's model by accounting for the actual variation of **eddy diffusivity** $\epsilon_M$ and **eddy thermal diffusivity** $\epsilon_H$ across the boundary layer, rather than assuming a fixed ratio. Using a more rigorous integration of the Prandtl mixing-length equations:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8}\left(1 + 5\sqrt{Pr} - 5\right)$$

**Applicability:** $0.5 < Pr < 10$ — most accurate for $Pr$ near 1 (e.g., gases).

**Comparison:**

| Feature | Prandtl | Von Kármán |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sub-layer model | Linear | Accounted for eddy variation |
| Velocity profile | 1/7th power | Universal (log law) |
| Range of $Pr$ | $0.6 - 50$ | $0.5 - 10$ |
| Form | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/8(1+5\sqrt{Pr}-5)$ |
| Empirical constants | None | Numerical 5, 5, 1 |
| Use today | Common | Less common; historical |

> **End of Part (a) — 7 marks**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Given Data:**
- Air: $T_\infty = 40^\circ C$, $T_w = 30^\circ C$
- $u = 4 \text{ m/s}$
- $f = 0.004$ (Fanning)
- $\rho = 1.12 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$
- $Pr = 0.71$, $Sc = 0.61$
- $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**(i) Heat transfer coefficient using Prandtl analogy:**

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2} = \frac{0.004}{2} = 0.002$$

Computing $Pr^{2/3}$: $(0.71)^{2/3}$

$$\ln(0.71) = -0.3425 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (2/3) \cdot \ln(0.71) = -0.2283$$

$$Pr^{2/3} = e^{-0.2283} = 0.7959$$

$$St = \frac{0.002}{0.7959} = 0.002513$$

$$h = St \cdot \rho c_p u = 0.002513 \times 1.12 \times 1005 \times 4$$

$$h = 11.31 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**(ii) Mass transfer coefficient using Chilton–Colburn analogy:**

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2} = 0.002$$

Computing $Sc^{2/3}$: $(0.61)^{2/3}$

$$\ln(0.61) = -0.4943 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (2/3) \cdot (-0.4943) = -0.3295$$

$$Sc^{2/3} = e^{-0.3295} = 0.7192$$

$$St_m = \frac{0.002}{0.7192} = 0.002781$$

$$k_c = St_m \cdot u = 0.002781 \times 4 = 0.01112 \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Results:**
- $h \approx 11.31 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$
- $k_c \approx 0.0111 \text{ m/s}$

> **End of Part (b) — 7 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the FILM temperature** for property evaluation in air-cooling problems.
> 2. **Mixing Fanning and Darcy friction factors** — KTU uses Fanning. If $C_D$ (Darcy) is given, convert: $f_{\text{Fanning}} = C_D/4$.
> 3. **Stanton number definition confusion** — $St = h/(\rho c_p u)$ for heat, $St_m = k_c/u$ for mass.
> 4. **Skipping the $Pr^{2/3}$ correction** in Colburn/Prandtl problems — examiners will deduct 2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions — at least 2 marks are reserved for "assumptions" in KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Reynolds Analogy:** $St = f/2$ — valid **only** for $Pr = 1$ and $Sc = 1$.
- **Prandtl Analogy:** $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$ — valid for $0.6 < Pr < 50$.
- **Von Kármán Analogy:** $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{f}{8}\left(1 + 5\sqrt{Pr} - 5\right)$ — valid for $0.5 < Pr < 10$.
- **Colburn $j$-factor (heat):** $j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = f/2$.
- **Colburn $j$-factor (mass):** $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = f/2$.
- **Chilton–Colburn Analogy:** $j_H = j_D$ — used to link **heat ↔ mass** transfer.
- **Stanton number (heat):** $St = h/(\rho c_p u)$.
- **Stanton number (mass):** $St_m = k_c / u$.
- **Friction factor (Fanning):** $f = 2\tau_w / (\rho u^2)$.
- All three transports are governed by the same **convective-diffusion equation**, hence analogous.
- Use **film temperature** $T_f = (T_w + T_\infty)/2$ for property evaluation.
- **KTU favourite equations:** Colburn, Chilton–Colburn, and Reynolds — at least one is asked every cycle.
- The $Pr^{2/3}$ (or $Sc^{2/3}$) factor corrects for differences in the **viscous sub-layer** transport of heat (or mass) vs. momentum.
- For liquids ($Pr \gg 1$): use **Colburn**; for gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$): **Reynolds** is acceptable as first approximation.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dalton's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dalton's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** (also known as **Dalton's Law of Additive Pressures**) is a fundamental gas law in thermodynamics and mass transfer that states:

> *"The total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases. Moreover, the partial pressure of each gas is the pressure that the gas would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume of the mixture at the same temperature."*

Mathematically, for a mixture containing $n$ components:

$$P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \dots + P_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

where:
- $P_{total}$ = total pressure of the gas mixture (in **Pa** or **bar**)
- $P_i$ = partial pressure of the $i^{th}$ component (in **Pa** or **bar**)
- $n$ = number of non-reacting gaseous components

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Dalton's Law forms the **cornerstone foundation** for the entire Module 3 topic of *Humidification, Dehumidification, and Psychrometry*. Without mastering this law, you cannot derive any humidity ratio, dew point, or wet-bulb temperature relations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a sealed room filled with three different types of balls bouncing around — red balls, blue balls, and green balls. Each type of ball behaves **independently** of the others. The total force exerted on the walls of the room is the sum of the forces from each ball type acting alone.

In a gas mixture:
- **Red balls = Nitrogen ($N_2$)**
- **Blue balls = Oxygen ($O_2$)**  
- **Green balls = Water Vapour ($H_2O$)**

The walls of the "room" (container) experience the **sum of all impacts** from each gas independently. The presence of one gas does not interfere with the kinetic energy of another. Therefore, each gas contributes its own *partial pressure* to the total, and they all simply **add up**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption:** Dalton's Law is **strictly valid** only for **ideal gases** (or real gases at low pressures and high temperatures where intermolecular forces are negligible). For high-pressure systems (e.g., industrial cylinders), significant deviations occur.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------------|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **273.15 K** (0 °C) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Logic Behind Dalton's Law

To understand *why* Dalton's Law works, let us break down the underlying logic step-by-step:

1. **Step 1 — Gas Independence:** In an ideal gas mixture, each gas molecule moves independently. The collisions between unlike molecules do not differ in any physical way from collisions between like molecules (this is the **Amagat postulate**).

2. **Step 2 — Apply Ideal Gas Law to Each Component:** For each individual gas $i$ occupying the total volume $V$ at temperature $T$:

$$P_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

3. **Step 3 — Sum the Partial Pressures:** Since all gases occupy the **same volume** $V$ and are at the **same temperature** $T$:

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{n_i R T}{V} = \frac{RT}{V} \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i = \frac{RT}{V} \cdot n_{total}$$

4. **Step 4 — Define Mole Fraction:** The **mole fraction** $y_i$ of each component is:

$$y_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}}$$

5. **Step 5 — Express Partial Pressure via Mole Fraction:** Dividing $P_i$ by $P_{total}$:

$$\frac{P_i}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_i RT/V}{n_{total} RT/V} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = y_i$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind It:** The mole fraction $y_i$ is **dimensionless** and represents the **fraction of total molecules** that belong to species $i$. Physically, $y_i$ also equals the **fraction of total pressure** contributed by species $i$.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dalton's Law is the **bedrock** of numerous engineering applications:

- **🌫️ Humidification Systems:** Calculating humidity ratios, dew points, and psychrometric properties in HVAC, drying, and air-conditioning systems.
- **🏭 Combustion Engineering:** Analyzing flue gas compositions ($CO_2$, $H_2O$, $N_2$, $O_2$) in furnaces and boilers.
- **🫁 Respiratory Medicine:** Designing oxygen masks and understanding partial pressure of oxygen ($P_{O_2}$) in blood.
- **🎈 Gas Cylinder Blending:** Industrial preparation of calibrated gas mixtures (e.g., welding gas mixtures of Argon + $CO_2$).
- **🌍 Atmospheric Science:** Modeling partial pressures of $O_2$, $N_2$, $H_2O$ vapour in the atmosphere.
- **⚗️ Distillation Columns:** Vapor-liquid equilibrium calculations in chemical process industries.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Dalton's Law (additive partial pressures) | Pa or bar |
| 2 | $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Partial pressure from mole fraction | Pa or bar |
| 3 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$ | Mole fraction definition | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_i V = n_i R T$ | Ideal gas law applied to component $i$ | Universal |
| 5 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1$ | Mole fractions must sum to unity | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{vapour} = y_{vapour} \cdot P_{total}$ | Partial pressure of vapour in moist air | Pa |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{P_{vapour}}{P_{sat}(T)}$ | Relative humidity definition | Dimensionless (0–1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The single most-examined equation from Dalton's Law in the **Humidification** module is the **partial pressure of water vapour in a moist air system**, expressed as $P_v = y_v \cdot P_{total}$. This bridges directly into the **humidity ratio** formula $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_{total} - P_v}$.

## 2.4 Limiting Conditions and Validity

Dalton's Law holds **exactly** for:
- **Ideal gases** (no intermolecular forces, negligible molecular volume)
- **Real gases at low pressures** (typically $P < 5$ bar) and **high temperatures**
- **Non-reacting mixtures** (no chemical combination between species)

It **fails** for:
- **High-pressure gas mixtures** (intermolecular forces become significant)
- **Polar gases** like $NH_3$ or $H_2O$ vapour near saturation
- **Gas mixtures with strong associative interactions** (e.g., $HCl$ + $H_2O$)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Partial Pressure from the Ideal Gas Law

### Starting Premise
Consider a mixture of $n$ ideal gases occupying volume $V$ at temperature $T$. Let $n_i$ be the number of moles of gas $i$.

### Step 1 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to the Total Mixture

For the entire mixture:

$$P_{total} \cdot V = n_{total} \cdot R \cdot T$$

where $n_{total} = n_1 + n_2 + \dots + n_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to Each Component (Dalton's Postulate)

Dalton postulated that each gas behaves **as if it alone occupies the entire volume** $V$ at temperature $T$:

$$P_i \cdot V = n_i \cdot R \cdot T$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $P_i$

$$P_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

### Step 4 — Sum All Partial Pressures

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

Factor out the constants $\frac{RT}{V}$:

$$P_{total} = \frac{RT}{V} \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i = \frac{RT}{V} \cdot n_{total}$$

### Step 5 — Establish the Mole Fraction Relationship

Divide the expression for $P_i$ by that of $P_{total}$:

$$\frac{P_i}{P_{total}} = \frac{\dfrac{n_i RT}{V}}{\dfrac{n_{total} RT}{V}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = y_i$$

### Step 6 — Final Form

$$\boxed{P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}} \quad \text{where} \quad y_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \frac{P_i}{P_{total}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Humidity Ratio ($\omega$) from Dalton's Law

This is the **most critical derivation** for KTU Module 3 (Psychrometry).

### Starting Premise
Moist air is a binary mixture of:
- **Dry air** (treated as a single pseudo-component with average molecular mass $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol)
- **Water vapour** (molecular mass $M_v = 18.02$ g/mol)

Total pressure $P_{total} = 101.325$ kPa (typically atmospheric).

### Step 1 — Define Humidity Ratio (Specific Humidity)

The **humidity ratio** $\omega$ is defined as the mass of water vapour per unit mass of dry air:

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{18.02 \cdot n_v}{28.97 \cdot n_a}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Dalton's Law to Partial Pressures

By Dalton's Law:

$$P_{total} = P_a + P_v$$

where:
- $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air
- $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour

### Step 3 — Express Mole Fractions

$$y_v = \frac{P_v}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_v}{n_v + n_a}$$

$$y_a = \frac{P_a}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_a}{n_v + n_a}$$

### Step 4 — Express the Ratio of Moles

$$\frac{n_v}{n_a} = \frac{P_v}{P_{total} - P_v} = \frac{P_v}{P_a}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into Humidity Ratio Definition

$$\omega = \frac{18.02}{28.97} \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{total} - P_v}$$

### Step 6 — Final Form

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{total} - P_v}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Standard Result:** The constant **0.622** is $\dfrac{M_{H_2O}}{M_{air}} = \dfrac{18.02}{28.97}$. This number appears in **every** psychrometric problem in the KTU university exam. Memorize it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Relative Humidity Connection

### Step 1 — Definition of Relative Humidity

Relative humidity $\phi$ is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation pressure of water vapour at the same temperature:

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{sat}(T)}$$

### Step 2 — Express Saturation Humidity Ratio

At saturation ($\phi = 1$), $P_v = P_{sat}(T)$. Substituting into the humidity ratio formula:

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{sat}(T)}{P_{total} - P_{sat}(T)}$$

### Step 3 — Generalize for Any $\phi$

Since $P_v = \phi \cdot P_{sat}(T)$:

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{\phi \cdot P_{sat}(T)}{P_{total} - \phi \cdot P_{sat}(T)}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification Example

**Given:** $T = 30$ °C, $P_{total} = 101.325$ kPa, $\phi = 0.60$.  
**From steam tables:** $P_{sat}(30°C) = 4.246$ kPa.

$$P_v = 0.60 \times 4.246 = 2.548 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{2.548}{101.325 - 2.548} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.548}{98.777}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \times 0.02580 = 0.01605 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation # | Starting Concept | Final Result | Engineering Application |
|--------------|------------------|--------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law + Independence | $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Gas mixture analysis |
| 2 | Dalton's Law + Definition of $\omega$ | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_{total} - P_v}$ | Psychrometric calculations |
| 3 | Definition of $\phi$ + Steam tables | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{\phi P_{sat}}{P_{total} - \phi P_{sat}}$ | HVAC design, drying |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
