<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drying — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Drying (Two Consecutive Steps)

1. **Migration of moisture** from the interior of the solid to the surface (controlled by diffusion, capillarity, or both).
2. **Evaporation of moisture** from the surface into the bulk gas stream (controlled by external heat and mass transfer).

### 2.1.2 Rate of Drying Curve

A typical plot of **drying rate (N)** versus **moisture content (X)** has the following zones:

| Zone | Moisture Range | Controlling Resistance | Description |
|------|---------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **A → B** (Initial adjustment) | $X_0 \to X_1$ | Transient | Solid heats up to wet-bulb temperature |
| **B → C** (Constant Rate) | $X_1 \to X_c$ | External (gas phase) | Surface is fully wet; rate governed by $T$, $v$, $H$ of air |
| **C → D** (First Falling Rate) | $X_c \to X^*$ | Internal + External | Surface starts to dry; "dry patches" appear |
| **D → E** (Second Falling Rate) | $X^* \to 0$ | Internal | Diffusion-controlled; rate approaches zero |

### 2.1.3 Constant-Rate Drying Equation

During the **B → C** zone, the surface behaves as a free water surface, so we can use the wet-bulb analogy. Two equivalent forms are commonly used in KTU problems:

$$N_c = \frac{h_T (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s} \quad \text{(heat-transfer based)}$$

$$N_c = k_y (H_w - H_g) \quad \text{(mass-transfer based)}$$

where $N_c$ is the constant drying flux (kg/m²·s), $h_T$ is the gas-side heat transfer coefficient, $T_g$ and $T_w$ are gas and wet-bulb temperatures, $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat of vaporisation at $T_w$, $k_y$ is the mass transfer coefficient, and $H_w$, $H_g$ are the humidities at the wet-bulb temperature and bulk gas.

---

## 2.2 Absorption — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Equilibrium & Henry's Law

For dilute physical absorption, the solute partial pressure at equilibrium is:

$$p_A^* = H \cdot x_A \quad \text{(Henry's Law)}$$

where $H$ is the Henry's constant (Pa·mol fraction⁻¹). **Large H → low solubility.**

### 2.2.2 Operating Line (Counter-Current Packed Column)

Material balance on the gas (A) and liquid (L) across the top section:

$$G_m (Y_1 - Y) = L_m (X - X_2)$$

Rearranged into the classical $Y = f(X)$ form:

$$Y = \frac{L_m}{G_m} X + \left( Y_1 - \frac{L_m}{G_m} X_1 \right)$$

This is a straight line on an $X$–$Y$ diagram, with slope $\frac{L_m}{G_m}$ and passing through the terminal compositions $(X_1, Y_1)$ and $(X_2, Y_2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Always use *molal* flow rates $L_m$ and $G_m$ (kmol inert solvent / inert gas per second), NOT mass-based rates, when using the Kremser or graphical method.

### 2.2.3 Kremser Equation (Analytical Solution)

For constant $\alpha = \frac{L_m}{m G_m}$ and equal molal overflow:

$$N_{OL} = \frac{1}{\ln \alpha} \ln\!\left[ (1 - \alpha)\,\frac{Y_1 - m X_1}{Y_2 - m X_1} + \alpha \right]$$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{\ln \alpha} \ln\!\left[ (1 - \alpha)\,\frac{Y_1 - m X_1}{Y_2 - m X_1} + \alpha \right] \quad \text{when } \alpha \neq 1$$

For $\alpha = 1$ (operating line parallel to equilibrium):

$$N_{OG} = N_{OL} = \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_2 - m X_1}$$

---

## 2.3 Distillation — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Vapour–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)

For an ideal binary mixture, Raoult's law combined with Dalton's law yields:

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\,x_A}$$

where the **relative volatility** is:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$

**KTU Rule of Thumb:** If $\alpha > 1.05$, the McCabe–Thiele method is straightforward; if $\alpha < 1.05$, separation is difficult and may need extractive or azeotropic distillation.

### 2.3.2 q-Line (Feed Condition Line)

The mole fraction of liquid in the feed is $q$. The q-line equation is:

$$y = \frac{q}{q - 1} x - \frac{z_F}{q - 1}$$

| Feed Condition | $q$ Value | Slope of q-line |
|----------------|----------|-----------------|
| Cold liquid (sub-cooled) | $> 1$ | Between 0 and 1 |
| Saturated liquid | $1$ | Vertical (infinite) |
| Two-phase (wet vapour) | $0 < q < 1$ | Negative |
| Saturated vapour | $0$ | Horizontal (zero) |
| Superheated vapour | $< 0$ | Positive |

### 2.3.3 Rectifying and Stripping Section Equations

**Rectifying (above feed):**

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**Stripping (below feed):**

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{L'}{V'} x_m - \frac{L' x_W}{V'}$$

**Operating line intersections:** The rectifying line and q-line intersect at the **feed point**; this point lies on the stripping line.

### 2.3.4 Minimum Reflux Ratio ($R_{min}$)

The q-line intersects the equilibrium curve at the **pinch point**. Drawing a line from $(x_D, x_D)$ to the intersection yields $R_{min}$:

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$$

### 2.3.5 Total Reflux / Minimum Stages

At total reflux ($D = 0$, $V = L$), the operating lines coincide with the diagonal $y = x$. The number of steps drawn between the diagonal and the equilibrium curve gives the **minimum number of stages $N_{min}$**, related to $N_{actual}$ by the **Gilliland correlation** or the simpler **Erbar–Maddox** rule.

---

## 2.4 Evaporation — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 Single-Effect Evaporator

A steady-state mass and energy balance on a single effect gives:

$$\text{Mass balance: } F = V + L$$
$$\text{Solute balance: } F x_F = L x_L$$
$$\text{Energy balance: } F h_F + S \lambda_S = L h_L + V H_V + \text{losses}$$

Neglecting sensible heat effects and losses:

$$V \approx \frac{S \lambda_S}{\lambda_V}$$

**Economy** of a single effect ≈ 0.8 – 0.9 (i.e., 1 kg steam evaporates ~0.85 kg water).

### 2.4.2 Multiple-Effect Evaporator

For an **N-effect** forward-feed evaporator with equal area assumption:

$$A = \frac{1}{U} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{\Delta T_i}$$

The **Duhring rule / Wang & Rautenbach approximation** for split of total temperature drop $\Delta T_{total}$:

$$\Delta T_i = \frac{Q_i / U_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} (Q_j / U_j)} \, \Delta T_{total}$$

**Approximate Economy of N-effect system:** $\text{Economy} \approx 0.85 \times N$ (forward feed, equal areas).

---

## 2.5 Leaching — Theoretical Framework

### 2.5.1 Single-Stage Leaching (Ideal Stage)

For an *ideal* single stage (extract and underflow leave in equilibrium), the operating point $(X, Y)$ lies on the **equilibrium / underflow curve**.

**Mass balances (solute-free solid basis):**

- **Total:** $L_0 + V_0 = L_N + V_N$
- **Solute:** $L_0 y_0 + V_0 x_0 = L_N y_N + V_N x_N$
- **Solvent:** $L_0 (1 - y_0) + V_0 (1 - x_0) = L_N (1 - y_N) + V_N (1 - x_N)$

### 2.5.2 Multistage Counter-Current Leaching

For $N$ stages, the operating line connecting terminal underflow and overflow is:

$$\frac{L_N - V_N}{L_0 - V_0} = \left( \frac{V_{N+1}}{V_1} \right)^N$$

When $V_{N+1} = 0$ (no fresh solvent, i.e., solvent is recirculated with the underflow):

$$\frac{L_N - V_N}{L_0 - V_0} = \left( \frac{V_1}{V_1} \right)^N = 1 \Rightarrow L_N - V_N = L_0 - V_0$$

When $V_{N+1} = V_1$ (theoretically infinite stages) and $L_N = V_N$, the system is at maximum possible separation.

### 2.5.3 Variable Underflow (Constant-Solvent-In-Underflow Assumption)

The underflow curve $y = f(x)$ relates the solute concentration $x$ in the overflow to the solute concentration $y$ in the underflow. The operating line is drawn between the underflow end-points, and stages are stepped off against the equilibrium curve.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Key Formula | Variables / Units | Validity / KTU Note |
|---|-----------|-------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Drying | $N_c = \dfrac{h_T (T_g - T_w)}{\lambda_s}$ | $N_c$ [kg/m²·s] | Constant-rate period only |
| 2 | Drying | $t_c = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c A}$ | $t_c$ [s] | Time in B → C zone |
| 3 | Drying | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s}{N_c A} \!\int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{X - X^*}$ | $t_f$ [s] | Falling-rate period |
| 4 | Drying | $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ | [s] | KTU favourite 14-marker |
| 5 | Absorption | $p_A^* = H x_A$ | $H$ [Pa] | Henry's law (dilute) |
| 6 | Absorption | $\alpha = \dfrac{L_m}{m G_m}$ | dimensionless | Absorption factor |
| 7 | Absorption | $N_{OG} = \dfrac{1}{\ln \alpha} \ln\!\left[ (1-\alpha)\,\dfrac{Y_1 - m X_1}{Y_2 - m X_1} + \alpha \right]$ | $N_{OG}$ [stages] | Kremser equation |
| 8 | Distillation | $\alpha = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$ | dimensionless | VLE constant for ideal systems |
| 9 | Distillation | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha-1)x}$ | mole fraction | Equilibrium curve |
| 10 | Distillation | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | mole fraction | q-line |
| 11 | Distillation | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}$ | dimensionless | Minimum reflux ratio |
| 12 | Distillation | $R = 1.3\,R_{min}$ (typical design) | dimensionless | KTU design heuristic |
| 13 | Evaporation | $V \approx S \,\lambda_S / \lambda_V$ | kg/h | Single effect, no losses |
| 14 | Evaporation | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \Delta T_i}$ | m² | Heat-transfer area |
| 15 | Evaporation | $\text{Economy} = V / S$ | kg/kg | Forward feed: $0.85N$ |
| 16 | Leaching | $L_0 - V_{N+1} = L_N - V_1$ | kg | Constant-underflow (Blackadder's rule) |
| 17 | Leaching | $\dfrac{L_N - V_N}{L_0 - V_0} = \left( \dfrac{V_{N+1}}{V_1} \right)^N$ | kg | Variable underflow (KTU 14-marker) |
| 18 | Leaching | $V_{N+1} = V_1 + L_0 - L_N$ (solute free) | kg | Mass balance shortcut |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Drying — Derivation of Total Drying Time (KTU Favourite)

### 3.1.1 Constant-Rate Period Time ($t_c$)

The drying rate is constant, equal to $N_c$. The mass of water removed per unit area in time $t_c$ equals the mass of dry solid $L_s$ times the change in moisture content $(X_1 - X_c)$:

$$N_c \, A \, t_c = L_s (X_1 - X_c)$$

Solving for $t_c$:

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \, A}$$

> **Logic:** Total water removed = (rate of removal) × (area) × (time). Set the two equal.

### 3.1.2 Falling-Rate Period Time ($t_f$)

During the falling rate, the differential moisture removed in time $dt$ is:

$$-\frac{dX}{dt} = \frac{N}{L_s} = \frac{N_c (X - X^*)}{L_s (X_c - X^*)}$$

This is the **linear falling rate model** (most common in KTU problems). Rearranging:

$$dt = -\frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c} \, \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

Integrating from $X_c$ to $X_2$:

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c} \Big[ \ln(X - X^*) \Big]_{X_2}^{X_c}$$

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c} \ln\!\left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

### 3.1.3 Total Drying Time

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = \frac{L_s}{N_c \, A} \left[ (X_1 - X_c) + (X_c - X^*) \ln\!\left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right) \right]$$

---

## 3.2 Distillation — Derivation of Rectifying Operating Line

A schematic of the top of the column (rectifying section):

```
        D (distillate)
        ↑
  ──────────────────
        |  R = L/D
        ↓
        V_n+1 ──→ y_{n+1}
  L_n ←── x_n
        |  V = L + D
        ↓
```

### Step 1 — Total Mass Balance (control volume from stage $n$ to top)

$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

### Step 2 — Component (more-volatile) Mass Balance

$$V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} = L_n \, x_n + D \, x_D$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $L_n = V_{n+1} - D$

$$V_{n+1} \, y_{n+1} = (V_{n+1} - D) x_n + D x_D$$

### Step 4 — Expand and Collect Terms

$$V_{n+1} y_{n+1} = V_{n+1} x_n - D x_n + D x_D$$

$$V_{n+1} (y_{n+1} - x_n) = D (x_D - x_n)$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Reflux Ratio $R = L_n / D$

Under the **constant molal overflow (CMO)** assumption, $L_n = L = R D$ and $V_{n+1} = V = (R+1) D$. Substituting:

$$(R+1) D (y_{n+1} - x_n) = D (x_D - x_n)$$

$$y_{n+1} - x_n = \frac{x_D - x_n}{R+1}$$

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

This is the **rectifying operating line**, with intercept $\dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ and slope $\dfrac{R}{R+1}$ on the $y$ vs $x$ diagram.

---

## 3.3 Evaporation — Derivation of Equal-Area Distribution of $\Delta T$

For an $N$-effect evaporator with heat transfer area $A$ equal in each effect:

$$A = \frac{Q_1}{U_1 \Delta T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{U_2 \Delta T_2} = \cdots = \frac{Q_N}{U_N \Delta T_N}$$

Total temperature drop:

$$\Delta T_{total} = \Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2 + \cdots + \Delta T_N$$

For a general effect $i$:

$$\Delta T_i = \frac{Q_i / U_i}{A}$$

Summing over all effects:

$$A = \frac{1}{A} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{U_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \sqrt{ \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{Q_i}{U_i} }$$

Wait — for **equal areas**, the area cancels because $A$ is the same for all effects:

$$A = \frac{Q_1}{U_1 \Delta T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{U_2 \Delta T_2}$$

Hence:

$$\Delta T_1 : \Delta T_2 = \frac{Q_1}{U_1} : \frac{Q_2}{U_2}$$

For effects with equal heat duty and $U$ (typical assumption):

$$\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{total}}{N}$$

For non-equal $U$:

$$\Delta T_i = \frac{(Q_i / U_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} (Q_j / U_j)} \, \Delta T_{total}$$

---

## 3.4 Leaching — Derivation of Multistage Counter-Current Operating Equation

Consider $N$ counter-current stages. The terminal streams are:

- **Feed slurry:** $L_0$ (kg/h) containing solvent $V_0$ and inert solid $B$.
- **Fresh solvent:** $V_{N+1}$ (kg/h).
- **Exit extract:** $V_1$ (kg/h).
- **Exit underflow:** $L_N$ (kg/h).

### Step 1 — Total Mass Balance (overall)

$$L_0 + V_{N+1} = L_N + V_1$$

### Step 2 — Inert Solid Balance (constant, since solid passes through unchanged)

$$L_0 \, s_0 = L_N \, s_N = B \quad \text{(constant in each effect, equal to B)}$$

### Step 3 — Solvent-Only Mass Balance (solute-free basis)

Define $V$ = total solvent (extract + entrained solvent in underflow) and $L$ = total liquid in underflow. Then:

$$V_{1} = L_N - V_N \quad \text{(solvent leaving with extract)}$$

For an *ideal counter-current* train with **constant underflow** (i.e., $L$ is the same in every stage) and an operating line drawn between the *terminal points* $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_{N+1}, y_{N+1})$:

$$\frac{L_N - V_N}{L_0 - V_0} = \left( \frac{V_{N+1}}{V_1} \right)^N$$

### Step 4 — Special Case: No Fresh Solvent ($V_{N+1} = 0$)

Setting $V_{N+1} = 0$:

$$\frac{L_N - V_N}{L_0 - V_0} = \left( \frac{0}{V_1} \right)^N = 0$$

Hence $L_N = V_N$, meaning the **exit underflow is at its incipient overflow composition** — a useful design constraint.

### Step 5 — Special Case: Variable Underflow, Single Stage

For a *single* ideal stage, $N = 1$:

$$L_1 - V_1 = (L_0 - V_0) \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

The operating line passes through the points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ on the $X$–$Y$ diagram; the equilibrium point on the underflow curve gives the single-stage composition.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Drying (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A wet solid is dried from initial moisture $X_1 = 0.30$ to final $X_2 = 0.05$ kg water/kg dry solid. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.15$ and the equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.02$. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s. The mass of bone-dry solid is $L_s = 50$ kg and the drying area is $A = 1.2$ m². Find the **total drying time** in hours.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Constant-Rate Period:**

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \, A}$$

$$t_c = \frac{50 \times (0.30 - 0.15)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.2}$$

$$t_c = \frac{50 \times 0.15}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{7.5}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_c = 41666.67 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Falling-Rate Period (linear model):**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \, A} \ln\!\left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{50 \times (0.15 - 0.02)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.2} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.15 - 0.02}{0.05 - 0.02} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{50 \times 0.13}{1.8 \times 10^{-4}} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.13}{0.03} \right)$$

$$t_f = 36111.11 \times \ln(4.3333)$$

$$t_f = 36111.11 \times 1.4663$$

$$t_f = 52949.07 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Total Time:**

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 41666.67 + 52949.07 = 94615.74 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours:**

$$t_{total} = \frac{94615.74}{3600} = 26.28 \text{ h}$$

**Answer:** The total drying time is **26.28 hours**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern Compliance:** Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks) and Part B (1 of 2 × 14 marks, with sub-parts a and b at 7 marks each). Model answers below are exhaustive and follow KTU board examiner's valuation key style.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define: (a) Free moisture, (b) Equilibrium moisture content, (c) Critical moisture content.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Free Moisture:** The moisture content in a wet solid in excess of its equilibrium moisture content under the prevailing drying conditions. It is the moisture that can be *removed* by drying; denoted $X_F = X - X^*$.

(b) **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** The limiting moisture content of a solid when it is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the surrounding air at a given temperature and humidity. No further net drying is possible below this value.

(c) **Critical Moisture Content ($X_c$):** The moisture content of the solid at which the **constant-rate drying period ends** and the **falling-rate period begins**. Below $X_c$, the surface is no longer fully saturated with water.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip (1 mark each):** Examiners expect a *definition* plus *one example or formula* for full marks. Always write $X^* = f(T, H, \text{solid})$ for the equilibrium content to show depth.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**What is relative volatility? Write its significance in distillation column design.**

**Model Answer:**

The **relative volatility** $\alpha_{AB}$ of a binary mixture is the ratio of the vapour pressures of the pure components, and is a measure of the *ease of separation* by distillation:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}} = \frac{K_A}{K_B}$$

**Significance:**

- If $\alpha > 1$, component A is the more volatile (lighter) component and rises to the top as distillate.
- If $\alpha = 1$, no separation is possible by simple distillation (azeotrope).
- If $\alpha > 1.05$, conventional McCabe–Thiele design is straightforward; if $\alpha < 1.05$, special techniques (extractive/azeotropic distillation, vacuum operation) are needed.
- Larger $\alpha \Rightarrow$ fewer theoretical stages required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Drying Problem

#### (a) [7 marks] Derive the expression for the time required to dry a wet solid in the falling-rate period under the linear-falling-rate model. A solid is dried from $X_1 = 0.35$ to $X_2 = 0.08$ kg water/kg dry solid. The critical and equilibrium moisture contents are $X_c = 0.18$ and $X^* = 0.03$ respectively. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s. If the bone-dry mass is $L_s = 100$ kg and drying area is $A = 2.5$ m², calculate the **total drying time** in hours.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Constant-Rate Period (B → C):**

The mass balance gives:

$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \, A} = \frac{100 \times (0.35 - 0.18)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.5}$$

$$t_c = \frac{100 \times 0.17}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{17}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 34000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Falling-Rate Period (C → D), Linear Model:**

$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \, A} \ln\!\left( \frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{100 \times (0.18 - 0.03)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.5} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.18 - 0.03}{0.08 - 0.03} \right)$$

$$t_f = \frac{100 \times 0.15}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} \ln\!\left( \frac{0.15}{0.05} \right) = 30000 \times \ln(3) = 30000 \times 1.0986 = 32958.4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Total Drying Time:**

$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 34000 + 32958.4 = 66958.4 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours:**

$$t_{total} = \frac{66958.4}{3600} = 18.60 \text{ h}$$

**Answer:** The total drying time is **18.6 hours**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mistake:** Students often write $t_c$ in *minutes* and $t_f$ in *hours* by mixing units, or forget to convert the final answer to the units requested in the problem (here, hours). **Always re-check the units of $L_s$, $N_c$, and $A$ and convert $t_{total}$ to the asked unit at the end.** Lose 1 mark for unit slip.

#### (b) [7 marks] A counter-current packed absorption column is to be designed to remove 90% of ammonia from a gas stream using water as solvent. The gas flow rate is 30 kmol/h with inlet $Y_1 = 0.05$ and outlet $Y_2 = 0.005$ (mole ratios of NH₃ to inert air). The equilibrium relation is $Y^* = 0.5 X$. Pure water is used ($X_2 = 0$). Determine the **minimum solvent flow rate** and the **number of transfer units ($N_{OG}$)** if the actual solvent flow is 1.5 times the minimum. Use Kremser equation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Solute-Free Gas Flow:**

$$G_m = \frac{30}{1 + Y_1} \approx 30 \times (1 - 0.05) \approx 28.5 \text{ kmol inert air/h}$$

For dilute systems, $G_m \approx 30$ kmol/h. We use $G_m = 30$ kmol/h (KTU convention for "inert basis").

**Step 2 — Material Balance (operating line passes through $(X_2, Y_2)$ and $(X_1, Y_1)$):**

$$G_m (Y_1 - Y_2) = L_m (X_1 - X_2)$$

$$30 \times (0.05 - 0.005) = L_m (X_1 - 0) \Rightarrow L_m X_1 = 30 \times 0.045 = 1.35$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Solvent (parallel to equilibrium line at top, i.e., operating line tangent to $Y^* = 0.5 X$):**

At minimum $L_m$, the operating line has slope $m = 0.5$ (parallel to equilibrium curve):

$$\left(\frac{L_m}{G_m}\right)_{min} = m = 0.5$$

$$L_{m,min} = 0.5 \times 30 = 15 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Solvent Flow:**

$$L_m = 1.5 \times L_{m,min} = 1.5 \times 15 = 22.5 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Top Liquid Composition:**

$$X_1 = \frac{1.35}{L_m} = \frac{1.35}{22.5} = 0.06$$

**Step 6 — Absorption Factor:**

$$\alpha = \frac{L_m}{m G_m} = \frac{22.5}{0.5 \times 30} = \frac{22.5}{15} = 1.5$$

**Step 7 — Kremser Equation for $N_{OG}$:**

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{\ln \alpha} \ln\!\left[ (1 - \alpha)\,\frac{Y_1 - m X_1}{Y_2 - m X_1} + \alpha \right]$$

Compute the inner fraction:

$$Y_1 - m X_1 = 0.05 - 0.5 \times 0.06 = 0.05 - 0.03 = 0.02$$

$$Y_2 - m X_1 = 0.005 - 0.03 = -0.025$$

$$\frac{Y_1 - m X_1}{Y_2 - m X_1} = \frac{0.02}{-0.025} = -0.8$$

$$(1 - \alpha) \times (-0.8) + \alpha = (1 - 1.5) \times (-0.8) + 1.5 = (-0.5) \times (-0.8) + 1.5 = 0.4 + 1.5 = 1.9$$

$$N_{OG} = \frac{1}{\ln 1.5} \ln(1.9) = \frac{0.6419}{0.4055} = 1.583$$

**Answer:** $L_{m,min} = 15$ kmol/h, $N_{OG} \approx 1.58$ transfer units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Two-Stream Direction:** In *counter-current* absorption, the *gas enters at the bottom with $Y_1$* and *liquid enters at the top with $X_2$*. Always label the gas-inlet end as point 1 and the liquid-inlet end as point 2, *or* the reverse — and **stay consistent** with the operating-line equation you use. The classic slip is mixing the indices, leading to a wrong operating-line slope. (Lose 2 marks.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Distillation + Evaporation

#### (a) [7 marks] A continuous fractionating column is to be designed to separate a binary mixture of benzene and toluene. The feed contains 40 mol% benzene, the distillate is 95 mol% benzene, and the bottoms is 5 mol% benzene. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$. The feed is a saturated liquid ($q = 1$). Using the McCabe–Thiele method, determine the **minimum reflux ratio $R_{min}$** and the **number of theoretical stages at $R = 1.5 R_{min}$** (use the Gilliland correlation or graphical method).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Find q-line Equation (saturated liquid, $q = 1$):**

For $q = 1$, the q-line is vertical: $x = z_F = 0.40$.

**Step 2 — Find Pinch Point on Equilibrium Curve:**

The equilibrium curve is given by:

$$y = \frac{2.47\,x}{1 + 1.47\,x}$$

At the pinch point, $x = 0.40$ (intersection of q-line and equilibrium curve):

$$y_q = \frac{2.47 \times 0.40}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.40} = \frac{0.988}{1 + 0.588} = \frac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6222$$

**Step 3 — Compute Minimum Reflux Ratio:**

$$R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} = \frac{0.95 - 0.6222}{0.6222 - 0.40} = \frac{0.3278}{0.2222} = 1.475$$

**Step 4 — Actual Reflux Ratio:**

$$R = 1.5 \times R_{min} = 1.5 \times 1.475 = 2.213$$

**Step 5 — Number of Stages — Gilliland Correlation:**

The Gilliland correlation uses the parameter:

$$X = \frac{R - R_{min}}{R + 1} = \frac{2.213 - 1.475}{2.213 + 1} = \frac{0.738}{3.213} = 0.2297$$

The corresponding $Y$ parameter from the **Molokanov equation** (KTU preferred closed form):

$$Y = 1 - \exp\!\left[ \frac{(1 + 54.4 X)(X - 1)}{(11 + 117.2 X)\sqrt{X}} \right]$$

Substituting $X = 0.2297$:

$$X - 1 = -0.7703, \quad \sqrt{X} = 0.4793$$

Numerator: $(1 + 54.4 \times 0.2297) \times (-0.7703) = (1 + 12.4957) \times (-0.7703) = 13.4957 \times (-0.7703) = -10.397$

Denominator: $0.4793 \times (11 + 117.2 \times 0.2297) = 0.4793 \times (11 + 26.9208) = 0.4793 \times 37.9208 = 18.176$

Exponent: $-10.397 / 18.176 = -0.5720$

$$Y = 1 - e^{-0.5720} = 1 - 0.5641 = 0.4359$$

**Step 6 — Number of Stages:**

$$N = \frac{N_{min}}{Y} \quad \text{where } N_{min} \text{ is from total-reflux stepping}$$

For total reflux, we count steps from $x = x_W = 0.05$ to $x = x_D = 0.95$. Using Fenske's equation as a check:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[ \left( \frac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \frac{1 - x_W}{x_W} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[ \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \left( \frac{0.95}{0.05} \right) \right]}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln 2.47} = \frac{\ln(361)}{0.9046} = \frac{5.889}{0.9046} = 6.51 \approx 6.5$$

**Step 7 — Actual Number of Stages:**

$$N = \frac{N_{min}}{Y} = \frac{6.51}{0.4359} = 14.93 \approx 15 \text{ theoretical stages}$$

Including reboiler (as an extra stage), $N_{theoretical, total} = 16$ (KTU convention).

**Step 8 — Feed Plate Location (Kirkhoff's fraction):**

$$\frac{N_{rectifying}}{N_{stripping}} \approx \left( \frac{N}{N_{min}} \right)^{0.566} \quad \text{(approximate)}$$

Or by stepping off the McCabe–Thiele diagram, the feed plate is identified where the operating line switches from rectifying to stripping — the stage that lies *closest* to the feed point intersection.

**Answer:** $R_{min} = 1.475$, $R = 2.213$, $N_{theoretical} \approx 15$–16 stages.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Total-Reflux Minimum Stages:** The Gilliland correlation requires $N_{min}$, NOT $N_{min} - 1$ (reboiler already excluded). Many students subtract 1 for the reboiler in the $N_{min}$ count, which then gets "re-added" wrongly. Lose 1 mark for the $N_{min}$ count.

#### (b) [7 marks] A **triple-effect forward-feed evaporator** concentrates a weak liquor from 10% to 50% solids by mass. The feed rate is 10,000 kg/h at 30°C. Steam is supplied at 120°C (latent heat 2200 kJ/kg) and the last effect operates at 50°C. Assume equal heat-transfer coefficients $U_1 = U_2 = U_3 = 2500$ W/m²·K, equal heat duties per effect, and no boiling-point elevation. Calculate the **steam economy, capacity, and the heating area per effect**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Water Removed:**

$$F x_F = L x_L \Rightarrow L = \frac{10{,}000 \times 0.10}{0.50} = 2000 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$V = F - L = 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000 \text{ kg water/h}$$

**Step 2 — Steam Economy (neglecting sensible heat and BPE, with equal areas):**

For a triple-effect forward feed, typical economy $\approx 2.5$ (rule of thumb: $0.85N$).

A more accurate value is obtained from the energy balance. For each effect, the heat-transfer rate is:

$$Q_i = U_i A_i \Delta T_i$$

Since $U$ and $A$ are equal and we have equal heat duties:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = Q_3 = \frac{Q_{total}}{3}$$

Total heat supplied by live steam: $Q_1 = S \lambda_S$ (assume sensible heat of feed and BPE are negligible).

Approximating the *latent heat of evaporation* as constant (since $\lambda$ varies only ~3% over 50–100°C), we can take $\lambda_V \approx 2300$ kJ/kg.

For each effect, water evaporated per effect: $V_i = Q_i / \lambda_V$.

Total $V = 3 V_i = 3 Q_i / \lambda_V \Rightarrow Q_i = V \lambda_V / 3$.

**Step 3 — Steam Rate:**

$$S = \frac{Q_1}{\lambda_S} = \frac{V \lambda_V}{3 \lambda_S} = \frac{8000 \times 2300}{3 \times 2200} = \frac{18{,}400{,}000}{6600} = 2787.9 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Steam Economy:**

$$\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{8000}{2787.9} = 2.87$$

**Step 5 — Total Temperature Drop:**

$$\Delta T_{total} = 120 - 50 = 70 \text{ K}$$

(We assume BPE = 0; effect 1 at 120 °C → effect 3 at 50 °C.)

Since $U_i$ and $A_i$ are equal, $\Delta T_i$ are equal:

$$\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = \Delta T_3 = \frac{70}{3} = 23.33 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Heat Duty per Effect:**

$$Q_i = \frac{V \lambda_V}{3} = \frac{8000 \times 2300}{3 \times 3600} = 1703.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Area per Effect:**

$$A_i = \frac{Q_i}{U_i \Delta T_i} = \frac{1703.7 \times 1000}{2500 \times 23.33}$$

$$A_i = \frac{1{,}703{,}700}{58{,}333.33} = 29.21 \text{ m}^2$$

**Answers:**
- **Capacity** = 8000 kg water/h
- **Steam economy** ≈ 2.87
- **Area per effect** ≈ 29.2 m²
- **Steam rate** ≈ 2788 kg/h

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Temperature Drop Distribution:** The most common error is computing $\Delta T_i$ as $70/3 = 23.33$ K *without* accounting for **boiling-point elevation (BPE)** and **hydrostatic head**, which the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly asks to be considered. If BPE is non-zero, the *available* $\Delta T$ is reduced and must be subtracted from $70$ K. Lose 2 marks for ignoring BPE in problems where it is given.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Universal Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Universal Pitfalls Across Module 3 (Where KTU Students Lose Marks):**
> 
> 1. **Units Inconsistency:** The biggest single source of lost marks in this module. Always state units explicitly at *every* numerical step. $X$ is dimensionless (kg water/kg dry solid); $L_s$ is kg; $N_c$ is kg/m²·s; $A$ is m²; hence $t$ comes out in seconds. Convert at the end.
> 
> 2. **Mole Fraction vs. Mole Ratio (Absorption & Distillation):** Use $X, Y$ in **mole ratio** (solute/solvent or solute/inert gas) for the operating line, and $x, y$ in **mole fraction** for the equilibrium curve (Raoult's law). Switching the convention inside one problem is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 
> 3. **Direction of Streams (Counter-Current vs. Co-Current):** For counter-current operations (absorption, leaching, multi-effect evaporators with backward feed), the terminal stream labels ($L_0, V_{N+1}, L_N, V_1$) must be assigned *consistently* with the chosen operating line. Reverse the labels = reverse the slope = wrong answer.
> 
> 4. **Linear vs. Diffusion Falling-Rate (Drying):** The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes the **linear-falling-rate model** (most common) from the **diffusion-controlled falling-rate model** ($N = -k_d (X - X^*)$ in differential form). Read the problem carefully — which model is being assumed?
> 
> 5. **Neglecting BPE in Evaporation:** In real multi-effect calculations, the **boiling-point elevation** reduces the available $\Delta T$ and shifts the temperature distribution. KTU examiners frequently include a small BPE (2–5 K) to test whether you adjust $\Delta T_{total}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔹 Drying — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] Drying is governed by both **heat transfer** (from gas to solid) and **mass transfer** (moisture from solid to gas).
- [ ] The drying curve has **two main zones**: **constant rate** (surface-saturated, external-controlled) and **falling rate** (dry patches appear, internal-controlled).
- [ ] Critical moisture content $X_c$ marks the transition between the two zones.
- [ ] Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ is the lower limit of drying; bound moisture lies below $X^*$.
- [ ] Constant-rate flux: $N_c = h_T (T_g - T_w) / \lambda_s = k_y (H_w - H_g)$.
- [ ] Falling-rate time (linear model): $t_f = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c A} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*} \right)$.
- [ ] Total drying time: $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$.

### 🔹 Absorption — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] Henry's law: $p_A^* = H x_A$ (for dilute physical absorption).
- [ ] Operating line slope = $L_m / G_m$ on a $X$–$Y$ plot (mole ratios).
- [ ] Equilibrium curve slope = $m = H/P$ on a $X$–$Y$ plot.
- [ ] **Absorption factor** $\alpha = L_m / (m G_m)$.
- [ ] If $\alpha = 1$: $N_{OG} = (Y_1 - Y_2)/(Y_2 - m X_1)$.
- [ ] If $\alpha \neq 1$: use the general Kremser equation.
- [ ] Counter-current operation is the standard for maximum driving force.

### 🔹 Distillation — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Raoult's law** for ideal binary: $y_A = \alpha x_A / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A]$.
- [ ] Relative volatility $\alpha = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$.
- [ ] **q-line** depends on feed condition: vertical for $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), slope $q/(q-1)$ otherwise.
- [ ] **Rectifying line** slope = $R/(R+1)$, intercept = $x_D/(R+1)$.
- [ ] **$R_{min}$** from pinch point: $R_{min} = (x_D - y_q)/(y_q - x_q)$.
- [ ] **Minimum stages** by Fenske's equation (or stepping at total reflux).
- [ ] **Actual stages** by Gilliland correlation (KTU favourite).
- [ ] Feed plate: stage closest to the intersection of q-line and rectifying line.

### 🔹 Evaporation — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Capacity** = total water evaporated per hour (kg/h).
- [ ] **Economy** = kg water evaporated per kg live steam fed.
- [ ] Single-effect economy ≈ 0.85; N-effect economy ≈ 0.85 N (approximate).
- [ ] **Forward feed**: feed and steam flow in the same direction; rising viscosity; cheap.
- [ ] **Backward feed**: feed enters the coolest effect, exits the hottest; handles viscous/heat-sensitive liquors.
- [ ] **Parallel feed**: feed enters every effect separately; useful for crystallising solutions.
- [ ] $\Delta T_i = \dfrac{Q_i / U_i}{\sum_j (Q_j / U_j)} \Delta T_{total}$; equal areas imply $\Delta T_i \propto 1/U_i$.
- [ ] Always account for **boiling-point elevation (BPE)** in industrial problems.

### 🔹 Leaching — Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Extract** = solvent + dissolved solute (overflow, clear liquid).
- [ ] **Raffinate** = spent solid + entrained solvent (underflow, slurry).
- [ ] **Underflow curve** relates $y$ (solute/solvent in underflow) to $x$ (solute/solvent in overflow).
- [ ] **Constant underflow** assumption: $L$ (kg solvent/kg inert solid) is fixed in every stage.
- [ ] **Operating-line equation** (variable underflow): $\dfrac{L_N - V_N}{L_0 - V_0} = \left( \dfrac{V_{N+1}}{V_1} \right)^N$.
- [ ] **Blackadder's rule** (constant underflow): $L_0 - V_{N+1} = L_N - V_1$.
- [ ] Stages are stepped between the operating line and the underflow curve.
- [ ] Industrial examples: oil from seeds (hexane), sugar from beets (water), copper from ore (acid).

### 🔹 Master "Big-Picture" Insight

> [!TIP]
> **Module 3 Unifying Theme:** All five operations obey the same **Two-Film / Mass-Transfer Driving Force Paradigm**:
> 
> $$\text{Rate} = (\text{Mass Transfer Coefficient}) \times (\text{Driving Force}) \times (\text{Area})$$
> 
> The differences are:
> - **Phases involved** (gas–solid, gas–liquid, liquid–vapour, liquid–solid).
> - **Nature of driving force** (humidity, partial pressure, mole fraction, concentration).
> - **Equilibrium relationship** (linear for drying/air–water, curved for VLE, etc.).
> 
> Master the McCabe–Thiele graphical stepping and the Kremser analytical analogue — they appear in *every* KTU question on this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying: Equilibrium Moisture Content, Bound and Unbound Moisture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drying: Equilibrium Moisture Content, Bound and Unbound Moisture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Drying

**Drying** is the process of thermally removing a volatile liquid (almost always water) from a wet solid, paste, or solution to yield a solid product. It is a simultaneous **heat and mass transfer operation** in which:

- **Heat** flows from the surrounding hot gas (air) to the wet solid.
- **Mass (moisture)** flows from inside the solid to the gas stream as vapor.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, drying is treated under *Module 3 – Mass Transfer Operations* and it serves as the bridge between psychrometrics (Module 2) and advanced separations such as crystallization and membrane separations (Module 4).

## 1.2 Moisture Content: The Central Quantity

The behaviour of a drying material is governed entirely by **how water is held inside the solid**. Engineers classify water inside a wet solid using the following strict, hierarchical definitions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moisture Content (M.C.)** is the ratio of the mass of moisture present in a solid to the mass of dry solid (bone-dry basis) or the mass of wet solid (wet basis).

**Dry Basis Moisture Content:**

$$X = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{dry solid}}} \;\; \left[\dfrac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg dry solid}}\right]$$

**Wet Basis Moisture Content:**

$$X_{w} = \frac{m_{\text{water}}}{m_{\text{water}} + m_{\text{dry solid}}} \;\; \left[\dfrac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg wet solid}}\right]$$

**Conversion relation (always tested in KTU):**

$$X = \frac{X_{w}}{1 - X_{w}} \quad \text{and} \quad X_{w} = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU convention: All drying calculations use the **dry basis (X)** unless otherwise stated. Wet basis is restricted to material-balance accounting.

## 1.3 Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) — The KTU Favourite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC), $X^{*}$ or $X_{e}$:** The moisture content a material will ultimately attain when it is left in contact with a gas of fixed temperature and humidity for an *infinite* time. At this point, the **partial pressure of water vapour at the solid surface equals the partial pressure of water vapour in the bulk gas**, so the net driving force for mass transfer becomes zero.

In simpler words, EMC is the **driest a material can ever get** when sitting in air of given temperature and relative humidity. You cannot dry a material below its EMC with that particular air — no matter how long you wait.

### 🌞 Real-World Analogy

Imagine you leave a wet cotton T-shirt on a clothesline on a humid Kerala monsoon day ($\phi = 90\%$). It will feel "air-dry" after some time but will still feel slightly damp. That residual dampness is the **Equilibrium Moisture Content** of cotton at 90% RH. The same shirt, hung on a dry January afternoon ($\phi = 40\%$), feels bone-dry — the EMC is much lower. The *actual* "driest possible" moisture is dictated by the air, not the dryer.

## 1.4 Bound Moisture and Unbound Moisture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bound Moisture** is the moisture held by the solid so tightly (by chemical/physical forces like adsorption in capillaries, cell walls, or by solution forces) that it exerts a vapour pressure **less than** the vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature. Hence, it **cannot be removed easily** by ordinary drying.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unbound Moisture** is the moisture held by the solid in such a way that it exerts a vapour pressure **equal to** the vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature. It is essentially "free" water held in voids/pores and is **relatively easy to remove**.

### 🧊 Real-World Analogy

Think of a kitchen sponge:
- The water dripping off when you lift it is **unbound** (it behaves exactly like free water).
- The water trapped inside the cells, which you have to squeeze or evaporate thermally, is **bound** moisture.

A sliced green chilli behaves similarly: surface water is unbound; water locked inside the cellular matrix is bound.

## 1.5 Free Moisture Content (FMC) — The Drying Driver

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Moisture Content (FMC):** $F = X - X^{*}$. This is the moisture *in excess* of the equilibrium value. **Only the free moisture can actually be removed** in a given drying operation. Bound moisture includes the equilibrium moisture plus any additional tightly-held water.

So the moisture universe of a wet solid is:

$$X_{\text{total}} = \underbrace{(X - X^{*})}_{\text{Free moisture (removable)}} + \underbrace{X^{*}}_{\text{Equilibrium moisture (NOT removable)}}$$

and

$$\text{Free Moisture} = \text{Unbound Moisture (often)} + \text{part of Bound Moisture}$$

## 1.6 Why This Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

| Concept | Driving Force? | Removable in Drying? | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unbound Moisture** | Yes (high) | Yes (easy) | Very High |
| **Free Moisture** | Yes | Yes (depends on air) | Very High |
| **Bound Moisture** | Reduced (low $p_{A,\text{surface}}$) | Difficult | High |
| **Equilibrium Moisture** | Zero | **No — never removed** | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sorption Isotherm — The Fingerprint of Drying

> [!NOTE]
> The **sorption isotherm** is the experimentally measured plot of **Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^{*}$)** versus **Relative Humidity ($\phi$)** of the surrounding air, at a fixed temperature.

It is the single most important curve in drying because it simultaneously reveals:
- The **EMC** at any given air humidity.
- The boundary between **bound** and **unbound** moisture.
- The boundary between **free** and **equilibrium** moisture.

### Reading the Sorption Isotherm

For a typical hygroscopic material (paper, textile, food, wood):

- **At $\phi = 0$ (perfectly dry air):** $X^{*} = 0$ for non-porous materials; small finite value for highly porous ones.
- **At $\phi = 1$ (saturated air):** $X^{*}$ reaches the **maximum** for that temperature.
- The curve is **non-linear**, sigmoidal (S-shaped), and characteristic of the material.

### Key Transitions on the Curve

| Region of Curve | Moisture Type Present | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Below the **kink/inflection point** | **Unbound moisture** | Vapour pressure of water = that of pure water |
| Above the kink point | **Bound moisture** | Vapour pressure < pure water (held by physical/chemical forces) |
| Value of $X^{*}$ at given $\phi$ | **Equilibrium moisture** | Non-removable in that air |
| Anything above $X^{*}$ at given $\phi$ | **Free moisture** | Removable |

## 2.2 Bound vs. Unbound — The Vapour Pressure Criterion (KTU Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical definition (the KTU-favoured one):**
> A given moisture content $X$ is **bound** if its equilibrium vapour pressure $p_{A,\text{surface}} < p_{A}^{sat}(T)$, where $p_{A}^{sat}(T)$ is the saturation pressure of water at the same temperature.
> If $p_{A,\text{surface}} = p_{A}^{sat}(T)$, the moisture is **unbound**.

This is the **only** definition accepted in KTU board-marked scripts because it is thermodynamically rigorous.

## 2.3 Hysteresis in Sorption Isotherms

> [!WARNING]
> **Hysteresis:** The desorption (drying) isotherm and the adsorption (wetting) isotherm of the same material **do not coincide** for many porous / capillary materials. The desorption curve sits *above* the adsorption curve, meaning $X^{*}_{\text{desorption}} > X^{*}_{\text{adsorption}}$ at the same $\phi$.

**Physical reasons for hysteresis:**
1. **Capillary condensation:** Liquid condensed in narrow pores cannot easily re-evaporate at the same $\phi$ at which it condensed (ink-bottle effect).
2. **Swelling of the solid matrix** during wetting.
3. **Irreversible binding** of water to hydrophilic sites.

**KTU implication:** The reported EMC depends on whether the material reached that state by *drying* or by *wetting*. Always specify "drying EMC" or "wetting EMC" in lab work.

## 2.4 Factors Affecting EMC

| Factor | Effect on EMC |
|---|---|
| ↑ Relative humidity $\phi$ | ↑ EMC (more moisture retained) |
| ↑ Temperature $T$ (at fixed $\phi$) | ↓ EMC (vapour pressure of water rises faster than internal binding) |
| Material nature (hygroscopic vs non-hygroscopic) | Hygroscopic materials (leather, paper) have high EMC; non-hygroscopic (sand, ceramics) have near-zero EMC |
| Pretreatment (drying vs wetting path) | Desorption EMC > Adsorption EMC (hysteresis) |

## 2.5 Critical Moisture Content ($X_{c}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Moisture Content ($X_{c}$):** The moisture content at which the **constant-rate drying period (CRDP) ends** and the **falling-rate drying period (FRDP) begins.** Below $X_{c}$, the surface can no longer be kept fully wet by internal moisture migration, and the drying rate begins to fall.

- $X_{c}$ is **not** a property of the air — it is a property of the **solid** and the **drying geometry** (thickness, surface area).
- $X_{c}$ is typically *higher* than the EMC of the material.

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{m_{w}}{m_{s}}$ | Dry-basis moisture content | $\text{kg water} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}$ dry solid |
| 2 | $X_{w} = \dfrac{m_{w}}{m_{w} + m_{s}}$ | Wet-basis moisture content | $\text{kg water} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}$ wet solid |
| 3 | $X = \dfrac{X_{w}}{1 - X_{w}}$ | Conversion wet → dry basis | — |
| 4 | $F = X - X^{*}$ | Free moisture content | $\text{kg water} \cdot \text{kg}^{-1}$ dry solid |
| 5 | $\text{Unbound} \Rightarrow p_{A,\text{surface}} = p_{A}^{sat}(T)$ | Vapour pressure criterion (unbound) | kPa |
| 6 | $\text{Bound} \Rightarrow p_{A,\text{surface}} < p_{A}^{sat}(T)$ | Vapour pressure criterion (bound) | kPa |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{p_{A,\text{bulk}}}{p_{A}^{sat}(T)}$ | Definition of relative humidity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $X^{*}(T, \phi)$ | EMC from sorption isotherm | $\text{kg/kg}$ |
| 9 | $N_{A} = k_{y}(Y_{s} - Y)$ | Mass transfer flux (drying) | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $r = \dfrac{-\text{d}X / \text{d}t}{A}$ | Drying rate definition | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{hr}^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $m_{s} = \dfrac{m_{1}}{1 + X_{1}} = \dfrac{m_{2}}{1 + X_{2}}$ | Conservation of dry solid | kg |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** EMC dictates the moisture content of the final tablet; too high → caking, too low → brittleness.
- **Food industry:** EMC determines the *water activity* ($a_{w}$) and hence microbial safety of dried milk, spices, etc.
- **Paper/textile industry:** EMC controls dimensional stability and stiffness.
- **Cement and ceramics:** EMC must be lowered below the safe storage value before bagging.

# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Wet Basis and Dry Basis Moisture Content

**Starting point:**
By definition, let:
- $m_{w}$ = mass of water in the wet solid [kg]
- $m_{s}$ = mass of truly dry (bone-dry) solid [kg]
- $m_{t}$ = total mass of wet solid [kg] = $m_{w} + m_{s}$

**Step 1 — Write the definitions explicitly.**

$$X_{w} = \frac{m_{w}}{m_{t}} = \frac{m_{w}}{m_{w} + m_{s}}$$

$$X = \frac{m_{w}}{m_{s}}$$

**Step 2 — Express $m_{w}$ in terms of $X$.**

From the second definition: $m_{w} = X \cdot m_{s}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the wet-basis expression.**

$$X_{w} = \frac{X \cdot m_{s}}{X \cdot m_{s} + m_{s}} = \frac{X \cdot m_{s}}{m_{s}(X + 1)} = \frac{X}{X + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X$.**

Cross-multiplying: $X_{w}(X + 1) = X$

$$X_{w} \cdot X + X_{w} = X$$

$$X_{w} = X - X_{w} \cdot X = X(1 - X_{w})$$

$$X = \frac{X_{w}}{1 - X_{w}}$$

**Final boxed form:**

$$\boxed{\,X = \frac{X_{w}}{1 - X_{w}}, \qquad X_{w} = \frac{X}{1 + X}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This conversion is *routinely* asked in KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions. Memorize it cold.

## 3.2 Derivation: Expression for Free Moisture Content

**Step 1 — Recall definitions.**

$$F = X - X^{*} \quad [\text{Free moisture content}]$$
$$B = X - X_{u} \quad [\text{Bound moisture content}]$$
$$U = X_{u} \quad [\text{Unbound moisture content}]$$

where $X_{u}$ is the moisture content at the kink/inflection point of the sorption isotherm (the boundary between bound and unbound).

**Step 2 — Note that $X^{*}$ is part of the bound moisture.** This is a *key* KTU point: even at $\phi < 1$, some bound moisture is held, and it equals the EMC.

**Step 3 — Combine to show the total moisture partition.**

$$X = \underbrace{(X - X^{*})}_{\text{Free}} + \underbrace{X^{*}}_{\text{Equilibrium (subset of bound)}}$$

and equivalently

$$X = \underbrace{(X - X_{u})}_{\text{Bound}} + \underbrace{X_{u}}_{\text{Unbound}}$$

These are the two complementary ways of slicing moisture in a wet solid.

## 3.3 Derivation: Drying Rate and Time for Constant-Rate Period

> [!NOTE]
> Although the topic statement focuses on moisture types, KTU almost always pairs it with the constant-rate drying equation.

**Step 1 — Define drying rate.**

$$r = \frac{-\text{d}W / \text{d}t}{A} = \frac{m_{s}\,(-\text{d}X/\text{d}t)}{A}$$

where $W = m_{s} \cdot X$ is total water on the solid.

**Step 2 — In CRDP, $r$ = constant = $R_{c}$.**

Integrating from $X_{1}$ (initial) to $X_{2} = X_{c}$ (critical):

$$\int_{0}^{t_{c}} R_{c} \, \text{d}t = \int_{X_{1}}^{X_{c}} \frac{m_{s}(-\text{d}X)}{A}$$

$$R_{c} \cdot t_{c} = \frac{m_{s}}{A}(X_{1} - X_{c})$$

**Step 3 — Solve for time.**

$$\boxed{\,t_{c} = \frac{m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{c})}{A \cdot R_{c}}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> The rate $R_{c}$ can be obtained from the **mass-transfer analogy**: $R_{c} = k_{y}(Y_{s} - Y_{\infty})\,M_{A}$ or, in heat-transfer form, $R_{c} = \dfrac{h(T - T_{w})}{h_{fg}}$ where $T_{w}$ is the wet-bulb temperature of the air.

## 3.4 Solved Analytical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Q (Module 3 typical):** A wet solid weighing 250 kg initially contains 30% moisture on a wet basis. The solid is dried to a final moisture of 8% wet basis. The EMC of the solid in the drying air is 4% dry basis. Calculate:
> (a) Initial and final moisture content on a dry basis.
> (b) Mass of water removed.
> (c) Free moisture at the start and at the end of drying.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Convert wet basis to dry basis.

$$X_{1} = \frac{X_{w,1}}{1 - X_{w,1}} = \frac{0.30}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \;\frac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg dry solid}}$$

$$X_{2} = \frac{X_{w,2}}{1 - X_{w,2}} = \frac{0.08}{1 - 0.08} = \frac{0.08}{0.92} = 0.0870 \;\frac{\text{kg water}}{\text{kg dry solid}}$$

**Part (b): Mass of bone-dry solid** (conserved throughout).

$$m_{s} = m_{1}(1 - X_{w,1}) = 250(1 - 0.30) = 250 \times 0.70 = 175 \;\text{kg dry solid}$$

Mass of water removed:

$$m_{\text{water, removed}} = m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{2}) = 175(0.4286 - 0.0870)$$

$$= 175 \times 0.3416 = 59.78 \;\text{kg water}$$

**Part (c): Free moisture content.**

Given $X^{*} = 0.04 \,\text{kg/kg}$ dry basis.

$$F_{1} = X_{1} - X^{*} = 0.4286 - 0.04 = 0.3886$$

$$F_{2} = X_{2} - X^{*} = 0.0870 - 0.04 = 0.0470$$

**Interpretation:** At the end, $0.047 \,\text{kg}$ of water per kg of dry solid is still *theoretically removable*, but only if we change the air to lower humidity. With the *current* air, drying stops here because $F_{2} \to 0$ would require $X = X^{*} = 0.04$.

> [!TIP]
> This is the type of multi-part problem that routinely appears in KTU Part B 14-mark questions. Always show the dry-solid mass calculation explicitly — it is the anchor for the rest of the problem.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: How to Identify Moisture Type from Given Data

| Given | Asked | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Wet-basis $X_{w}$ | Dry-basis $X$ | Apply $X = \dfrac{X_{w}}{1 - X_{w}}$ |
| Initial mass $m_{1}$ and $X_{w,1}$ | Dry solid $m_{s}$ | $m_{s} = m_{1}(1 - X_{w,1})$ |
| Two wet-basis moistures | Water removed | $\Delta m_{w} = m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{2})$ |
| $X$ and $X^{*}$ | Free moisture | $F = X - X^{*}$ |
| Sorption isotherm (graph) | Bound vs unbound at given $\phi$ | Read off the kink point $X_{u}$ |
| Air at $\phi$, $T$ | Type of moisture held by solid | Cross-check $X$ against isotherm |

# 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the following terms with one example each: (a) Equilibrium moisture content, (b) Bound moisture, (c) Unbound moisture.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC):** The moisture content a material attains when it is in prolonged contact with air of a given temperature and relative humidity, such that the partial pressure of water vapour at the material surface equals that in the bulk air. *Example:* A piece of paper kept in a closed room at 25 °C and 60% RH will reach an EMC of about 0.07 kg water/kg dry paper.

**(b) Bound Moisture:** The moisture in a solid that exerts a vapour pressure less than that of pure water at the same temperature, indicating it is held by physical/chemical forces within the solid. *Example:* Water absorbed in the cell walls of dry wood at low humidity.

**(c) Unbound Moisture:** The moisture that exerts a vapour pressure equal to that of pure water at the same temperature, indicating it is held loosely in pores and voids. *Example:* Water held in the open pores of a wet sand bed above the EMC of sand.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**A wet solid is dried from 50% to 10% moisture on a wet basis. Calculate the moisture removed per kg of dry solid.**

**Model Answer:**

Wet basis to dry basis:

$$X_{1} = \frac{0.50}{1 - 0.50} = 1.00 \;\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

$$X_{2} = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} = 0.1111 \;\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

Moisture removed per kg of dry solid:

$$\Delta X = X_{1} - X_{2} = 1.00 - 0.1111 = 0.8889 \;\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta X = 0.889 \;\text{kg water per kg dry solid}$.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B convention: Two alternatives with internal choice. Both options A and B are provided below; in the exam, only one is offered. Either can be the question paper alternative.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / similar pattern]`

**(a)** Define and distinguish between **bound moisture**, **unbound moisture**, **free moisture**, and **equilibrium moisture content** for a wet solid being dried. Use a labelled sorption isotherm to illustrate. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pharmaceutical powder is dried in a batch tray dryer. The initial wet mass is 800 kg with a moisture content of 0.40 kg water/kg dry solid. The drying proceeds in two stages: in the **constant-rate period** the drying rate is $R_{c} = 2.5 \,\text{kg m}^{-2}\text{hr}^{-1}$ until the critical moisture content $X_{c} = 0.18 \,\text{kg/kg}$. The **tray area is $A = 4 \,\text{m}^{2}$**. The EMC of the powder in the drying air is $X^{*} = 0.04 \,\text{kg/kg}$. Find:
(i) the mass of bone-dry solid, (ii) the time required to reach the critical moisture, and (iii) the mass of water that still remains *free* in the solid at $X = X_{c}$. (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Sorption Isotherm

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Bound Moisture** | Moisture whose vapour pressure at the solid surface is *less than* the vapour pressure of pure water at the same temperature. Held by adsorption, capillary, or solution forces. Cannot be removed easily. |
| **Unbound Moisture** | Moisture whose vapour pressure equals that of pure water at the same temperature. Held loosely in pores. Behaves like free water. |
| **Free Moisture** | The amount of moisture *above* the EMC at the prevailing air state: $F = X - X^{*}$. This is the moisture that is *driving* the drying process. |
| **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC)** | The moisture content at which the solid is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the air — no net mass transfer. Non-removable in that air. |

**Sorption Isotherm Sketch (description for answer script):**

- X-axis: Relative humidity $\phi$ (0 → 1).
- Y-axis: Equilibrium moisture $X^{*}$.
- The curve is sigmoidal.
- Below the inflection point (low $\phi$): the moisture is **bound**.
- Above the inflection point: the moisture behaves as **unbound**.
- At any $\phi$, the $X^{*}$ value defines the **EMC**, and the difference $X - X^{*}$ is the **free moisture**.

**Mark-splitting tip:**
- Definitions: 4 marks.
- Distinguishing relationships ($F = X - X^{*}$, etc.): 1 mark.
- Isotherm diagram with labels: 2 marks.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $m_{1} = 800 \,\text{kg}$ (initial wet mass)
- $X_{1} = 0.40 \,\text{kg/kg}$
- $R_{c} = 2.5 \,\text{kg m}^{-2}\text{hr}^{-1}$
- $X_{c} = 0.18 \,\text{kg/kg}$
- $A = 4 \,\text{m}^{2}$
- $X^{*} = 0.04 \,\text{kg/kg}$

**(i) Mass of dry solid:**

$$m_{s} = \frac{m_{1}}{1 + X_{1}} = \frac{800}{1 + 0.40} = \frac{800}{1.40} = 571.43 \;\text{kg dry solid}$$

**(ii) Time to reach critical moisture:**

Using the CRDP time formula:

$$t_{c} = \frac{m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{c})}{A \cdot R_{c}} = \frac{571.43 \times (0.40 - 0.18)}{4 \times 2.5}$$

$$= \frac{571.43 \times 0.22}{10} = \frac{125.71}{10} = 12.57 \;\text{hours}$$

**(iii) Free moisture remaining at $X = X_{c}$:**

$$F_{c} = X_{c} - X^{*} = 0.18 - 0.04 = 0.14 \;\text{kg/kg}$$

Total free water still present in the solid at $X_{c}$:

$$m_{F} = m_{s} \cdot F_{c} = 571.43 \times 0.14 = 80.0 \;\text{kg water}$$

**Final answers:** (i) $m_{s} = 571.4 \;\text{kg}$ (ii) $t_{c} = 12.57 \;\text{h}$ (iii) $F_{c} = 0.14 \,\text{kg/kg}$, i.e. $80 \,\text{kg}$ of free water.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / similar pattern]`

**(a)** What is **hysteresis** in sorption isotherms? Explain with a neat sketch why the desorption and adsorption isotherms differ. State **two engineering consequences** of hysteresis in industrial dryers. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wet granular solid is dried from an initial moisture of $0.80 \,\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$ to a final moisture of $0.10 \,\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$. The initial wet mass of the feed is 1500 kg. The EMC in the operating air is $0.03 \,\text{kg/kg}$ and the critical moisture is $0.25 \,\text{kg/kg}$. The constant-rate drying rate is $R_{c} = 3.0 \,\text{kg m}^{-2}\text{hr}^{-1}$ for a total tray area of $5 \,\text{m}^{2}$. Calculate:
(i) the total moisture removed, (ii) the time for the constant-rate period, and (iii) the **free moisture** present in the solid when drying is stopped. (7 marks)

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Hysteresis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis** in a sorption isotherm refers to the phenomenon where the **equilibrium moisture content of a solid at a given relative humidity is different depending on whether the solid reached that state by adsorption (wetting) or desorption (drying).** The desorption isotherm typically lies *above* the adsorption isotherm.

**Reasons for hysteresis:**
1. **Capillary condensation in narrow pores** — once liquid has condensed in a capillary, it is held by the meniscus and is harder to evaporate (the "ink-bottle" pore effect).
2. **Swelling and relaxation** of the solid matrix during wetting, exposing new sites for binding.
3. **Chemisorption and irreversible binding** of water to hydrophilic groups in biopolymers (cellulose, starch, protein).

**Two engineering consequences:**
1. **The driest achievable moisture** in a given air is fixed by the *desorption* isotherm, not the average. A dryer that aims to bring a product below the *adsorption* EMC is over-designed.
2. **Re-wetted products (e.g. dried food that absorbs humidity during storage)** can have a moisture content *higher* than the operating EMC reading on the desorption curve — leading to caking, microbial growth, or spoilage.

**Sketch (to be drawn by student):**
- X-axis: $\phi$ (0 → 1).
- Y-axis: $X^{*}$.
- Plot two sigmoidal curves: the upper one is the **desorption** curve, the lower one is the **adsorption** curve.
- Shade the region between them and label it "Hysteresis loop."

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $X_{1} = 0.80 \;\text{kg/kg}$
- $X_{2} = 0.10 \;\text{kg/kg}$
- $m_{1} = 1500 \;\text{kg}$
- $X^{*} = 0.03 \;\text{kg/kg}$
- $X_{c} = 0.25 \;\text{kg/kg}$
- $R_{c} = 3.0 \;\text{kg m}^{-2}\text{hr}^{-1}$
- $A = 5 \;\text{m}^{2}$

**(i) Total moisture removed:**

Dry solid mass:

$$m_{s} = \frac{m_{1}}{1 + X_{1}} = \frac{1500}{1 + 0.80} = \frac{1500}{1.80} = 833.33 \;\text{kg}$$

Total water removed:

$$m_{w,\text{removed}} = m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{2}) = 833.33 \times (0.80 - 0.10) = 833.33 \times 0.70 = 583.33 \;\text{kg water}$$

**(ii) Time for the constant-rate period:**

$$t_{c} = \frac{m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{c})}{A \cdot R_{c}} = \frac{833.33 \times (0.80 - 0.25)}{5 \times 3.0}$$

$$= \frac{833.33 \times 0.55}{15} = \frac{458.33}{15} = 30.56 \;\text{hours}$$

**(iii) Free moisture at $X = X_{2}$:**

$$F_{2} = X_{2} - X^{*} = 0.10 - 0.03 = 0.07 \;\text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

This is the *minimum* free moisture still present — the drying air could in principle continue to remove it, but it represents a moisture that is *just* above the equilibrium value.

**Final answers:** (i) $m_{w,\text{removed}} = 583.33 \;\text{kg}$ (ii) $t_{c} = 30.56 \;\text{h}$ (iii) $F_{2} = 0.07 \;\text{kg/kg}$.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert wet basis to dry basis** before any drying calculation. KTU scripts are routinely penalized 1–2 marks for this. Always do this conversion *first*, before everything else.
> 2. **Confusing $X^{*}$ with the final moisture in a batch operation.** $X^{*}$ is the *lowest* moisture achievable in a *given* drying air — not necessarily the final moisture of an actual batch.
> 3. **Assuming $X_{c}$ is a property of the air.** It is a property of the *solid and geometry*. Examiners deduct marks if the script implies otherwise.
> 4. **Drawing the sorption isotherm without labelling axes, kink point $X_{u}$, and EMC region.** A diagram without these three elements is graded zero for the diagram portion.
> 5. **Mixing up bound and equilibrium moisture in calculations.** Bound moisture contains the equilibrium moisture plus extra tightly-held water. $X_{b} = X - X_{u}$, while $X^{*} \leq X_{b}$ always.
> 6. **Omitting units** on the final numerical answer. KTU board examiners routinely dock ½ mark for missing units.
> 7. **Writing $X$ without a basis.** Always say "kg water/kg **dry** solid" or "kg water/kg **wet** solid" — never just "$X = 0.4$".

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Moisture content $X$** is always expressed on a **dry basis** in KTU drying problems. Use $X = \dfrac{X_{w}}{1 - X_{w}}$ to convert.
- **Dry solid mass is conserved**: $m_{s} = \dfrac{m}{1 + X}$ for any state of the solid.
- **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC, $X^{*}$)** is the moisture a solid reaches when in prolonged contact with air of fixed $T$ and $\phi$. It is **not removable** in that air — drying stops at $X = X^{*}$.
- **Free moisture** $F = X - X^{*}$ is the actual *driving force* for drying; it is what gets removed.
- **Bound moisture** exerts vapour pressure *less* than pure water; **unbound** exerts vapour pressure *equal* to pure water. This is the KTU-favoured test.
- The **sorption isotherm** is the master plot: $X^{*}$ vs $\phi$ at constant $T$. It defines EMC, the bound/unbound boundary (kink at $X_{u}$), and the free moisture envelope.
- **Hysteresis:** desorption $X^{*}$ > adsorption $X^{*}$. Caused by capillary condensation, swelling, and irreversible binding.
- ↑ $\phi$ ⇒ ↑ $X^{*}$; ↑ $T$ (at fixed $\phi$) ⇒ ↓ $X^{*}$.
- **Critical moisture $X_{c}$** is the boundary between constant-rate and falling-rate drying periods. It is a property of the solid + geometry, not the air.
- **CRDP time:** $t_{c} = \dfrac{m_{s}(X_{1} - X_{c})}{A \cdot R_{c}}$ — memorize the form, the variables, and the units.
- For a typical KTU 14-mark problem, the recipe is: *find $m_{s}$ → find $X_{1}, X_{2}$ on dry basis → compute $F$ values → compute times using CRDP/FRDP equations*.
- **Always state** the basis (dry or wet), the units, and the assumptions (steady state, no chemical reaction) in your final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Moisture Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Critical Moisture Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Critical Moisture Content (X_c)** is defined as the moisture content (expressed on a dry basis, kg water/kg dry solid) of a wet solid at which the **constant rate drying period terminates** and the **falling rate drying period commences**.

In other words, it is the **transition point** on a drying rate curve where the mechanism of moisture removal changes from being controlled by **external mass transfer** (vaporization from the free surface) to being controlled by **internal mass transfer** (moisture migration from inside the solid to the surface).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Critical Moisture Content is a **fundamental concept in psychrometric drying** and forms the basis for designing **batch dryers**, calculating **drying times**, and determining the **equilibrium moisture content (X*)** of biological, pharmaceutical, and food materials.

The mathematical representation is:

$$X_c = \text{Mass of moisture (kg)} \big/ \text{Mass of bone-dry solid (kg)}$$

**Units of Critical Moisture Content:** $\text{kg water / kg dry solid}$ (dimensionless ratio).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge Story 💧

Imagine you have just dipped a **sponge** into a bucket of water and lifted it out:

- **Stage 1 (Constant Rate Period):** The sponge is dripping. Water on the *surface* is evaporating freely. As long as surface water exists, the drying rate depends only on air conditions (temperature, humidity, velocity), not on the sponge's internal structure. This is the **constant rate period**.

- **Transition Point (Critical Moisture Content):** The moment the *last drop* on the surface disappears, but the sponge is *still wet inside*. This exact moment is the **Critical Moisture Content (X_c)**. Below this, water is no longer freely available at the surface.

- **Stage 2 (Falling Rate Period):** Now water must travel from the *interior* of the sponge to the surface. The rate of drying *falls* because internal diffusion is much slower than surface evaporation. This is the **falling rate period**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Critical moisture content is the "last drop" — the point at which the easy-to-remove surface moisture is gone, and only the hard-to-extract bound/internal moisture remains.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Critical Moisture Content | $X_c$ | **0.1 to 2.0 kg water/kg dry solid** |
| Equilibrium Moisture Content | $X^*$ | Material-specific (kg water/kg dry solid) |
| Initial Moisture Content | $X_1$ | $X_1 > X_c$ (for drying to proceed in CR period) |
| Final Moisture Content | $X_2$ | $X_2 < X_c$ (in FR period) |
| Free Moisture Content | $X - X^*$ | kg water/kg dry solid |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Drying ALWAYS proceeds from $X_1$ (initial) → $X_2$ (final), and the constant rate period is valid only when $X > X_c$. Once $X \leq X_c$, falling rate equations govern the process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Drying Rate Curve — Full Theoretical Breakdown

The drying rate curve plots **drying rate (N_A)** on the Y-axis against **moisture content (X)** on the X-axis. It is divided into distinct regions:

### 🔹 Region A-B: Initial Transient / Adjustment Period
- Solid heats up from feed temperature to wet-bulb temperature.
- Surface moisture begins to evaporate.
- Duration is usually **short** and often neglected in engineering calculations.

### 🔹 Region B-C: Constant Rate Drying Period (CRDP)
- Surface is completely saturated with free moisture.
- All heat supplied is used as **latent heat of vaporization**.
- Drying rate $N_A$ is **constant** and equals the rate of pure water evaporation.
- Mechanism: **External mass transfer controlled** (vapor diffuses through air boundary layer).
- This period ends at **Point C**, where $X = X_c$ (the critical moisture content).

### 🔹 Region C-D: First Falling Rate Period
- Surface starts to dry out (unsaturated surface).
- The wetted area progressively shrinks ("shrinking core" model).
- Rate falls as the dry patch grows.

### 🔹 Region D-E: Second Falling Rate Period
- Internal moisture movement is the controlling mechanism.
- Rate is governed by **diffusion of liquid/vapor** through the solid matrix.
- Approaches zero as $X \rightarrow X^*$ (equilibrium moisture content).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For most KTU problems, the drying curve is approximated as **B-C-D-E**, ignoring the initial adjustment A-B segment for simplicity.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{h_t (T - T_w)}{\lambda_s}$ | Constant rate drying flux (kg/m²·s) | CR Period |
| 2 | $N_c = k_y (H_s - H)$ | Mass transfer based form of CR rate | CR Period |
| 3 | $N_c = \dfrac{h_t}{\rho \lambda_s} \cdot (P_s - P_w)$ | Combined heat-mass transfer analogy | CR Period |
| 4 | $t_c = \dfrac{L_s}{N_c \cdot A} (X_1 - X_c)$ | Time in constant rate period (s) | Batch dryer time |
| 5 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s}{A} \int_{X_2}^{X_c} \dfrac{dX}{N}$ | Time in falling rate period (general) | FR Period |
| 6 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \ln\left[\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$ | Time in FR (diffusion-controlled, straight line approximation) | FR Period |
| 7 | $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ | Total batch drying time | Design |
| 8 | $X_c = f(\text{porosity, thickness, drying conditions})$ | Empirical; depends on material & system | Estimation |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $h_t$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
> - $T, T_w$ = bulk gas & wet-bulb temperatures (K)
> - $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporization at $T_w$ (J/kg)
> - $L_s$ = mass of dry solid (kg)
> - $A$ = drying surface area (m²)
> - $H_s, H$ = humidity at saturation & bulk (kg water/kg dry air)
> - $P_s, P_w$ = vapor pressure at $T_w$ & partial pressure in bulk (Pa)
> - $X^*$ = equilibrium moisture content (kg/kg)
> - $k_y$ = mass transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·ΔY)

---

## 2.3 Factors Affecting Critical Moisture Content

The value of $X_c$ is **NOT a pure material property** — it depends on both the solid and the drying conditions.

### A. Material Properties
1. **Porosity** — Higher porosity → Lower $X_c$ (water drains easily, less surface retention).
2. **Pore size distribution** — Bimodal pores give complex curves.
3. **Initial moisture content** — Higher $X_1$ generally → Higher $X_c$.
4. **Thickness of bed** — Thicker bed → Higher $X_c$ (drying front takes longer to penetrate).
5. **Surface tension and capillary structure.**

### B. Drying Conditions
1. **Air velocity** — Higher velocity → Lower $X_c$ (faster surface removal).
2. **Air temperature** — Higher temperature → Lower $X_c$.
3. **Air humidity** — Lower humidity → Lower $X_c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** $X_c$ is *experimentally determined*, not theoretically predicted. The standard test is to plot drying rate vs. moisture content and identify the "knee" of the curve.

---

## 2.4 Industrial Utility of Critical Moisture Content

| Industry | Application of $X_c$ |
|----------|---------------------|
| **Food Processing** | Designing spray dryers for milk, coffee, eggs |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Lyophilization (freeze drying) cycle design |
| **Ceramics & Refractories** | Controlling drying shrinkage and cracking |
| **Paper & Pulp** | Predicting drying loads in paper machines |
| **Agriculture** | Grain drying silo design and energy optimization |
| **Textile Industry** | Stenter frame operation parameters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world impact:** A wrong estimate of $X_c$ in a pharmaceutical dryer can lead to **over-drying** (wasting energy, degrading heat-sensitive APIs) or **under-drying** (causing spoilage during storage).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Constant Rate Drying Time ($t_c$)

The constant rate period is governed entirely by heat and mass transfer from a **saturated surface**. The derivation combines:

- A **mass balance** on the solid.
- A **heat transfer equation** for convective drying.

### Step 1: Moisture Evaporation Rate
The rate at which water leaves the surface equals the constant drying rate $N_c$ (kg water / m²·s):

$$\text{Rate of moisture loss} = N_c \cdot A \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

### Step 2: Mass Balance on the Solid
The mass of water in the solid at any time $t$ is $L_s \cdot X$. Therefore:

$$-\frac{d(L_s X)}{dt} = N_c \cdot A$$

Since $L_s$ (mass of dry solid) is constant:

$$-L_s \frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \cdot A$$

### Step 3: Separation of Variables
Rearranging the equation:

$$dt = -\frac{L_s}{N_c \cdot A} \, dX$$

### Step 4: Integration Across the Constant Rate Period
Integrating from $X_1$ (initial) to $X_c$ (critical), corresponding to time $0$ to $t_c$:

$$\int_{0}^{t_c} dt = -\frac{L_s}{N_c \cdot A} \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$

$$t_c = \frac{L_s}{N_c \cdot A} (X_1 - X_c)$$

> **Result:** The time required to dry a material from $X_1$ to $X_c$ in the constant rate period.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Falling Rate Drying Time ($t_f$) — Straight-Line Approximation

In the falling rate period, the drying rate $N$ is no longer constant; it decreases as a function of $X$. A common **empirical approximation** (McCabe, Smith, Harriott) is:

$$N = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

This assumes $N \to N_c$ when $X = X_c$ and $N \to 0$ when $X = X^*$.

### Step 1: Starting Mass Balance (Falling Rate)
$$-L_s \frac{dX}{dt} = N \cdot A$$

### Step 2: Substitute Empirical Relation
$$-L_s \frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*} \cdot A$$

### Step 3: Rearrange and Separate Variables
$$dt = -\frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \cdot \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

### Step 4: Integrate from $X_c$ to $X_2$ (time $0$ to $t_f$)
$$\int_{0}^{t_f} dt = -\frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*}$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Integral
$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \ln\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

> **Result:** Total falling rate drying time from $X_c$ to final moisture $X_2$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem Statement
A batch dryer contains **50 kg of dry solid** ($L_s = 50$ kg) with a drying surface area of $A = 1.5$ m². The material is dried from initial moisture $X_1 = 0.40$ to final $X_2 = 0.05$ kg water/kg dry solid. The equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg, and the **critical moisture content** is $X_c = 0.20$ kg/kg. The constant rate of drying is $N_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s. Calculate the **total drying time**.

### Given Data
- $L_s = 50$ kg
- $A = 1.5$ m²
- $X_1 = 0.40$, $X_2 = 0.05$, $X_c = 0.20$, $X^* = 0.02$ (kg/kg)
- $N_c = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s

### Step 1: Constant Rate Period Time
$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A}$$

$$t_c = \frac{50 \times (0.40 - 0.20)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5}$$

$$t_c = \frac{50 \times 0.20}{2.25 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10.0}{2.25 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$t_c = 44{,}444.44 \text{ s} \approx 12.35 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 2: Falling Rate Period Time
$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \ln\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

Calculate numerator factor:
$$\frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} = \frac{50 \times (0.20 - 0.02)}{1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5} = \frac{50 \times 0.18}{2.25 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{9.0}{2.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 40{,}000 \text{ s}$$

Calculate logarithmic term:
$$\ln\left[\frac{0.20 - 0.02}{0.05 - 0.02}\right] = \ln\left[\frac{0.18}{0.03}\right] = \ln(6) = 1.7918$$

Therefore:
$$t_f = 40{,}000 \times 1.7918 = 71{,}672 \text{ s} \approx 19.91 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 3: Total Drying Time
$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 12.35 + 19.91 = 32.26 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Notice that the **falling rate period** ($19.91$ h) is *longer* than the constant rate period ($12.35$ h) in this case. This is typical for materials with significant bound moisture — the bulk of industrial drying energy is spent in the **falling rate regime**.

### Step 4: Verification via Dimensional Analysis
$$[t_c] = \frac{\text{kg} \times (\text{kg/kg})}{\text{kg/m}^2\text{s} \times \text{m}^2} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kg/s}} = \text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing CR vs. FR Periods

| Feature | Constant Rate Period (CR) | Falling Rate Period (FR) |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Moisture range** | $X_1$ to $X_c$ | $X_c$ to $X_2$ |
| **Surface condition** | Fully wet | Partially dry |
| **Controlling resistance** | External (gas film) | Internal (solid matrix) |
| **Rate controlling step** | Heat/mass transfer to surface | Liquid/vapor diffusion inside |
| **Drying rate** | Constant ($N_c$) | Decreasing ($N < N_c$) |
| **Temperature of solid** | Wet-bulb temperature | Rises from $T_w$ to $T$ |
| **Heat transfer equation** | $q = h_t(T - T_w)$ | Variable |
| **Duration dependence** | Linear in $(X_1 - X_c)$ | Logarithmic in free moisture |
| **Design implication** | Larger air flow helps | Larger air flow has limited effect |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Drying Process with Critical Moisture Content

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Wet Solid with Initial Moisture X1] --> B[Pre-Heating Stage A-B]
    B --> C{Is X greater than Xc?}
    C -->|Yes, X in X1 to Xc| D[Constant Rate Drying Period B-C]
    D --> E[Surface fully saturated with free moisture]
    E --> F[Heat transfer controls process]
    F --> G[Calculate time tc using tc = Ls times X1 minus Xc divided by Nc times A]
    G --> H{X reaches Xc point C on curve}
    C -->|No, X already below Xc| I[Skips directly to Falling Rate Period]
    H --> J[Falling Rate Drying Period C-D-E]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Surface partially dry, internal moisture migration]
    K --> L[Diffusion controls process]
    L --> M[Calculate time tf using straight-line approximation]
    M --> N{X reaches final X2}
    N -->|No| J
    N -->|Yes| O[End: Solid at X2 with equilibrium X-star]
    O --> P[Total Drying Time t total equals tc plus tf]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Batch Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Process Inputs]
        A1[Wet Feed Material X1]
        A2[Hot Air Stream T, H, velocity]
    end

    subgraph DRYER[Batch Dryer Chamber]
        B1[Heating Zone A-B]
        B2[Constant Rate Zone B-C]
        B3[Transition Point Critical Xc]
        B4[Falling Rate Zone C-D-E]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control and Measurement]
        C1[Temperature Sensor]
        C2[Humidity Sensor]
        C3[Mass Balance for Moisture Tracking]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Process Outputs]
        D1[Dried Solid at X2]
        D2[Moisture-laden Exhaust Air]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> D1
    B2 -.evaporates free moisture.-> D2
    B4 -.carries bound moisture.-> D2
```
