```mermaid
    C1 -.monitors.-> B1
    C2 -.monitors.-> B2
    C3 -.feeds back.-> B3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph: Drying Rate Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CURVE[Drying Rate N vs Moisture X]
        P1[A: Initial Point, X equals X1, low rate]
        P2[B: Start of CR Period, X equals X1]
        P3[C: Critical Point, X equals Xc, end of CR]
        P4[D: End of First FR, X equals Xd]
        P5[E: End of Second FR, X equals X-star, rate is 0]
    end

    P1 -->|Heating transient| P2
    P2 -->|Constant rate Nc| P3
    P3 -->|First falling rate| P4
    P4 -->|Second falling rate| P5
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Drying Rate vs. Moisture Content

```
   N (kg/m².s) │
                │  ★ A (transient)
            N_c │─────────────────★ B ──────────★ C  (CR Period)
                │                                 │
                │                                 │\   First FR
                │                                 │ \  
                │                                 │  \  
                │                                 │   \★ D
                │                                 │    \  
                │                                 │     \  Second FR
                │                                 │      \  
              0 │_________________________________│_______\★ E
                │                                 │       \  
                X*          X2           Xc         X1
```

> **Reading the curve from right to left:** Moisture decreases (drying proceeds) from $X_1$ → $X_c$ → $X_2$ → $X^*$, while drying rate starts constant, then falls to zero at equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define critical moisture content. How does it influence the design of a batch dryer?**

**Model Answer:**
Critical moisture content ($X_c$) is the moisture content of a wet solid (kg water/kg dry solid) at which the constant rate drying period ends and the falling rate drying period begins.

**Influence on design:**
1. It determines the duration of the **constant rate period** ($t_c$).
2. Materials with **high $X_c$** spend more time in CR period → larger dryers, higher air flow.
3. Materials with **low $X_c$** enter FR early → diffusion-controlled → longer drying, more sensitive to internal structure.
4. It is essential for **sizing the dryer chamber** and selecting **fan/blower capacity**.
5. It affects the **energy consumption profile** of the drying operation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$) in the falling rate drying period?**

**Model Answer:**
The equilibrium moisture content ($X^*$) represents the lowest moisture content a material can attain under given drying conditions — it is the moisture that cannot be removed by air of that temperature and humidity.

**Significance in falling rate period:**
1. The drying rate $N \to 0$ as $X \to X^*$.
2. The **driving force** for drying in FR period is the **free moisture** content, defined as $(X - X^*)$.
3. It appears in the falling rate time equation: $t_f \propto \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
4. Materials with **high $X^*$** (hygroscopic, biological) are difficult to dry completely and need **lower humidity air** or **higher temperature**.
5. It sets the **theoretical lower limit** of drying.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

---

### 📌 **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** A solid is dried from an initial moisture content of $X_1 = 0.38$ kg/kg to a final $X_2 = 0.04$ kg/kg. The **critical moisture content** is $X_c = 0.25$ kg/kg and the **equilibrium moisture content** is $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg. The mass of dry solid is $L_s = 80$ kg, drying area is $A = 2.0$ m², and the constant drying rate is $N_c = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s.

**Calculate:**
(i) The time required in the constant rate period. (5 marks)
(ii) The time required in the falling rate period. (5 marks)
(iii) Total drying time. (1 mark)

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting critical moisture content** in detail. (4 marks)

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a):

**Given:** $X_1 = 0.38$, $X_2 = 0.04$, $X_c = 0.25$, $X^* = 0.02$, $L_s = 80$ kg, $A = 2.0$ m², $N_c = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s.

**(i) Constant Rate Period Time:**
$$t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A} = \frac{80 \times (0.38 - 0.25)}{2.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.0}$$

$$t_c = \frac{80 \times 0.13}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10.4}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 20{,}800 \text{ s} \approx 5.78 \text{ h}$$

**(ii) Falling Rate Period Time (Straight-line Approximation):**
$$t_f = \frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \ln\left[\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$$

Compute constant factor:
$$\frac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} = \frac{80 \times (0.25 - 0.02)}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{80 \times 0.23}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{18.4}{5.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 36{,}800 \text{ s}$$

Compute log term:
$$\ln\left[\frac{0.25 - 0.02}{0.04 - 0.02}\right] = \ln\left[\frac{0.23}{0.02}\right] = \ln(11.5) = 2.4423$$

$$t_f = 36{,}800 \times 2.4423 = 89{,}876.7 \text{ s} \approx 24.97 \text{ h}$$

**(iii) Total Drying Time:**
$$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 5.78 + 24.97 = 30.75 \text{ hours}$$

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b):
Factors affecting $X_c$:
1. **Material porosity** — Highly porous materials have lower $X_c$.
2. **Thickness of the bed/slab** — Thicker solids retain more surface moisture → higher $X_c$.
3. **Initial moisture content** — Higher $X_1$ often shifts $X_c$ upward.
4. **Air velocity** — Higher velocity removes surface moisture faster → lower $X_c$.
5. **Air temperature & humidity** — Hot, dry air lowers $X_c$.
6. **Internal structure** — Capillary and pore size distribution strongly influence $X_c$.

---

### 📌 **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the **expression for the time required in the constant rate drying period** in a batch dryer. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pharmaceutical granular material is dried in a tray dryer. The following data are obtained:

| Time (h) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|----------|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture (kg/kg) | 0.55 | 0.45 | 0.35 | 0.27 | 0.21 | 0.16 | 0.12 | 0.09 | 0.07 |

**Plot the drying curve** (moisture vs. time) and identify the **critical moisture content**. If the constant rate is $N_c = 2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m²·s and $L_s = 30$ kg, $A = 1.0$ m², calculate the time required to dry from $X_1 = 0.55$ to $X_2 = 0.10$ kg/kg. (7 marks)

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a):

Starting with the mass balance on dry solid:
$$-L_s \frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \cdot A$$

In the constant rate period, $N_c$ is independent of $X$ (since the surface remains saturated). Separating variables and integrating from $X_1$ (at $t = 0$) to $X_c$ (at $t = t_c$):

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A}}$$

This gives the **time in constant rate drying period** — the duration of pure surface evaporation.

#### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b):

**Plotting and Identification:**
- From the data table, observe the moisture drop pattern:
  - From $t = 0$ to $t = 2$ h: 0.55 → 0.35 (drop of 0.20, i.e., 0.10/h)
  - From $t = 2$ to $t = 4$ h: 0.35 → 0.21 (drop of 0.14, i.e., 0.07/h)
  - From $t = 4$ to $t = 6$ h: 0.21 → 0.12 (drop of 0.09, i.e., 0.045/h)
  - From $t = 6$ to $t = 8$ h: 0.12 → 0.07 (drop of 0.05, i.e., 0.025/h)

- The rate of moisture removal changes between $t = 2$ and $t = 3$ h — a clear **"knee"** appears near $X = 0.27$ kg/kg.
- Therefore, **$X_c \approx 0.25$ to 0.27 kg/kg**.

**Calculation of Total Drying Time** (assuming $X_c = 0.25$, $X^* \approx 0.05$ for illustration):

Constant rate period:
$$t_c = \frac{30 \times (0.55 - 0.25)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0} = \frac{30 \times 0.30}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{9.0}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 45{,}000 \text{ s} = 12.5 \text{ h}$$

Falling rate period:
$$t_f = \frac{30 \times (0.25 - 0.05)}{2.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.0} \ln\left[\frac{0.25 - 0.05}{0.10 - 0.05}\right] = \frac{6.0}{2.0 \times 10^{-4}} \ln(4)$$

$$t_f = 30{,}000 \times 1.3863 = 41{,}589 \text{ s} \approx 11.55 \text{ h}$$

Total: $t_{total} \approx 12.5 + 11.55 = 24.05$ hours.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> ⚠️ **Common mistakes students make on Critical Moisture Content problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $X^*$** — The falling rate equation uses **free moisture** $(X - X^*)$, not absolute moisture. Marks are deducted heavily for this.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up the equations** — CR period uses $(X_1 - X_c)$, FR period uses $(X_c - X^*)$ and $(X_2 - X^*)$. DO NOT swap these terms.
> 
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — Convert all times to **seconds** before plugging into formulas that use $N_c$ in kg/m²·s. Marks lost for unit errors.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the initial transient A-B** — KTU expects students to state this assumption explicitly in answers.
> 
> 5. **Using $L_s$ in kg but $A$ in cm²** — Always use SI units throughout the calculation.
> 
> 6. **Treating $X_c$ as a material constant** — It is **system-dependent**. Always state that $X_c$ is experimentally determined.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the log term in $t_f$** — Some students use a linear approximation in FR period without justification, which costs 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

✅ **Definition:** Critical moisture content ($X_c$) is the moisture content at which the constant rate drying period ends and the falling rate drying period begins.

✅ **Location on curve:** $X_c$ is at the "knee" or "breakpoint" of the drying rate vs. moisture content plot.

✅ **CR Period Equation:** $t_c = \dfrac{L_s (X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A}$

✅ **FR Period Equation (straight-line):** $t_f = \dfrac{L_s (X_c - X^*)}{N_c \cdot A} \ln\left[\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right]$

✅ **Total Drying Time:** $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$

✅ **$X_c$ is NOT a pure material property** — it depends on drying conditions (air velocity, temperature, humidity, bed thickness).

✅ **Controlling mechanism transition:** External mass transfer (CR) → Internal moisture diffusion (FR).

✅ **In CR period**, solid temperature = wet-bulb temperature of air. In FR period, it rises towards the dry-bulb temperature.

✅ **For most industrial drying operations**, the FR period consumes **more time and energy** than the CR period.

✅ **Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** is the theoretical limit — drying stops at this point.

✅ **Free moisture content** = $X - X^*$, and is the **true driving force** in falling rate period.

✅ **KTU standard assumption:** Neglect the initial adjustment period (A-B) unless explicitly given.

✅ **Common $X_c$ values:** 0.1–0.3 for porous solids; 0.5–2.0 for fibrous/biological materials (rough range).

✅ **Always write the units** in the final answer: typically convert to **hours** for readability.

✅ **Experimentally determine $X_c$** by plotting drying rate vs. moisture content and locating the breakpoint.

✅ **Drying rate expression (CR):** $N_c = \dfrac{h_t(T - T_w)}{\lambda_s}$ — combines heat transfer analogy.

✅ **Remember:** $N_c$ is a *flux* (kg/m²·s), not a *total rate*. The total rate is $N_c \cdot A$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time of Drying, Rate of Drying, and Drying Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Time of Drying, Rate of Drying, and Drying Curves ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Drying** is a mass transfer operation in which the moisture content of a wet solid is reduced by thermal means, involving simultaneous heat and mass transfer. The driving force is the vapor pressure difference between the moisture in the solid and the moisture in the bulk drying gas. Mathematically, it is governed by the **drying flux equation**: $$N = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$ where $N$ is the **drying flux (kg water/hr·m²)**, $L_s$ is the **dry solid mass per unit drying area (kg dry solid/m²)**, $X$ is the **moisture content (dry basis)**, and $t$ is time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition:** Drying is the final separation step where a volatile liquid (usually water) is removed from a solid/liquid mixture to produce a solid product of desired low moisture content. The KTU 2024 scheme classifies this as a *simultaneous heat and mass transfer* operation under **Module 3: Mass Transfer Operations**. ### 1.2 Moisture Content Terminologies (Must Memorize for KTU) | Term | Symbol | Definition | |------|--------|------------| | **Moisture Content (Dry Basis)** | $X$ | $\dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\ solid}}$ (kg water / kg dry solid) | | **Moisture Content (Wet Basis)** | $X_w$ | $\dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total\ wet\ solid}}$ (kg water / kg wet solid) | | **Equilibrium Moisture Content** | $X^*$ | Moisture content of solid in equilibrium with drying air (cannot be removed by that air) | | **Free Moisture Content** | $X - X^*$ | Moisture that *can* be removed by the given drying air conditions | | **Bound Moisture** | — | Moisture held tightly by the solid (vapor pressure < pure water) | | **Unbound Moisture** | — | Moisture held loosely (vapor pressure = pure water) | | **Critical Moisture Content** | $X_c$ | Moisture content at which the constant rate period ends and falling rate begins | **Conversion Formulae (Frequently Tested):** $$X = \frac{X_w}{1 - X_w} \qquad \text{and} \qquad X_w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Wet Sponge in the Sun 🧽☀️ > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Picture a fully soaked sponge left under the hot afternoon sun. > - **First few minutes (Constant Rate Period):** Water literally *pools* on the surface and evaporates at a steady pace — the rate of evaporation depends only on sun, air, and humidity, NOT on the sponge. This is the **constant rate period**. > - **Then the surface dries (First Falling Rate):** The outer surface becomes "tacky". Water from inside now has to *migrate* outward. The rate slows because the sponge controls the process. > - **Last stages (Second Falling Rate):** The deep, tightly held water slowly diffuses out. The drying is now very slow and is governed by internal diffusion of moisture. > - **Truly bone-dry? Never!** Even under intense sun, the sponge retains *some* moisture in equilibrium with the air — this is the **equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** that physically *cannot* be removed by simple air drying. This simple sponge model directly maps onto the **four periods of drying** in industrial batch dryers. ### 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU Hot-Favorite > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Weightage):** Drying calculations carry **~15–20%** of KTU end-semester marks in Heat & Mass Transfer. The combination of *derivation + numerical* on time of drying is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in the KTU University Examination (as observed in previous Dec 2019, Dec 2022, June 2023 papers). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Periods of Drying — A Geankoplis-Standard Analysis When a wet solid is placed in a batch dryer with constant inlet air conditions, the moisture content $X$ vs. time $t$ curve (called the **drying curve**) and the corresponding drying rate $N$ vs. $X$ curve (called the **rate of drying curve**) exhibit **four distinct periods**: #### **Period I — Initial Adjustment / Transient Period (A → B)** - A brief unsteady period where the solid surface temperature rises to the wet-bulb temperature. - Drying rate *increases* rapidly (if starting cold) or *decreases* slightly. - Duration is **very short** and usually **neglected** in KTU calculations. #### **Period II — Constant Rate Period (B → C)** - The **entire surface is saturated** with free moisture (unbound water). - Drying rate is **constant at $N_c$** and equal to the rate of pure water evaporation. - Rate is governed *entirely* by **external mass/heat transfer** (air-side conditions, NOT solid properties). - All heat transferred from air → surface is used as **latent heat of vaporization**. - Surface temperature ≈ **wet-bulb temperature** of the air. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Constant Rate"?** Because liquid water on the surface behaves as if it were a free water surface — its vapor pressure equals that of pure water at the wet-bulb temperature. The rate is independent of the solid, just like evaporation from a pond. #### **Period III — First Falling Rate Period (C → D)** - Surface moisture has dropped. The wetted surface area **progressively decreases** ("unsaturated surface drying"). - Drying rate decreases *linearly* (in some solids) or *non-linearly* with $X$. - The plane of evaporation **recedes into the solid**. - This is sometimes called the **unsaturated surface drying period**. #### **Period IV — Second Falling Rate Period (D → E)** - The **plane of evaporation** is fully inside the solid. - Moisture transport is now controlled by **internal diffusion** of liquid + vapor through the pores. - Rate decreases more gradually and approaches zero as $X \to X^*$. - Drying is governed by **internal mass transfer** — solid properties dominate. - This is the longest and most difficult period to model. ### 2.2 The Rate of Drying Curve (Plot of $N$ vs $X$) This is the **most important diagram** in this module. It looks like this (qualitatively): - Horizontal line at $N = N_c$ from $B$ to $C$ (constant rate) - Straight or curved line dropping from $(X_c, N_c)$ down to $(X^*, 0)$ **Key Points on the Curve:** - **Point B:** Rate is constant. Free moisture surface fully wets the solid. - **Point C (Critical Point):** $X = X_c$, $N = N_c$. The transition point. - **Point D:** End of first falling rate, start of second falling rate (sometimes merged as one falling rate). - **Point E:** $X = X^*$, $N = 0$. Equilibrium — no more drying possible. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule:** The **constant rate period exists ONLY if $X_1 > X_c$** (initial moisture > critical). If $X_1 \le X_c$, drying is *entirely* in the falling rate period from the start. ### 2.3 Mechanism of Moisture Movement (Theory) In the falling rate period, moisture moves by **three mechanisms** simultaneously: 1. **Liquid diffusion** through the solid (capillary action) 2. **Vapor diffusion** through pores (after vaporization inside the solid) 3. **Capillary flow** in porous materials (driven by surface tension gradients) > [!NOTE] > **Why KTU Tests This:** The transition from external (constant rate) to internal (falling rate) control is the *conceptual foundation* of dryer design. The critical moisture content $X_c$ is **NOT a material property** — it depends on drying conditions, solid thickness, and surface characteristics. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋 | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $N = -L_s \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Defining equation for drying flux | kg/hr·m² | | 2 | $X = \dfrac{X_w}{1-X_w}$ | Wet basis → Dry basis conversion | kg/kg | | 3 | $t_c = \dfrac{L_s}{N_c}(X_1 - X_c)$ | Time in **Constant Rate Period** | hr | | 4 | $t_f = L_s \displaystyle\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \dfrac{dX}{N}$ | Time in **Falling Rate Period** (general integral form) | hr | | 5 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s}{N_c}(X_c - X_2)$ | Falling rate time for **linear** $N$ vs $X$ (KTU shortcut) | hr | | 6 | $t_{total} = t_c + t_f$ | Total drying time | hr | | 7 | $N = N_c \cdot \dfrac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$ | Linear falling rate approximation | kg/hr·m² | | 8 | $t_{total} = \dfrac{L_s}{N_c}(X_1 - X^*)$ | Total time if drying to equilibrium with linear CRP+FRP | hr | | 9 | $L_s = \dfrac{m_s}{A_{dry}}$ | Dry solid loading per unit area | kg/m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on absolute values:** In the logarithmic forms of the falling rate integral (used in advanced derivations), the absolute value of $(X - X^*)$ appears. Always write $\vert X - X^* \vert$ in KTU answer scripts — do **not** use the vertical pipe character `|` inside markdown table cells, as it breaks rendering. ### 2.5 Engineering Applications — Real-World Utility | Industry | Drying Application | |----------|--------------------| | **Pharmaceuticals** | Drying of granules, tablets, Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) | | **Food Processing** | Spray drying of milk, coffee, eggs; tray drying of fruits | | **Cement / Ceramics** | Rotary kiln drying of limestone, clay, ores | | **Pulp & Paper** | Drying of paper sheets (most energy-intensive step) | | **Agriculture** | Paddy, wheat, spice drying | | **Polymers** | Drying of PET, Nylon pellets before extrusion | | **Detergents** | Spray drying of detergent powders | > [!NOTE] > **Industry Insight:** Industrial dryers are among the **most energy-intensive** unit operations — drying accounts for ~12–20% of total energy use in chemical process industries. Accurate prediction of drying time directly impacts **capital cost** (dryer size) and **operating cost** (energy, throughput). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Time of Drying in the Constant Rate Period **Starting Point:** The fundamental drying flux equation: $$N = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$ **Step 1: Apply the constant rate condition** In the constant rate period, $N$ is constant and equals $N_c$. Therefore: $$N_c = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$ **Step 2: Separate the variables** $$N_c \, dt = -L_s \, dX$$ **Step 3: Integrate over the constant rate region** The limits: time goes from $0$ to $t_c$ (duration of CRP), and moisture content decreases from $X_1$ (initial) to $X_c$ (critical): $$\int_0^{t_c} N_c \, dt = -L_s \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$ **Step 4: Evaluate the integrals** $$N_c \cdot t_c \Big|_0^{t_c} = -L_s \cdot X \Big|_{X_1}^{X_c}$$ $$N_c (t_c - 0) = -L_s (X_c - X_1)$$ **Step 5: Simplify and solve for $t_c$** $$N_c \cdot t_c = L_s (X_1 - X_c)$$ $$\boxed{\, t_c = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_1 - X_c) \,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This 7-mark derivation is a *guaranteed KTU question*. You must explicitly state the limits of integration, justify the constant rate assumption, and use correct units. Marks are awarded for each of these four steps — **skipping any step costs 1–2 marks**. --- ### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Linear Falling Rate — Time of Drying **Step 1: Define the linear falling rate model** Assume the rate $N$ drops linearly from $(X_c, N_c)$ to $(X_2, 0)$ on the $N$ vs. $X$ plot. The equation of this line is: $$N = N_c \left[ \frac{X - X_2}{X_c - X_2} \right]$$ **Step 2: Substitute into the drying flux equation** $$N_c \left[ \frac{X - X_2}{X_c - X_2} \right] = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$ **Step 3: Separate variables and integrate** $$dt = -\frac{L_s(X_c - X_2)}{N_c} \cdot \frac{dX}{X - X_2}$$ Integrate from $t = 0$ (when $X = X_c$) to $t = t_f$ (when $X = X_2$): $$\int_0^{t_f} dt = -\frac{L_s(X_c - X_2)}{N_c} \int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X_2}$$ **Step 4: Evaluate the RHS integral** $$\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X_2} = \ln \vert X - X_2 \vert \Big|_{X_c}^{X_2} = \ln \vert X_2 - X_2 \vert - \ln \vert X_c - X_2 \vert = -\ln(X_c - X_2)$$ (Note: the $\ln(0)$ at the upper limit is undefined, which is why **KTU uses the simplification** below.) **Step 5: KTU Linear Approximation (the practical shortcut)** For board-exam problems, KTU expects the **simplified form**, treating the falling rate as approximately linear and using the area of the triangle on the $N$ vs $X$ plot: $$\boxed{\, t_f = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_c - X_2) \,}$$ This formula is the **most tested** falling-rate equation in KTU exams. --- ### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Total Drying Time (Combined CRP + Linear FRP) Combining the two derived expressions: $$t_{total} = t_c + t_f$$ $$\boxed{\, t_{total} = \frac{L_s}{N_c} \left[ (X_1 - X_c) + (X_c - X_2) \right] = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_1 - X_2) \,}$$ This elegant result — **Total time depends only on the start and end moisture** — holds *only* under the linear CRP + linear FRP model. --- ### 3.4 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — Full Numerical (KTU Style) **Problem:** A wet granular material is dried in a batch tray dryer from a moisture content of $0.38$ to $0.04$ kg water/kg dry solid. The **critical moisture content** is $X_c = 0.18$ kg/kg dry solid, and the **equilibrium moisture content** is $X^* = 0.02$ kg/kg dry solid. The **constant rate of drying** is $N_c = 0.50$ kg/hr·m² and the **dry solid loading** on the trays is $L_s = 25$ kg dry solid/m². Calculate: - (a) Time required for the constant rate period - (b) Time required for the falling rate period (assume linear) - (c) Total drying time **Given Data:** $$X_1 = 0.38, \quad X_2 = 0.04, \quad X_c = 0.18, \quad X^* = 0.02$$ $$L_s = 25 \text{ kg/m}^2, \quad N_c = 0.50 \text{ kg/hr·m}^2$$ #### **Part (a): Constant Rate Period** Apply the derived formula: $$t_c = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_1 - X_c)$$ $$t_c = \frac{25}{0.50} (0.38 - 0.18)$$ $$t_c = 50 \times 0.20$$ $$\boxed{\, t_c = 10 \text{ hr} \,}$$ #### **Part (b): Falling Rate Period (Linear)** $$t_f = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_c - X_2)$$ $$t_f = \frac{25}{0.50} (0.18 - 0.04)$$ $$t_f = 50 \times 0.14$$ $$\boxed{\, t_f = 7 \text{ hr} \,}$$ #### **Part (c): Total Drying Time** $$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 10 + 7$$ $$\boxed{\, t_{total} = 17 \text{ hr} \,}$$ **Cross-check using the combined formula:** $$t_{total} = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_1 - X_2) = \frac{25}{0.50} (0.38 - 0.04) = 50 \times 0.34 = 17 \text{ hr} \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Verification Pattern:** The "shortcut formula" $t_{total} = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_2)$ gives the **same** result only if the falling rate is **linear** and **passes through zero at $X_2$**. In the general (non-linear) case, the integral must be evaluated numerically or graphically. --- ### 3.5 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — When Initial Moisture < Critical **Problem:** A solid with initial moisture $X_1 = 0.15$ kg/kg dry solid (which is **less than** the critical moisture $X_c = 0.25$ kg/kg dry solid) is dried to $X_2 = 0.05$ kg/kg dry solid. The constant rate of drying is $N_c = 0.40$ kg/hr·m², and $L_s = 10$ kg/m². Find the drying time. **Key Insight:** Since $X_1 < X_c$, the **entire drying is in the falling rate period**. There is NO constant rate phase. $$t_c = 0 \text{ (no CRP exists)}$$ $$t_f = \frac{L_s}{N_c} (X_c - X_2) = \frac{10}{0.40} (0.25 - 0.05) = 25 \times 0.20 = 5 \text{ hr}$$ $$\boxed{\, t_{total} = t_f = 5 \text{ hr} \,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often blindly apply $t_c = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_c)$ even when $X_1 < X_c$, which gives a **negative** time. This is physically meaningless. Always check: **if $X_1 \le X_c$, set $t_c = 0$**. --- ### 3.6 GRAPHICAL METHOD for Falling Rate (KTU Concept) When the falling rate is **non-linear**, the integral $t_f = L_s \displaystyle\int \frac{dX}{N}$ is solved **graphically**: 1. From experimental data, plot $N$ vs $X$ in the falling rate region. 2. Recalculate as $\frac{1}{N}$ vs $X$. 3. The **area under the curve** of $\frac{1}{N}$ vs $X$ from $X_c$ to $X_2$, multiplied by $L_s$, gives $t_f$. 4. This is often implemented as the **trapezoidal rule** in practice. This method is asked in KTU 2-mark conceptual questions: *"How is drying time in the falling rate period obtained experimentally?"* — Answer: **Graphical integration of $1/N$ vs $X$.** --- ### 3.7 Summary Table — When to Use Which Formula | Scenario | Drying Behavior | Time Formula | |----------|-----------------|---------------| | $X_1 > X_c$, $X_2 > X_c$ | **All CRP** | $t = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_2)$ | | $X_1 > X_c$, $X_2 < X_c$ (linear FRP) | **CRP + linear FRP** | $t = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_c) + (L_s/N_c)(X_c - X_2)$ | | $X_1 > X_c$, $X_2 = X^*$ (linear FRP) | **CRP + linear FRP to eq.** | $t = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X^*)$ | | $X_1 \le X_c$ (linear FRP) | **All FRP** | $t = (L_s/N_c)(X_c - X_2)$ | | Non-linear FRP (general) | **General** | $t = L_s \int dX/N$ (graphical) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — A Typical Batch Tray Dryer System ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Wet Solid
Feed
X = X1"] --> B["Tray Dryer
Chamber"] H["Heated Air
Tin, Hin, Yi"] --> B B --> C["Dried Solid
Discharge
X = X2"] B --> D["Moist Exhaust Air
Tout, Hout, You"] E["Recycle Fan"] -.-> H D -.-> E style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#d84315,color:#000 style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style B fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style D fill:#b2ebf2,stroke:#006064,color:#000 style E fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 The Rate of Drying Curve — Periods and Critical Points ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Drying Rate N"]:::axis P1["Period I
Initial Adjustment
A to B
(transient)"]:::p1 P2["Period II
Constant Rate
B to C
N = Nc constant"]:::p2 P3["Period III
First Falling Rate
C to D
unsaturated surface"]:::p3 P4["Period IV
Second Falling Rate
D to E
internal diffusion"]:::p4 PtB["Point B
Surface saturates
Surface Temp = T_wb"]:::pt PtC["Point C
Critical Point
X = Xc, N = Nc"]:::ptcrit PtD["Point D
End of FRP-I
Plane of evap. recedes"]:::pt PtE["Point E
Equilibrium
X = X*, N = 0"]:::pteq P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 P2 -.-> PtB P3 -.-> PtC P3 -.-> PtD P4 -.-> PtE classDef axis fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef p1 fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef p2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef p3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef p4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 classDef pt fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#424242,color:#000 classDef ptcrit fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef pteq fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Moisture Content — Dry Basis vs Wet Basis Conversion Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Given Moisture Content"] --> B{"Basis?"} B -->|Wet Basis Xw| C["Convert to Dry Basis:
X = Xw / 1 - Xw"] B -->|Dry Basis X| D["Use directly in
drying equations"] C --> D D --> E["Apply:
tc = Ls/Nc * X1 - Xc"] D --> F["Apply:
tf = Ls/Nc * Xc - X2"] E --> G["Total Time
t = tc + tf"] F --> G style A fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style B fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style E fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style F fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style G fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Decision Flow — How to Choose the Right Drying-Time Formula ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start: Have X1, X2, Xc, Ls, Nc"] --> B{"Is X1 greater than Xc?"} B -->|Yes| C{"Is X2 greater than Xc?"} B -->|No| D["All drying in FRP
tc = 0"] C -->|Yes| E["Only CRP active
t = Ls/Nc times X1 - X2"] C -->|No| F{"Is FRP linear?"} F -->|Yes| G["Use combined formula:
t = Ls/Nc times X1 - X2"] F -->|No| H["Graphical integration:
plot 1/N vs X, find area"] D --> I["t = Ls/Nc times Xc - X2"] E --> J["Final drying time t"] G --> J H --> J I --> J style A fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style B fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style J fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** **Define the following terms with respect to drying operations:** **(i) Equilibrium moisture content** **(ii) Free moisture content** **(iii) Bound moisture** **Model Answer (3 marks = 3 × 1 each):** **(i) Equilibrium Moisture Content ($X^*$):** The moisture content of a solid at which the solid is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the partial pressure of water vapor in the surrounding gas. At $X = X^*$, the net rate of moisture transfer is zero, and **no further drying is possible** under the prevailing air conditions. *(1 mark)* **(ii) Free Moisture Content ($X - X^*$):** The moisture content of a solid **in excess of the equilibrium moisture content**. It is the portion that can *actually* be removed by drying under the given conditions. Free moisture = Total moisture $-$ Equilibrium moisture. *(1 mark)* **(iii) Bound Moisture:** The moisture in a solid that exerts a vapor pressure **less than** that of pure water at the same temperature. This moisture is held by physical-chemical forces (adsorption, capillary retention) and is *not* all removable by simple air drying. *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]** **Explain the constant rate period in drying operations. What controls the rate in this period?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** During the constant rate period, the surface of the solid remains **completely saturated with free (unbound) moisture**. The moisture evaporates as if from a free water surface, and the rate of drying is governed entirely by **external heat and mass transfer conditions** — namely, the temperature, humidity, and velocity of the drying air. The solid surface temperature remains approximately constant at the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air, and all heat supplied is utilized as the **latent heat of vaporization**. The rate is **independent of the solid's nature or moisture content**, which is why it is called "constant rate." *(3 marks)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall (Part A):** Examiners often deduct 1 mark if the answer does not mention that the surface temperature equals the **wet-bulb temperature**, or if you incorrectly state that the rate depends on the solid properties (it does NOT in the constant rate period). --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]** **(a) Derive the expression for the time of drying during the constant rate period in a batch dryer, starting from the fundamental drying flux equation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: State the governing equation (1 mark)** The fundamental drying flux equation is: $$N = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$ where: - $N$ = drying flux (kg water evaporated per hour per m² of drying area) - $L_s$ = mass of dry solid per unit drying area (kg/m²) - $X$ = moisture content (kg water / kg dry solid) - $t$ = time (hr) **Step 2: Apply the constant rate condition (1 mark)** In the constant rate period, the drying rate is constant and equal to $N_c$. Hence: $$N_c = -L_s \frac{dX}{dt}$$ **Step 3: Separate variables (1 mark)** $$N_c \, dt = -L_s \, dX$$ **Step 4: Set the limits of integration (1 mark)** The constant rate period extends from $t = 0$ (when $X = X_1$, the initial moisture) to $t = t_c$ (when $X = X_c$, the critical moisture): $$\int_0^{t_c} N_c \, dt = -L_s \int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX$$ **Step 5: Evaluate the integrals (2 marks)** $$N_c \cdot [t]_0^{t_c} = -L_s \cdot [X]_{X_1}^{X_c}$$ $$N_c \cdot t_c = -L_s (X_c - X_1) = L_s(X_1 - X_c)$$ **Step 6: Final expression (1 mark)** $$\boxed{\, t_c = \frac{L_s}{N_c}(X_1 - X_c) \,}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** The constant rate drying time is directly proportional to the dry solid loading $L_s$ and the moisture range $(X_1 - X_c)$, and inversely proportional to the constant drying flux $N_c$. Doubling the air velocity (which increases $N_c$) halves the drying time. --- **(b) A wet solid is dried in a tray dryer from a moisture content of $0.30$ to $0.05$ kg water/kg dry solid. The critical moisture content is $0.15$ kg/kg dry solid. The constant drying rate is $1.5$ kg/hr·m². If the dryer holds $100$ kg of dry solid with a drying area of $25$ m², find the total drying time. Assume linear falling rate period. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Organize the given data (1 mark)** $$X_1 = 0.30 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad X_2 = 0.05 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad X_c = 0.15 \text{ kg/kg}$$ $$N_c = 1.5 \text{ kg/hr·m}^2$$ $$m_s = 100 \text{ kg dry solid}, \quad A = 25 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Calculate dry solid loading (1 mark)** $$L_s = \frac{m_s}{A} = \frac{100}{25} = 4 \text{ kg dry solid/m}^2$$ **Step 3: Verify drying regime (1 mark)** Since $X_1 = 0.30 > X_c = 0.15$ **and** $X_2 = 0.05 < X_c = 0.15$, drying proceeds in **two stages**: CRP followed by FRP. **Step 4: Calculate constant rate period time (1 mark)** $$t_c = \frac{L_s}{N_c}(X_1 - X_c) = \frac{4}{1.5}(0.30 - 0.15) = 2.6667 \times 0.15 = 0.400 \text{ hr}$$ **Step 5: Calculate falling rate period time (1 mark)** $$t_f = \frac{L_s}{N_c}(X_c - X_2) = \frac{4}{1.5}(0.15 - 0.05) = 2.6667 \times 0.10 = 0.2667 \text{ hr}$$ **Step 6: Total drying time (1 mark)** $$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 0.400 + 0.2667 = 0.6667 \text{ hr}$$ $$\boxed{\, t_{total} \approx 0.667 \text{ hr} \approx 40 \text{ minutes} \,}$$ **Verification (1 mark):** Using the combined formula: $$t_{total} = \frac{L_s}{N_c}(X_1 - X_2) = \frac{4}{1.5}(0.30 - 0.05) = 2.6667 \times 0.25 = 0.6667 \text{ hr} \checkmark$$ --- ### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the different periods of drying in a batch tray dryer. Also discuss the mechanism of moisture movement in each period. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sketch: Drying Rate Curve (N vs X)** — *as shown in Section 4.2 above (3 marks for sketch + labeling)* **Period I — Initial Adjustment / Transient Period (A → B):** A short unsteady start-up period during which the wet solid's surface temperature rises from the initial temperature to the wet-bulb temperature of the drying air. The drying rate may increase or decrease briefly. The mechanism is heat transfer-dominated as the solid thermally equilibrates. *(1 mark)* **Period II — Constant Rate Period (B → C):** The surface remains completely saturated with free (unbound) moisture. The plane of vaporization is at the surface. Drying occurs as if from a free water surface, governed by **external mass transfer** (convective removal of vapor by the air). Heat for vaporization is supplied by convection from the air. The surface temperature equals the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air. Rate is **constant at $N_c$**, independent of solid properties. *(1.5 marks)* **Period III — First Falling Rate Period (C → D):** The wetted area on the surface progressively decreases (unsaturated surface drying). The plane of vaporization starts to recede *into* the solid. Moisture transport occurs by **capillary flow** in the pores. Rate decreases as the wetted area shrinks. *(1 mark)* **Period IV — Second Falling Rate Period (D → E):** The plane of vaporization is fully inside the solid. Moisture moves by **internal diffusion** — both liquid diffusion through the solid matrix and vapor diffusion through pores. The rate of drying is now controlled entirely by the **internal mass transfer resistance**. The rate gradually approaches zero as $X \to X^*$ (the equilibrium moisture content). *(1.5 marks)* --- **(b) A wet granular material is dried in a batch tray dryer from a moisture content of $0.38$ to $0.04$ kg water/kg dry solid. The critical moisture content is $0.18$ kg/kg dry solid, and the equilibrium moisture content is $0.02$ kg/kg dry solid. The constant rate of drying is $0.50$ kg/hr·m², and the dry solid loading is $25$ kg/m². Calculate: (i) the time required for the constant rate period, (ii) the time required for the falling rate period, and (iii) the total drying time. Assume linear falling rate behavior. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Data extraction (1 mark)** $$X_1 = 0.38, \quad X_2 = 0.04, \quad X_c = 0.18, \quad X^* = 0.02 \text{ (kg/kg dry solid)}$$ $$L_s = 25 \text{ kg/m}^2, \quad N_c = 0.50 \text{ kg/hr·m}^2$$ **Step 2: Check drying regime (1 mark)** $X_1 = 0.38 > X_c = 0.18$ → CRP exists $X_2 = 0.04 < X_c = 0.18$ → FRP also exists $X_2 = 0.04 > X^* = 0.02$ → Final moisture is above equilibrium (drying stops at $X_2$) **Step 3: Constant rate period (2 marks)** $$t_c = \frac{L_s}{N_c}(X_1 - X_c) = \frac{25}{0.50}(0.38 - 0.18) = 50 \times 0.20$$ $$\boxed{\, t_c = 10 \text{ hours} \,}$$ **Step 4: Falling rate period (2 marks)** $$t_f = \frac{L_s}{N_c}(X_c - X_2) = \frac{25}{0.50}(0.18 - 0.04) = 50 \times 0.14$$ $$\boxed{\, t_f = 7 \text{ hours} \,}$$ **Step 5: Total drying time (1 mark)** $$t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 10 + 7$$ $$\boxed{\, t_{total} = 17 \text{ hours} \,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Most Common Mistakes on This Topic:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert wet-basis to dry-basis** (causes 50% error in numerical answers). Always check: *"Is the moisture given in wet or dry basis?"* > 2. **Using $X^*$ instead of $X_2$** in the falling rate formula when $X_2 > X^*$. The correct upper limit is $X_2$ (the actual final moisture), not $X^*$. > 3. **Computing $L_s$ incorrectly** — students often forget to divide dry solid mass by drying area. Without $L_s$, the entire numerical is wrong. > 4. **Assuming CRP exists for all $X_1 > X_c$ cases** without checking the sign of $(X_1 - X_c)$. If $X_1 < X_c$, $t_c = 0$ (no constant rate period). > 5. **Unit mismatches** — if $N_c$ is given in kg/hr·m², time comes out in **hours** (consistent). If $N_c$ is in kg/s·m², convert to kg/hr·m² first by multiplying by 3600. > 6. **Forgetting the factor of "dry solid" in $L_s$** — the moisture content must be on a **dry basis** for the formulas to be dimensionally consistent. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📌 Use this section as your **last-night KTU revision checklist**: - ✅ **Two moisture bases** — always convert to **dry basis** before applying drying formulas. Formula: $X = X_w/(1 - X_w)$ - ✅ **Four drying periods** — Initial transient (AB) → Constant rate (BC) → First falling rate (CD) → Second falling rate (DE) - ✅ **Constant rate period** is governed by **external mass transfer**; surface temperature = **wet-bulb temperature** - ✅ **Falling rate period** is governed by **internal mass transfer** (diffusion through solid) - ✅ **Critical moisture content $X_c$** is NOT a material property — depends on drying conditions, solid thickness - ✅ **Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** is the absolute lower limit — cannot be removed by air drying - ✅ **Time in CRP:** $t_c = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_c)$ - ✅ **Time in linear FRP:** $t_f = (L_s/N_c)(X_c - X_2)$ - ✅ **Total time (linear case):** $t_{total} = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_2)$ - ✅ **General FRP integral:** $t_f = L_s \int dX/N$ — solved graphically via $1/N$ vs $X$ plot - ✅ **If $X_1 \le X_c$:** no constant rate period; $t_c = 0$ - ✅ **Dry solid loading:** $L_s = m_s/A$ (must compute from given data) - ✅ **Free moisture = $X - X^*$** — only this is removable by air drying - ✅ **Bound moisture** is held by physical/chemical forces; **unbound moisture** behaves like pure water - ✅ **KTU's favorite question types:** derivation of $t_c$ formula (7 marks) + numerical on total drying time (7 marks) = 14 marks - ✅ **Always include units** in intermediate steps and final answer — KTU examiners award 0.5 mark for proper unit handling - ✅ **Linear FRP assumption is the KTU default** unless the problem explicitly says "non-linear" or "graphical" - ✅ **Higher air velocity → higher $N_c$ → shorter drying time** (this is why industrial dryers use high-velocity air) - ✅ **Industrial relevance:** drying consumes 12–20% of total industrial energy — accurate time estimation is critical for economics - ✅ **Cross-check** your answer using $t_{total} = (L_s/N_c)(X_1 - X_2)$ for the linear case > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In the 14-mark Module 3 question, examiners almost always test: (a) derivation of $t_c$ (7 marks) + (b) a numerical with both CRP and FRP (7 marks). Practice **at least 5 numerical problems** from previous KTU papers (Dec 2019, Dec 2022, June 2023, Dec 2023) covering: (i) $X_1 > X_c$ with $X_2 < X_c$, (ii) $X_1 < X_c$ (no CRP), and (iii) wet-to-dry basis conversion. Master these and you secure **a guaranteed 14/14 on this topic**.

topics.title: Equipment for Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equipment for Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying Equipment (Dryers)** are **unit operations devices** designed to remove a volatile liquid (most commonly **water**, but also organic solvents) from a solid, semi-solid, or liquid feed by **thermally driven evaporation** into a hot, dry gaseous medium (usually **air**, **flue gas**, or an **inert gas**). The driving force is the **partial pressure gradient** of the vapor at the solid–gas interface, governed simultaneously by heat transfer (supplying latent heat of vaporization) and mass transfer (diffusion of vapor into the bulk gas).

In KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, drying equipment is classified under **Convective Mass Transfer Operations with Simultaneous Heat Transfer**, distinguishing it from purely mechanical dewatering (filtration, centrifugation) or purely thermal evaporation (evaporators).

**Standard Engineering Metrics** used in dryer design:
- **Moisture content** (wet basis, $X_w$, or dry basis, $X_d$) in **kg water / kg dry solid**
- **Drying rate** $N = -\frac{m_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt}$ in **kg/m²·s**
- **Specific energy consumption** in **kJ/kg water evaporated**
- **Thermal efficiency** $\eta_{th}$ in **%**
- **Throughput / Capacity** in **kg wet feed / hr**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Laundry Line vs. The Industrial Dryer"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy**: Imagine you have just washed a cotton shirt and hang it on a clothesline on a windy, sunny day. Three things happen simultaneously: (1) **sun** provides heat to evaporate water, (2) **wind** carries away the water vapor, and (3) the **cotton fabric surface** allows moisture to diffuse outward.
>
> An **industrial dryer** is essentially the engineered, controlled, supercharged version of this clothesline. The "sun" is replaced by **finned steam coils, electric heaters, or hot flue gas**; the "wind" is replaced by a **calibrated blower** pushing heated air at a controlled velocity; and the "fabric surface" is replaced by **trays, belts, fluidized particles, or atomized droplets** that maximize surface area per unit volume.

The critical engineering difference is **uniformity and control**: a clothesline dries a shirt in 6 hours unevenly, while a **spray dryer** dries milk powder in **3 seconds** uniformly inside a stainless-steel chamber.

## 1.3 Classification Overview (KTU Syllabus Snapshot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU's Module 3 emphasizes dryers grouped by **(a) mode of operation** (batch/continuous), **(b) mode of heat transfer** (convection/conduction/radiation/dielectric), and **(c) physical state of feed** (solid/slurry/paste/liquid).

The principal dryers prescribed for the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| # | Dryer | Operation Mode | Heat Transfer Mode |
|---|-------|----------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Tray (Shelf) Dryer | Batch | Convection + Conduction |
| 2 | Tunnel Dryer | Continuous | Convection |
| 3 | Rotary Dryer | Continuous | Convection |
| 4 | Spray Dryer | Continuous | Convection |
| 5 | Fluidized Bed Dryer | Continuous | Convection |
| 6 | Drum Dryer | Continuous | Conduction |
| 7 | Freeze Dryer | Batch | Sublimation (Heat + Mass) |

These form the backbone of any KTU Board Exam question on Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Equipment Analysis — Operating Principle, Construction & Application

### 2.1.1 Tray (Shelf) Dryer — *The Workhorse of Batch Drying*

**Construction**: An insulated rectangular chamber housing a stack of **horizontal perforated trays** (typically 20–40) loaded manually. Each tray is supported on **hollow steel shelves** through which steam or hot water circulates. A **centrifugal blower** circulates heated air across the trays in a cross-flow or through-flow pattern.

**Operating Principle**:
- Heated air enters from one side, sweeps across the wet solids spread in a thin layer (1–5 cm), and exits saturated with vapor.
- Heat is supplied **simultaneously by convection** (from hot air) **and conduction** (from the steam-heated tray bottom).
- This dual-mode heat supply is the key reason tray dryers are versatile.

**Typical Applications**: Pharmaceuticals (heat-sensitive APIs), dyestuffs, pigments, catalysts, food products, granular pastes, and small-batch specialty chemicals.

**Performance Data (Typical)**:

$$
\eta_{th,tray} \approx 25\% - 40\%
$$

$$
\text{Drying Rate } N = 0.5 - 3.0 \ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}}
$$

**Advantages**: Low capital cost, simple operation, excellent for **heat-sensitive** and **toxic** materials (enclosed system), flexibility in handling different batches.

**Disadvantages**: High labor cost (batch loading/unloading), non-uniform drying, large floor area per unit capacity.

### 2.1.2 Tunnel Dryer — *The Continuous Conveyor Cousin of the Tray Dryer*

**Construction**: A long rectangular tunnel (10–25 m) through which **trays mounted on trolleys or a chain conveyor** move counter-current or co-current to the heated air stream.

**Operating Principle**: Trays are loaded at one end, travel slowly through zones of progressively changing air temperature and humidity (often with **multiple temperature zones**), and emerge dried at the other end.

**Typical Applications**: Ceramic articles, leather, lumber, extruded noodles, and bakery products.

> [!NOTE]
> **Co-current vs Counter-current**: In **co-current** flow, hottest air meets wettest solid → faster initial drying but milder final stage (good for **heat-sensitive** materials). In **counter-current** flow, hottest air meets driest solid → better final-stage moisture control but risk of overheating wet feed.

### 2.1.3 Rotary Dryer — *The Industrial Tumbler*

**Construction**: An inclined, rotating, hollow steel cylinder (length/diameter ratio ≈ 4–10) fitted with internal **lifting flights (baffles)**. Wet feed enters at the higher end; hot air flows either co-currently or counter-currently.

**Operating Principle**: Rotation lifts the solids via flights, then **cascades them as a curtain** through the hot gas stream. This curtain mechanism provides enormous gas–solid contact area, enabling high heat and mass transfer rates.

**Volume of Dryer (KTU Standard Formula)**:

$$
V = \frac{W_s}{R_v}
$$

where $V$ = dryer volume (m³), $W_s$ = mass of water to be evaporated (kg/hr), $R_v$ = volumetric evaporation rate (kg/m³·hr).

**Volumetric heat transfer coefficient (typical)**:

$$
U_v = 100 - 300 \ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{K}}
$$

**Typical Applications**: Fertilizers, ores, sand, minerals, sugar, cement, coal.

**Disadvantages**: Not suitable for very fine, sticky, or dusty materials (entrainment losses), high mechanical wear.

### 2.1.4 Spray Dryer — *The Atomization Marvel*

**Construction**: A tall cylindrical chamber with a conical bottom, fitted with an **atomizer** (centrifugal disk, pressure nozzle, or two-fluid nozzle) at the top. A high-capacity blower supplies heated air (often co-current).

**Operating Principle**: Liquid feed (solution, slurry, emulsion) is **atomized into 10–500 µm droplets** at the top. Each droplet is dried in a fraction of a second as it falls through the hot air, becoming a dry particle that is separated by **cyclone separators** and **bag filters**.

**Critical Equation — Droplet Drying Time**:

$$
t_d = \frac{\rho_p \cdot d_p^2 \cdot \Delta H_{vap}}{8 \cdot k_g \cdot (T_g - T_{sat}) \cdot M_w / M_v}
$$

where $d_p$ = droplet diameter, $\rho_p$ = density, $\Delta H_{vap}$ = latent heat, $k_g$ = gas thermal conductivity, $M_w, M_v$ = molecular weights.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why droplets dry so fast**: Surface area scales with $d_p^2$ but mass scales with $d_p^3$. Halving droplet diameter reduces mass by 8× but only increases surface-to-volume ratio by 2× — meaning **tiny droplets evaporate extremely fast**.

**Typical Applications**: Milk powder, instant coffee, detergent powders, pharmaceuticals (encapsulation), pigments, ceramics.

**Residence Time**: Typically **3–30 seconds** — making it ideal for highly heat-sensitive products.

### 2.1.5 Fluidized Bed Dryer — *The Boiling Sand Phenomenon*

**Construction**: A rectangular or cylindrical vessel with a **perforated distributor plate** at the bottom. Hot air is forced upward through the plate at a velocity equal to or exceeding the **minimum fluidization velocity**:

$$
u_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{d_p \cdot \rho_g}, \quad Re_{mf} \approx 20 \text{ to } 1000
$$

**Operating Principle**: At $u \geq u_{mf}$, the bed of particles behaves like a **boiling liquid** — intense mixing, enormous gas–solid contact area, and uniform temperature throughout the bed. Drying rates can be 5–10× higher than tray drying.

**Typical Applications**: Granular polymers, pharmaceuticals (granules), fertilizers, food grains.

**Critical Constraint**: Particle size **40 µm – 6 mm**. Below this, entrainment dominates; above this, channeling occurs.

### 2.1.6 Drum Dryer — *The Conduction Specialist*

**Construction**: A slowly rotating, internally steam-heated **metallic drum** (1–3 m diameter). A thin film of slurry is applied either by **dipping** (lower portion submerged in feed pan) or by **splashing** from an applicator roll.

**Operating Principle**: As the drum rotates, the slurry film (~0.1–1 mm thick) loses moisture into the heated drum surface; a **doctor blade** scrapes off the dried film as the drum completes one revolution.

**Heat Transfer Equation (drum surface)**:

$$
q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}
$$

where $U \approx 1000 - 2000 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = \pi D L$, and $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ is the log-mean temperature difference between steam and product.

**Typical Applications**: Starch, yeast, milk products, potato flakes, sodium-based chemicals.

### 2.1.7 Freeze Dryer (Lyophilizer) — *The Premium Preservation Technology*

**Operating Principle**: Water is removed by **sublimation** — solid ice transforms directly to vapor without passing through the liquid phase — under high vacuum (typically **10–100 Pa**). Heat is supplied conductively through shelves; vapor is captured on a cold condenser at **−40 °C to −80 °C**.

**Heat Transfer During Sublimation**:

$$
q = \frac{k_s}{\delta_s}(T_{shelf} - T_{ice})
$$

where $k_s$ is the thermal conductivity of the **dried product layer** and $\delta_s$ is its thickness. **Note:** As drying proceeds, $\delta_s$ grows, causing the sublimation rate to **decline sharply** with time — this is a classic KTU problem scenario.

**Typical Applications**: Injectable pharmaceuticals (vaccines, antibiotics), biological samples, premium instant coffee, astronaut food, archival preservation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** — every formula here is a high-probability KTU exam question.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $X_w = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{wet}} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{water}+m_{dry}}$ | Lab reporting |
| 2 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $X_d = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry}}$ | Design calculations |
| 3 | Conversion | $X_d = \dfrac{X_w}{1-X_w}$ | Switching units |
| 4 | Drying rate | $N = -\dfrac{m_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Tray/rotary capacity |
| 5 | Constant rate drying time | $t_c = \dfrac{m_s(X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A}$ | Batch time estimation |
| 6 | Falling rate (1st period) | $t_f = \dfrac{m_s}{A k_X}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Diffusion-limited drying |
| 7 | Latent heat supplied | $Q = W_s \cdot \Delta H_{vap}$ | Energy balance |
| 8 | Air heating (sensible) | $Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Blower heater sizing |
| 9 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{T_{in}-T_{out}}{T_{in}-T_{wb}}$ | Dryer performance |
| 10 | Rotary dryer volume | $V = W_s / R_v$ | Equipment sizing |
| 11 | Spray dryer droplet time | $t_d \propto d_p^{\,2}$ | Atomization design |
| 12 | Fluidization velocity | $u_{mf} = Re_{mf}\mu_g/(d_p\rho_g)$ | Bed design |
| 13 | Drum heat transfer | $q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Drum sizing |
| 14 | Psychrometric humidity | $Y = 0.622 \dfrac{p_w}{P-p_w}$ | Air-mass balance |
| 15 | Mass balance on dryer | $\dot{m}_{a,1}(Y_2-Y_1) = W_s$ | Air flow sizing |
| 16 | Freeze-drying (sublimation) | $q = k_s \Delta T / \delta_s$ | Lyophilization cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> All **vertical bars** in formulas use the math mode $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. For example, $\Delta T \vert_{LMTD}$ instead of $|\Delta T|$.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

**Pharmaceutical Industry**: Spray dryers and freeze dryers enable stable, water-soluble injectable drugs (e.g., **insulin, mRNA vaccines, antibiotics**). A 2024-era biotech facility cannot exist without these.

**Food Industry**: Spray-dried milk powder has a **shelf life of 2+ years** at ambient temperature — a feat impossible with refrigeration alone. This revolutionized global nutrition distribution post-WWII.

**Cement & Minerals**: Rotary dryers handle throughputs of **100–500 tonnes/hr**, dwarfing any batch dryer's capacity.

**Polymer & Specialty Chemicals**: Fluidized bed dryers are used for **PVC, polyethylene granules, and catalyst supports** with precise moisture control to within ±0.05%.

**Climate & Sustainability**: Selecting the right dryer can reduce specific energy consumption from **6,000 kJ/kg water** (inefficient tray dryer) down to **2,500–3,500 kJ/kg water** (efficient spray or fluidized bed dryer) — a **50%+ reduction** in $\text{CO}_2$ emissions.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention**: Every equation must be traceable, dimensionally consistent, and expressed in **SI units** unless explicitly stated otherwise. The following derivations follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** marking scheme: each logical step carries proportionate weight.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Drying Time for Batch Tray Dryer

### Problem Context
A wet solid is dried in a tray dryer from initial moisture content $X_1$ (dry basis) to final moisture content $X_2$. The constant rate drying period exists from $X_1$ to the critical moisture content $X_c$, followed by a falling rate period (assumed **first-order in $X - X^*$**) down to the equilibrium moisture content $X^*$ at $X_2$. Derive the **total drying time equation**.

### Step 1 — Define the Drying Rate

The general drying rate expression is:

$$
N = -\frac{m_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt}
$$

This says: *"the rate of moisture loss per unit area equals the negative rate of change of moisture content times the mass of bone-dry solid."*

### Step 2 — Constant Rate Period (from $X_1$ to $X_c$)

In the constant rate period, $N = N_c = \text{constant}$. Rearranging and integrating:

$$
\int_{0}^{t_c} dt = -\frac{m_s}{A\,N_c}\int_{X_1}^{X_c} dX
$$

$$
t_c = \frac{m_s(X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \, A}
$$

### Step 3 — Falling Rate Period (from $X_c$ to $X_2$)

In the falling rate period, the rate is proportional to the **free moisture** $(X - X^*)$:

$$
N = k_X (X - X^*)
$$

where $k_X$ has units of **kg/m²·s·(dry basis)**. Substituting into the rate definition:

$$
-\frac{m_s}{A}\frac{dX}{dt} = k_X(X - X^*)
$$

$$
\frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{k_X A}{m_s}\,dt
$$

### Step 4 — Integrate from $t_c$ to $t_f$ and $X_c$ to $X_2$

$$
\int_{X_c}^{X_2}\frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{k_X A}{m_s}\int_{t_c}^{t_f} dt
$$

$$
\ln(X - X^*)\Big\vert_{X_c}^{X_2} = -\frac{k_X A}{m_s}(t_f - t_c)
$$

$$
\ln(X_2 - X^*) - \ln(X_c - X^*) = -\frac{k_X A}{m_s}(t_f - t_c)
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right) = -\frac{k_X A}{m_s}(t_f - t_c)
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $t_f$

$$
t_f = t_c + \frac{m_s}{k_X A}\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Total Drying Time

$$
\boxed{\,t_{total} = \frac{m_s(X_1 - X_c)}{N_c A} + \frac{m_s}{k_X A}\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation**: The first term captures the **surface-evaporation-dominated** stage; the second term captures the **internal-diffusion-dominated** stage. In pharmaceutical drying, the falling-rate term often dominates **70–90%** of total drying time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rotary Dryer Diameter and Length Sizing

### Problem Context
Design a counter-current rotary dryer to evaporate $W_s = 1{,}500$ kg/hr of water from a mineral ore. Volumetric evaporation rate is $R_v = 15$ kg/m³·hr, length-to-diameter ratio is $L/D = 6$, and mass velocity of gas $G = 1.2$ kg/m²·s. Find **(a)** dryer volume, **(b)** diameter, **(c)** length.

### Step 1 — Dryer Volume

$$
V = \frac{W_s}{R_v} = \frac{1500}{15} = 100 \ \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Cross-sectional Area

The volume of a cylinder is $V = A \cdot L = (\pi D^2/4) \cdot L$. Substituting $L = 6D$:

$$
V = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}\cdot 6D = \frac{3\pi D^3}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Diameter

$$
D^3 = \frac{2V}{3\pi} = \frac{2 \cdot 100}{3\pi} = \frac{200}{9.4248} = 21.22 \ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
D = (21.22)^{1/3} = 2.77 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 4 — Length

$$
L = 6D = 6 \times 2.77 = 16.6 \ \text{m}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification — Volumetric Gas Flow Rate

Cross-sectional area $A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (2.77)^2/4 = 6.03$ m². Then volumetric flow = $G \cdot A / \rho_g = 1.2 \cdot 6.03 / 1.2 = 6.03$ m³/s (assuming $\rho_g = 1.2$ kg/m³ for hot air). Check residence time: $t_{res} = L / u_g = 16.6 / 1.0 = 16.6$ s — reasonable for granular mineral drying.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spray Dryer Droplet Evaporation Time

### Problem Context
A spray dryer atomizes whole milk (density $\rho_p = 1{,}030$ kg/m³, initial droplet diameter $d_p = 200$ µm) into air at $T_g = 180$ °C. Surface temperature is at wet-bulb $T_{sat} = 70$ °C. Estimate the **drying time** of a single droplet using the simplified Ranz-Marshall correlation.

### Step 1 — Ranz-Marshall Nusselt Number for a Sphere

$$
Nu = 2.0 + 0.6 \, Re^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}
$$

For $d_p = 200$ µm, $Re \ll 1$, so $Nu \approx 2.0$.

### Step 2 — Heat Transfer Coefficient

$$
Nu = \frac{h d_p}{k_g} \ \Rightarrow \ h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_g}{d_p} = \frac{2.0 \cdot 0.030}{200 \times 10^{-6}} = 300 \ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^2\text{K}}
$$

(using $k_g = 0.030$ W/m·K for air at $\sim 100$ °C)

### Step 3 — Energy Balance for a Single Droplet

The droplet diameter decreases as water evaporates. Assuming quasi-steady-state with $T_{surface} = T_{sat}$:

$$
\rho_p \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \cdot \Delta H_{vap} \cdot \left(-\frac{dd_p}{dt}\right) = h \pi d_p^2 (T_g - T_{sat})
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify and Integrate

$$
-\frac{dd_p}{dt} = \frac{6 h (T_g - T_{sat})}{\rho_p \Delta H_{vap}}
$$

This is **independent of $d_p$** (the $d_p$ cancels!) — a classical result for the constant-rate drying of an isolated droplet. Integrating from $d_p = d_{p,0}$ to $d_p = 0$:

$$
t_d = \frac{\rho_p \, \Delta H_{vap} \, d_{p,0}}{6 h (T_g - T_{sat})}
$$

### Step 5 — Plug in Numbers

Using $\Delta H_{vap} = 2.4 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $T_g - T_{sat} = 110$ K:

$$
t_d = \frac{1030 \cdot 2.4 \times 10^6 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \cdot 300 \cdot 110} = \frac{494{,}400}{198{,}000} \approx 2.5 \ \text{s}
$$

### Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> A **200 µm milk droplet** dries in **2.5 seconds** — comfortably within the typical **3–30 second** residence time of a spray dryer chamber. This confirms why spray dryers dominate **heat-sensitive food and pharma applications**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Dryer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **single most KTU-relevant summary** for viva and exam answers. Memorize the *why* behind each row.

| Feed Form | Production Scale | Heat Sensitivity | Recommended Dryer | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid (solution, emulsion) | Continuous | High (e.g., milk, enzymes) | **Spray Dryer** | Atomization → massive area → seconds |
| Paste / Slurry | Continuous | Moderate | **Drum Dryer** | Thin film on hot drum, scraped off |
| Granular solid, free-flowing | Continuous | Low–moderate | **Fluidized Bed Dryer** | Excellent mixing, uniform T |
| Coarse chunks (ore, fertilizer) | Continuous | Low (robust) | **Rotary Dryer** | High throughput, rugged |
| Pharmaceutical batch | Small batch | Very high | **Tray / Freeze Dryer** | Gentle, controlled, sterile |
| Long strips (leather, noodles) | Continuous | Low–moderate | **Tunnel Dryer** | Counter-current flow, large area |
| Heat-sensitive bioproduct | Batch | Very high | **Freeze Dryer** | Sublimation → no thermal damage |

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Drying Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Drying Equipment] --> rootB[Batch Mode]

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Spray Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFeed[Liquid Feed Tank] --> nodePump[Feed Pump]
    nodePump --> nodeAtom[Atomizer Disk or Nozzle]
    nodeAir[Fresh Air Inlet] --> nodeHeater[Air Heater]
    nodeHeater --> nodeBlower[Blower Fan]
    nodeBlower --> nodeChamber[Drying Chamber]
    nodeAtom --> nodeChamber
    nodeChamber --> nodeCyclone[Primary Cyclone Separator]
    nodeChamber --> nodeBag[Bag Filter]
    nodeCyclone --> nodeProduct[Dried Product Outlet]
    nodeBag --> nodeProduct
    nodeChamber --> nodeExhaust[Wet Exhaust Air to Stack]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the flow**: Liquid feed and heated air converge at the drying chamber. The atomizer creates 10–500 µm droplets; each droplet dries in seconds. Dried powder is captured in cyclones and bag filters while moist exhaust leaves through the stack.

## 4.3 Block Architecture of a Rotary Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWetFeed[Wet Feed Hopper] --> nodeFeedInlet[Feed Inlet High End]
    nodeFeedInlet --> nodeDrum[Inclined Rotating Cylinder With Flights]
    nodeAirIn[Hot Air Blower Inlet] --> nodeAirHeater[Air Heater Furnace]
    nodeAirHeater --> nodeDrum
    nodeDrum --> nodeDischarge[Dried Solid Discharge Low End]
    nodeDrum --> nodeExhaust[Moist Air Cyclone Dust Collector]
    nodeExhaust --> nodeStack[Exhaust Stack]
```

## 4.4 Functional Layout of a Fluidized Bed Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeWetPart[Wet Granular Feed] --> nodeTopFeed[Top or Side Feed Inlet]
    nodeTopFeed --> nodeBed[Perforated Distributor Plate]
    nodeBed --> nodeFluidizedZone[Gas Solid Fluidized Zone]
    nodeBlower[Hot Air Blower] --> nodeHeater2[Steam or Electric Heater]
    nodeHeater2 --> nodePlenum[Windbox Plenum]
    nodePlenum --> nodeBed
    nodeFluidizedZone --> nodeDischarge2[Dried Product Discharge]
    nodeFluidizedZone --> nodeFinesBagFilter[Bag Filter for Entrained Fines]
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture of a Freeze Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeVial[Glass Vial With Frozen Product] --> nodeShelf[Heated Shelf -40 to 40 C]
    nodeShelf --> nodeDriedLayer[Dried Product Layer Forms]
    nodeDriedLayer --> nodeIceFront[Sublimation Ice Front Recedes]
    nodeIceFront --> nodeVaporFlow[Water Vapor Flows Outward]
    nodeVaporFlow --> nodeCondenser[Cold Condenser at -80 C]
    nodeCondenser --> nodeIceOnCond[Ice Deposits on Condenser Coil]
    nodeVacuum[Vacuum Pump Maintains 10 to 100 Pa] --> nodeChamberVac[Drying Chamber Under Vacuum]
```

## 4.6 Heat and Mass Transfer Pathways in a Tray Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSteam[Steam Supply 1.5 to 3 bar] --> nodeCoil[Hollow Shelf Coils]
    nodeCoil --> nodeCond[Heat Conducted Through Tray Floor]
    nodeHotAir[Hot Air 80 to 150 C] --> nodeConv[Convective Heat To Top Surface]
    nodeCond --> nodeWetLayer[Wet Solid Layer On Tray]
    nodeConv --> nodeWetLayer
    nodeWetLayer --> nodeEvap[Water Evaporates From Surface]
    nodeEvap --> nodeVapor[Vapor Joins Sweep Air]
    nodeVapor --> nodeExit[Moist Air Exhausts To Blower]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are **direct, definition-based, and require a short explanation** in **3–4 sentences** plus a **sketch or formula** where applicable.

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> *"Classify dryers based on the mode of heat transfer. Give one example for each class."*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines expected):**

Dryers are classified by **mode of heat transfer** into four categories:

1. **Convection Dryers**: Heat is supplied by hot gas (usually air) flowing over the wet solid. Examples: **Tray Dryer, Rotary Dryer, Spray Dryer, Fluidized Bed Dryer, Tunnel Dryer**.

2. **Conduction Dryers**: Heat is supplied through a hot surface in direct contact with the wet material. Examples: **Drum Dryer, Cylinder Dryer**.

3. **Radiation Dryers**: Heat is supplied by infrared lamps or radiant heaters. Examples: **Infrared Tunnel Dryer** used for paint drying and surface coatings.

4. **Dielectric / Microwave Dryers**: Heat is generated internally within the material by high-frequency electromagnetic fields. Examples: **Microwave Vacuum Dryer** used for specialty ceramics and pharmaceutical powders.

KTU Module 3 primarily emphasizes **convection dryers**, but recognizing all four modes is essential for board examinations.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Model]**

> *"Explain the principle of operation of a spray dryer with a neat sketch. Mention two industrial applications."*

**Model Answer (10–12 lines expected):**

In a **spray dryer**, the **liquid feed** (solution, slurry, or emulsion) is pumped to an **atomizer** (centrifugal disk, pressure nozzle, or two-fluid nozzle) located at the top of a large cylindrical drying chamber with a conical bottom.

The atomizer disperses the liquid into **fine droplets of 10–500 µm diameter**. These droplets come into contact with a **co-current stream of hot air** (typically **150–250 °C**), evaporate rapidly (within **3–30 seconds**), and form dry powder particles that fall to the conical bottom.

The dried product is separated from the moist exhaust air by **cyclone separators** and **bag filters**. The driving forces are **heat transfer** from hot gas to droplet and **mass transfer** of vapor away from the droplet.

**Two Industrial Applications**:
- **Milk powder** production (from whole milk or skim milk).
- **Detergent powder** and instant coffee manufacturing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **sub-part (a) — 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) — 7 marks**. Students must answer the **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B per module).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> *"A wet solid is dried from an initial moisture content of $X_1 = 0.40$ kg water/kg dry solid to a final moisture content of $X_2 = 0.05$ kg water/kg dry solid in a tray dryer. The critical moisture content is $X_c = 0.20$ and the equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.02$. The constant drying rate is $N_c = 1.5$ kg/m²·hr, and the falling-rate constant is $k_X = 0.8$ kg/m²·hr·(dry basis). The mass of bone-dry solid on the tray is $m_s = 50$ kg, and the tray area is $A = 1.5$ m². Calculate the total drying time."*

**Model Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Constant Rate Drying Time $t_c$**

$$
t_c = \frac{m_s(X_1 - X_c)}{N_c \cdot A} = \frac{50 \times (0.40 - 0.20)}{1.5 \times 1.5}
$$

$$
t_c = \frac{50 \times 0.20}{2.25} = \frac{10}{2.25} = 4.44 \ \text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Falling Rate Drying Time $t_f$**

$$
t_f = \frac{m_s}{k_X \cdot A}\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{50}{0.8 \times 1.5}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.20 - 0.02}{0.05 - 0.02}\right)
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{50}{1.2}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.18}{0.03}\right) = 41.67 \times \ln(6) = 41.67 \times 1.7918 = 74.66 \ \text{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Drying Time**

$$
t_{total} = t_c + t_f = 4.44 + 74.66 = 79.10 \ \text{h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight**: The falling-rate period dominates **94%** of total drying time. This is why diffusion-limited drying is the design bottleneck in pharmaceutical and food drying.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> *"Compare spray dryer, fluidized bed dryer, and rotary dryer based on: (i) feed form, (ii) mode of heat transfer, (iii) residence time, (iv) typical capacity, (v) two industrial applications each."*

**Model Answer (Tabular, expected):**

| Parameter | Spray Dryer | Fluidized Bed Dryer | Rotary Dryer |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Feed Form | Liquid (solution, slurry, emulsion) | Free-flowing granules (40 µm–6 mm) | Coarse lumps, crystals, pastes |
| (ii) Heat Transfer Mode | Convection (gas–droplet) | Convection (intense gas–solid) | Convection (cascading curtain) |
| (iii) Residence Time | 3–30 seconds | 2–60 minutes | 15–60 minutes |
| (iv) Typical Capacity | 100 kg/hr – 25 tonnes/hr (water evap.) | 50 kg/hr – 50 tonnes/hr (feed) | 1 – 500 tonnes/hr (feed) |
| (v) Applications | Milk powder, instant coffee, detergent | Polymer granules, fertilizers, salt | Ore, cement raw mix, coal, sugar |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> *"Design a rotary dryer to evaporate 2,000 kg of water per hour from a fertilizer grade (NPK). The volumetric evaporation rate is $R_v = 18$ kg/m³·hr, and the recommended length-to-diameter ratio is $L/D = 8$. Calculate the (i) dryer volume, (ii) diameter, and (iii) length. Also estimate the heat duty if the latent heat of vaporization is 2,400 kJ/kg."*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dryer Volume**

$$
V = \frac{W_s}{R_v} = \frac{2000}{18} = 111.11 \ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Diameter**

Using $V = (\pi D^2/4) \cdot L = (\pi D^2/4) \cdot 8D = 2\pi D^3$:

$$
D^3 = \frac{V}{2\pi} = \frac{111.11}{6.2832} = 17.68 \ \text{m}^3
$$

$$
D = (17.68)^{1/3} = 2.60 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Length**

$$
L = 8D = 8 \times 2.60 = 20.8 \ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Heat Duty**

$$
Q = W_s \cdot \Delta H_{vap} = 2000 \times 2400 = 4.8 \times 10^6 \ \text{kJ/hr}
$$

Converting to kW: $Q = 4.8 \times 10^6 / 3600 = 1333.3 \ \text{kW}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often confuse **volumetric heat transfer coefficient $U_v$** (W/m³·K) with **volumetric evaporation rate $R_v$** (kg/m³·hr). They are completely different — $R_v$ gives **mass of water removed per unit dryer volume per hour**, while $U_v$ is used in **heat transfer rate** equations. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> *"With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a fluidized bed dryer. What is the condition for minimum fluidization? Write the Ergun equation."*

**Model Answer:**

**Construction**: A fluidized bed dryer consists of a **rectangular or cylindrical vessel** with a **perforated distributor plate** at the bottom. Hot air is supplied via a **blower** through a **windbox (plenum chamber)** below the distributor. The wet granular feed is introduced either from the top or through a side inlet. Fines entrained in the exhaust are recovered via a **bag filter**.

**Working**: When the air velocity exceeds the **minimum fluidization velocity $u_{mf}$**, the bed of particles begins to behave like a **boiling liquid** — particles are suspended, well-mixed, and exhibit intense gas–solid contact. Moisture evaporates rapidly and is carried away by the exhaust air. The dried product is discharged from the side or bottom.

**Condition for Minimum Fluidization**: The drag force on the particles equals the net buoyant weight of the bed:

$$
\Delta P \cdot A = (1-\varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_p - \rho_g) g \cdot A \cdot H_{mf}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
u_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu_g}{d_p \cdot \rho_g}, \quad Re_{mf} \approx \frac{d_p \, u_{mf} \rho_g}{\mu_g}
$$

**Ergun Equation** (pressure drop through a packed/fixed bed):

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{H} = \frac{150 (1-\varepsilon)^2 \mu_g u}{\varepsilon^3 d_p^2} + \frac{1.75 (1-\varepsilon) \rho_g u^2}{\varepsilon^3 d_p}
$$

The first term dominates in the **laminar (viscous) regime** ($Re < 20$); the second dominates in the **turbulent (inertial) regime** ($Re > 1000$). At $u = u_{mf}$, the pressure drop equals the bed weight per unit area.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students lose marks in Drying Equipment questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion** between **wet basis** and **dry basis** moisture content. KTU's *Treybal* notation uses dry basis — converting back to wet basis incorrectly is a classic 2-mark loss.
>
> 2. **Neglecting the latent heat of vaporization** in energy balances. Some students use sensible heat only and get unrealistic drying times.
>
> 3. **Confusing $u_{mf}$ (minimum fluidization velocity)** with $u_t$ (terminal settling velocity). $u_{mf} < u_t$; operating between them ensures stable fluidization without entrainment.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to draw a labelled diagram** in descriptive answers. KTU awards **1–2 marks** specifically for a neat sketch with components labelled.
>
> 5. **Mixing up co-current and counter-current** in tunnel and rotary dryer problems — they have **opposite** temperature profiles and **opposite** applications (heat-sensitive vs. moisture-sensitive).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — read this 5 minutes before walking into the exam hall.

### Core Equipment Knowledge
- **Tray Dryer** → Batch, dual-mode heat (convection + conduction), flexible but labor-intensive.
- **Tunnel Dryer** → Continuous conveyor version of tray dryer; long residence time.
- **Rotary Dryer** → Tumbling curtain mechanism; coarse, robust materials; high throughput.
- **Spray Dryer** → Atomization → 10–500 µm droplets → seconds; liquid feeds; heat-sensitive.
- **Fluidized Bed Dryer** → Boiling bed at $u \geq u_{mf}$; granular feeds; high heat transfer.
- **Drum Dryer** → Conduction via heated rotating drum; pastes and slurries.
- **Freeze Dryer** → Sublimation under vacuum; ultra-heat-sensitive pharmaceuticals.

### Critical Formulae (Must Memorize)
- $t_c = m_s(X_1 - X_c)/(N_c A)$
- $t_f = (m_s/k_X A)\ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$
- $V_{rotary} = W_s/R_v$
- $t_{droplet} \propto d_p^{\,2}$ (classical spray-drying result)
- $\Delta P_{bed}$ via **Ergun equation**
- $\eta_{th} = (T_{in} - T_{out})/(T_{in} - T_{wb})$

### Industrial-Application Mapping
- **Liquid + heat-sensitive** → Spray Dryer
- **Granular + continuous** → Fluidized Bed
- **Coarse + rugged** → Rotary
- **Paste + thin film** → Drum
- **Pharma + ultra-pure** → Freeze Dryer

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Always state units explicitly (kg, m², m³·hr, etc.).
- Always include a labelled diagram for descriptive answers.
- Convert moisture content basis correctly: $X_d = X_w/(1 - X_w)$.
- Distinguish $u_{mf}$ vs $u_t$.
- Mention co-current vs counter-current explicitly when relevant.

### Conceptual Cross-Links
- **Drying** sits at the intersection of **heat transfer** (conduction, convection, radiation) and **mass transfer** (diffusion in solids and gases).
- It is **simultaneous**, not sequential — unlike evaporation (purely thermal) or absorption (purely mass).
- The **psychrometric chart** is the primary tool for analyzing gas-phase behavior; Module 2 of KTU builds the foundation.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Equipment for Drying**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEPOT413 — Heat & Mass Transfer*
