# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *Define the term "Packing Factor" used in the design of packed columns. State its units. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`*

**Model Answer:**

> The **packing factor $F_p$** is an empirical constant that characterizes the capacity and pressure-drop performance of a particular packing geometry. It is defined as:
> $$F_p = \dfrac{a}{\varepsilon^3}$$
> where $a$ is the specific surface area ($\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$) and $\varepsilon$ is the void fraction (dimensionless).
>
> **Units:** $\mathbf{m^{-1}}$ (SI) or $\mathbf{ft^{-1}}$ (older texts).
>
> A *lower* value of $F_p$ indicates a more open, efficient packing (low pressure drop, high flooding limit). It is used in the **Generalized Pressure Drop Correlation (GPDC, Eckert Chart)** to compute flooding gas velocity and operating pressure drop.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for units/significance)*

---

### **Q2.** *Distinguish between Random Packings and Structured Packings. Give two examples of each. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Random Packings | Structured Packings |
|---|---|---|
| **Installation** | Dumped randomly into the column | Precisely stacked modules |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Efficiency (HETP)** | Moderate | Very high (low HETP) |
| **Pressure drop** | Moderate–High | Very low |
| **Scale-up predictability** | Difficult | Easy |
| **Examples** | Raschig ring, Pall ring, Berl saddle, Intalox saddle | Sulzer Mellapak, Goodloe wire mesh, Montz-Pak, Flexeramic |

*(3 marks: 1 for each difference + 1 for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 4 — Internal Choice (Answer ANY ONE of the following)**

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** *With the help of neat sketches, describe the constructional features and typical applications of the following packings: (i) Raschig ring, (ii) Pall ring, (iii) Berl saddle, (iv) Intalox saddle. Discuss the relative advantages of Pall rings over Raschig rings.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A packed absorption column is to be designed for a gas flow of $G = 1.2\ \text{kg/s}$ and a liquid flow of $L = 2.5\ \text{kg/s}$. The packing selected is 25-mm metal Pall rings, having $a = 215\ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$ and $\varepsilon = 0.94$. The gas-side overall mass transfer coefficient is $K_G\,a = 0.08\ \text{kmol/(m}^3\cdot\text{s)}$ and the operating line is essentially straight. Determine the hydraulic diameter of the packing.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) (7 marks)**

**Raschig Ring (1.5 marks):**
A hollow right circular cylinder with length equal to its outer diameter. Available in ceramic (acid-resistant) and metal (carbon steel, stainless steel). Sharp internal edges create turbulence but cause higher pressure drop. Used historically in absorption of $\text{HCl}$, $\text{SO}_2$, and in small laboratory columns.

**Pall Ring (1.5 marks):**
A Raschig ring with **rectangular windows punched in the side walls and tongues bent inward** into the bore. The tongues redistribute the liquid film over the entire surface, dramatically increasing the effective wetted area and lowering pressure drop compared to a plain Raschig ring of the same nominal size. Common in $\text{CO}_2$ absorption, ammonia scrubbing.

**Berl Saddle (1 mark):**
A curved, saddle-shaped ceramic piece (resembles a horse collar). The curved, non-nesting geometry means adjacent saddles do not sit flat on each other, leaving more open void space and providing a continuously varying surface. Lower pressure drop than Raschig rings.

**Intalox Saddle (1 mark):**
A modified Berl saddle with a flared, notched rim. The "tongue" protrusions further break the liquid film, increasing turbulence. Available in ceramic, metal ("IMTP"), and plastic. Used in distillation, scrubbing, and $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ removal.

**Why Pall rings are better than Raschig rings (2 marks):**
- The bent tongues **destroy the boundary layer**, increasing mass-transfer coefficient $K_G$.
- Higher void fraction → **lower pressure drop** (typically 30–50 % less).
- Higher flooding velocity → higher throughput capacity.
- Better liquid distribution → **no dry spots** or wall-channeling.
- Mechanical strength is comparable when made of metal.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) (7 marks)**

**Given:** $a = 215\ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$, $\varepsilon = 0.94$.

**Formula:**
$$D_h = \dfrac{4\,\varepsilon}{a}$$

**Calculation:**

$$
D_h = \dfrac{4 \times 0.94}{215}
$$

$$
D_h = \dfrac{3.76}{215}
$$

$$
D_h \approx 0.01749\ \text{m} \approx 17.5\ \text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The hydraulic diameter of the 25-mm Pall-ring packing is **$D_h \approx 17.5\ \text{mm}$**. This value will be used in computing the Reynolds number to verify whether the gas flow is laminar, transitional, or turbulent inside the packing channels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often mistakenly substitute the nominal packing size (25 mm) as the hydraulic diameter. Always apply the formula $D_h = 4\varepsilon/a$ — the nominal size is **not** the hydraulic diameter.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** *List the desirable properties of an ideal tower packing. Explain how the packing factor, specific surface area, and void fraction influence the design and operation of a packed column.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A packed column uses 50-mm ceramic Intalox saddles. The data given is: $a = 120\ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$, $\varepsilon = 0.78$, $F_p = 145\ \text{m}^{-1}$. For a gas–liquid system, the operating superficial mass velocities are $G' = 1.5\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$ and $L' = 4.0\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$. Calculate the hydraulic diameter and discuss how an increase in the void fraction (for the same surface area) would affect the column's pressure drop.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) (7 marks)**

**Desirable Properties of an Ideal Tower Packing (3.5 marks):**

1. **High specific surface area** $a$ → large interfacial area → high mass transfer.
2. **High void fraction** $\varepsilon$ → low pressure drop, high gas-handling capacity.
3. **Low packing factor** $F_p$ → efficient in GPDC correlations.
4. **Chemically inert** to both gas and liquid streams.
5. **Mechanically strong** to withstand bed weight and operating vibrations.
6. **Cheap and easily available** in standard sizes (typically 15 mm – 75 mm).
7. **Good wettability** by the liquid (ceramic > metal > plastic for water-like liquids).
8. **Low weight** to reduce column support cost.
9. **Consistent geometry** → predictable, repeatable performance.
10. **Resistance to fouling, scaling, and thermal shock.**

**Influence of Properties on Design (3.5 marks):**

- **Specific surface area $a$:** Determines the interfacial area for mass transfer. A higher $a$ means the column can be **shorter** for the same duty. But it also implies smaller void passages → higher pressure drop → risk of premature flooding.

- **Void fraction $\varepsilon$:** Determines the free volume for gas flow. Higher $\varepsilon$ → **lower pressure drop** → lower blower cost → higher flooding limit. However, the surface area is also typically lower (geometric trade-off).

- **Packing factor $F_p$:** An empirical combination $F_p = a/\varepsilon^3$ used in the Eckert GPDC. **Lower $F_p$ = more open packing** = lower pressure drop at a given gas/liquid flow = more economical operation.

The designer must **balance** these three — a high-$a$, high-$\varepsilon$, low-$F_p$ packing is the engineering optimum, but rarely achievable in practice.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) (7 marks)**

**Given:** $a = 120\ \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$, $\varepsilon = 0.78$.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic Diameter:**

$$
D_h = \dfrac{4\,\varepsilon}{a} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.78}{120}
$$

$$
D_h = \dfrac{3.12}{120} = 0.026\ \text{m} = 26\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Effect of Increasing Void Fraction (for the same $a$):**

From the hydraulic-diameter formula $D_h = 4\varepsilon/a$:
- An increase in $\varepsilon$ → $D_h$ **increases linearly**.
- Larger hydraulic diameter → **lower flow velocity** at the same superficial velocity.
- Therefore, the **Reynolds number decreases**: $Re_p = \rho_G u_G D_h / \mu_G \downarrow$.
- The **Ergun equation** (for pressure drop in packed beds) gives:

$$
\dfrac{\Delta P}{Z} = \dfrac{150\,(1-\varepsilon)^2}{\varepsilon^3} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_G\,u_G}{d_p^2} + \dfrac{1.75\,(1-\varepsilon)}{\varepsilon^3} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_G\,u_G^2}{d_p}
$$

- Both the viscous term (1/$\varepsilon^3$) and the inertial term (1/$\varepsilon^3$) **decrease** as $\varepsilon$ increases.
- Therefore, **the pressure drop $\Delta P$ decreases monotonically as $\varepsilon$ increases**, and the **flooding velocity $u_{G,f}$ increases** (per the GPDC chart).

**Conclusion:** The hydraulic diameter is **26 mm**, and increasing void fraction (for the same $a$) lowers the pressure drop, raises the flooding limit, and reduces operating cost — a clear engineering benefit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** When asked to "list the desirable properties," students often give a long, unstructured list and miss the *elaboration on $F_p$, $a$, $\varepsilon$*. KTU examiners award marks for **mapping each property to its engineering effect** — not just naming it. Always include: "**Property → effect on HETP / pressure drop / flooding**."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✔ **Tower packings** = inert solid contact devices placed inside columns to promote gas–liquid contact.
- ✔ **Three classes:** Random (dumped), Structured (regular), and Grid packings.
- ✔ **Common random packings:** Raschig ring, Pall ring, Berl saddle, Intalox saddle, Lessing ring, Tellerette.
- ✔ **Common structured packings:** Sulzer (Mellapak), Goodloe (wire mesh), Montz-Pak, Flexeramic.
- ✔ **Most important properties:** Specific surface area $a$, void fraction $\varepsilon$, packing factor $F_p$, bulk density, HETP, HTU.
- ✔ **Hydraulic diameter formula:** $\displaystyle D_h = \dfrac{4\,\varepsilon}{a}$ — units are **metres**.
- ✔ **HTU formula:** $\displaystyle H_{OG} = \dfrac{G_s}{K_G \, a \, P_{BM}}$ — gas-side height of transfer unit.
- ✔ **Packing factor definition:** $\displaystyle F_p = \dfrac{a}{\varepsilon^3}$ — units $\mathbf{m^{-1}}$.
- ✔ **Lower $F_p$ = better packing** (low $\Delta P$, high flooding limit).
- ✔ **Trade-off:** Higher $a$ → better mass transfer but higher $\Delta P$.
- ✔ **Materials:** Ceramic (corrosion-resistant, brittle), metal (strong, not for strong acids), plastic (cheap, low-temperature limit).
- ✔ **Pall rings outperform Raschig rings** in: void fraction, pressure drop, mass-transfer coefficient, flooding velocity.
- ✔ **Structured packings** dominate in **vacuum distillation** where $\Delta P$ must be very low.
- ✔ **Grid packings** are used in **cooling towers** (very large columns, low efficiency).
- ✔ For KTU numerical problems, **always include units** of $F_p$ and $D_h$ — losing unit marks is the #1 deduction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solubility of Gas in a Liquid and Choice of Solvent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Solubility of Gas in a Liquid and Choice of Solvent # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Gas Solubility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solubility of a Gas in a Liquid** is the maximum amount of a gas that can dissolve in a given quantity of liquid at a specified temperature and pressure, under equilibrium conditions. It is fundamentally governed by **Henry's Law** in mass transfer engineering. **Henry's Law (1803) — The Governing Equation:** At constant temperature, the partial pressure of a gas above a liquid is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the dissolved gas in the liquid at dilute concentrations. Mathematically, Henry's Law is expressed as: $$p_A = H \cdot x_A$$ Where: - $p_A$ = Partial pressure of gas A in the gas phase (Pa or atm) - $H$ = Henry's Law constant (Pa or atm) - $x_A$ = Mole fraction of gas A dissolved in the liquid (dimensionless) **Alternate forms commonly used in Mass Transfer:** $$y_A = m \cdot x_A \quad \text{(when expressed in mole fractions of both phases)}$$ $$p_A = H \cdot C_A \quad \text{(when expressed in concentration, where } C_A \text{ is in kmol/m}^3\text{)}$$ Where **m** is the slope of the equilibrium curve (also called the **distribution coefficient** or **equilibrium constant**), and is dimensionless when both phases are expressed in mole fractions. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Magnet and Iron Filing Metaphor** > > Imagine a glass of cold water and a glass of hot water. If you drop sugar (analogous to gas molecules) into both: > - **Cold water** = more sugar dissolves (because it holds them tighter, like a stronger magnet) > - **Hot water** = less sugar dissolves (the "magnet" weakens with temperature) > > Similarly, when a gas is brought into contact with a liquid: > - Some gas molecules get "captured" (dissolved) by the liquid > - At equilibrium, the rate of gas molecules escaping the liquid equals the rate entering it > - **Henry's constant H acts like the "strength of the magnet"** — a low H means strong affinity (high solubility), and a high H means weak affinity (low solubility). **Engineering Reality:** - **Ammonia in water** → Very low H → Highly soluble → Strong chemical/physical interaction - **Oxygen in water** → Moderate H → Low solubility → Weak interaction - **Helium in water** → Very high H → Almost insoluble → Negligible interaction --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ > > **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)**: $T = 273.15 \text{ K}$, $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ > > **Typical Henry's Constant Units**: > - $\text{atm}$ (when $x_A$ is in mole fraction) > - $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{kPa}$ (SI units) > - $\text{atm·m}^3/\text{kmol}$ (when $C_A$ is in molar concentration) --- ## 1.4 Concept of "Choice of Solvent" In absorption operations (a key mass transfer unit operation), selecting the right solvent is critical. The criteria include: 1. **High Solubility** for the solute gas (low Henry's constant) 2. **High Selectivity** — should preferentially absorb the desired gas 3. **Low Volatility** — to minimize solvent loss in the gas stream 4. **Chemical Stability** — non-corrosive, non-decomposing 5. **Easy Regenerability** — must release the absorbed gas easily upon heating/pressure reduction 6. **Low Viscosity** — for better mass transfer rates 7. **Safety and Cost** — non-toxic, non-flammable, economical > [!NOTE] > **Example — CO₂ Capture**: Monoethanolamine (MEA) is preferred over water because CO₂ has a much higher solubility in MEA (chemical absorption) than in pure water (physical absorption). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Theory of Gas Dissolution When a gas-liquid interface is established, molecules continuously cross the interface in both directions: 1. **Gas → Liquid (Absorption)**: Driven by the concentration gradient 2. **Liquid → Gas (Desorption)**: Driven by the tendency of dissolved molecules to escape At **thermodynamic equilibrium**, these two rates are equal, and the bulk concentrations satisfy Henry's Law. --- ## 2.2 Operational Steps in Equilibrium Analysis The complete logical sequence for analyzing gas-liquid equilibrium is: - **Step 1:** Identify the solute gas (A) and the solvent (B). - **Step 2:** Obtain or determine the Henry's constant (H) at the operating temperature. - **Step 3:** Express the equilibrium relationship: $y_A^* = m \cdot x_A$ or $p_A^* = H \cdot x_A$. - **Step 4:** Determine the equilibrium curve $y_A^*$ vs. $x_A$ graphically. - **Step 5:** Compare with operating line to determine the driving force for mass transfer. - **Step 6:** Use the equilibrium data to design the absorber height (via HTU/NTU or Kremser equations). --- ## 2.3 Factors Affecting Gas Solubility | **Factor** | **Effect on Solubility** | **Reason** | |---|---|---| | ↑ Temperature | ↓ Solubility (for most gases) | Higher kinetic energy → gas escapes more easily | | ↑ Pressure | ↑ Solubility (linearly) | More gas molecules forced into solution | | Chemical reaction with solvent | ↑↑ Solubility (drastically) | Gas is consumed, allowing more to dissolve | | Polarity match | ↑ Solubility | "Like dissolves like" principle | | Molecular weight of gas | Variable | Depends on interactions | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this Formula Sheet — Essential for KTU Examinations** | **Equation** | **Formula** | **Application** | |---|---|---| | Henry's Law (Partial Pressure form) | $p_A = H \cdot x_A$ | Direct solubility calculation | | Henry's Law (Mole fraction form) | $y_A = m \cdot x_A$ | Absorption design | | Relation between H and m | $m = \dfrac{H}{P_T}$ | Unit conversion | | Temperature correction of H | $\ln\left(\dfrac{H_2}{H_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Find H at any T | | Pressure correction | $C_A = \dfrac{p_A}{H} \cdot \rho_{solvent}$ | Concentration calculation | | Raoult's Law (for comparison) | $p_A = x_A \cdot P_A^{sat}$ | Ideal solution baseline | | Modified Henry's (with concentration) | $p_A = H' \cdot C_A$ | Engineering calculations | | Equilibrium Curve | $Y_A^* = \dfrac{m \cdot X_A}{1 + (1-m)X_A}$ | McCabe-Thiele type plot | Where: - $P_T$ = Total system pressure - $\Delta H_{sol}$ = Heat of solution (J/mol), negative for exothermic dissolution - $R$ = Universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K) - $Y_A$, $X_A$ = Mole ratios (moles of A per mole of carrier/solvent) --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility **Where Gas Solubility Knowledge is Applied:** 1. **Air Pollution Control** — Removing SO₂, CO₂, H₂S from flue gas using absorbents 2. **Natural Gas Sweetening** — Removing H₂S and CO₂ using amine solutions 3. **Oxygenation of Water** — Designing aerators for water treatment plants 4. **Carbonated Beverages** — Maintaining CO₂ pressure in soft drinks 5. **Scrubbers in Industry** — Removing acidic gases (HCl, SO₃) from process streams 6. **Biomedical Engineering** — Blood oxygenators, decompression sickness calculations 7. **Petroleum Refining** — Hydrogenation processes, gas-oil contactors --- ## 2.6 Choice of Solvent — Selection Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Why Solvent Selection Matters:** A poor solvent choice can lead to: > - Excessive absorber heights (low solubility → low mass transfer driving force) > - High operating costs (excessive solvent circulation) > - Solvent loss (high volatility) > - Equipment corrosion (reactive solvents) | **Property** | **Ideal Solvent Characteristic** | **Reason** | |---|---|---| | Solubility (low H) | Very high | Reduces solvent circulation rate | | Selectivity | Very high | Minimizes co-absorption of other gases | | Vapor Pressure | Low | Prevents solvent loss with exit gas | | Viscosity | Low (< 5 cP) | Better mass transfer coefficients | | Thermal Stability | High | Withstands regeneration temperatures | | Chemical Reactivity | Inert with equipment | Prevents corrosion | | Cost | Low | Economic viability | | Toxicity | Low | Worker safety and environmental | **Examples of Common Solvent Choices:** | **Gas to be Absorbed** | **Preferred Solvent** | **Type** | |---|---|---| | CO₂ | Monoethanolamine (MEA), DEA, MDEA | Chemical | | SO₂ | Water, NaOH solution, limestone slurry | Physical/Chemical | | H₂S | Amines, Fe-EDTA, ZnO | Chemical | | NH₃ | Water | Physical | | HCl | Water | Physical | | Benzene vapors | Heavy oil, absorbents | Physical | | VOCs | Activated carbon, silicone oil | Physical | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Henry's Law from Thermodynamic Principles ### Starting Point: Chemical Potential Equality at Equilibrium At gas-liquid equilibrium, the chemical potential of the dissolved gas equals that in the gas phase: $$\mu_A^{gas} = \mu_A^{liquid}$$ ### Step 1: Express Chemical Potential in Gas Phase For an ideal gas mixture, the chemical potential is: $$\mu_A^{gas} = \mu_A^{0,gas}(T) + RT \ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right)$$ Where: - $\mu_A^{0,gas}(T)$ = Standard chemical potential of A in gas phase at temperature T - $p^0$ = Standard pressure (1 atm or 101.325 kPa) - $R$ = Universal gas constant ### Step 2: Express Chemical Potential in Liquid Phase For a real solution, the chemical potential is: $$\mu_A^{liquid} = \mu_A^{0,liq}(T) + RT \ln(\gamma_A \cdot x_A)$$ Where: - $\gamma_A$ = Activity coefficient of A in the liquid - $x_A$ = Mole fraction of A in liquid ### Step 3: Equate the Two Chemical Potentials $$\mu_A^{0,gas}(T) + RT \ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right) = \mu_A^{0,liq}(T) + RT \ln(\gamma_A \cdot x_A)$$ ### Step 4: Isolate the Partial Pressure $$RT \ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right) = \left[\mu_A^{0,liq}(T) - \mu_A^{0,gas}(T)\right] + RT \ln(\gamma_A \cdot x_A)$$ $$\ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_A^{0,liq} - \mu_A^{0,gas}}{RT} + \ln(\gamma_A) + \ln(x_A)$$ ### Step 5: Define Henry's Constant The Henry's constant is defined as: $$H = p^0 \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{\mu_A^{0,gas} - \mu_A^{0,liq}}{RT}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\gamma_A^\infty}$$ Where $\gamma_A^\infty$ is the activity coefficient at infinite dilution. ### Step 6: Simplify for Ideal Dilute Solution For **ideal dilute solutions** ($\gamma_A \to \gamma_A^\infty$ ≈ constant, and at infinite dilution): $$p_A = H \cdot x_A$$ This is **Henry's Law** ✓ --- ## 3.2 Temperature Dependence of Henry's Constant (van't Hoff Type) The Henry's constant varies with temperature according to: $$\dfrac{d(\ln H)}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{RT^2}$$ ### Step-by-Step Integration: **Step 1:** Separate variables $$d(\ln H) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{RT^2} \, dT$$ **Step 2:** Integrate between two temperature limits $$\int_{H_1}^{H_2} d(\ln H) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{RT^2} \, dT$$ **Step 3:** Assume $\Delta H_{sol}$ is constant over the temperature range $$\ln\left(\dfrac{H_2}{H_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left[-\dfrac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$ **Step 4:** Final Result $$\boxed{\ln\left(\dfrac{H_2}{H_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)}$$ **Interpretation:** - For most gases, dissolution is **exothermic** ($\Delta H_{sol} < 0$) - As $T_2 > T_1$, $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1) < 0$, making $\ln(H_2/H_1) < 0$, so $H_2 < H_1$ - Wait — correction: For exothermic dissolution, H **increases** with T, meaning solubility **decreases** with temperature ✓ --- ## 3.3 Conversion Between Different Forms of Henry's Law ### Form 1 (Partial Pressure form): $$p_A = H \cdot x_A$$ ### Form 2 (Mole Fraction form, m): $$y_A = m \cdot x_A$$ ### Derivation of Relationship: From Dalton's Law for the gas phase: $$p_A = y_A \cdot P_T$$ Substituting into Form 1: $$y_A \cdot P_T = H \cdot x_A$$ $$y_A = \dfrac{H}{P_T} \cdot x_A$$ Therefore: $$m = \dfrac{H}{P_T}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** When $H < P_T$, we get $m < 1$ (favorable equilibrium for absorption). When $H > P_T$, we get $m > 1$ (unfavorable equilibrium). --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Oxygen has a Henry's constant of $4.01 \times 10^4$ atm at 25°C in water. Air at 1 atm contains 21% O₂. Find the mole fraction of O₂ dissolved in water at equilibrium. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the partial pressure of O₂ $$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P_T = 0.21 \times 1 = 0.21 \text{ atm}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Henry's Law $$p_{O_2} = H \cdot x_{O_2}$$ $$0.21 = 4.01 \times 10^4 \cdot x_{O_2}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $x_{O_2}$ $$x_{O_2} = \dfrac{0.21}{4.01 \times 10^4} = 5.236 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Result:** $x_{O_2} = 5.24 \times 10^{-6}$ (very low — confirms O₂ is sparingly soluble in water) --- ## 3.5 Solvent Selection: Step-by-Step Engineering Analysis **Given:** Design an absorber to remove 95% of SO₂ from a flue gas stream using an appropriate solvent. **Step 1: Identify the system** - Solute: SO₂ (acidic, water-soluble) - Carrier gas: N₂, CO₂, O₂ - Possible solvents: Water, NaOH, Lime (Ca(OH)₂), Amines **Step 2: Compare Henry's constants at operating T (say 50°C)** | Solvent | H (atm) | Relative Solubility | |---|---|---| | Water | ~ 50 | Moderate | | NaOH (5%) | Effectively infinite (chemical absorption) | Very High | | Ca(OH)₂ slurry | Very high | Very High | **Step 3: Apply Selection Criteria** - Solubility: NaOH > Lime > Water ✓ - Cost: Water < Lime < NaOH - Regenerability: Water (easy) > NaOH (difficult) - Disposal: Lime → CaSO₃ (gypsum — useful) > NaOH → Na₂SO₃ (waste) **Step 4: Final Selection** For **single-use throwaway** scrubbers → **Lime slurry** (economical, gives useful gypsum byproduct) For **regenerative** systems with high efficiency → **NaOH or amines** --- ## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix for Solvent Choice | **Decision Criterion** | **Weight (%)** | **Water Score** | **NaOH Score** | **Amine Score** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Solubility | 25 | 5 | 9 | 10 | | Cost | 20 | 10 | 5 | 4 | | Regenerability | 20 | 10 | 6 | 9 | | Selectivity | 15 | 6 | 7 | 9 | | Stability | 10 | 10 | 9 | 8 | | Safety | 10 | 10 | 8 | 7 | | **Weighted Score** | **100** | **8.05** | **7.25** | **7.85** | **Interpretation:** For general-purpose absorption, water remains highly competitive due to cost and availability, but specialty gases need specialty solvents. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Gas-Liquid Equilibrium Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Gas Inlet
yA_in] --> nodeB[Gas-Liquid Contact Zone
Absorber] nodeB --> nodeC[Gas Outlet
yA_out] nodeC --> nodeD[Lean Gas Stream] nodeB --> nodeE[Liquid Outlet
xA_out] nodeE --> nodeF[Rich Solvent] nodeG[Solvent Inlet
xA_in] --> nodeB nodeH[Equilibrium Curve
yA_star vs xA] -.-> nodeB nodeH --> nodeI[Henry Law Zone
Low Concentration Linear] nodeH --> nodeJ[Non-Linear Zone
High Concentration] nodeI --> nodeK[Operating Line
Mass Balance] nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL[Driving Force
yA - yA_star] nodeL --> nodeM[Mass Transfer Rate] ``` ## 4.2 Solvent Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start: Identify Gas to Absorb] --> nodeB{Is Gas Reactive?} nodeB -->|Yes - Acidic| nodeC[Consider Alkaline Solvent
NaOH, Amines, Lime] nodeB -->|Yes - Basic| nodeD[Consider Acidic Solvent
Water, H2SO4] nodeB -->|No - Inert| nodeE{Is High Solubility Needed?} nodeE -->|Yes| nodeF[Check Henry Constant
Look for H less than P] nodeE -->|No| nodeG[Use Water or Inert Oil] nodeC --> nodeH{Regeneration Required?} nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Use Amines
MEA, DEA, MDEA] nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Use NaOH or Lime
Single Pass] nodeF --> nodeK{Selectivity Important?} nodeK -->|Yes| nodeL[Use Specialty Solvent
Mixed Amines] nodeK -->|No| nodeM[Use Water or Cheap Solvent] nodeI --> nodeN[Final Selection] nodeJ --> nodeN nodeL --> nodeN nodeM --> nodeN nodeG --> nodeN nodeN --> nodeO[End: Final Solvent Chosen] ``` ## 4.3 Henry's Law Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input Parameters nodeA1[Partial Pressure pA] nodeA2[Temperature T] nodeA3[Henry Constant H] end subgraph Computation Block nodeB1[Equilibrium Relation
pA equals H times xA] nodeB2[Mole Fraction Form
yA equals m times xA] nodeB3[Concentration Form
CA equals pA by H] end subgraph Output Results nodeC1[Mole Fraction xA] nodeC2[Solubility CA] nodeC3[Equilibrium Curve] end nodeA1 --> nodeB1 nodeA2 --> nodeB3 nodeA3 --> nodeB1 nodeB1 --> nodeC1 nodeB2 --> nodeC1 nodeB3 --> nodeC2 nodeC1 --> nodeC3 nodeC2 --> nodeC3 ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Complete Absorption System ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph InputStage node1[Hot Gas Inlet
Containing Solute A] end subgraph ContactingStage node2[Absorber Column] node3[Packing or Trays] node4[Gas Liquid Interface] end subsection Equilibrium Stage node5[Henry Law Equilibrium
yA star equals m times xA] node6[Operating Line
From Mass Balance] node7[Driving Force
yA minus yA star] end subgraph OutputStage node8[Purified Gas Out] node9[Rich Solvent Out] end node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node2 node2 --> node8 node2 --> node9 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define Henry's Law. State its mathematical form and explain the significance of Henry's constant.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Henry's Law states that at constant temperature, the partial pressure of a gas in equilibrium with a solution is directly proportional to the mole fraction of the dissolved gas in the solution. $$p_A = H \cdot x_A$$ **Significance of Henry's Constant:** - A **low value of H** indicates **high solubility** of the gas in the liquid (strong affinity) - A **high value of H** indicates **low solubility** (weak affinity) - It is temperature-dependent and is the key parameter in absorption column design --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **List any six criteria for the selection of a solvent for gas absorption operations.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The six criteria for solvent selection are: 1. **High Solubility** — Low Henry's constant for the solute gas 2. **High Selectivity** — Should absorb the desired gas preferentially 3. **Low Volatility** — To minimize solvent loss with exit gas 4. **Chemical Stability** — Non-corrosive and thermally stable 5. **Low Viscosity** — For good mass transfer rates 6. **Easy Regenerability** — Should release absorbed gas upon heating/pressure swing --- ## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided) ### **Question A:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive Henry's Law from thermodynamic principles. State all assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** The Henry's constant for CO₂ in water at 25°C is $1.64 \times 10^3$ atm. Calculate the concentration of CO₂ (in mol/L) dissolved in water exposed to air containing 0.04% CO₂ at 1 atm total pressure. (7 marks) ### **Question B:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Discuss the factors affecting the solubility of a gas in a liquid. How does temperature influence gas solubility? (7 marks) **(b)** A flue gas containing 3% SO₂ is to be treated with water in a packed absorber at 30°C and 1 atm. The Henry's constant for SO₂ in water at 30°C is 50 atm. Determine: (i) the equilibrium mole fraction of SO₂ in water, and (ii) the value of m for this system. (7 marks) --- ## ✅ COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS ### **Solution to Question A:** #### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions:** 1. The gas phase behaves as an ideal gas 2. The liquid solution is dilute (ideal behavior at infinite dilution) 3. Temperature is constant 4. The system is at thermodynamic equilibrium **Derivation Steps:** **Step 1:** At equilibrium, the chemical potentials are equal: $$\mu_A^{gas} = \mu_A^{liquid}$$ **Step 2:** Express the chemical potential in the gas phase (ideal gas): $$\mu_A^{gas} = \mu_A^{0,gas}(T) + RT \ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Express the chemical potential in the liquid phase: $$\mu_A^{liquid} = \mu_A^{0,liq}(T) + RT \ln(\gamma_A \cdot x_A)$$ **Step 4:** Equate both expressions: $$\mu_A^{0,gas}(T) + RT \ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right) = \mu_A^{0,liq}(T) + RT \ln(\gamma_A \cdot x_A)$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange to isolate $p_A$: $$RT \ln\left(\dfrac{p_A}{p^0}\right) = \left[\mu_A^{0,liq}(T) - \mu_A^{0,gas}(T)\right] + RT \ln(\gamma_A \cdot x_A)$$ **Step 6:** For dilute solutions, $\gamma_A \to \gamma_A^\infty$ = constant: $$p_A = \underbrace{p^0 \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{\mu_A^{0,gas} - \mu_A^{0,liq}}{RT}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\gamma_A^\infty}}_{H} \cdot x_A$$ **Step 7:** Final Henry's Law: $$\boxed{p_A = H \cdot x_A}$$ #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $H = 1.64 \times 10^3$ atm - $y_{CO_2} = 0.04\% = 0.0004$ - $P_T = 1$ atm **Step 1:** Calculate partial pressure of CO₂: $$p_{CO_2} = y_{CO_2} \cdot P_T = 0.0004 \times 1 = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ atm}$$ **Step 2:** Apply Henry's Law to find mole fraction: $$x_{CO_2} = \dfrac{p_{CO_2}}{H} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.64 \times 10^3} = 2.439 \times 10^{-7}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to molar concentration (assuming water density = 1000 kg/m³, M_water = 18 kg/kmol): Molar concentration of water ≈ 55.5 kmol/m³ $$C_{CO_2} = x_{CO_2} \cdot C_{water} = 2.439 \times 10^{-7} \times 55.5 \text{ kmol/m}^3$$ $$C_{CO_2} = 1.354 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^3 = 1.354 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Final Answer:** $C_{CO_2} \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-2}$ mol/m³ (very low solubility of CO₂ in pure water) --- ### **Solution to Question B:** #### **Part (a) — Factors Affecting Gas Solubility (7 marks):** **1. Effect of Temperature:** - For most gases, dissolution is **exothermic** ($\Delta H_{sol} < 0$) - By Le Chatelier's principle, increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward gas phase - **Solubility decreases with increasing temperature** - Governed by: $\ln(H_2/H_1) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{sol}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ **2. Effect of Pressure:** - Solubility is **directly proportional** to partial pressure (Henry's Law) - Doubling the pressure doubles the dissolved concentration **3. Nature of the Gas:** - Easily liquefiable gases (NH₃, SO₂, HCl) are more soluble - Permanent gases (O₂, H₂, N₂) are less soluble - Polar gases dissolve better in polar solvents **4. Nature of the Solvent:** - Chemical reaction between gas and solvent greatly enhances solubility - Example: CO₂ in MEA (chemical absorption) vs. CO₂ in water (physical) **5. Molecular Size:** - Smaller molecules generally have higher diffusion rates **6. Salting Out Effect:** - Dissolved salts in water reduce gas solubility (salting out) #### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $y_{SO_2} = 0.03$ (3% mole fraction) - $T = 30°C = 303.15$ K - $P_T = 1$ atm - $H_{SO_2} = 50$ atm **Step 1:** Partial pressure of SO₂: $$p_{SO_2} = y_{SO_2} \cdot P_T = 0.03 \times 1 = 0.03 \text{ atm}$$ **Step 2 (i):** Apply Henry's Law to find equilibrium mole fraction: $$x_{SO_2}^* = \dfrac{p_{SO_2}}{H} = \dfrac{0.03}{50} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 3 (ii):** Calculate the equilibrium constant m: $$m = \dfrac{H}{P_T} = \dfrac{50}{1} = 50$$ **Step 4:** Verification using $y = m \cdot x$: $$y_{SO_2} = 50 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} = 0.03 \checkmark$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $x_{SO_2}^* = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ - (ii) $m = 50$ (this is a very high value — indicates SO₂ absorption with water is unfavorable, requiring many stages) --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How to Avoid Them):** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency in H**: Students often mix units (atm vs. Pa) without converting. Always verify whether $H$ and $P_T$ are in the same units before computing $m$. > > 2. **Forgetting Total Pressure**: When converting Henry's Law from $p_A = H x_A$ to $y_A = m x_A$, students forget to divide by $P_T$. **Critical formula:** $m = H/P_T$ > > 3. **Sign Error in Temperature Correction**: The van't Hoff equation has a **negative sign**. Writing it as positive leads to wrong conclusions about temperature effects. > > 4. **Confusing Henry's Law with Raoult's Law**: Henry's Law applies to the **solute** (gas, dilute component); Raoult's Law applies to the **solvent** (in excess). They are NOT interchangeable. > > 5. **Assuming Ideal Behavior**: For non-ideal systems (concentrated solutions), corrections using activity coefficients or Poynting corrections are required. > > 6. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivations**: KTU examiners specifically look for the assumptions listed. Skipping them costs 1-2 marks. > > 7. **Not Drawing the Equilibrium Curve**: When asked about absorption design, always sketch the equilibrium curve and operating line. Missing diagrams lose easy marks. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Solubility of Gas in Liquid:** ### **Core Concepts:** - ✅ Henry's Law: $p_A = H \cdot x_A$ (valid only for **dilute solutions**) - ✅ Equilibrium form: $y_A = m \cdot x_A$, where $m = H/P_T$ - ✅ Henry's constant is **temperature-dependent** (not pressure-dependent) - ✅ Lower H = Higher solubility = More favorable absorption - ✅ Gas dissolution is generally **exothermic** → solubility ↓ with ↑ temperature ### **Key Numerical Tools:** - ✅ Concentration form: $C_A = p_A / H \cdot \rho$ - ✅ Temperature correction (van't Hoff): $\ln(H_2/H_1) = -(\Delta H_{sol}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ - ✅ Mole ratio form for McCabe-Thiele: $Y_A^* = mX_A / [1 + (1-m)X_A]$ ### **Solvent Selection Hierarchy:** - ✅ First choice: **Water** (cheap, safe, easily available) - ✅ Chemical absorption: **Amines (MEA, DEA, MDEA)** for acid gases - ✅ Specialty: **Organic solvents** for specific organic vapors - ✅ Always check: H, selectivity, volatility, viscosity, cost, regenerability ### **High-Yield Constants to Remember:** - ✅ Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) - ✅ STP: $T = 273.15$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa - ✅ Pure Water Density: $1000$ kg/m³ at 4°C - ✅ Molar concentration of pure water: $\approx 55.5$ kmol/m³ ### **Common Exam Traps:** - ⚠️ Don't confuse **partial pressure form** ($H$ has units) with **mole fraction form** ($m$ is dimensionless) - ⚠️ "Solubility increases with pressure" is true ONLY at constant temperature - ⚠️ Henry's Law gives equilibrium; **actual** concentration depends on mass transfer rates ### **One-Line Memory Aid:** > **"Henry Helps High-Heavy gases Hide Happily in H₂O"** — Henry's Law governs gas hiding (dissolving) in water, with H as the constant that determines how well they hide.

topics.title: Distillation: Plate Efficiency, Rectification, and Construction of Fractionating Column
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Distillation: Plate Efficiency, Rectification, and Construction of Fractionating Column

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Plate Efficiency — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Efficiency (Tray Efficiency)** is a quantitative measure of the **actual approach** of a distillation plate (tray) to **thermodynamic equilibrium** between the vapor and liquid phases leaving that plate. Because real contact between vapor and liquid on an industrial tray is finite (typically **1 to 5 seconds** of contact time), the exiting streams do not reach true phase equilibrium.

The efficiency $\eta$ is mathematically defined as the **ratio of the actual change in composition on a plate to the change that would occur if the plate were operating at thermodynamic equilibrium**.

**Standard Industrial Range:** $0.5 \leq E_M \leq 0.85$ (typical Murphree vapor efficiency for sieve and valve trays).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-Storey Hotel Check-In" Model

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a fractionating column as a **50-floor hotel** where each floor is a single plate/tray.
> - **Vapor bubbles** = Guests moving UP from lower floors (carrying their luggage = volatile components).
> - **Liquid droplets** = Guests moving DOWN from upper floors (carrying their belongings = less volatile components).
> - At **EACH floor (plate)**, a receptionist tries to sort luggage — taking the volatile stuff to the floor above and leaving the heavy stuff to fall down.
> - In an **IDEAL/equilibrium plate**, the receptionist is **perfect** — 100% of the volatile luggage is redirected upward. This is the *theoretical* plate.
> - In **REALITY**, the receptionist is only **70% efficient** (Murphree efficiency = 0.7). So only 70% of the maximum possible transfer happens.
> - A **column with 70% efficient plates** needs **MORE plates** than McCabe-Thiele predicts to achieve the same separation — that adjustment is the *Overall Column Efficiency* (often ~50–60%).

## 1.2 Rectification — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rectification** refers to the **upper section of a continuous distillation column**, located **between the feed plate and the top of the column**, where **vapor is enriched (rectified) into a high-purity overhead distillate product** by counter-current contact with the descending reflux liquid.

The two sections of a continuous distillation column are:
- **Rectifying (Enriching) Section:** Above the feed plate. The operating line equation: $y = \frac{R}{R+1}x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$.
- **Stripping (Exhausting) Section:** Below the feed plate. Operating line: $y = \frac{L'}{V'}x - \frac{W x_W}{V'}$.

## 1.3 Fractionating Column Construction — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Fractionating Column** is a tall, vertical, cylindrical pressure vessel containing **internal contacting devices** (trays or packing) that provide **multi-stage vapor–liquid contacting** for the separation of a liquid mixture into its components based on differences in **relative volatility (α)**.

**Key construction components:**
1. **Shell (Cylindrical body)** — usually **carbon steel** for non-corrosive services; stainless steel for food/pharma.
2. **Trays / Plates** — sieve, valve, bubble-cap, or structured packing.
3. **Weir (Inlet & Outlet)** — maintains liquid level on the tray.
4. **Downcomer (Reflux return)** — channels liquid to the next lower tray.
5. **Reboiler** (bottom) — provides vapor boil-up.
6. **Condenser** (top) — condenses overhead vapor into distillate + reflux.
7. **Reflux Drum** — separates vapor and liquid condensate.
8. **Pressure rating** — typically **0.3 kPa to 35 kPa gauge**, designed per **ASME Section VIII Div. 1**.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Multi-Stage Water Purifier"

> [!NOTE]
> A fractionating column is just like a **RO (Reverse Osmosis) water purifier with 20 stages** stacked vertically. Each stage removes a little more impurity. The "stage efficiency" tells you how *perfect* each membrane is. You need MORE stages if each one is less efficient — same as needing more real plates if Murphree efficiency is low.

---

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (At a Glance)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Industrial Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Relative Volatility | $\alpha$ | $1.2 \leq \alpha \leq 50$ | dimensionless |
| Murphree Vapor Efficiency | $E_{MV}$ | $0.5 - 0.85$ | dimensionless |
| Murphree Liquid Efficiency | $E_{ML}$ | $0.5 - 0.9$ | dimensionless |
| Point Efficiency | $E_{OG}$ | $0.6 - 0.9$ | dimensionless |
| Overall Column Efficiency | $E_o$ | $0.4 - 0.7$ | dimensionless |
| Reflux Ratio | $R$ | $1.2 R_{min} \leq R \leq 2 R_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| Plate Spacing | — | $0.45 - 0.9$ | m |
| Column Height-to-Diameter | $H/D$ | $10 - 30$ | dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Definitions of Plate Efficiency

### 2.1.1 Murphree Vapor Plate Efficiency ($E_{MV}$ or $E_M$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most commonly used efficiency** in KTU exams. It compares the actual vapor composition change across a tray to the change that would occur at equilibrium.

For the more-volatile component (MVC), the **Murphree vapor efficiency** for the $n$-th plate is defined as:

$$E_{MV,n} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

Where:
- $y_n$ = actual vapor composition leaving plate $n$ (vapor going UP)
- $y_{n+1}$ = vapor composition entering plate $n$ from below
- $y_n^*$ = composition of vapor in **equilibrium** with the liquid leaving plate $n$ (i.e., $y^* = f(x_n)$)
- $0 \leq E_{MV} \leq 1$

**Why this matters:** In KTU problems, you are typically given $E_{MV}$ and asked to use the McCabe-Thiele method with a **"stepping correction"** (see Section 3).

### 2.1.2 Murphree Liquid Plate Efficiency ($E_{ML}$)

$$E_{ML,n} = \frac{x_{n-1} - x_n}{x_{n-1} - x_n^*}$$

- $x_{n-1}$ = liquid entering plate $n$ from above
- $x_n$ = actual liquid leaving plate $n$ downward
- $x_n^*$ = liquid composition that would be in equilibrium with vapor leaving the plate

### 2.1.3 Overall (Point-to-Point) Murphree Vapor Efficiency

This is the efficiency referenced when the **entire tray** is considered as a single mixing stage:

$$E_{MV} = \frac{\bar{y}_n - y_{n+1}}{\bar{y}_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

where $\bar{y}_n$ is the **average** vapor composition leaving the plate.

### 2.1.4 Point Efficiency ($E_{OG}$ or $E_{mv}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Point efficiency** is the LOCAL efficiency at a *single point* on the tray where vapor bubbles through a small liquid element. It accounts for **mass transfer resistance** at the bubble–liquid interface.

$$E_{OG} = \frac{y - y_{n+1}}{y^* - y_{n+1}}$$

This is governed by the **two-film theory** and the relation:

$$E_{OG} = 1 - e^{-N_{OG}}$$

where $N_{OG}$ is the **Number of Overall Gas-Phase Transfer Units**.

### 2.1.5 Overall Column Efficiency ($E_o$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The simplest (and most widely used in industry) efficiency metric. Used to convert **theoretical plates** from McCabe-Thiele to **actual plates** required.

$$E_o = \frac{N_{theoretical}}{N_{actual}}$$

## 2.2 Relationship Between Point Efficiency and Murphree Plate Efficiency

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**. The relationship depends on the **liquid flow pattern** across the tray:

| Liquid Flow Regime | Relationship |
|---|---|
| **Plug Flow (Lewis Case 1)** | $E_{MV} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1\right)$ |
| **Backmixing (Lewis Case 2)** | $E_{MV} = E_{OG}$ |
| **Perfect Mixing (Lewis Case 3)** | $E_{MV} = \frac{\ln(1 + E_{OG}(\lambda - 1)/\lambda)}{(\lambda - 1)/\lambda}$ |

where $\lambda = mV/L$ is the **stripping factor** (also called the **volatility factor** reciprocal), $m$ is the local slope of the equilibrium curve, $V$ is molar vapor flow, $L$ is molar liquid flow.

## 2.3 Rectification Section — Operating Line Theory

### 2.3.1 Mass Balance Derivation (Quick Recap)

A control volume around the top of the column (from tray $n$ upward) gives:

$$V y_{n+1} = L x_n + D x_D$$

where $R = L/D$ is the **reflux ratio** and $V = L + D = (R+1)D$.

Solving:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1}x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

The **rectifying operating line** always passes through:
- $y$-intercept: $\dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$
- $x$-intercept: $x_D$
- Point $(x_D, x_D)$ on the 45° line

### 2.3.2 Special Rectification Cases

- **Total Reflux** ($R \to \infty$, $D \to 0$): operating line coincides with the 45° diagonal; minimum number of stages.
- **Minimum Reflux** ($R_{min}$): operating line passes through the **intersection of the q-line and equilibrium curve**; infinite number of stages.
- **Operating Reflux**: $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) R_{min}$ (industrial optimum).

## 2.4 Fractionating Column — Engineering Design Considerations

### 2.4.1 Column Diameter (Sieve Tray Flooding Criterion)

$$D_{col} = \sqrt{\frac{4 V_{actual}}{u_f \cdot \pi \cdot \rho_V}}$$

where $u_f$ is the flooding velocity (0.6 – 1.2 m/s at atmospheric pressure).

### 2.4.2 Column Height

$$H = (N_{actual} - 1) \cdot t_s + \text{top} + \text{bottom} \text{ disengagement}$$

where $t_s$ = tray spacing (**450 mm** to **900 mm**); top and bottom disengagement add **~0.6 m** each.

### 2.4.3 Tray Hydraulics — Pressure Drop

Souders-Brown correlation for flooding velocity:

$$u_f = C_{SB} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_V}{\rho_V}}$$

where $C_{SB}$ is the **Souders-Brown coefficient** (0.06 – 0.12 m/s).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — every KTU 14-mark question will draw from at least 3 of these formulas.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Murphree Vapor Efficiency | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$ | Tray-by-tray stepping |
| 2 | Murphree Liquid Efficiency | $E_{ML} = \dfrac{x_{n-1} - x_n}{x_{n-1} - x_n^*}$ | Liquid composition stepping |
| 3 | Point Efficiency | $E_{OG} = 1 - e^{-N_{OG}}$ | Local mass transfer |
| 4 | Stripping Factor | $\lambda = mV/L$ | Tray flow regime |
| 5 | Lewis Case 1 (Plug Flow) | $E_{MV} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1\right)$ | $E_{OG} \to E_{MV}$ |
| 6 | Overall Column Efficiency | $E_o = N_{theoretical}/N_{actual}$ | Real plate count |
| 7 | Rectifying Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Above feed plate |
| 8 | Stripping Operating Line | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$ | Below feed plate |
| 9 | Reflux Ratio Relation | $V = (R+1)D, \quad L = RD$ | Material balance |
| 10 | q-line Equation | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Feed condition |
| 11 | Fenske Eq. (Min. Stages) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[(x_D/x_B)(x_B^{1-\alpha}/x_D^{1-\alpha})\right]}{\ln \alpha}$ | Total reflux |
| 12 | Underwood Eq. (Min. Reflux) | $\sum \dfrac{q_i z_{F,i}}{(\alpha_i - \theta)} = 1$ | $R_{min}$ |
| 13 | Souders-Brown Flooding | $u_f = C_{SB}\sqrt{(\rho_L - \rho_V)/\rho_V}$ | Column diameter |
| 14 | Column Height | $H = (N_{actual}-1)t_s + 1.2 \text{ m}$ | Mechanical design |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Fractionating Columns:**
> 1. **Petroleum Refining** — Crude oil atmospheric & vacuum distillation (200–400 plates, H = 50–60 m).
> 2. **LNG Production** — Cryogenic distillation of methane/ethane/propane at $-160°\text{C}$.
> 3. **Air Separation Units (ASU)** — Separation of $\text{N}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, $\text{Ar}$ at cryogenic temperatures.
> 4. **Ethylene Plants** — Cracking furnace + primary fractionator separates gasoline, diesel, naphtha.
> 5. **Ethanol Production** — Beer column concentrates ethanol from fermentation broth ($x_F \approx 0.1$, $x_D \approx 0.95$).
> 6. **Pharmaceutical Solvent Recovery** — GMP-compliant stainless steel columns.
> 7. **Cogeneration Plants** — Boiler feed water de-aeration by steam stripping.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Murphree Vapor Efficiency from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Start with Mass Transfer on a Single Plate

Consider plate $n$ with:
- Vapor entering from below: $V$ (mol/s), composition $y_{n+1}$
- Vapor leaving above: $V$ (mol/s), composition $y_n$
- Liquid entering from above: $L$ (mol/s), composition $x_{n-1}$
- Liquid leaving below: $L$ (mol/s), composition $x_n$

> **Step 1:** Component mass balance on the more-volatile component (MVC) for plate $n$:

$$
V y_{n+1} + L x_{n-1} = V y_n + L x_n
$$

> **Step 2:** Rearrange to express the **vapor composition change** across the plate:

$$
V(y_n - y_{n+1}) = L(x_{n-1} - x_n)
$$

> **Step 3:** Define the **equilibrium composition** $y_n^* = f(x_n)$ — the composition the vapor *would* have if it were in perfect equilibrium with the exiting liquid $x_n$.

> **Step 4:** Define the Murphree efficiency as the ratio of **actual** to **maximum possible** vapor composition change:

$$
E_{MV} = \frac{\text{Actual change in } y}{\text{Maximum possible change in } y}
$$

$$
E_{MV} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}
$$

> **Step 5:** This is the fundamental definition. **It is dimensionless** and bounded: $0 \leq E_{MV} \leq 1$.

> **Step 6:** When $E_{MV} = 1$, the plate is a **theoretical (ideal) plate** — vapor and liquid leaving are in equilibrium. When $E_{MV} < 1$, the plate is "short" of equilibrium and more real plates are required.

> **Result:** The standard Murphree vapor efficiency equation is derived purely from definition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Point Efficiency and Murphree Efficiency (Lewis Case 1 — Plug Flow)

### 3.2.1 Physical Setup

On a real sieve tray, liquid flows **horizontally** across the tray (from inlet downcomer to outlet downcomer), while vapor rises **vertically** through the perforations. At a given $x$-position along the tray:
- Vapor of composition $y_{n+1}$ enters.
- Liquid of composition $x$ (which varies with position) contacts the vapor.
- A **local** mass transfer process occurs governed by point efficiency $E_{OG}$.

### 3.2.2 Local Mass Transfer at a Differential Element

Consider a differential element of tray length $dZ$ at position $Z$ from the inlet weir. Let:
- $x(Z)$ = liquid composition at position $Z$ (varies along tray).
- $y(Z)$ = vapor composition at position $Z$.

For a small element of length $dZ$:

**Liquid side mass balance:**

$$
L \cdot dx = -N_A \cdot dA
$$

where $dA$ is the differential interfacial area and $N_A$ is the molar flux of MVC.

**Point efficiency definition (local):**

$$
E_{OG} = \frac{y(Z) - y_{n+1}}{y^*(Z) - y_{n+1}}
$$

where $y^* = f(x)$ is the local equilibrium vapor composition.

### 3.2.3 Coupling Through Two-Film Theory

From the two-film model, the local rate of mass transfer is:

$$
d(y - y_{n+1}) = -d(y^* - y_{n+1}) \cdot \frac{dN_A}{N_A^{total}}
$$

After integration across the tray and algebraic manipulation (using $mV/L = \lambda$ where $m = dy^*/dx$ is the local slope of equilibrium curve):

**Final Result (Lewis Case 1 — Plug Flow of liquid):**

$$
\boxed{E_{MV} = \frac{1}{\lambda}\left(e^{\lambda \cdot E_{OG}} - 1\right)}
$$

> **Limiting behavior check:**
> - If $\lambda = 1$ (operating line slope = equilibrium line slope), then $E_{MV} = E_{OG} \cdot e^{E_{OG} - 1}$ — close but not equal.
> - If $\lambda \to 0$ (horizontal equilibrium curve), then $E_{MV} \to E_{OG}$.
> - If $\lambda \to \infty$ (steep equilibrium curve), $E_{MV}$ can exceed $E_{OG}$ — **plate efficiency can be HIGHER than point efficiency!**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Rectifying Section Operating Line from Mass Balance

### 3.3.1 Control Volume Definition

Enclose the top portion of the column:
- From plate $n$ to the top of the column.
- Including the condenser and reflux drum.

### 3.3.2 Total Mass Balance

$$
V_{n+1} = L_n + D
$$

(since the condenser does not accumulate material at steady state).

### 3.3.3 Component (MVC) Mass Balance

$$
V_{n+1} \cdot y_{n+1} = L_n \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D
$$

### 3.3.4 Constant Molal Overflow (CMO) Assumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **CMO assumption** is central to the McCabe-Thiele method: $V$ and $L$ are CONSTANT from tray to tray in each section. This is valid when:
> - Molar latent heats of vaporization are similar.
> - Sensible heat effects and heat of mixing are negligible.
> - No significant chemical reaction.

Under CMO: $V_{n+1} = V$ and $L_n = L$ (constants in the rectifying section).

### 3.3.5 Derive the Operating Line

Solving the component balance for $y_{n+1}$:

$$
y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D x_D}{V}
$$

Substitute $L = RD$ and $V = (R+1)D$:

$$
y_{n+1} = \frac{RD}{(R+1)D} x_n + \frac{D x_D}{(R+1)D}
$$

$$
y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}
$$

$$
\boxed{y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}}
$$

### 3.3.6 Intercept and Slope Identification

- **Slope of operating line:** $\dfrac{R}{R+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}$
- **y-intercept:** $\dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$
- **x-intercept:** $x_D$ (when $y = 0$)
- **Operating line intersects the 45° diagonal at $(x_D, x_D)$**

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

> [!TIP]
> **Typical KTU 14-mark question:** A sieve-tray distillation column is being used to separate a benzene-toluene mixture. Given the equilibrium data and the operating conditions below, calculate the number of **actual plates** required using the McCabe-Thiele method with a Murphree efficiency of $E_{MV} = 0.7$.

**Given:**
- $x_F = 0.45$, $x_D = 0.95$, $x_B = 0.05$
- $q = 1$ (saturated liquid feed)
- $R = 1.5 R_{min}$
- $E_{MV} = 0.7$ (constant across all plates)

**Step 1 — Equilibrium data (benzene-toluene, atmospheric):**

| $x$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.60 | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.90 | 0.95 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 0.115 | 0.215 | 0.380 | 0.510 | 0.620 | 0.715 | 0.795 | 0.860 | 0.915 | 0.960 | 0.980 |

**Step 2 — Find $R_{min}$:**

The q-line for $q=1$ is vertical: $x = x_F = 0.45$.

At $x = 0.45$, $y^* = 0.665$ (interpolating from data).

The rectifying operating line passes through $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$ and the q-line intersection $(0.45, 0.665)$.

Slope at $R_{min}$: 

$$
m_{min} = \frac{0.95 - 0.665}{0.95 - 0.45} = \frac{0.285}{0.50} = 0.57
$$

So $\dfrac{R_{min}}{R_{min}+1} = 0.57 \Rightarrow R_{min} = 0.57/0.43 = 1.326$.

**Step 3 — Operating R:**

$$
R = 1.5 \times 1.326 = 1.99 \approx 2.0
$$

Operating line: $y = \dfrac{2}{3}x + \dfrac{0.95}{3} = 0.667x + 0.317$

**Step 4 — McCabe-Thiele theoretical stepping:**

Stepping from $(0.95, 0.95)$ down using the operating line and equilibrium curve yields **$N_{theoretical} \approx 12$** (including reboiler as one theoretical stage).

**Step 5 — Apply Murphree efficiency correction:**

The Murphree correction at each step is to scale the **vertical step** between operating line and equilibrium curve by $E_{MV}$:

$$
\Delta y_{actual} = E_{MV} \cdot \Delta y_{theoretical} = 0.7 \cdot \Delta y_{theoretical}
$$

After applying this correction at every plate, the stepping yields **$N_{actual} \approx 17$**.

**Step 6 — Overall column efficiency:**

$$
E_o = \frac{N_{theoretical}}{N_{actual}} = \frac{12}{17} \approx 0.706
$$

> **Note:** $E_o \approx E_{MV}$ when operating and equilibrium lines are approximately parallel. This is the **Lewis Case 1 approximation** and is widely used as a shortcut in industry.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Efficiency for a KTU Question

| Question Pattern | Use This Efficiency |
|---|---|
| "Find the actual number of plates given $E_o$" | $E_o$ directly |
| "Given Murphree efficiency, find actual plates" | $E_{MV}$ with McCabe-Thiele step correction |
| "Tray-by-tray composition profile" | $E_{MV}$ or $E_{ML}$ applied at each plate |
| "Local mass transfer analysis" | $E_{OG}$ from two-film theory |
| "Relationship between tray and point efficiency" | Lewis Case 1, 2, or 3 formula |
| "Constant molar overflow is invalid" | **Must use $E_{ML}$**, not $E_{MV}$ |
| "Flooding and column diameter" | Souders-Brown + $C_{SB}$ correlation |
| "Number of stages for given $R$" | McCabe-Thiele + Fenske/Underwood |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
