# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Overall Process Architecture of a Continuous Fractionating Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Mixture xF, q, T_F] --> B[Feed Plate Location - Optimum]
    B --> C[Rectifying Section - Enriching]
    C --> D[Top Plate - Overhead Vapor y_N]
    D --> E[Total Condenser]
    E --> F[Reflux Drum]
    F --> G[Distillate Product x_D]
    F --> H[Reflux Pump]
    H --> I[Reflux Liquid L, x_D]
    I --> C
    B --> J[Stripping Section - Exhausting]
    J --> K[Reboiler]
    K --> L[Bottom Product x_B]
    K --> M[Reboiled Vapor V_prime]
    M --> J
    N[Steam / Heating Utility] --> K
    O[Cooling Water] --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Single Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Liquid Inlet Downcomer L, x_n-1] --> B1[Inlet Weir]
    B1 --> C1[Liquid Pool on Tray - Active Zone]
    C1 --> D1[Outlet Weir]
    D1 --> E1[Outlet Downcomer]
    E1 --> F1[Liquid to Next Lower Plate L, x_n]
    G1[Vapor Rises from Below V, y_n+1] --> C1
    C1 --> H1[Vapor Leaves Upward V, y_n]
    subgraph MassTransferZone [Active Mass Transfer Zone]
    C1
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tray Types Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Tray Contacting Devices] --> B[Bubble-Cap Tray]
    A --> C[Sieve Tray - Perforated]
    A --> D[Valve Tray - Movable]
    A --> E[Specialty Trays]
    B --> B1[Fixed Cap, Vapor Channels via Riser Slots]
    C --> C1[5 to 13 mm Holes, Simple and Cheap]
    D --> D1[Movable Disc Lifts with Vapor Rate]
    E --> E1[Examples: Linde, Koch-Glitsch, Sulzer]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Efficiency Relationship Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Local Vapor-Liquid Contact - Bubble] --> B[Point Efficiency E_OG]
    B --> C{Liquid Flow Regime on Tray}
    C -->|Plug Flow| D[Lewis Case 1: E_MV = e^lambda E_OG - 1 over lambda]
    C -->|Backmixing| E[Lewis Case 2: E_MV = E_OG]
    C -->|Perfect Mixing| F[Lewis Case 3: E_MV = ln formula]
    D --> G[Murphree Plate Efficiency E_MV]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[All Plates Average]
    H --> I[Overall Column Efficiency E_o]
    I --> J[Number of Actual Plates N_actual]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Rectification vs Stripping Section (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopRegion [Top of Column - Rectification]
    A1[Overhead Vapor y_N] --> A2[Condenser]
    A2 --> A3[Reflux Drum]
    A3 --> A4[Distillate x_D]
    A3 --> A5[Reflux Return - L = R*D]
    end
    subgraph MidRegion [Feed Plate - Section Boundary]
    A5 --> B1[Feed xF enters]
    B1 --> B2[Optimum Feed Plate Location]
    end
    subgraph BotRegion [Bottom of Column - Stripping]
    B2 --> C1[Stripping Section]
    C1 --> C2[Reboiler - Q_R supplied]
    C2 --> C3[Bottom Product x_B]
    C2 --> C4[Boiled-up Vapor V_prime]
    C4 --> C1
    end
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — McCabe-Thiele Stepping with Murphree Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start at x = x_D on 45 deg line] --> B[Move Vertically to Operating Line y_op]
    B --> C[Actual Step = 0.7 times Theoretical Step]
    C --> D[Move to Equilibrium Curve y_eq - x_new]
    D --> E[x_new vs x_B Comparison]
    E -->|x_new greater than x_B| B
    E -->|x_new less than or equal to x_B| F[Count Actual Plates N_actual]
    F --> G[Apply Overall Efficiency Check E_o = N_theo/N_actual]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define Murphree plate efficiency. Why is it always less than 1 for real distillation columns?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Murphree plate efficiency $E_{MV}$ is defined as the ratio of the actual change in vapor composition across a tray to the change that would occur if the tray were operating at thermodynamic equilibrium:
>
> $$E_{MV} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$
>
> $E_{MV} < 1$ for real columns because the vapor–liquid contact time on industrial trays is finite (**1–5 seconds**), and the contact pattern is imperfect. Mass transfer is limited by:
> - Finite interfacial area between bubbles and liquid.
> - Non-ideal liquid distribution (channeling, weeping, entrainment).
> - Bypassing of liquid in stagnant zones.
>
> Only in the hypothetical limit of **infinite contact time and perfect mixing** would $E_{MV}$ approach 1.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Distinguish between Rectifying Section and Stripping Section of a continuous distillation column.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Rectifying Section | Stripping Section |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Above feed plate | Below feed plate |
| Function | Enriches vapor to high purity distillate | Strips residual MVC from bottoms |
| Operating line | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}x - \dfrac{W x_W}{V'}$ |
| Vapor flow $V$ | $V = (R+1)D$ | $V' = (R+1)D - (1-q)F$ |
| Liquid flow $L$ | $L = RD$ | $L' = RD + qF$ |
| Topmost stream | Overhead vapor to condenser | Liquid to reboiler |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B: **14 marks** = sub-parts **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Provide two alternative questions (A and B) with sub-parts and complete solutions.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

**A binary mixture of A (MVC) and B is to be separated in a continuous sieve-tray distillation column. The feed is saturated liquid with $x_F = 0.40$. The distillate should have $x_D = 0.95$ and bottoms $x_B = 0.05$. The relative volatility is $\alpha = 2.5$. Murphree vapor efficiency is constant at $E_{MV} = 0.65$. The reflux ratio is $R = 2.0$.**

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **minimum number of theoretical stages** at total reflux using the **Fenske equation**, and the **minimum reflux ratio** using the **Underwood equation**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the McCabe-Thiele method, determine the **number of actual plates** required. Compute the **overall column efficiency** $E_o$. Comment on whether $R = 2.0$ is an economically reasonable choice.

---

#### Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Min. Stages and Min. Reflux

**Step 1 — Fenske Equation for Minimum Stages:**

The Fenske equation (for two components) is:

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\frac{1-x_B}{x_B}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\left(\frac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\right]}{\ln 2.5}
$$

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln(19 \times 19)}{\ln 2.5} = \frac{\ln 361}{0.9163} = \frac{5.886}{0.9163}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{min} \approx 6.43 \text{ stages} \approx 7 \text{ stages (rounded up)}}
$$

> This includes the reboiler as an equilibrium stage. So the column needs at least 6 actual equilibrium stages above the reboiler, or 7 total counting the reboiler.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Curve from Relative Volatility:**

The relative volatility relation:

$$
y = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}
$$

For $\alpha = 2.5$:

| $x$ | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.60 | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.90 | 0.95 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 0.115 | 0.217 | 0.385 | 0.522 | 0.625 | 0.714 | 0.789 | 0.855 | 0.909 | 0.957 | 0.980 |

**Step 3 — Minimum Reflux Ratio (Underwood):**

For saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$), Underwood parameter $\theta = 1$ (between the two roots), and:

$$
\sum \frac{q_i z_{F,i}}{\alpha_i - \theta} = 1
$$

With one feed component, the simplified Underwood equation gives:

$$
R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q}
$$

For $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), the q-line is vertical at $x = x_F = 0.40$, intersecting equilibrium at $y_q = 0.625$.

$$
R_{min} = \frac{0.95 - 0.625}{0.625 - 0.40} = \frac{0.325}{0.225} = 1.444
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{min} \approx 1.44}
$$

> **Industrial heuristic check:** Operating $R = 1.5 R_{min} = 2.17$ is recommended. We are using $R = 2.0$, which is close to but slightly below this optimum.

---

### Part (b) — Actual Plates, Overall Efficiency, and Economic Analysis

**Step 1 — Operating Line Equations:**

For $R = 2.0$, rectifying operating line:

$$
y = \frac{2}{3}x + \frac{0.95}{3} = 0.667x + 0.317
$$

For $q = 1$ (saturated liquid), the q-line is vertical at $x = 0.40$. It intersects the rectifying line at:

$$
y = 0.667(0.40) + 0.317 = 0.267 + 0.317 = 0.584
$$

Intersection point with stripping line: $(0.40, 0.584)$.

For saturated liquid feed, the stripping line passes through $(x_B, x_B) = (0.05, 0.05)$ and $(0.40, 0.584)$:

Slope $= (0.584 - 0.05)/(0.40 - 0.05) = 0.534/0.35 = 1.526$

Stripping operating line: $y = 1.526x - 0.026$

**Step 2 — McCabe-Thiele Theoretical Stepping:**

Starting from $(0.95, 0.95)$ on the 45° line, alternating horizontal/vertical steps between the operating line and equilibrium curve, the count yields approximately:

$$
N_{theoretical} \approx 12 \text{ (including reboiler)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Murphree Correction:**

For each step in the McCabe-Thiele construction, the vertical distance between the operating line and equilibrium curve is reduced by a factor of $E_{MV} = 0.65$:

$$
\Delta y_{actual} = E_{MV} \cdot \Delta y_{theoretical}
$$

This effectively stretches each "theoretical step" into a longer horizontal segment. The new step count:

$$
N_{actual} \approx 18 \text{ plates (including reboiler)}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall Column Efficiency:**

$$
E_o = \frac{N_{theoretical}}{N_{actual}} = \frac{12}{18} = 0.667
$$

> **Note:** $E_o = 0.667$ is slightly higher than $E_{MV} = 0.65$ because of the curvature relationship (Lewis Case 1 with $\lambda \neq 1$).

**Step 5 — Economic Analysis of $R = 2.0$:**

- $R = 2.0$ vs $R_{min} = 1.44$: ratio = $1.39 \times R_{min}$.
- **Industry rule:** $R = 1.2 R_{min}$ to $2.0 R_{min}$. Here, $1.39 R_{min}$ is in the **lower end** of the range.
- **Trade-off:** Low $R$ → low reboiler + condenser duty (low operating cost) but high plate count (high capital cost). High $R$ → opposite.
- **Verdict:** $R = 2.0$ is on the **energy-conservative side**. An optimal design might be $R = 1.5 R_{min} = 2.17$, which would require slightly fewer plates at marginally higher energy cost.

$$
\boxed{\text{Answer: } N_{actual} = 18, \quad E_o = 0.667, \quad R = 2.0 \text{ is borderline economical.}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**A packed column is used for the separation of a methanol-water mixture. The following data are given:**

- Distillate composition: $x_D = 0.98$ (mol fraction methanol)
- Bottoms composition: $x_B = 0.02$
- Feed: saturated liquid, $x_F = 0.50$
- Murphree vapor efficiency: $E_{MV} = 0.70$
- Relative volatility: $\alpha = 3.5$
- Pressure: 1 atm

**(a) [7 marks]** Calculate the **minimum reflux ratio** and the **operating reflux ratio** (using $R = 1.5 R_{min}$). Derive the rectifying operating line equation and identify the **y-intercept** and **slope**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compute the **minimum number of stages** by Fenske equation, the **theoretical plate count** by McCabe-Thiele, and the **actual plate count** using the given Murphree efficiency. If tray spacing is $0.6$ m, compute the **height of the column (active section)**.

---

#### Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Min. Reflux, Operating Reflux, Operating Line

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Curve from $\alpha = 3.5$:**

$$
y = \frac{3.5x}{1 + 2.5x}
$$

| $x$ | 0.02 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.60 | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.90 | 0.98 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ | 0.067 | 0.280 | 0.438 | 0.553 | 0.636 | 0.700 | 0.750 | 0.790 | 0.823 | 0.849 | 0.859 |

**Step 2 — q-line for Saturated Liquid ($q = 1$):** Vertical at $x_F = 0.50$, intersecting equilibrium at $y_q = 0.700$.

**Step 3 — Minimum Reflux:**

$$
R_{min} = \frac{x_D - y_q}{y_q - x_q} = \frac{0.98 - 0.700}{0.700 - 0.50} = \frac{0.280}{0.200}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = 1.40}
$$

**Step 4 — Operating Reflux:**

$$
R = 1.5 R_{min} = 1.5 \times 1.40 = 2.10
$$

**Step 5 — Rectifying Operating Line:**

$$
y = \frac{R}{R+1}x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{2.10}{3.10}x + \frac{0.98}{3.10}
$$

$$
\boxed{y = 0.6774x + 0.3161}
$$

- **Slope:** $0.6774$
- **y-intercept:** $0.3161$
- **x-intercept:** $0.98$ (intersects 45° line at $(x_D, x_D)$)

---

### Part (b) — Min. Stages, Theoretical Plates, Actual Plates, Column Height

**Step 1 — Minimum Stages (Fenske):**

$$
N_{min} = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{0.98}{0.02}\right)\left(\frac{0.98}{0.02}\right)\right]}{\ln 3.5} = \frac{\ln(49 \times 49)}{1.2528} = \frac{\ln 2401}{1.2528} = \frac{7.783}{1.2528}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{min} \approx 6.21 \text{ stages} \approx 7 \text{ stages (rounded)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Plates by McCabe-Thiele:**

Using the operating line $y = 0.6774x + 0.3161$ and the equilibrium curve, McCabe-Thiele stepping from $(0.98, 0.98)$ down through the feed point $(0.50, 0.700)$ to $(0.02, 0.02)$ yields:

$$
N_{theoretical} \approx 10 \text{ (including reboiler)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Murphree Efficiency:**

The vertical jump correction: $\Delta y_{actual} = 0.70 \cdot \Delta y_{theoretical}$.

Step count increases to:

$$
N_{actual} \approx 14 \text{ plates (including reboiler)}
$$

**Step 4 — Column Height:**

Active height between top tray and bottom tray (excluding top and bottom disengagement):

$$
H_{active} = (N_{actual} - 1) \times t_s = (14 - 1) \times 0.6 = 13 \times 0.6
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{active} = 7.8 \text{ m}}
$$

Adding 0.6 m for top disengagement and 0.6 m for bottom:

$$
H_{total} = 7.8 + 0.6 + 0.6 = 9.0 \text{ m}
$$

> **Design Verdict:** A 9-m tall column is reasonable for a methanol-water separation at atmospheric pressure. Tray diameter (from Souders-Brown) would be in the range of **0.8 m to 1.5 m** depending on throughput.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 WAYS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THIS MODULE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E_{MV}$ with $E_o$** — A common error is to apply $E_o$ in a tray-by-tray stepping problem. $E_o$ is only for direct conversion $N_{actual} = N_{theo}/E_o$. For stepping, you MUST use $E_{MV}$ (or $E_{ML}$) to correct each step.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Constant Molal Overflow (CMO)** — If the problem says "non-constant molal overflow" or "wide boiling mixture," the simple operating line equations are INVALID. You must use the **Ponchon-Savarit method** (enthalpy–concentration diagram) instead.
>
> 3. **Incorrect q-line for Feed Condition** — Always identify the feed state correctly:
>    - $q = 1$ → saturated liquid (vertical q-line)
>    - $q = 0$ → saturated vapor (horizontal q-line)
>    - $q > 1$ → subcooled liquid
>    - $0 < q < 1$ → two-phase feed
>
> 4. **Misapplying the Fenske Equation** — Fenske gives $N_{min}$ at **TOTAL REFLUX** only. Students often compute $N_{min}$ and use it as the actual plate count, forgetting the operating reflux.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to Count the Reboiler as a Stage** — In KTU convention, the reboiler is a **theoretical stage**. So if McCabe-Thiele stepping gives 11 steps and one of them is the reboiler, the answer is 10 plates + 1 reboiler = 11 total.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this checklist for last-minute revision:**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] Murphree Vapor Efficiency: $E_{MV} = (y_n - y_{n+1})/(y_n^* - y_{n+1})$
- [ ] Murphree Liquid Efficiency: $E_{ML} = (x_{n-1} - x_n)/(x_{n-1} - x_n^*)$
- [ ] Point Efficiency: $E_{OG} = 1 - e^{-N_{OG}}$
- [ ] Overall Column Efficiency: $E_o = N_{theoretical}/N_{actual}$
- [ ] Stripping Factor: $\lambda = mV/L$

### Lewis Case Formulas (Plug Flow default in KTU)
- [ ] Case 1 (Plug Flow): $E_{MV} = (e^{\lambda E_{OG}} - 1)/\lambda$
- [ ] Case 2 (Backmixing): $E_{MV} = E_{OG}$
- [ ] Case 3 (Perfect Mixing): $E_{MV} = \ln(1 + E_{OG}(\lambda - 1)/\lambda)/[(\lambda - 1)/\lambda]$

### Operating Line Equations
- [ ] Rectifying: $y = [R/(R+1)]x + [x_D/(R+1)]$ — **above feed plate**
- [ ] Stripping: $y = (L'/V')x - (W x_W/V')$ — **below feed plate**
- [ ] q-line: $y = [q/(q-1)]x - [z_F/(q-1)]$ — **at feed plate**

### Shortcut Equations
- [ ] Fenske (Min. Stages, total reflux): $N_{min} = \ln[(x_D x_B')/(x_B x_D')]/\ln\alpha$
- [ ] Underwood (Min. Reflux): $\sum q_i z_{F,i}/(\alpha_i - \theta) = 1$
- [ ] $R_{min}$ shortcut: $R_{min} = (x_D - y_q)/(y_q - x_q)$ for $q = 1$ feed

### Column Design Quick Numbers
- [ ] Tray spacing: **0.45 m to 0.9 m** (typical **0.6 m**)
- [ ] Top/bottom disengagement: **0.6 m** each
- [ ] Industrial reflux ratio: $R = (1.2 \text{ to } 2.0) R_{min}$
- [ ] Souders-Brown $C_{SB}$: 0.06 – 0.12 m/s
- [ ] Sieve tray hole diameter: **5 mm to 13 mm**
- [ ] Liquid weir height: **40 mm to 90 mm**

### Fractionating Column Components (Memorize)
- [ ] Shell (carbon steel / SS)
- [ ] Trays (sieve, valve, bubble-cap)
- [ ] Weir (inlet and outlet)
- [ ] Downcomer (reflux)
- [ ] Reboiler (bottom — heat source)
- [ ] Condenser (top — cooling)
- [ ] Reflux drum
- [ ] Pressure rating per **ASME Sec. VIII Div. 1**

### Industrial Application Areas (For 2-mark short questions)
- [ ] Petroleum refining (atmospheric & vacuum distillation)
- [ ] Air separation units (ASU)
- [ ] Ethylene cracking plants
- [ ] LNG production (cryogenic)
- [ ] Ethanol fermentation concentration
- [ ] Solvent recovery (pharma)

### Common Traps & Pitfalls
- [ ] $E_{MV}$ can be **greater than 1** in some Lewis Case 1 situations (when $\lambda > 1$)
- [ ] $E_o$ and $E_{MV}$ are **NOT the same** when equilibrium and operating lines have different slopes
- [ ] Reboiler is a theoretical stage — **always count it**
- [ ] Total condenser assumed unless otherwise stated
- [ ] CMO assumption fails for wide-boiling mixtures
- [ ] $E_{OG} = 0$ means zero mass transfer; $E_{OG} = 1$ means local equilibrium

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Theoretical Plates by McCabe Thiele Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Theoretical Plates by McCabe Thiele Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **McCabe–Thiele Method** is a rigorous, graphical analytical procedure used in chemical engineering for determining the number of theoretical equilibrium stages (theoretical plates or trays) required to achieve a specified degree of separation between two components in a **continuous binary distillation column**. It was developed in 1925 by Warren L. McCabe and Ernest W. Thiele.

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition (Board Examination Standard)
> **McCabe-Thiele Method:** "An analytical-graphical technique based on the principle of alternating use of the material balance (operating lines) and the vapor-liquid equilibrium relationship (equilibrium curve) to step off the number of theoretical stages required for a given binary distillation separation."

The method assumes:
- Constant molar overflow (CMO)
- Negligible heat of mixing
- Constant operating pressures

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE] Plain English Explanation
> Imagine you are climbing a staircase between two floors. The **floor you start on** represents the *feed composition*, and the **floor you need to reach** represents the *distillate composition*. Each step you climb represents **one theoretical plate** where vapor and liquid meet, exchange mass, and approach equilibrium. The **equilibrium curve** acts as the "riser" of the step (where vaporization occurs) and the **operating line** acts as the "tread" (material balance constraint). The McCabe-Thiele method simply draws these steps on a graph between the equilibrium curve and the operating lines until the desired separation is achieved.

### 1.3 Key Terminology — KTU Must-Know Glossary

| Term | Definition | Symbol |
|------|------------|--------|
| Theoretical Plate | A hypothetical stage where vapor and liquid leaving are in perfect thermodynamic equilibrium | $N$ |
| Equilibrium Curve | Graph of vapor mole fraction ($y^*$) vs. liquid mole fraction ($x$) at constant pressure | $y^* = f(x)$ |
| Rectifying Section | Upper portion of the column (above feed plate), enriching the vapor in MVC | — |
| Stripping Section | Lower portion of the column (below feed plate), stripping MVC from liquid | — |
| Operating Line | Material balance relationship between $y$ and $x$ in a section | — |
| Reflux Ratio | Ratio of liquid returned to distillate removed | $R = L/D$ |
| q-value | Thermal condition of feed (moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed) | $q$ |

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- The method works on the **x-y plane** where both axes are in mole fractions (range: 0 to 1).
- Standard reference pressure for equilibrium data: **1 atm** (101.325 kPa).
- Equilibrium relationship typically expressed via relative volatility ($\alpha$) for ideal systems: $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Two Pillars of McCabe-Thiele

The entire method rests on **alternating application** of two fundamental equations at each stage:

1. **Phase Equilibrium Relationship** (Thermodynamics): $y^* = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha-1)x]$
2. **Material Balance Relationship** (Mass Conservation): The operating line.

### 2.2 Component Material Balances — Derivation Framework

#### 2.2.1 Rectifying Section Operating Line (Above Feed)

For a control volume enclosing the top of the column and tray $n$:

$$V_{n+1} \cdot y_{n+1} = L_n \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

Under **Constant Molar Overflow (CMO)**: $L_n = L$ and $V_{n+1} = V$ (constant), so $V = L + D$.

The rectifying operating line (ROL) becomes:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

where $R = L/D$ is the reflux ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT] Board Exam Tip
> The ROL always passes through two fixed points: $(x_D, x_D)$ on the $y = x$ diagonal and $(x_D/(R+1), x_D)$ on the y-axis. The slope is $R/(R+1)$.

#### 2.2.2 Stripping Section Operating Line (Below Feed)

For a control volume enclosing the bottom of the column and tray $m$:

$$\bar{L} \cdot x_m = \bar{V}_{m+1} \cdot y_{m+1} - W \cdot x_W$$

Under CMO: $\bar{L} = L + qF$ and $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - W$.

The stripping operating line (SOL):

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{\bar{V}}$$

This line passes through $(x_W, x_W)$ on the diagonal and intersects the ROL at the feed stage point.

#### 2.2.3 The q-line (Feed Condition Line)

The locus of intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines depends on feed thermal condition:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

> [!NOTE] Special Feed Conditions (q-values)
> - **Saturated liquid feed:** $q = 1$ → vertical line at $x = z_F$
> - **Saturated vapor feed:** $q = 0$ → horizontal line at $y = z_F$
> - **Sub-cooled liquid:** $q > 1$ → line slopes upward to right
> - **Superheated vapor:** $q < 0$ → line slopes downward
> - **Two-phase feed:** $0 < q < 1$ → line slopes downward to right

### 2.3 Limiting Conditions

#### 2.3.1 Total Reflux Condition ($R \to \infty$)

When no product is withdrawn ($D = 0, W = 0$), all condensate returns as reflux. The operating lines **coincide with the diagonal** $y = x$. This represents the **minimum number of theoretical plates** ($N_{min}$), useful via the **Fenske Equation**:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[ \left( \dfrac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \right) \left( \dfrac{1 - x_W}{x_W} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

#### 2.3.2 Minimum Reflux Condition ($R_{min}$)

The operating line intersects the equilibrium curve at the feed point, giving an **infinite number of plates**. The pinch point is typically:
- For saturated liquid feed: intersection of q-line with equilibrium curve at $x = z_F$.

### 2.4 Real Plates and Efficiency

The McCabe-Thiele method gives **theoretical** plates. Actual plates are calculated using **Murphree Vapor Efficiency**:

$$E_{Mv} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y^* - y_{n+1}}$$

Actual number of plates: $N_{actual} = N_{theoretical} / E_{Mv}$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $y = \dfrac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$ | Equilibrium curve (constant $\alpha$) | mole fraction |
| 2 | $y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Rectifying Operating Line (ROL) | — |
| 3 | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | q-line (feed condition) | — |
| 4 | $y_{m+1} = \dfrac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x_m - \dfrac{W x_W}{\bar{V}}$ | Stripping Operating Line (SOL) | — |
| 5 | $\bar{L} = L + qF$ | Stripping section liquid flow | mol/s |
| 6 | $\bar{V} = \bar{L} - W$ | Stripping section vapor flow | mol/s |
| 7 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Fenske Equation | plates |
| 8 | $E_{Mv} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y^* - y_{n+1}}$ | Murphree Vapor Efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $V = D(R+1)$ | Vapor flow in rectifying section | mol/s |
| 10 | $L = R \cdot D$ | Liquid flow (reflux) | mol/s |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE] Industry Applications
> - **Petroleum Refining:** Design of crude oil distillation towers.
> - **Cryogenic Air Separation:** Production of high-purity oxygen and nitrogen.
> - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Solvent recovery and purification of API intermediates.
> - **Beverage Industry:** Ethanol-water separation for spirits production.
> - **Chemical Manufacturing:** Purification of monomers, fine chemicals, and specialty products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Rectifying Section Operating Line (ROL)

**Starting Point — Control Volume around top of column including condenser:**

Consider an envelope from the top tray down to tray $n$ above the feed. Under steady state:

- Vapor $V$ enters tray $n+1$ from below with composition $y_{n+1}$
- Liquid $L$ leaves tray $n$ going down to tray $n-1$ with composition $x_n$
- Distillate $D$ leaves the condenser with composition $x_D$
- Reflux $L$ returns from condenser

**Material Balance on More Volatile Component (MVC):**

$$V \cdot y_{n+1} = L \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

**Total Material Balance:**

$$V = L + D$$

**Introducing Reflux Ratio:** $R = L/D \Rightarrow L = R \cdot D$ and $V = D(R+1)$

Substitute into the component balance:

$$D(R+1) y_{n+1} = R \cdot D \cdot x_n + D \cdot x_D$$

Dividing throughout by $D(R+1)$:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{R}{R+1} x_n + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:**
- y-intercept: When $x_n = 0$, $y_{n+1} = x_D/(R+1)$
- x-intercept: When $y_{n+1} = 0$, $x_n = -x_D/R$ (negative, lies outside plot)
- Slope: $m = R/(R+1) < 1$
- Always passes through $(x_D, x_D)$ on the diagonal.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Stripping Section Operating Line (SOL)

**Control Volume from tray $m$ below feed to the reboiler:**

- Vapor $\bar{V}$ leaves tray $m+1$ going up with composition $y_{m+1}$
- Liquid $\bar{L}$ enters tray $m$ from above with composition $x_m$
- Bottoms $W$ leaves reboiler with composition $x_W$

**Component Material Balance:**

$$\bar{L} \cdot x_m = \bar{V} \cdot y_{m+1} - W \cdot x_W$$

**Total Material Balance:**

$$\bar{L} = \bar{V} + W$$

**CMO Relationships** (using feed condition $q$):

$$\bar{L} = L + qF, \quad \bar{V} = V + (q-1)F = D(R+1) + (q-1)F$$

The SOL rearranged:

$$y_{m+1} = \frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} x_m - \frac{W x_W}{\bar{V}}$$

**Geometric Interpretation:**
- Passes through $(x_W, x_W)$ on the $45°$ diagonal.
- Slope = $\bar{L}/\bar{V}$ (typically $> 1$).
- Intersects ROL at the feed stage point.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The q-line Equation

At the **feed plate**, the rectifying and stripping operating lines must intersect. Writing both lines and eliminating the common point gives the locus:

For saturated liquid feed ($q = 1$): The feed is entirely liquid entering, so $L' = L + F$, $V' = V$. By material balance on feed plate: $L' = L + qF$.

General feed balance: $qF = $ liquid fraction of feed. The relationship between flows on either side of feed plate is:

$$\bar{L} - L = qF, \quad \bar{V} - V = (q-1)F$$

The intersection point of ROL and SOL satisfies both lines. Eliminating $y$:

$$\frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

The line through this intersection point and the feed composition $(z_F, z_F)$ yields the **q-line**:

$$y = \frac{q}{q-1} x - \frac{z_F}{q-1}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (Full Board-Style Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
A continuous distillation column separates a benzene-toluene mixture. Given:
- Feed composition: $z_F = 0.40$ (mole fraction benzene)
- Distillate composition: $x_D = 0.95$
- Bottoms composition: $x_W = 0.05$
- Reflux ratio: $R = 1.5$
- Feed condition: Saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- Relative volatility: $\alpha = 2.47$

**Required:** Number of theoretical plates and the feed plate location.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Equilibrium Curve Equation**

$$y^* = \frac{2.47 x}{1 + (2.47 - 1) x} = \frac{2.47 x}{1 + 1.47 x}$$

**Step 2: Rectifying Operating Line**

$$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} x + \frac{0.95}{2.5} = 0.60 x + 0.38$$

- y-intercept = 0.38, slope = 0.60
- Passes through $(0.95, 0.95)$

**Step 3: Stripping Operating Line**

For $q = 1$ (saturated liquid): $\bar{L} = L + F$, $\bar{V} = V = D(R+1) = 2.5D$

Need $D/F$ ratio. Overall balance: $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$
$$D + W = F, \quad 0.40 F = 0.95 D + 0.05 W$$

Solving: $D/F = 0.389$, $W/F = 0.611$

$$\bar{L} = R \cdot D + F = 1.5(0.389)F + F = 1.583 F$$
$$\bar{V} = D(R+1) = 2.5(0.389)F = 0.972 F$$

Slope of SOL:
$$\frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} = \frac{1.583}{0.972} = 1.628$$

SOL passes through $(0.05, 0.05)$:
$$y = 1.628 x - 1.628(0.05) + 0.05 = 1.628 x - 0.0314$$

**Step 4: q-line (for $q = 1$):**
$$x = z_F = 0.40 \text{ (vertical line)}$$

**Step 5: Stepping off Plates**

Starting from $(x_D, x_D) = (0.95, 0.95)$, draw horizontal to equilibrium curve, then vertical to operating line. The transfer from ROL to SOL occurs at the feed point.

**Sample Computation — Plate 1:**
- Start: $(0.95, 0.95)$
- Horizontal to equilibrium: $y^* = 2.47(0.95)/[1 + 1.47(0.95)] = 0.991$
- New x: find $x$ on ROL when $y = 0.95$: $0.95 = 0.60 x + 0.38 \Rightarrow x = 0.950$

The stepping yields approximately:
- Rectifying plates: 5 stages (including partial reboiler)
- Stripping plates: 4 stages
- Feed plate: Stage 6 (counting from top)

**Total theoretical plates = 9 (including reboiler as a stage) or 8 actual trays.**

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart Summary

| Condition | Operating Line Slope | Behavior |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| $R \to \infty$ (Total Reflux) | 1.0 (along diagonal) | Minimum plates |
| $R = R_{min}$ | Variable (pinch point) | Infinite plates |
| $R_{min} < R < \infty$ | $R/(R+1)$ | Finite plates |
| $q = 1$ (saturated liquid) | Vertical q-line at $x = z_F$ | Liquid enters feed plate |
| $q = 0$ (saturated vapor) | Horizontal q-line at $y = z_F$ | Vapor enters feed plate |
| $q > 1$ (cold liquid) | Negative slope | Sub-cooled liquid |
| $q < 0$ (superheated vapor) | Positive slope $< 1$ | Vapor feed |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 McCabe-Thiele Stepping Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start at Point xD,xD on diagonal]) --> H1[Draw horizontal line to equilibrium curve]
    H1 --> V1[Mark intersection point - new x value]
    V1 --> Q1{Is x below feed intersection point?}
    Q1 -- No --> H2[Draw vertical line down to Rectifying Operating Line]
    H2 --> V2[Mark new point on ROL]
    V2 --> H1
    Q1 -- Yes --> H3[Switch to Stripping Operating Line]
    H3 --> V3[Draw vertical line down to SOL]
    V3 --> H4[Draw horizontal line to equilibrium curve]
    H4 --> V4[Mark intersection - new x value]
    V4 --> Q2{Is x greater than or equal to xW?}
    Q2 -- No --> H3
    Q2 -- Yes --> Count[Count total steps - theoretical plates]
    Count --> End([Total Theoretical Plates Determined])
```

### 4.2 Distillation Column — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TopSection[Rectifying Section]
        Cond[Condenser] --> RefluxDrum[Reflux Drum]
        RefluxDrum --> RefluxLine[Reflux Return L]
        RefluxDrum --> Distillate[D Distillate xD]
    end

    subgraph MiddleSection[Feed Plate Zone]
        FeedIn[Feed F zF] --> FeedPlate[Feed Plate nF]
    end

    subgraph BottomSection[Stripping Section]
        FeedPlate --> StrippingPlates[Stripping Plates]
        StrippingPlates --> Reboiler[Reboiler]
        Reboiler --> VaporReturn[Vapor Return bar V]
        Reboiler --> Bottoms[W Bottoms xW]
    end

    RefluxLine --> TopPlates[Rectifying Plates]
    TopPlates --> FeedPlate
    VaporReturn --> StrippingPlates

    style TopSection fill:#e1f5ff
    style MiddleSection fill:#fff4e1
    style BottomSection fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.3 McCabe-Thiele Plot — Coordinate Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plot y-x axes from 0 to 1] --> P2[Draw 45 degree diagonal line y equals x]
    P2 --> P3[Plot equilibrium curve y equals alpha x over 1 plus alpha minus 1 times x]
    P3 --> P4[Mark point xD,xD on diagonal]
    P4 --> P5[Compute ROL slope R divided by R plus 1]
    P5 --> P6[Draw Rectifying Operating Line through xD,xD]
    P6 --> P7[Draw q-line through zF,zF with slope q over q minus 1]
    P7 --> P8[Mark intersection point of ROL and q-line]
    P8 --> P9[Draw Stripping Operating Line through xW,xW and intersection point]
    P9 --> P10[Begin stepping from xD,xD to equilibrium curve]
    P10 --> P11[Count stages until xW is reached]
```

### 4.4 Feed Condition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Feed[Feed Enters Column] --> QType{What is thermal condition?}
    QType -- Saturated Liquid --> Q1[q equals 1: Vertical q-line at x equals zF]
    QType -- Saturated Vapor --> Q0[q equals 0: Horizontal q-line at y equals zF]
    QType -- Cold Liquid --> QGT[q greater than 1: Negative slope line]
    QType -- Superheated Vapor --> QLT[q less than 0: Positive slope line]
    QType -- Two Phase --> QTP[0 less than q less than 1: Downward sloping line]
    Q1 --> Out[Determine intersection point of ROL and q-line]
    Q0 --> Out
    QGT --> Out
    QLT --> Out
    QTP --> Out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
*Explain the significance of the q-line in the McCabe-Thiele method. How does the feed condition affect its slope?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The q-line represents the locus of intersection of rectifying and stripping operating lines. It indicates the thermal condition of the feed.
- Saturated liquid feed: $q = 1$ → vertical q-line at $x = z_F$
- Saturated vapor feed: $q = 0$ → horizontal q-line at $y = z_F$
- Cold liquid: $q > 1$ → negative slope
- Superheated vapor: $q < 0$ → positive slope
- Two-phase: $0 < q < 1$ → downward slope $q/(q-1)$

The intersection of q-line with the rectifying operating line determines the feed plate location. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
*Define the term "theoretical plate" and explain Murphree vapor efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **theoretical plate** is a hypothetical stage where the exiting vapor and liquid streams are in complete thermodynamic equilibrium. It represents the ideal contact between phases.

**Murphree Vapor Efficiency** ($E_{Mv}$) is defined as the ratio of the actual change in vapor composition on a tray to the change that would occur if the tray were at equilibrium:

$$E_{Mv} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y^* - y_{n+1}}$$

where $y^*$ is the vapor composition in equilibrium with $x_n$. If $E_{Mv} = 1$, the plate is ideal; if $E_{Mv} < 1$, the actual plate is less effective than a theoretical plate. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *(14 Marks)*

A binary distillation column is used to separate a mixture of methanol and water. The following data are given:

- Feed composition: $z_F = 0.30$ (mole fraction methanol)
- Distillate composition: $x_D = 0.90$
- Bottoms composition: $x_W = 0.05$
- Reflux ratio: $R = 2.0$
- Feed condition: Saturated liquid ($q = 1$)
- Relative volatility of methanol-water: $\alpha = 3.5$

**Part (a): Determine the equation of the equilibrium curve and operating lines. Construct the McCabe-Thiele diagram showing all relevant lines. (7 Marks)**

**Part (b): Using the constructed diagram, determine the number of theoretical plates required and locate the feed plate. Also calculate the minimum number of plates using Fenske equation. (7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Equilibrium Curve**

$$y^* = \frac{\alpha x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x} = \frac{3.5 x}{1 + 2.5 x}$$

Sample values:
- At $x = 0.10$: $y^* = 0.350 / 1.25 = 0.280$
- At $x = 0.30$: $y^* = 1.050 / 1.75 = 0.600$
- At $x = 0.50$: $y^* = 1.750 / 2.25 = 0.778$
- At $x = 0.70$: $y^* = 2.450 / 2.75 = 0.891$
- At $x = 0.90$: $y^* = 3.150 / 3.25 = 0.969$

**Step 2: Rectifying Operating Line**

$$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{2}{3} x + \frac{0.90}{3} = 0.667 x + 0.300$$

- Slope = 0.667
- y-intercept = 0.300
- Passes through $(0.90, 0.90)$

**Step 3: q-line ($q = 1$, saturated liquid):**

$$x = z_F = 0.30 \text{ (vertical line)}$$

**Step 4: Stripping Operating Line**

Material balance: $F = D + W$, $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$
$$0.30 F = 0.90 D + 0.05 W$$
$$D/F = (0.30 - 0.05)/(0.90 - 0.05) = 0.294, \quad W/F = 0.706$$

Flows:
$$\bar{V} = D(R+1) = 2.294 \cdot F \cdot 3 / F = 0.882 F$$
$$\bar{L} = R \cdot D + F = 2(0.294)F + F = 1.588 F$$

Slope of SOL:
$$\frac{\bar{L}}{\bar{V}} = \frac{1.588}{0.882} = 1.800$$

SOL passing through $(0.05, 0.05)$:
$$y = 1.800 x - 1.800(0.05) + 0.05 = 1.800 x - 0.040$$

**Intersection Point of ROL and SOL (Feed point):**
$$0.667 x + 0.300 = 1.800 x - 0.040$$
$$0.340 = 1.133 x$$
$$x_{int} = 0.300, \quad y_{int} = 0.667(0.300) + 0.300 = 0.500$$

**(7 marks: Equilibrium curve derivation + ROL + q-line + SOL + intersection)**

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Number of Theoretical Plates via Stepping**

Beginning at $(x_D, x_D) = (0.90, 0.90)$:

| Stage | $x_n$ | $y_n$ (on op line) | $y^*$ (equilibrium) | Operating Line |
|-------|-------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 0.900 | 0.900 | 0.969 | ROL |
| 2 | 0.832 | 0.855 | 0.948 | ROL |
| 3 | 0.760 | 0.807 | 0.918 | ROL |
| 4 | 0.671 | 0.747 | 0.873 | ROL |
| 5 | 0.580 | 0.687 | 0.821 | ROL |
| 6 (Feed) | 0.469 | 0.613 | 0.731 | SOL |
| 7 | 0.339 | 0.570 | 0.625 | SOL |
| 8 | 0.229 | 0.372 | 0.488 | SOL |
| 9 | 0.139 | 0.210 | 0.347 | SOL |
| Reboiler | 0.050 | 0.050 | — | — |

**Total theoretical stages = 9 (including reboiler)**
**Number of theoretical plates = 8 actual trays + 1 reboiler = 9 stages**
**Feed plate is at stage 6** (counted from the top).

**Step 2: Minimum Number of Plates (Fenske Equation):**

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_D}{1-x_D}\right)\left(\dfrac{1-x_W}{x_W}\right)\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{0.90}{0.10}\right)\left(\dfrac{0.95}{0.05}\right)\right]}{\ln 3.5}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln(9 \times 19)}{\ln 3.5} = \frac{\ln(171)}{1.253} = \frac{5.142}{1.253} = 4.10$$

$N_{min} \approx 5$ plates **(including reboiler)**

**(7 marks: Step-by-step plate count + feed plate location + Fenske calculation)**

---

#### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *(14 Marks)*

A continuous fractionating column operating at 1 atm separates an ethanol-water mixture. The equilibrium data for this system (mole fraction ethanol) are:

| x | 0.00 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.30 | 0.40 | 0.50 | 0.60 | 0.70 | 0.80 | 0.90 | 1.00 |
|---|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| y | 0.00 | 0.43 | 0.53 | 0.58 | 0.62 | 0.66 | 0.70 | 0.76 | 0.83 | 0.92 | 1.00 |

Given: $x_F = 0.45$, $x_D = 0.85$, $x_W = 0.05$, $R = 1.5$, saturated liquid feed.

**Part (a): Draw the equilibrium curve on x-y coordinates. Derive the rectifying operating line equation and determine its y-intercept. (7 Marks)**

**Part (b): Draw the q-line and determine the feed plate location. Calculate the number of theoretical stages required including the reboiler. (7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):**

The equilibrium curve is plotted using the tabulated data points. Standard plot:
- x-axis: liquid mole fraction (0 to 1)
- y-axis: vapor mole fraction (0 to 1)
- Curve starts at (0,0), passes through (0.5, 0.66), and approaches (1, 1)
- Plot is concave toward the diagonal (typical for non-ideal ethanol-water)

**Rectifying Operating Line:**

$$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} x + \frac{0.85}{2.5}$$

$$y = 0.60 x + 0.34$$

**y-intercept = 0.34** (when $x = 0$, $y = 0.34$)
**x-intercept** (when $y = 0$): $x = -0.567$ (outside plot region)
**Slope = 0.60**; passes through $(0.85, 0.85)$.

**(7 marks: Plot description + ROL derivation + intercepts)**

---

**Part (b):**

**q-line:** For saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$, so:
$$x = z_F = 0.45 \text{ (vertical line)}$$

**Intersection point of ROL and q-line:**
$$y = 0.60(0.45) + 0.34 = 0.270 + 0.34 = 0.610$$

So intersection at $(0.45, 0.610)$ — this is the **feed point**.

**Stripping Operating Line:** 
Passes through $(0.05, 0.05)$ and $(0.45, 0.610)$:

Slope $= (0.610 - 0.05)/(0.45 - 0.05) = 0.560/0.400 = 1.400$

$$y = 1.400 x - 1.400(0.05) + 0.05 = 1.400 x - 0.020$$

**Stepping off Plates:**

Starting from $(0.85, 0.85)$, alternating horizontal to equilibrium curve and vertical to operating line.

| Step | Action | x-coordinate | y-coordinate |
|------|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Start | — | 0.850 | 0.850 |
| 1 | Vertical to ROL | 0.850 | 0.850 |
| 2 | Horizontal to eq. | 0.788 | 0.823 |
| 3 | Vertical to ROL | 0.689 | 0.753 |
| 4 | Horizontal to eq. | 0.620 | 0.712 |
| 5 | Vertical to ROL | 0.535 | 0.661 |
| 6 | Horizontal to eq. | 0.455 | 0.615 |
| 7 | Vertical to SOL (crossing feed point) | 0.421 | 0.569 |
| 8 | Horizontal to eq. | 0.330 | 0.520 |
| 9 | Vertical to SOL | 0.243 | 0.320 |
| 10 | Horizontal to eq. | 0.155 | 0.247 |
| 11 | Vertical to SOL | 0.092 | 0.109 |
| 12 | Horizontal to eq. | 0.045 | 0.067 |
| 13 | Vertical to SOL | 0.050 | 0.050 |
| Reboiler | — | 0.050 | 0.050 |

**Total theoretical stages ≈ 13** (12 plates + 1 reboiler)
**Feed plate location: Stage 7** (counted from top, where crossing occurs at $x \approx 0.421$)

**(7 marks: q-line + intersection + SOL + plate count + feed plate)**

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Mistake:** Forgetting to switch from ROL to SOL at the feed stage. **Marks lost:** 1-2 marks on plate count.
> 2. **Mistake:** Using wrong slope formula for SOL. **Correct:** $\bar{L}/\bar{V}$, NOT $R/(R+1)$.
> 3. **Mistake:** Treating the reboiler as a regular plate. **Correct:** Reboiler counts as one theoretical stage but is hardware (not a tray).
> 4. **Mistake:** Calculating $D/F$ and $W/F$ incorrectly. **Verify:** $D/F + W/F = 1$ and $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$.
> 5. **Mistake:** Plotting equilibrium curve above the diagonal for some points (only happens for non-ideal minimum-boiling azeotropes like ethanol-water). For ethanol-water, an azeotrope exists near $x \approx 0.89$.
> 6. **Mistake:** Forgetting that $x_D, y^*$ values may exceed 1.0 due to plotting error. Always verify dimensional correctness.
> 7. **Mistake:** For saturated vapor feed ($q = 0$), drawing q-line as horizontal but writing $x = z_F$. Correct: $y = z_F$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist
> - **McCabe-Thiele** = graphical method for theoretical plates in **binary distillation**.
> - Based on **two alternating principles**: phase equilibrium + material balance.
> - **Assumes CMO** (Constant Molar Overflow).
> - **Rectifying Operating Line:** $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1} x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ — passes through $(x_D, x_D)$, slope $R/(R+1)$.
> - **Stripping Operating Line:** passes through $(x_W, x_W)$ and feed intersection point.
> - **q-line:** $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ — represents feed thermal condition.
> - **Saturated liquid:** $q = 1$, vertical q-line at $x = z_F$.
> - **Saturated vapor:** $q = 0$, horizontal q-line at $y = z_F$.
> - **Total reflux** → minimum plates via **Fenske equation**.
> - **Minimum reflux** → infinite plates (pinch point at q-line and equilibrium).
> - **Murphree efficiency** converts theoretical to actual plates: $N_{actual} = N_{theoretical}/E_{Mv}$.
> - **Stepping rule:** Horizontal → equilibrium; Vertical → operating line.
> - **Switch from ROL to SOL** at the feed plate stage.
> - **Reboiler** counts as one theoretical stage.
> - **Equilibrium curve** for constant $\alpha$: $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1)x]$.
> - **Optimal feed plate** = stage where switching lines gives minimum total plates.
> - **Always verify** material balance: $F = D + W$ and $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Volatility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relative Volatility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Volatility ($\alpha_{AB}$)** is defined as the ratio of the vapor–liquid equilibrium distribution (K-value) of component A to that of component B, under the same temperature and pressure. It quantifies the **ease of separation** between two components by distillation.

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{(y_A / x_A)}{(y_B / x_B)}$$

Where:
- $y_A, y_B$ = mole fractions of A and B in the **vapor phase**
- $x_A, x_B$ = mole fractions of A and B in the **liquid phase**
- $K_A, K_B$ = equilibrium vaporization ratios (K-values)

For a **binary system** where A is the more volatile component:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{y_A / x_A}{(1 - y_A) / (1 - x_A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **By KTU 2024 convention**, the more volatile component is always subscripted first (A > B in volatility), so $\alpha_{AB} \geq 1$.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Pass"

Imagine two hikers (A and B) trying to climb out of a valley into the vapor phase (the mountain top). Hiker A is a champion mountaineer — he reaches the top easily. Hiker B is exhausted and rarely makes it. **Relative volatility is like comparing their success rates.** If A succeeds **twice as often** as B, then $\alpha = 2$ — separation is straightforward. If their success rates are **identical** ($\alpha = 1$), no amount of effort will separate them — this is the famous **azeotropic condition**.

### Critical Interpretation Rules

> [!NOTE]
> **Magnitude Guide for KTU Examinations:**
>
> | $\alpha$ Value | Physical Meaning |
> | :---: | :--- |
> | $\alpha = 1$ | Azeotrope — separation by ordinary distillation is **impossible** |
> | $1 < \alpha < 1.05$ | Very difficult — requires **extractive / azeotropic** distillation |
> | $1.05 < \alpha < 2$ | Moderate difficulty — requires **many equilibrium stages** |
> | $\alpha > 2$ | Easy separation — **standard distillation** is feasible |

**Physical Constants Referenced:**
- Universal Gas Constant: $R = 8.314 \; \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- Standard Reference: 1 atm total pressure (most undergraduate problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Framework — Why and How

**Step 1 — Why do we need relative volatility?**
Equilibrium stage design (McCabe–Thiele, Ponchon–Savarit) requires the full x–y equilibrium curve. For a binary system with **constant $\alpha$**, this curve is fully defined by a single parameter — drastically simplifying analysis.

**Step 2 — How is it derived from Raoult's Law?**
For an ideal binary solution obeying Raoult's Law ($p_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$), and Dalton's Law for vapor ($p_i = y_i P$):

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P}$$

Therefore:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$

This holds **only when Raoult's Law is valid** (ideal solutions). For non-ideal systems, activity coefficients must be introduced:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{\gamma_A P_A^{sat}}{\gamma_B P_B^{sat}}$$

**Step 3 — How does it behave with temperature?**
Since $P_i^{sat}$ is a strong function of temperature, $\alpha$ varies along the column. However, for many systems (e.g., benzene–toluene), the variation is mild, and an **average $\alpha$** is used in design.

**Step 4 — How does it simplify the equilibrium curve?**
The x–y equilibrium relation for a binary system with constant $\alpha$ becomes:

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$$

This is the **most-tested equation** in KTU Module 4 problems.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every equation a KTU student must memorize for Relative Volatility problems.**

| # | Equation / Relation | Variables / Units | Application |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$ | Dimensionless | General definition |
| 2 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B}$ | Dimensionless | K-value definition |
| 3 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$ | Pressure ratio | Ideal solutions (Raoult's Law) |
| 4 | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{\gamma_A P_A^{sat}}{\gamma_B P_B^{sat}}$ | Activity $\gamma$ | Non-ideal solutions |
| 5 | $y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) \, x_A}$ | Mole fraction | Binary equilibrium curve |
| 6 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ (x_{LK,D}/x_{LK,B}) (x_{HK,B}/x_{HK,D}) \right]}{\ln(\alpha)}$ | Number of stages | **Fenske Equation** (Total Reflux) |
| 7 | $(y_A/x_A) = \alpha \, \dfrac{1 - x_A}{1 - y_A}$ | Dimensionless | Alternative form for binary |
| 8 | $\alpha_{BA} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha_{AB}}$ | Dimensionless | Reciprocity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subscript Convention in Formula 6:**
> - LK = Light Key (more volatile in distillate)
> - HK = Heavy Key (less volatile in distillate)
> - D = Distillate composition, B = Bottoms composition

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Petroleum Refining | Crude oil fractionation into gasoline, diesel, kerosene |
| Pharmaceutical | Solvent recovery and purification |
| Cryogenic Air Separation | Separation of N₂, O₂, Ar |
| Food Industry | Alcohol distillation (ethanol–water) |
| Petrochemicals | Ethylene–ethane, propylene–propane splitters |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — The Equilibrium Curve Equation $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1)x]$

**Starting Point — Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) for Binary Mixture:**

$$\alpha = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$

**Step 1 — Express in terms of mole fractions only**

Using $y_B = 1 - y_A$ and $x_B = 1 - x_A$ (binary constraint):

$$\alpha = \frac{y_A / x_A}{(1 - y_A) / (1 - x_A)}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply**

$$\alpha \, (1 - y_A)(x_A) = y_A (1 - x_A)$$

Expanding both sides:

$$\alpha \, x_A - \alpha \, x_A \, y_A = y_A - y_A \, x_A$$

**Step 3 — Collect $y_A$ terms on one side**

Bring all $y_A$ terms to the left-hand side:

$$\alpha \, x_A = y_A - y_A \, x_A + \alpha \, x_A \, y_A$$

Factor $y_A$ from the right-hand side:

$$\alpha \, x_A = y_A \left[ 1 - x_A + \alpha \, x_A \right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $y_A$**

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 - x_A + \alpha \, x_A}$$

Rewrite the denominator by adding and subtracting $x_A$:

$$y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$$

**Result — Final Equilibrium Relation:**

$$\boxed{\,y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation B — The Fenske Equation (Minimum Number of Stages at Total Reflux)

**Physical Premise:**
At total reflux, no product is withdrawn. The operating lines for both rectifying and stripping sections coincide with the diagonal $y = x$. Under this condition, the number of stages required is the **minimum possible** for the given separation.

**Step 1 — Define the K-value for component i**

$$K_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_i = K_i \, x_i$$

**Step 2 — Relative volatility definition**

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_A = \alpha \, K_B$$

**Step 3 — VLE across successive stages**

At total reflux, consider vapor leaving stage $n$ to be in equilibrium with liquid on stage $n$, and this liquid passes down to stage $n+1$ where it contacts vapor from stage $n+2$, etc.

For stage $n$:

$$\left(\frac{y_A}{y_B}\right)_n = \alpha \, \left(\frac{x_A}{x_B}\right)_n$$

For stage $n+1$:

$$\left(\frac{y_A}{y_B}\right)_{n+1} = \alpha \, \left(\frac{x_A}{x_B}\right)_{n+1}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the operating line equality**

At total reflux, the rectifying operating line becomes $y_{n+1} = x_n$ (vapor composition equals liquid composition on adjacent stages). So:

$$\left(\frac{x_A}{x_B}\right)_{n+1} = \left(\frac{y_A}{y_B}\right)_n$$

Chaining across $N_{min}$ stages from the reboiler (bottom) to the condenser (top):

$$\left(\frac{x_{A,N}}{x_{B,N}}\right)_{top} = \alpha^{N_{min}} \left(\frac{x_{A,0}}{x_{B,0}}\right)_{bottom}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce product compositions**

The top stage liquid composition equals the distillate composition $x_D$ (total condenser), and the reboiler liquid equals the bottoms composition $x_B$:

$$\frac{x_{A,D}}{x_{B,D}} = \alpha^{N_{min}} \cdot \frac{x_{A,B}}{x_{B,B}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $N_{min}$**

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$N_{min} \ln(\alpha) = \ln\left(\frac{x_{A,D}}{x_{B,D}}\right) - \ln\left(\frac{x_{A,B}}{x_{B,B}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,N_{min} = \frac{\ln\left[\left(\dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,B}}\right)\left(\dfrac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{HK,D}}\right)\right]}{\ln(\alpha)}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A benzene–toluene mixture has $\alpha_{BT} = 2.47$. Calculate the vapor composition $y$ in equilibrium with a liquid containing $x = 0.30$ mol fraction benzene.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the equilibrium equation**

$$y = \frac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} = \frac{2.47 \times 0.30}{1 + (2.47 - 1) \times 0.30}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.7410$$

**Step 3 — Denominator**

$$\text{Denominator} = 1 + (1.47)(0.30) = 1 + 0.4410 = 1.4410$$

**Step 4 — Final computation**

$$y = \frac{0.7410}{1.4410} = 0.5142$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since $y > x$ (0.5142 > 0.30), benzene concentrates in the vapor phase, confirming it is the more volatile component. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relative Volatility in a Distillation Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Feed: xA, xB] --> B[Equilibrium Stage N]
    C[Vapor Feed: yA, yB] --> B
    B --> D[Vapor Leaving: yA_N, yB_N]
    B --> E[Liquid Leaving: xA_N, xB_N]
    D --> F[Relative Volatility Definition]
    E --> F
    F --> G["alpha_AB = yA/xA divided by yB/xB"]
    G --> H{Compare alpha to 1}
    H -->|alpha > 1| I[Separation Feasible]
    H -->|alpha = 1| J[Azeotrope - No Separation]
    H -->|alpha less than 1| K[Invert Labels]
    I --> L[Apply Fenske Equation]
    L --> M[Compute N_min]
    M --> N[Design Distillation Column]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Inputs and Outputs of Relative Volatility Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        P1[Liquid xA]
        P2[Liquid xB]
        P3[Saturation Pressure PA_sat]
        P4[Saturation Pressure PB_sat]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[Computation Block]
        Q1[K_A = PA_sat / P]
        Q2[K_B = PB_sat / P]
        Q3[alpha_AB = K_A / K_B]
        Q4[y_A = alpha xA / 1 + alpha-1 xA]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Results]
        R1[Equilibrium yA]
        R2[Minimum Stages N_min]
        R3[Separation Feasibility Flag]
    end
    P1 --> Q4
    P3 --> Q1
    P4 --> Q2
    Q1 --> Q3
    Q2 --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q4
    Q3 --> R2
    Q4 --> R1
    Q3 --> R3
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Flow — Constant vs Variable Alpha

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose VLE Model] --> T{Constant alpha assumption valid?}
    T -->|Yes, narrow boiling range| U[Use Single alpha value]
    T -->|No, wide boiling range| V[Compute alpha per stage]
    U --> W1[Apply Fenske directly]
    V --> W2[Stage-wise alpha integration]
    W1 --> X[Design Column with N_min]
    W2 --> X
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical stage in the analysis. The flow proceeds from input parameters (compositions and pressures) through the K-value and relative volatility computation, ultimately yielding either the equilibrium vapor composition or the minimum number of stages for design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define relative volatility. What is its significance in distillation column design?**

**Model Answer:**
Relative volatility $\alpha_{AB}$ is defined as the ratio of the K-values (or vapor–liquid distribution coefficients) of two components A and B in a mixture:

$$\alpha_{AB} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{y_A / x_A}{y_B / x_B}$$

For ideal solutions, this simplifies to the ratio of vapor pressures. Its **significance** is threefold:
1. **Quantitative measure of separability** — the higher $\alpha$, the easier the separation.
2. **Determines minimum stages** via Fenske equation.
3. **Identifies azeotropes** — when $\alpha = 1$, separation by ordinary distillation is impossible.

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **State the Fenske equation. Under what operating condition is it applicable?**

**Model Answer:**
The Fenske equation gives the **minimum number of equilibrium stages** required for a specified separation at total reflux:

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,B}}\right) \left(\dfrac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{HK,D}}\right) \right]}{\ln(\alpha_{LK,HK})}$$

**Operating condition:** Total reflux — meaning no feed is introduced and no products are withdrawn. The rectifying and stripping operating lines both coincide with the diagonal $y = x$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

**Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 carries an internal choice. Students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relation $y_A = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A}$ for a binary ideal solution obeying Raoult's Law.

**Model Solution:**

Starting from Raoult's Law:

$$p_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat} \quad \text{and} \quad p_i = y_i \, P$$

Dividing:

$$\frac{y_i}{x_i} = \frac{P_i^{sat}}{P} = K_i$$

For two components A and B:

$$\alpha = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$

Now, for the vapor phase:

$$K_A = \frac{y_A}{x_A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_A = K_A \, x_A$$

$$K_B = \frac{y_B}{x_B} = \frac{1 - y_A}{1 - x_A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - y_A = K_B (1 - x_A)$$

Forming the ratio $\alpha = \dfrac{y_A / x_A}{(1 - y_A)/(1 - x_A)}$:

$$\alpha \, x_A (1 - y_A) = (1 - x_A) y_A$$

Expanding:

$$\alpha \, x_A - \alpha \, x_A y_A = y_A - x_A y_A$$

Rearranging:

$$\alpha \, x_A = y_A (1 - x_A + \alpha \, x_A)$$

$$\therefore \quad y_A = \frac{\alpha \, x_A}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A benzene–toluene mixture containing 40 mol% benzene is to be separated to give a distillate with 90 mol% benzene and bottoms with 5 mol% benzene. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.50$. Calculate the **minimum number of equilibrium stages** using the Fenske equation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Light Key (LK) and Heavy Key (HK):**
- LK = Benzene (more volatile)
- HK = Toluene (less volatile)

**Step 2 — List compositions:**

| Stream | $x_{LK}$ (Benzene) | $x_{HK}$ (Toluene) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Distillate (D) | 0.90 | 0.10 |
| Bottoms (B) | 0.05 | 0.95 |

**Step 3 — Apply Fenske Equation:**

$$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,B}}\right) \left(\dfrac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{HK,D}}\right) \right]}{\ln(\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$\frac{x_{LK,D}}{x_{LK,B}} = \frac{0.90}{0.05} = 18.0$$

$$\frac{x_{HK,B}}{x_{HK,D}} = \frac{0.95}{0.10} = 9.5$$

$$\text{Product} = 18.0 \times 9.5 = 171.0$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithms:**

$$\ln(171.0) = 5.1417$$

$$\ln(2.50) = 0.9163$$

**Step 6 — Final computation:**

$$N_{min} = \frac{5.1417}{0.9163} = 5.612$$

**Step 7 — Round up (since stages must be whole numbers):**

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 6 \; \text{stages}}$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of relative volatility in distillation column design. Discuss the limiting cases $\alpha = 1$ and $\alpha = \infty$.

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:**
1. **Predicts ease of separation** — a higher $\alpha$ means a smaller column is needed (fewer trays, lower reflux).
2. **Determines reflux ratio** via Underwood's equation.
3. **Computes minimum stages** via Fenske equation.
4. **Guides selection** between ordinary, extractive, or azeotropic distillation.

**Limiting Case 1: $\alpha = 1$ (Azeotrope)**
- The vapor and liquid compositions are identical ($y_A = x_A$).
- No separation is achievable by ordinary distillation.
- Examples: Ethanol–water at 95.6 mol% ethanol; benzene–cyclohexane.
- **Solution:** Use extractive distillation (add a third solvent) or pressure-swing distillation.

**Limiting Case 2: $\alpha \to \infty$**
- The volatile component is infinitely more volatile than the heavy component.
- Only **one ideal stage** is needed for complete separation.
- Practically achieved when vapor pressures differ by orders of magnitude (e.g., separating a dissolved gas from a liquid).

**Intermediate Cases:**
- $\alpha < 1.05$: Requires hundreds of stages or specialized techniques.
- $\alpha = 1.05$ to $2$: Standard distillation, moderate column height.
- $\alpha > 2$: Economical, compact columns.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a benzene–toluene system at 1 atm, the relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$. Calculate the vapor composition in equilibrium with liquid containing $x = 0.30$ mol fraction benzene. Also compute the equilibrium data for $x = 0.1, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9$ and tabulate.

**Model Solution:**

**Equilibrium Relation:**

$$y = \frac{\alpha \, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x} = \frac{2.47 \, x}{1 + 1.47 \, x}$$

**Detailed Calculation for $x = 0.30$:**

$$y = \frac{2.47 \times 0.30}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.30} = \frac{0.7410}{1.4410} = 0.5142$$

**Tabulated Equilibrium Data:**

| Liquid $x$ (Benzene) | Vapor $y$ (Benzene) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 0.10 | 0.2154 |
| 0.30 | 0.5142 |
| 0.50 | 0.7118 |
| 0.70 | 0.8521 |
| 0.90 | 0.9570 |

**Sample Computation for $x = 0.50$:**

$$y = \frac{2.47 \times 0.50}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.50} = \frac{1.2350}{1.7350} = 0.7118$$

**Sample Computation for $x = 0.90$:**

$$y = \frac{2.47 \times 0.90}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.90} = \frac{2.2230}{2.3230} = 0.9570$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** At every point, $y > x$, confirming benzene is the more volatile component and concentrates preferentially in the vapor phase. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the logarithm base** — Fenske equation uses **natural logarithm (ln)**, not $\log_{10}$. Marks lost if conversion is wrong by factor of 2.303.
> 2. **Confusing LK and HK subscripts** — Always write $(x_{LK,D}/x_{LK,B})$ first, then $(x_{HK,B}/x_{HK,D})$. Reversing the order gives the reciprocal of the correct argument.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up** — $N_{min}$ must be the **next whole number** (ceiling). Writing $N_{min} = 5.6$ without rounding loses the final mark.
> 4. **Using $P$ instead of $P_i^{sat}$** — In Raoult's Law, the K-value uses **saturation pressure of component i**, not total pressure (unless total pressure equals saturation pressure, which is only true for pure components).
> 5. **Mixing up $\alpha_{AB}$ vs $\alpha_{BA}$** — Always maintain the convention that A is the more volatile component. The relation $\alpha_{BA} = 1/\alpha_{AB}$ must be applied if reversed.
> 6. **Azeotrope trap** — Never conclude that $\alpha > 1$ guarantees easy separation. Always check $\alpha \to 1$ at any composition.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relative Volatility (Module 4)**

- [x] **Definition:** $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{y_A/x_A}{y_B/x_B}$ — dimensionless
- [x] **Ideal solution form:** $\alpha = P_A^{sat} / P_B^{sat}$ (ratio of vapor pressures)
- [x] **Non-ideal form:** $\alpha = (\gamma_A P_A^{sat}) / (\gamma_B P_B^{sat})$ with activity coefficients
- [x] **Binary equilibrium equation:** $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x]$ — **most-tested formula**
- [x] **Fenske equation:** $N_{min} = \ln[(x_{LK,D}/x_{LK,B})(x_{HK,B}/x_{HK,D})] / \ln(\alpha)$ — for total reflux
- [x] **Azeotrope condition:** $\alpha = 1$ — ordinary distillation fails
- [x] **Convention:** A is always the more volatile component, so $\alpha_{AB} \geq 1$
- [x] **Reciprocity:** $\alpha_{BA} = 1/\alpha_{AB}$
- [x] **Typical $\alpha$ values:** Benzene–Toluene $\approx 2.47$; Ethanol–Water $\approx 2.5$ (forms azeotrope)
- [x] **Variation:** $\alpha$ generally decreases as the mixture becomes richer in the heavy component
- [x] **Effect of pressure:** Lower pressure typically **increases $\alpha$** (favoring separation) for most systems
- [x] **Design impact:** Higher $\alpha$ $\Rightarrow$ fewer stages, lower reflux ratio, smaller column diameter
- [x] **Limiting cases:** $\alpha = 1$ (azeotrope, impossible), $\alpha \to \infty$ (one stage suffices)
- [x] **Units:** Always **dimensionless** — never attach units to $\alpha$
- [x] **KTU favorite trick:** Always recompute $N_{min}$ by **ceiling** to next whole integer

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Relative Volatility** | **KTU 2024 Scheme | PEPOT413**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation: Single and Multiple Effect Evaporators, Performance of Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT413 - HEAT & MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evaporation: Single and Multiple Effect Evaporators, Performance of Evaporators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Evaporation?

**Evaporation** is a unit operation in which a volatile solvent (most commonly **water**) is vaporized from a non-volatile solute (such as salts, sugars, or alkalies) in order to produce a concentrated solution. The process is fundamentally a **heat-transfer operation accompanied by a phase change**, in which the feed solution is boiled at a temperature determined by the pressure of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Evaporation lies at the intersection of **Heat Transfer** and **Mass Transfer**. The solvent leaves as vapor while the solute remains in the liquid phase, thereby achieving separation and concentration.

The volatile component removed is called the **vapour** (or overhead product), and the concentrated residual liquid is called the **thick liquor** or simply the **liquor**. A piece of equipment used to carry out this operation is called an **evaporator**.

### Formal KTU Definition

> An **evaporator** is a heat exchanger designed to concentrate a solution by removing the solvent (typically water) through vaporization, with the latent heat of vaporization being supplied by a heating medium (most commonly **saturated steam** at a pressure higher than that of the boiling liquid).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

**The Tea-Pan Analogy** 🍵

Imagine boiling water in a tea pan on a stove to make thick **chai concentrate**. As the water boils off, the chai becomes thicker and stronger. The flame supplies the heat needed to vaporize the water. The steam escapes into the air, and the leftover is your concentrated chai.

In an industrial evaporator, this is exactly what happens — but instead of a tea pan, we use a closed vessel called a **calandria** or **shell-and-tube heat exchanger**, and instead of a flame, we use **steam** as the heating medium. The steam gives up its **latent heat** to the solution, vaporizes the water, and the resulting **vapour** is then condensed and/or reused in the next stage.

## 1.3 Why is Evaporation Important?

- **Sugar industry**: Concentrating cane juice from ~15% to ~70% solids before crystallization.
- **Salt production**: Sea water → brine → crystalline salt.
- **Dairy industry**: Concentrating milk from ~12% to ~50% total solids.
- **Caustic & Chlor-alkali**: Concentrating NaOH solution.
- **Paper & Pulp**: Concentrating black liquor for recovery boilers.
- **Pharmaceuticals & Chemicals**: Producing syrups, extracts, and intermediates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Evaporation ≠ Drying. In evaporation, the solute is **non-volatile** and remains in the liquid. In drying, the moisture is removed as vapour from a **solid** matrix. Evaporation produces a more concentrated **liquid**, drying produces a **solid**.

## 1.4 Methods of Feeding Steam & Vapour

| Configuration | Description |
|---|---|
| **Single Effect** | Steam used in only one evaporator; vapour from the boiling liquid is discarded or condensed. |
| **Multiple Effect (Forward Feed)** | Feed and steam/vapour flow in the same direction (1st → 2nd → 3rd). |
| **Multiple Effect (Backward Feed)** | Feed flows opposite to the steam/vapour flow direction. |
| **Multiple Effect (Parallel Feed)** | Feed is supplied separately to each effect; vapour flows in series. |

## 1.5 Performance Criteria — Economy & Capacity

Two fundamental metrics are used to evaluate evaporator performance:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Capacity** = Total mass of water evaporated per unit time (kg/h or kg/s).
> - **Steam Economy** = (Mass of water evaporated) / (Mass of live steam supplied). It is a **dimensionless ratio**.

A higher economy means the system uses steam more efficiently. A single-effect evaporator has an economy of less than 1 (typically 0.8–0.9), while a triple-effect evaporator can approach an economy of ~2.5–3.0.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Evaporator

A standard industrial evaporator consists of:

1. **Calandria (Heating Section)** — A bundle of tubes inside a shell. Steam condenses on the shell side, transferring its latent heat through the tube walls to the boiling liquid on the tube side.
2. **Vapour Space** — The region above the boiling liquid where the vapour separates from the liquid.
3. **Vapour Outlet** — Carries the solvent vapour away for condensation or use in the next effect.
4. **Condensate Outlet** — Drains the condensed heating steam.
5. **Feed Inlet & Concentrated Liquor Outlet** — Handles the feed and the thick liquor discharge.

## 2.2 Single Effect Evaporator — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 System Description

A **single-effect evaporator** uses steam in only one vessel. The vapour generated from the boiling liquid is sent to a **condenser** (and often a vacuum pump) and is not reused for heating.

**Reference Diagram (Schematic):**

```
   LIVE STEAM  →  ┌──────────────┐  ←  CONDENSATE OUT
                 │              │
                 │  CALANDRIA   │
                 │  (Shell)     │
                 │              │
                 └──────┬───────┘
                        │
                 ┌──────┴───────┐
                 │   TUBES      │ ← BOILING LIQUID
                 │              │    (FEED)
                 └──────┬───────┘
                        │
                   VAPOUR  →→→  CONDENSER
                        │
                 LIQUOR (CONCENTRATED) OUT
```

### 2.2.2 Material Balance

Let:
- $F$ = mass flow rate of feed (kg/h)
- $L$ = mass flow rate of concentrated liquor leaving (kg/h)
- $V$ = mass flow rate of vapour produced (kg/h)
- $x_F$ = mass fraction of solute in feed
- $x_L$ = mass fraction of solute in concentrated liquor

**Overall Mass Balance:**
$$F = L + V$$

**Solute (Solids) Balance:**
$$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$

Combining these two:
$$V = F - L = F \left( 1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L} \right)$$

### 2.2.3 Energy Balance

Let:
- $T_s$ = temperature of saturated steam (K)
- $T_F$ = temperature of feed (K)
- $T_v$ = temperature of vapour leaving (K)
- $T_1$ = temperature of boiling liquid (K)
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of the steam (kJ/kg)
- $\lambda_v$ = latent heat of the vapour produced from the solution (kJ/kg)
- $C_p$ = specific heat of the feed/liquor (kJ/kg·K)

**Total Heat Supplied by Steam** (assuming condensate leaves at $T_s$):
$$Q_s = S \cdot \lambda_s$$

where $S$ = mass flow rate of live steam (kg/h).

**Heat Required** = Sensible heat to raise feed to boiling + Sensible heat due to boiling point elevation + Latent heat to vaporize water:
$$Q_s = F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_1 - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda_v$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $F \cdot C_p \cdot (T_1 - T_F)$ accounts for heating the feed from its entry temperature $T_F$ to the boiling point $T_1$. This is sometimes called the **"preheating load."**

### 2.2.4 Steam Economy of a Single Effect

$$\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v + \frac{F \cdot C_p (T_1 - T_F)}{V}}$$

For most practical situations where $F \cdot C_p (T_1 - T_F)$ is small compared to $V \cdot \lambda_v$ (i.e., feed enters near boiling point), the economy approaches $\lambda_s / \lambda_v$. Since $\lambda_s$ and $\lambda_v$ are roughly similar at low pressures, economy ≈ 0.8 to 0.9.

---

## 2.3 Multiple Effect Evaporator — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Concept

In a **multiple-effect evaporator**, the vapour produced in the first effect is used as the **heating medium** for the second effect. This cascading continues for $n$ effects, drastically reducing live steam consumption.

**Key Idea:** The pressure (and therefore boiling temperature) decreases progressively from effect 1 to effect $n$, so that vapour from effect 1 can boil the liquid in effect 2, and so on.

### 2.3.2 Pressure and Temperature Profile

| Effect | Pressure | Temperature | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Effect 1 | Highest (P₁) | Highest (T₁) | Heated by live steam |
| Effect 2 | Lower (P₂) | Lower (T₂) | Heated by vapour from Effect 1 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Effect n | Lowest (Pₙ) | Lowest (Tₙ) | Often under vacuum |

The temperature differences must be sufficient to transfer heat: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta T_i = T_1 - T_n$.

### 2.3.3 Material and Energy Balance for n Effects

**Total Material Balance** (over the whole system):
$$F = L_n + V_{\text{total}}$$

where $V_{\text{total}} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n$ is the total vapour produced and $L_n$ is the final concentrated liquor.

**Overall Solids Balance:**
$$F \cdot x_F = L_n \cdot x_L$$

**Steam Economy (Overall):**
$$E = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n}{S}$$

**Approximate Rule of Thumb (for equal feed rates and equal areas per effect):**
$$E \approx \frac{0.8 \times n}{1 + (0.1 \times n)} \quad \text{(Carn's correlation, rough estimate)}$$

A more commonly cited approximation is:
$$E \approx 0.85 \times n \quad \text{(for small } n\text{)}$$

So for $n = 3$ effects, $E \approx 2.5$ to $3.0$.

---

## 2.4 Feeding Arrangements in Multiple Effect Evaporators

### 2.4.1 Forward Feed
- Feed enters **Effect 1** and flows to **Effect n** in the same direction as steam/vapour.
- Feed moves from **high pressure → low pressure** (so it flows naturally by gravity).
- **Disadvantage:** The most concentrated liquor meets the **coldest** heating vapour, leading to high viscosity and low heat-transfer coefficient in the last effect.

### 2.4.2 Backward Feed
- Feed enters **Effect n** and flows to **Effect 1**, **opposite** to the steam/vapour direction.
- Concentrated liquor meets the **hottest** heating vapour → better heat transfer for viscous liquids.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires **pumps** between effects because flow is from low to high pressure.

### 2.4.3 Parallel Feed
- Feed is supplied **separately** to each effect, and the concentrated liquor from each is the final product.
- Used for crystallizing solutions (sugar, salt) where the slurry cannot be pumped easily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Forward feed is most commonly asked because it's the simplest and most natural. Always draw the schematic clearly and label pressures $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ and temperatures $T_1 > T_2 > T_3$.

---

## 2.5 Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) and Duhring's Rule

The boiling point of a solution is **higher** than that of pure water at the same pressure. This is called **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**.

$$\Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = T_{\text{solution}} - T_{\text{water}}$$

**Duhring's Rule:** The boiling point of a solution at one pressure is a **linear function** of the boiling point of pure water at another pressure.

$$\frac{T_{\text{solution at } P_1} - T_{\text{solution at } P_2}}{T_{\text{water at } P_1} - T_{\text{water at } P_2}} = \text{constant}$$

This is used to estimate BPE at the operating pressure from tabulated BPE values at standard pressure.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = L + V$ | Overall mass balance (single effect) |
| 2 | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | Solute (solids) balance |
| 3 | $V = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Water evaporated |
| 4 | $Q = S \cdot \lambda_s = F \cdot C_p (T_1 - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda_v$ | Energy balance (single effect) |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | Steam economy (single effect) |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n}{S}$ | Steam economy (n effects) |
| 7 | $C = V_{\text{total}}$ | Capacity (kg water/h) |
| 8 | $\sum \Delta T_i = T_1 - T_n$ | Total available temperature difference |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{BPE}} = T_{\text{soln}} - T_{\text{water}}$ | Boiling point elevation |
| 10 | $A_i = \dfrac{Q_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | Heat-transfer area per effect |
| 11 | $E_{\text{multi}} \approx 0.8 \text{ to } 0.85 \times n$ | Approximate economy for n effects |
| 12 | $q_i = U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T_i$ | Rate of heat transfer per effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Always write units alongside numerical values. Marks are often lost when students write "2.5" without specifying "kg water/kg steam."

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Energy Audit**: Multiple-effect evaporators can reduce steam consumption by up to **70%** compared to a single-effect system — critical in energy-intensive industries.
- **Heat Integration**: The cascading vapour streams can be coupled with **Multiple Effect Distillation (MED)** and **Mechanical Vapour Recompression (MVR)** for seawater desalination.
- **Process Intensification**: Modern evaporators combine evaporation and crystallization in **Forced Circulation Evaporators (FCE)** for fouling-prone solutions.
